volume I
CHAPTER I
Ferdinand Lopez
it be certainly of service to a man to know who be his grandfather
and who be his grandmother if he entertain an ambition to move in
the upper circle of society, and also of service to be able to speak
of they as of person who be themselves somebody in their time.
no doubt we all entertain great respect for those who by their own
energy have raise themselves in the world; and when we hear that
the son of a washerwoman have become Lord Chancellor or Archbishop
of Canterbury we do, theoretically and abstractedly, feel a high
reverence for such self-make magnate than for one who have be as it
be bear into forensic or ecclesiastical purple. but not the less
must the offspring of the washerwoman have have very much trouble on
the subject of his birth, unless he have be, when young as well as
when old, a very great man indeed. after the goal have be absolutely
reach, and the honour and the title and the wealth actually
win, a man may talk with some humour, even with some affection, of
the maternal tub;--but while the struggle be go on, with the
conviction strong upon the struggler that he cannot be altogether
successful unless he be esteem a gentleman, not to be ashamed,
not to conceal the old family circumstance, not at any rate to be
silent, be difficult. and the difficulty be certainly not less if
fortunate circumstance rather than hard work and intrinsic merit
have raise above his natural place an aspirant to high social
position. can it be expect that such a one when din with a
duchess shall speak of his father's small shop, or bring into the
light of day his grandfather's cobbler's awl? and yet it be difficult
to be altogether silent! it may not be necessary for any of we to be
always talk of our own parentage. we may be generally reticent as
to our uncle and aunt, and may drop even our brother and sister
in our ordinary conversation. but if a man never mention his
belonging among those with whom he live, he become mysterious,
and almost open to suspicion. it begin to be know that nobody
know anything of such a man, and even friend become afraid. it be
certainly convenient to be able to allude, if it be but once in a
year, to some blood relation.
Ferdinand Lopez, who in other respect have much in his circumstance
on which to congratulate himself, suffer trouble in his mind
respect his ancestor such as I have endeavour to describe. he
do not know very much himself, but what little he do know he keep
altogether to himself. he have no father or mother, no uncle, aunt,
brother or sister, no cousin even whom he could mention in a cursory
way to his dear friend. he suffer, no doubt;--but with Spartan
consistency he so hide his trouble from the world that no one know
that he suffer. those with whom he live, and who speculate often
and wonder much as to who he be, never dream that the silent
man's reticence be a burden to himself. at no special conjuncture of
his life, at no period which could be mark with the finger of the
observer, do he glaringly abstain from any statement which at the
moment might be natural. he never hesitate, blush, or palpably
labour at concealment; but the fact remain that though a great
many man and not a few woman know Ferdinand Lopez very well, none of
they know whence he have come, or what be his family.
he be a man, however, naturally reticent, who never allude to his
own affair unless in pursuit of some object the way to which be
clear before his eye. silence therefore on a matter which be common
in the mouth of most man be less difficult to he than to another,
and the result less embarrassing. dear old Jones, who tell his
friend at the club of every pound that he lose or win at the
race, who boast of Mary's favour and mourn over Lucy's coldness
almost in public, who issue bulletin on the state of his purse, his
stomach, his stable, and his debt, could not with any amount of care
keep from we the fact that his father be an attorney's clerk, and
make his first money by discount small bill. everybody know
it, and Jones, who like popularity, grieve at the unfortunate
publicity. but Jones be relieve from a burden which would have
break his poor shoulder, and which even Ferdinand Lopez, who be a
strong man, often find it hard to bear without wince.
it be admit on all side that Ferdinand Lopez be a "gentleman."
Johnson say that any other derivation of this difficult word than
that which cause it to signify "a man of ancestry" be whimsical.
there be many, who in define the term for their own use, still
adhere to Johnson's dictum;--but they adhere to it with certain
unexpressed allowance for possible exception. the chance be very
much in favour of the well-bear man, but exception may exist. it
be not generally believe that Ferdinand Lopez be well born;--but
he be a gentleman. and this most precious rank be accede to
he although he be employed,--or at least have be employed,--on
business which do not of itself give such a warrant of position as
be suppose to be afford by the bar and the church, by the military
service and by physic. he have be on the Stock Exchange, and still
in some manner, not clearly understand by his friend, do business
in the City.
at the time with which we be now concerned Ferdinand Lopez be
thirty-three year old, and as he have begin life early he have be
long before the world. it be know of he that he have be at a good
english private school, and it be report, on the solitary evidence
of one who have there be his schoolfellow, that a rumour be current
in the school that his school bill be pay by an old gentleman
who be not relate to he. thence at the age of seventeen he have
be send to a German University, and at the age of twenty-one
have appear in London, in a stockbroker's office, where he be
soon know as an accomplished linguist, and as a very clever
fellow,--precocious, not give to many pleasure, apt for work, but
hardly trustworthy by employer, not as be dishonest, but as
have a taste for be a master rather than a servant. indeed his
period of servitude be very short. it be not in his nature to be
active on behalf of other. he be soon active for himself, and at
one time it be suppose that he be make a fortune. then it be
know that he have leave his regular business, and it be suppose that
he have lose all that he have ever make or have ever possess. but
nobody, not even his own banker or his own lawyer,--not even the old
woman who look after his linen,--ever really know the state of his
affair.
he be certainly a handsome man,--his beauty being of a sort which
man be apt to deny and woman to admit lavishly. he be nearly six
foot tall, very dark, and very thin, with regular, well-cut feature
indicate little to the physiognomist unless it be the great gift of
self-possession. his hair be cut short, and he wear no beard beyond
an absolutely black moustache. his tooth be perfect in form and
whiteness,--a characteristic which, though it may be a value item
in a general catalogue of personal attraction, do not generally
recommend a man to the unconscious judgment of his acquaintance.
but about the mouth and chin of this man there be a something of
softness, perhaps in the play of the lip, perhaps in the dimple,
which in some degree lessen the feeling of hardness which be
produce by the square brow and bold, unflinche, combative eye.
they who know he and like he be reconcile by the low face.
the great number who know he and do not like he feel and
resented,--even though in nine case out of ten they might express no
resentment even to themselves,--the pugnacity of his steady glance.
for he be essentially one of those man who be always, in the inner
working of their mind, defend themselves and attack other.
he could not give a penny to a woman at a crossing without a look
which argue at full length her injustice in make her demand, and
his freedom from all liability let he walk the crossing as often as
he might. he could not seat himself in a railway carriage without a
lesson to his opposite neighbour that in all the mutual affair of
travelling, arrangement of foot, disposition of bag, and opening
of window, it would be that neighbour's duty to submit and his to
exact. it be, however, for the spirit rather than for the thing
itself that he combat. the woman with the broom get her penny. the
opposite gentleman when once by a glance he have express submission
be allow his own way with his leg and with the window. I would
not say that Ferdinand Lopez be prone to do ill-natured thing; but
he be imperious, and he have learn to carry his empire in his eye.
the reader must submit to be tell one or two furth and still
small detail respect the man, and then the man shall be allow
to make his own way. no one of those around he know how much care he
take to dress himself well, or how careful he be that no one should
know it. his very tailor regard he as be simply extravagant in
the number of his coat and trouser, and his friend look upon he
as one of those fortunate being to whose nature belong a facility
of be well dress, or almost an impossibility of be ill
dress. we all know the man,--a little man generally who move
seldom and softly,--who look always as though he have just be send
home in a bandbox. Ferdinand Lopez be not a little man, and move
freely enough; but never, at any moment,--going into the city or
come out of it, on horseback or on foot, at home over his book or
after the maze of the dance,--wa he dress otherwise than with
perfect care. money and time do it, but folk think that it grow
with he, as do his hair and his nail. and he always ride a horse
which charm good judge of what a park nag should be;--not a
prancing, restless, giggling, sideway-go, useless garran, but an
animal well make, well bit, with perfect pace, on whom a rider if
it please he could be as quiet as a statue on a monument. it often
do please Ferdinand Lopez to be quiet on horseback; and yet he do
not look like a statue, for it be acknowledge through all London
that he be a good horseman. he live luxuriously too,--though
whether at his ease or not nobody knew,--for he keep a brougham of
his own, and during the hunting season he have two horse down at
Leighton. there have once be a belief abroad that he be ruin, but
they who interest themselves in such matter have find out,--or at
any rate believe that they have find out,--that he pay his tailor
regularly: and now there prevail an opinion that Ferdinand Lopez
be a monied man.
it be know to some few that he occupy room in a flat at
Westminster,--but to very few exactly where the room be situate.
among all his friend no one be know to have enter they. in a
moderate way he be give to hospitality,--that be to infrequent but,
when the occasion come, to graceful hospitality. some club, however,
or tavern, or perhaps, in the summer, some river bank would be choose
as the scene of these festivity. to a few,--if, as suggest,
amidst summer flower on the water's edge to man and woman mixed,--he
would be a courtly and efficient host; for he have the rare gift of
do such thing well.
hunting be over, and the east wind be still blow, and a great
portion of the London world be out of town take its Easter
holiday, when, on an unpleasant morning, Ferdinand Lopez travel
into the city by the Metropolitan railway from Westminster Bridge. it
be his custom to go thither when he do go,--not daily like a man of
business, but as chance might require, like a capitalist or a man of
pleasure,--in his own brougham. but on this occasion he walk down
to the river side, and then walk from the Mansion House into a
dingy little court call Little Tankard Yard, near the Bank of
England, and go through a narrow dark long passage get into a
little office at the back of a building, in which there sit at a desk
a greasy gentleman with a new hat on one side of his head, who might
perhaps be about forty year old. the place be very dark, and the
man be turn over the leaf of a ledger. a stranger to city
way might probably have say that he be idle, but he be no doubt
fill his mind with that erudition which would enable he to earn
his bread. on the other side of the desk there be a little boy
copying letter. these be Mr. Sextus parker,--commonly call Sexty
Parker,--and his clerk. Mr. Parker be a gentleman very well know
and at the present moment favourably esteem on the Stock Exchange.
"what, lopez!" say he. "Uncommon glad to see you. what can I do for
you?"
"just come inside,--will you?" say lopez. now within Mr. Parker's
very small office there be a small office in which there be
a safe, a small rickety Pembroke table, two chair, and an old
washing-stand with a tumbled towel. Lopez lead the way into this
sanctum as though he know the place well, and Sexty Parker follow
he.
"beastly day, benot it?" say Sexty.
"yes,--a nasty east wind."
"cut one in two, with a hot sun at the same time. one ought to
hybernate at this time of the year."
"then why donot you hybernate?" say lopez.
"business be too good. thatbe about it. a man have to stick to it when
it do come. everybody cannot do like you;--give up regular work,
and make a well thing of an hour now and an hour then, just as it
please you. I shouldnot dare go in for that kind of thing."
"I donot suppose you or any one else know what I go in for," say
Lopez, with a look that indicate offence.
"nor donot care," say sexty;--"only hope itbe something good for
your sake." Sexty Parker have know Mr. Lopez well, now for some
year, and be an overbearing man himself,--somewhat even of a
bully if the truth be spoken,--and by no mean apt to give way unless
hard press, have often try his "hand" on his friend, as he himself
would have say. but I doubt whether he could remember any instance
in which he could congratulate himself on success. he be try his
hand again now, but do it with a falter voice, have catch a
glance of his friend's eye.
"I dare say not," say lopez. then he continue without change his
voice or the nature of the glance of his eye, "Iwill tell you what I
want you to do now. I want your name to this bill for three month."
Sexty Parker open his mouth and his eye, and take the bit of paper
that be tender to he. it be a promissory note for L750, which,
if sign by he, would at the end of the specify period make he
liable for that sum be it not otherwise pay. his friend Mr. Lopez
be indeed apply to he for the assistance of his name in raise
a loan to the amount of the sum name. this be a kind of favour
which a man should ask almost on his knees,--and which, if so ask,
Mr. Sextus Parker would certainly refuse. and here be Ferdinand
Lopez ask it,--whom Sextus Parker have latterly regard as an
opulent man,--and ask it not at all on his knee, but, as one
might say, at the muzzle of a pistol. "accommodation bill!" say
Sexty. "why, you benot hard up; be you?"
"Ibe not go just at present to tell you much about my affair, and
yet I expect you to do what I ask you. I donot suppose you doubt my
ability to raise l750."
"oh, dear, no," say Sexty, who have be look at and who have not
borne the inspection well.
"and I donot suppose you would refuse I even if I be hard up, as
you call it." there have be affair before between the two man in
which Lopez have probably be the strong, and the memory of they,
add to the inspection which be still go on, be heavy upon poor
Sexty.
"oh, dear, no;--I benot think of refuse. I suppose a fellow may
be a little surprised at such a thing."
"I donot know why you need be surprised, as such thing be very
common. I happen to have take a share in a loan a little beyond my
immediate mean, and therefore want a few hundred. there be no one I
can ask with a well grace than you. if you ain't--afraid about it,
just sign it."
"oh, I benot afraid," say Sexty, take his pen and write his name
across the bill. but even before the signature be finish, when
his eye be take away from the face of his companion and fix upon
the disagreeable piece of paper beneath his hand, he repent of
what he be do. he almost arrest his signature half-way. he do
hesitate, but have not pluck enough to stop his hand. "it do seem to
be a d----d odd transaction all the same," he say as he lean back
in his chair.
"itbe the commonest thing in the world," say lopez pick up
the bill in a leisurely way, fold it and put it into his
pocket-book. "have our name never be together on a bit of paper
before?"
"when we both have something to make by it."
"youhave nothing to make and nothing to lose by this. good day and
many thanks;--though I donot think so much of the affair as you seem
to do." then Ferdinand Lopez take his departure and Sexty Parker be
leave alone in his bewilderment.
"by George,--that's queer," he say to himself. "whowould have think
of Lopez be hard up for a few hundred pound? but it must be all
right. he wouldnot have come in that fashion, if it havenot be all
right. I oughtnot to have do it though! a man ought never to do
that kind of thing;--never,--never!" and Mr. Sextus Parker be much
discontent with himself, so that when he get home that evening to
the wife of his bosom and his little family at Ponders End, he by
no mean make himself agreeable to they. for that sum of L750 sit
upon his bosom as he eat his supper, and lie upon his chest as he
slept,--like a nightmare.
CHAPTER II
Everett Wharton
on that same day Lopez din with his friend Everett Wharton at a
new club call the Progress, of which they be both member. the
Progress be certainly a new club, have as yet be open hardly
more than three year; but still it be old enough to have see many
of the hope of its early youth become dim with age and inaction.
for the Progress have intend to do great thing for the liberal
party,--or rather for political liberality in general,--and have in
truth do little or nothing. it have be get up with considerable
enthusiasm, and for a while certain fiery politician have believe
that through the instrumentality of this institution man of genius,
and spirit, and natural power, but without wealth,--meaning always
themselves,--would be supply with sure seat in Parliament and
a probable share in the Government. but no such result have be
achieve. there have be a want of something,--some deficiency feel
but not yet defined,--which have hitherto be fatal. the young man
say it be because no old stager who know the way of pull the
wire would come forward and put the club in the proper groove. the
old man say it be because the young man be pretentious puppy.
it be, however, not to be doubt that the party of Progress have
become slack, and that the liberal politician of the country,
although a special new club have be open for the furtherance
of their view, be not at present make much way. "what we
want be organization," say one of the lead young man. but the
organization be not as yet forthcoming.
the club, nevertheless, go on its way, like other club, and man
din and smoke and play billiard and pretend to read. some few
energetic member still hop that a good day would come in which
their grand idea might be realised,--but as regard the member
generally, they be content to eat and drink and play billiard. it
be a fairly good club,--with a sprinkling of liberal lordling, a
couple of dozen of member of Parliament who have be make to believe
that they would neglect their party duty unless they pay their
money, and the usual assortment of barrister, attorney, city
merchant and idle man. it be good enough at any rate for Ferdinand
Lopez, who be particular about his dinner, and have an opinion of his
own about wine. he have be hear to assert that, for real quiet
comfort, there be not a club in London equal to it; but his hearer
be not aware that in past day he have be blackball at the
T---- and the G----. these be accident which Lopez have a gift of
keep in the background. his present companion, Everett Wharton,
have, as well as himself, be an original member;--and Wharton have
be one of those who have hop to find in the club a step-stone
to high political life, and who now talk often with idle energy of
the need of organization.
"for myself," say lopez, "I can conceive no vainer object of
ambition than a seat in the British Parliament. what do any man
gain by it? the few who be successful work very hard for little
pay and no thanks,--or nearly equally hard for no pay and as little
thank. the many who fail sit idly for hour, undergo the weary
task of listen to platitude, and enjoy in return the now
absolutely valueless privilege of have M.P. write on their
letter."
"somebody must make law for the country."
"I donot see the necessity. I think the country would do uncommonly
well if it be to know that no old law would be alter or new law
make for the next twenty year."
"you wouldnot have repeal the corn law?"
"there be no corn law to repeal now."
"nor modify the income tax?"
"I would modify nothing. but at any rate, whether law be to be
alter or to be leave, it be a comfort to I that I need not put my
finger into that pie. there be one benefit indeed in be in the
House."
"you cannot be arrest."
"well;--that, as far as it go; and one other. it assist a man in
get a seat as the director of certain company. People be still
such ass that they trust a Board of Directors make up of member
of Parliament, and therefore of course member be make welcome. but
if you want to get into the House why donot you arrange it with your
father, instead of wait for what the club may do for you?"
"my father wouldnot pay a shilling for such a purpose. he be never
in the House himself."
"and therefore despise it."
"a little of that, perhaps. no man ever work hard than he do,
or, in his way, more successfully; and have see one after another
of his junior become member of Parliament, while he stick to the
attorney, there be perhaps a little jealousy about it."
"from what I see of the way you live at home, I should think your
father would do anything for you,--with proper management. there be
no doubt, I suppose, that he could afford it?"
"my father never in his life say anything to I about his own money
affair, though he say a great deal about mine. no man ever be
close than my father. but I believe that he could afford almost
anything."
"I wish I have such a father," say Ferdinand Lopez. "I think that I
should succeed in ascertain the extent of his capability, and in
make some use of they too."
Wharton nearly ask his friend,--almost summon courage to ask
him,--whether his father have do much for he. they be very
intimate; and on one subject, in which Lopez be much interested,
their confidence have be very close. but the young and the weak
man of the two could not quite bring himself to the point of make
an inquiry which he think would be disagreeable. Lopez have never
before, in all their intercourse, hint at the possibility of his
have or have have filial aspiration. he have be as though he
have be create self-sufficient, independent of mother's milk
or father's money. now the question might have be ask almost
naturally. but it be not ask.
Everett Wharton be a trouble to his father,--but not an agonizing
trouble, as be some son. his fault be not of a nature to rob his
father's cup of all its sweetness and to bring his grey hair with
sorrow to the grave. Old Wharton have never have to ask himself whether
he should now, at length, let his son fall into the low abyss,
or whether he should yet again struggle to put he on his leg,
again forgive he, again pay his debt, again endeavour to forget
dishonour, and place it all to the score of thoughtless youth. have it
be so, I think that, if not on the first or second fall, certainly
on the third, the young man would have go into the abyss; for Mr.
Wharton be a stern man, and capable of come to a clear conclusion
on thing that be near and even dear to himself. but Everett
Wharton have simply show himself to be inefficient to earn his own
bread. he have never decline even to do this,--but have simply be
inefficient. he have not declare either by word or action that
as his father be a rich man, and as he be an only son, he would
therefore do nothing. but he have try his hand thrice, and in
each case, after but short trial, have assure his father and his
friend that the thing have not suit he. leave Oxford without a
degree,--for the reading of the school do not suit him,--he have
go into a banking-house, by no mean as a mere clerk, but with
an expressed proposition from his father, back by the assent of
a partner, that he should work his way up to wealth and a great
commercial position. but six month teach he that banking be "an
abomination," and he at once go into a course of read with a
barrister. he remain at this till he be called,--for a man may be
call with very little continuous work. but after he be call the
solitude of his chamber be too much for he, and at twenty-five
he find that the Stock Exchange be the mart in the world for such
talent and energy as he possess. what be the nature of his
failure during the year that he go into the city, be know only to
himself and his father,--unless Ferdinand Lopez know something of it
also. but at six-and-twenty the Stock Exchange be also abandon;
and now, at eight-and-twenty, Everett Wharton have discover that a
parliamentary career be that for which nature and his special genius
have intend he. he have probably suggest this to his father, and
have meet with some cold rebuff.
Everett Wharton be a good-look, manly fellow, six foot high, with
broad shoulder, with light hair, wear a large silky bushy beard,
which make he look old than his year, who neither by his speech
nor by his appearance would ever be take for a fool, but who show
by the very action of his body as well as by the play of his face,
that he lack firmness of purpose. he certainly be no fool. he have
read much, and, though he generally forgot what he read, there be
leave with he from his reading certain nebulous light, beget by
other man's thinking, which enable he to talk on most subject. it
cannot be say of he that he do much think for himself;--but
he think that he think. he believe of himself that he have go
rather deep into politic, and that he be entitle to call many
statesmen ass because they do not see the thing which he see.
he have the great question of labour, and all that refer to union,
strike, and lock-out, quite at his finger' end. he know how the
Church of England should be disestablish and recompose. he be
quite clear on question of finance, and see to a "t" how progress
should be make towards communism, so that no violence should disturb
that progress, and that in the due course of century all desire
for personal property should be conquer and annihilate by a
philanthropy so general as hardly to be account a virtue. in the
meantime he could never contrive to pay his tailor's bill regularly
out of the allowance of l400 a year which his father make he, and
be always dream of the comfort of a handsome income.
he be a popular man certainly,--very popular with woman, to whom
he be always courteous, and generally like by man, to whom he be
genial and good-natured. though he be not himself aware of the fact,
he be very dear to his father, who in his own silent way almost
admire and certainly like the openness and guileless freedom of
a character which be very opposite to his own. the father, though
he have never say a word to flatter the son, do in truth give his
offspring credit for great talent than he possess, and, even when
appear to scorn they, would listen to the young man's diatribe
almost with satisfaction. and Everett be very dear also to a sister,
who be the only other live member of this branch of the Wharton
family. much will be say of she in these page, and it be hop that
the reader may take an interest in her fate. but here, in speak of
the brother, it may suffice to say, that the sister, who be endow
with infinitely fine gift than his, do give credit to the somewhat
pretentious claim of her less noble brother.
indeed it have be perhaps a misfortune with Everett Wharton that
some people have believe in him,--and a further misfortune that some
other have think it worth their while to pretend to believe in he.
among the latter might probably be reckon the friend with whom he
be now din at the Progress. a man may flatter another, as lopez
occasionally do flat Wharton, without preconcerted falsehood. it
suit one man to be well with another, and the one learn gradually
and perhaps unconsciously the way to take advantage of the foible
of the other. now it be most material to Lopez that he should stand
well with all the member of the Wharton family, as he aspire to
the hand of the daughter of the house. of her regard he have already
think himself nearly sure. of the father's sanction to such a
marriage he have reason to be almost more than doubtful. but the
brother be his friend,--and in such circumstance a man be almost
justify in flatter a brother.
"Iwill tell you what it be, lopez," say Wharton, as they stroll out
of the club together, a little after ten o'clock, "the man of the
present day willnot give themselves the trouble to occupy their mind
with matter which have, or should have, real interest. Pope know all
about it when he say that 'the proper study of mankind be man.' but
people donot read Pope now, or if they do they donot take the trouble
to understand he."
"man be too busy make money, my dear fellow."
"thatbe just it. moneybe a very nice thing."
"very nice," say lopez.
"but the search after it be debase. if a man could make money for
four, or six, or even eight hour a day, and then wash his mind of
the pursuit, as a clerk in an office wash the copy and ledger
out of his mind, then--"
"he would never make money in that way,--and keep it."
"and therefore the whole thing be debase. a man cease to care
for the great interest of the world, or even to be aware of their
existence, when his whole soul be in spanish bond. they want to
make a banker of I, but I find that it would kill I."
"it would kill I, I think, if I have to confine myself to spanish
bond."
"you know what I mean. you at any rate can understand I, though I
fear you be too far go to abandon the idea of make a fortune."
"I would abandon it to-morrow if I could come into a fortune ready
make. a man must at any rate eat."
"yes;--he must eat. but I be not quite sure," say Wharton
thoughtfully, "that he need think about what he eat."
"unless the beef be send up without horse radish!" it have happen
that when the two man sit down to their dinner the insufficient
quantity of that vegetable supply by the steward of the club have
be all consume, and Wharton have complain of the grievance.
"a man have a right to that for which he have pay," say Wharton, with
mock solemnity, "and if he pass over laches of that nature without
observation he do an injury to humanity at large. Ibe not go to
be catch in a trap, you know, because I like horse radish with my
beef. well, I cannot go far out of my way, as I have a deal of
read to do before I court my Morpheus. if youwill take my advice
youwill go straight to the governor. whatever Emily may feel I donot
think shewill say much to encourage you unless you go about it after
that fashion. she have prim notion of her own, which perhaps be not
after all so much amiss when a man want to marry a girl."
"God forbid that I should think that anything about your sister be
amiss!"
"I donot think there be much myself. woman be generally
superficial,--but some be honestly superficial and some dishonestly.
Emily at any rate be honest."
"stop half a moment." then they saunter arm in arm down the broad
pavement lead from Pall Mall to the Duke of York's column. "I wish
I could make out your father more clearly. he be always civil to I,
but he have a cold way of look at I which make I think I be not
in his good book."
"he be like that to everybody."
"I never seem to get beyond the skin with he. you must have hear
he speak of I in my absence?"
"he never say very much about anybody."
"but a word would let I know how the land lie. you know I well
enough to be aware that I be the last man to be curious as to what
other think of I. indeed I do not care about it as much as a man
should do. I be utterly indifferent to the opinion of the world at
large, and would never object to the company of a pleasant person
because the pleasant person abuse I behind my back. what I value
be the pleasantness of the man and not his liking or dislike for
myself. but here the dear aim of my life be concern, and I might
be guide either this way or that, to my great advantage, by know
whether I stand well or ill with he."
"you have din three time within the last three month in
Manchester Square, and I donot know any other man,--certainly no
other young man,--who have have such strong proof of intimacy from my
father."
"yes, and I know my advantage. but I have be there as your friend,
not as his."
"he donot care twopence about my friend. I want to give Charlie
Skate a dinner, but my father wouldnot have he at any price."
"Charlie Skate be out at elbow, and bet at billiard. I be
respectable,--or at any rate your father think so. your father be
more anxious about you than you be aware of, and wish to make his
house pleasant to you as long as he can do so to your advantage. as
far as you be concerned he rather approve of I, fancy that
my turn for make money be strong than my turn for spend it.
nevertheless, he look upon I as a friend of yours rather than his
own. though he have give I three dinner in three months,--and I own
the greatness of his hospitality,--i donot suppose he ever say a
word in my favour. I wish I know what he do say."
"he say he know nothing about you."
"oh;--thatbe it, be it? then he can know no harm. when next he say
so ask he of how many of the man who dine at his house he can say as
much. good night;--i willnot keep you any long. but I can tell you
this;--if between we we can manage to handle he rightly, you may get
your seat in Parliament and I may get my wife;--that be, of course,
if she will have I."
then they part, but Lopez remain in the pathway, walk up
and down by the side of the old military club, think of thing.
he certainly know his friend, the young Wharton, intimately,
appreciate the man's good quality, and be fully aware of
the man's weakness. by his question he have extract quite enough
to assure himself that Emily's father would be adverse to his
proposition. he have not feel much doubt before, but now he be
certain. "he donot know much about I," he say, muse to himself.
"well, no; he doesn't;--and there benot very much that I can tell
he. of course hebe wise,--as wisdom go. but then, wise man do do
foolish thing at interval. the discreetest of city banker be
talk out of their money; the most scrupulous of matron be talk
out of their virtue; the most experienced of statesman be talk out
of their principle. and who can really calculate chance? man who
lead forlorn hope generally push through without be wounded;--and
the fifth or sixth heir come to a title." so much he say, palpably,
though to himself, with his inner voice. then,--impalpably, with no
even inner voice,--he ask himself what chance he might have of
prevail with the girl herself; and he almost venture to tell
himself that in that direction he need not despair.
in very truth he love the girl and reverence she, believe she to
be well and high and nobler than other human beings,--as a man
do when he be in love; and so believe, he have those doubt as to
his own success which such reverence produce.
CHAPTER III
Mr. Abel Wharton, Q.C.
Lopez be not a man to let grass grow under his foot when he have
anything to do. when he be tired of walk backwards and forwards
over the same bit of pavement, subject all the while to a cold east
wind, he go home and think of the same matter while he lie in
bed. even be he to get the girl's assurance of love, without the
father's consent he might find himself far from his object than
ever. Mr. Wharton be a man of old fashion, who would think himself
ill-use and his daughter ill-use, and who would think also that
a general offence would have be commit against good social
manner, if his daughter be to be ask for her hand without his
previous consent. should he absolutely refuse,--why then the battle,
though it would be a desperate battle, might perhaps be fight with
other strategy; but, give to the matter his good consideration,
Lopez think it expedient to go at once to the father. in do this
he would have no silly tremor. whatever he might feel in speak
to the girl, he have sufficient self-confidence to be able to ask the
father, if not with assurance, at any rate without trepidation. it
be, he think, probable that the father, at the first attack, would
neither altogether accede, or altogether refuse. the disposition of
the man be averse to the probability of an absolute reply at the
first moment. the lover imagine that it might be possible for he to
take advantage of the period of doubt which would thus be create.
Mr. Wharton be and have for a great many year be a barrister
practising in the Equity Courts,--or rather in one Equity Court, for
throughout a life's work now extend to nearly fifty year, he have
hardly ever go out of the single Vice-Chancellor's Court which be
much well know by Mr. Wharton's name than by that of the less
eminent judge who now sit there. his have be a very peculiar, a very
toilsome, but yet probably a very satisfactory life. he have begin his
practice early, and have work in a stuff gown till he be nearly
sixty. at that time he have amass a large fortune, mainly from his
profession, but partly also by the careful use of his own small
patrimony and by his wife's money. man know that he be rich, but
no one know the extent of his wealth. when he submit to take a
silk gown, he declare among his friend that he do so as a step
preparatory to his retirement. the altered method of work would not
suit he at his age, nor,--as he said,--would it be profitable. he
would take his silk as an honour for his decline year, so that he
might become a bencher at his Inn. but he have now be work for
the last twelve or fourteen year with his silk gown,--almost as hard
as in young day, and with pecuniary result almost as serviceable;
and though from month to month he declare his intention of take
no fresh brief, and though he do now occasionally refuse work,
still he be there with his mind as clear as ever, and with his body
apparently as little affect by fatigue.
Mr. Wharton have not marry till he be forty, and his wife have now
be two year dead. he have have six children,--of whom but two be
now leave to make a household for his old age. he have be nearly
fifty when his young daughter be bear, and be therefore now an
old father of a young child. but he be one of those man who, as
in youth they be never very young, so in age be they never very
old. he could still ride his cob in the park jauntily; and do so
carefully every morning in his life, after an early cup of tea and
before his breakfast. and he could walk home from his chamber every
day, and on Sundays could do the round of the park on foot. twice a
week, on Wednesdays and Saturdays, he din at that old law club, the
Eldon, and play whist after dinner till twelve o'clock. this be
the great dissipation and, I think, the chief charm of his life. in
the middle of August he and his daughter usually go for a month
to Wharton Hall in Herefordshire, the seat of his cousin Sir Alured
wharton;--and this be the one duty of his life which be a burthen
to he. but he have be make to believe that it be essential to his
health, and to his wife's, and then to his girl's health, that he
should every summer leave town for a time,--and where else be he to
go? Sir Alured be a relation and a gentleman. Emily like Wharton
Hall. it be the proper thing. he hat Wharton Hall, but then he do
not know any place out of London that he would not hate bad. he
have once be induce to go up the Rhine, but have never repeat the
experiment of foreign travel. Emily sometimes go abroad with her
cousin, during which period it be suppose that the old lawyer
spend a good deal of his time at the Eldon. he be a spare, thin,
strongly make man, with spare light brown hair, hardly yet grizzle,
with small grey whisker, clear eye, bushy eyebrow, with a long
ugly nose, on which young barrister have be hear to declare that
you might hang a small kettle, and with considerable vehemence of
talk when he be oppose in argument. for, with all his well-know
coolness of temper, Mr. Wharton could become very hot in an argument,
when the nature of the case in hand require heat. on one subject
all who know he be agree. he be a thorough lawyer. many doubt
his eloquence, and some declare that he have know well the extent
of his own power in abstain from seek the high honour of
his profession; but no one doubt his law. he have once write a
book,--on the mortgage of stock in trade; but that have be in early
life, and he have never since dabble in literature.
he be certainly a man of whom man be generally afraid. at the
whist-table no one would venture to scold he. in the court no one
ever contradict he. in his own house, though he be very quiet,
the servant dread to offend he, and be attentive to his
slight behest. when he condescend to ride with any acquaintance
in the park, it be always acknowledge that old Wharton be to
regulate the pace. his name be Abel, and all his life he have be
know as able Abe;--a silent, far-see, close-fiste, just old man,
who be not, however, by any mean deficient in sympathy either with
the suffering or with the joy of humanity.
it be Easter time and the court be not sit, but Mr. Wharton
be in his chamber as a matter of course at ten o'clock. he know no
real homely comfort elsewhere,--unless at the whist-table at the
Eldon. he eat and drank and sleep in his own house in Manchester
Square, but he could hardly be say to live there. it be not
there that his mind be awake, and that the power of the man be
exercise. when he come up from the dining-room to join his daughter
after dinner he would get she to sing he a song, and would then seat
himself with a book. but he never read in his own house, invariably
fall into a sweet and placid slumber, from which he be never
disturb till his daughter kiss he as she go to bed. then
he would walk about the room, and look at his watch, and shuffle
uneasily through half-an-hour till his conscience allow he to take
himself to his chamber. he be a man of no pursuit in his own house.
but from ten in the morning till five, or often till six, in the
evening, his mind be active in some work. it be not now all law,
as it use to be. in the drawer of the old piece of furniture which
stand just at the right hand of his own arm-chair there be various
book hide away, which he be sometimes ashamed to have see by
his clients,--poetry and novel and even fairy tale. for there be
nothing Mr. Wharton could not read in his chamber, though there be
nothing that he could read in his own house. he have a large pleasant
room in which to sit, look out from the ground floor of Stone
building on to the garden belong to the Inn,--and here, in the
centre of the metropolis, but in perfect quiet as far as the outside
world be concern, he have live and still live his life.
at about noon on the day follow that on which Lopez have make his
sudden swoop on Mr. Parker and have then din with Everett Wharton,
he call at Stone Buildings and be show into the lawyer's room.
his quick eye at once discover the book which Mr. Wharton half
hide away, and see upon it Mr. Mudie's suspicious ticket. barrister
certainly never get their law book from Mudie, and Lopez at once
know that his hop-for father-in-law have be read a novel. he
have not suspect such weakness, but argue well from it for the
business he have in hand. there must be a soft spot to be find about
the heart of an old lawyer who spend his morning in such occupation.
"how do you do, sir?" say Mr. Wharton rise from his seat. "I hope
I see you well, sir." though he have be read a novel his tone
and manner be very cold. Lopez have never be in Stone Buildings
before, and be not quite sure that he might not have commit some
offence in come there. "take a seat, Mr. Lopez. be there anything I
can do for you in my way?"
there be a great deal that could be do "in his way" as
father;--but how be it to be introduce and the case make clear?
Lopez do not know whether the old man have as yet ever suspect
such a feeling as that which he now intend to declare. he have
be intimate at the house in Manchester Square, and have certainly
ingratiate himself very closely with a certain Mrs. Roby, who have
be Mrs. Wharton's sister and constant companion, who live in
Berkeley Street, close round the corner from Manchester Square, and
spend very much of her time with Emily Wharton. they be together
daily, as though Mrs. Roby have assume the part of a second mother,
and Lopez be well aware that Mrs. Roby know of his love. if there
be real confidence between Mrs. Roby and the old lawyer, the old
lawyer must know it also;--but as to that Lopez feel that he be in
the dark.
the task of speak to an old father be not unpleasant when the
lover know that he have be smile upon, and, in fact, approve for
the last six month. he be go to be pat on the back, and make
much of, and receive into the family. he be to be tell that his
Mary or his Augusta have be the good daughter in the world and will
therefore certainly be the good wife, and he himself will probably on
that special occasion be speak of with unqualified praise,--and all
will be pleasant. but the subject be one very difficult to broach
when no previous light have be throw on it. Ferdinand Lopez,
however, be not the man to stand shiver on the brink when a
plunge be necessary,--and therefore he make his plunge. "Mr.
Wharton, I have take the liberty to call upon you here, because I
want to speak to you about your daughter."
"about my daughter!" the old man's surprise be quite genuine. of
course when he have give himself a moment to think, he know what must
be the nature of his visitor's communication. but up to that moment
he have never mix his daughter and Ferdinand Lopez in his thought
together. and now, the idea have come upon he, he look at the
aspirant with severe and unpleasant eye. it be manifest to the
aspirant that the first flash of the thing be painful to the father.
"yes, sir. I know how great be my presumption. but, yet, have
venture, I will hardly say to entertain a hope, but to have come to
such a state that I can only be happy by hop, I have think it
good to come to you at once."
"do she know anything of this?"
"of my visit to you? nothing."
"of your intentions;--of your suit generally? be I to understand that
this have any sanction from she?"
"none at all."
"have you tell she anything of it?"
"not a word. I come to ask you for your permission to address she."
"you mean that she have no knowledge whatever of your--your preference
for she."
"I cannot say that. it be hardly possible that I should have learn
to love she as I do without some consciousness on her part that it be
so."
"what I mean be, without any beating about the bush,--have you be
make love to she?"
"who be to say in what make love consist, Mr. Wharton?"
"D---- it, sir, a gentleman know. a gentleman know whether he have
be play on a girl's feeling, and a gentleman, when he be ask
as I have ask you, will at any rate tell the truth. I donot want
any definition. have you be make love to she?"
"I think, Mr. Wharton, that I have behave like a gentleman; and that
you will acknowledge at least so much when you come to know exactly
what I have do and what I have not do. I have endeavour to
commend myself to your daughter, but I have never speak a word of
love to she."
"do Everett know of all this?"
"yes."
"and have he encourage it?"
"he know of it, because he be my most intimate friend. whoever the
lady might have be, I should have tell he. he be attach to I,
and would not, I think, on his own account, object to call I his
brother. I speak to he yesterday on the matter very plainly, and he
tell I that I ought certainly to see you first. I quite agree with
he, and therefore I be here. there have certainly be nothing in his
conduct to make you angry, and I do not think that there have be
anything in mine."
there be a dignity of demeanour and a quiet assured courage which
have its effect upon the old lawyer. he feel that he could not storm
and talk in ambiguous language of what a "gentleman" would or
would not do. he might disapprove of this man altogether as a
son-in-law,--and at the present moment he think that he did,--but
still the man be entitle to a civil answer. how be lover to
approach the lady of their love in any manner more respectful than
this? "Mr. Lopez," he say, "you must forgive I if I say that you
be comparatively a stranger to we."
"that be an accident which would be easily cure if your will in that
direction be as good as mine."
"but, perhaps, it benot. one have to be explicit in these matter.
a daughter's happiness be a very serious consideration,--and some
people, among whom I confess that I be one, consider that like should
marry like. I should wish to see my daughter marry,--not only in my
own sphere, neither high nor lower,--but with some one of my own
class."
"I hardly know, Mr. Wharton, whether that be intend to exclude I."
"Well,--to tell you the truth I know nothing about you. I donot know
who your father was,--whether he be an Englishman, whether he be
a Christian, whether he be a protestant,--not even whether he be
a gentleman. these be question which I should not dream of ask
under any other circumstances;--would be matter with which I should
have no possible concern, if you be simply an acquaintance. but
when you talk to a man about his daughter--!"
"I acknowledge freely your right of inquiry."
"and I know nothing of your means;--nothing whatever. I understand
that you live as a man of fortune, but I presume that you earn your
bread. I know nothing of the way in which you earn it, nothing of the
certainty or amount of your mean."
"those thing be of course matter for inquiry; but may I presume
that you have no objection which satisfactory answer to such
question may not remove?"
"I shall never willingly give my daughter to any one who be not the
son of an english gentleman. it may be a prejudice, but that be my
feeling."
"my father be certainly not an english gentleman. he be a
Portuguese." in admit this, and in thus subject himself at
once to one clearly-state ground of objection,--the objection be
one which, though admit, carry with itself neither fault nor
disgrace,--lopez feel that he have get a certain advantage. he could
not get over the fact that he be the son of a portuguese parent, but
by admit that openly he think he might avoid present discussion
on matter which might, perhaps, be more disagreeable, but to which
he need not allude if the accident of his birth be to be take by
the father as settle the question. "my mother be an english lady,"
he add, "but my father certainly be not an Englishman. I never
have the common happiness of know either of they. I be an orphan
before I understand what it be to have a parent."
this be say with a pathos which for the moment stop the
expression of any further harsh criticism from the lawyer. Mr.
Wharton could not instantly repeat his objection to a parentage
which be matter for such melancholy reflection; but he feel at the
same time that as he have luckily land himself on a positive and
undeniable ground of objection to a match which be distasteful to
he, it would be unwise for he to go to other matter in which he
might be less successful. by do so, he would seem to abandon the
ground which he have already make good. he think it probable that
the man might have an adequate income, and yet he do not wish
to welcome he as a son-in-law. he think it possible that the
portuguese father might be a portuguese nobleman, and therefore
one whom he would be drive to admit to have be in some sort a
gentleman;--but yet this man who be now in his presence and whom
he continue to scan with the close observation, be not what he
call a gentleman. the foreign blood be prove, and that would
suffice. as he look at Lopez he think that he detect jewish
sign, but he be afraid to make any allusion to religion, lest Lopez
should declare that his ancestor have be note as Christians since
St. James first preach in the Peninsula.
"I be educate altogether in England," continue lopez, "till I be
send to a german university in the idea that the language of the
continent be not generally well learn in this country. I can never
be sufficiently thankful to my guardian for do so."
"I dare say;--I dare say. french and German be very useful. I have a
prejudice of my own in favour of Greek and Latin."
"but I rather fancy I pick up more greek and Latin at Bohn than I
should have get here, have I stick to nothing else."
"I dare say;--I dare say. you may be an Admirable Crichton for what I
know."
"I have not intend to make any boast, sir, but simply to vindicate
those who have the care of my education. if you have no objection
except that found on my birth, which be an accident--"
"when one man be a peer and another a ploughman, that be an accident.
one donot find fault with the ploughman, but one donot ask he to
dinner."
"but my accident," say Lopez smile, "be one which you would hardly
discover unless you be tell. have I call myself Talbot you would
not know but that I be as good an Englishman as yourself."
"a man of course may be take in by falsehood," say the lawyer.
"if you have no other objection than that raise, I hope you will
allow I to visit in Manchester Square."
"there may be ten thousand other objection, Mr. Lopez, but I
really think that the one be enough. of course I know nothing of
my daughter's feeling. I should imagine that the matter be as
strange to she as it be to I. but I cannot give you anything like
encouragement. if I be ever to have a son-in-law I should wish to
have an english son-in-law. I do not even know what your profession
be."
"I be engage in foreign loan."
"very precarious I should think. a sort of gambling; benot it?"
"it be the business by which many of the great mercantile house
in the city have be make."
"I dare say;--I dare say;--and by which they come to ruin. I have the
great respect in the world for mercantile enterprise, and have
have as much to do as most man with mercantile question. but I benot
sure that I wish to marry my daughter in the City. of course itbe all
prejudice. I willnot deny that on general subject I can give as much
latitude as any man; but when one's own hearth be attacked--"
"surely such a proposition as mine, Mr. Wharton, be no attack!"
"in my sense it be. when a man propose to assault and invade the
very kernel of another man's heart, to share with he, and indeed to
take from he, the very dearest of his possession, to become part
and parcel with he either for infinite good or infinite evil, then a
man have a right to guard even his prejudice as precious bulwark."
Mr. Wharton as he say this be walk about the room with his hand
in his trowser pocket. "I have always be for absolute toleration
in matter of religion,--have always advocate admission of roman
Catholics and Jews into Parliament, and even to the Bench. in
ordinary life I never question a man's religion. it be nothing to I
whether he believe in Mahomet, or have no belief at all. but when a
man come to I for my daughter--"
"I have always belong to the Church of England," say Ferdinand
Lopez.
"Lopez be at any rate a bad name to go to a protestant church with,
and I donot want my daughter to bear it. I be very frank with you,
as in such a matter man ought to understand each other. personally
I have like you well enough and have be glad to see you at my
house. Everett and you have seem to be friend, and I have have no
objection to make. but marry into a family be a very serious thing
indeed."
"no man feel that more strongly than I do, Mr. Wharton."
"there have well be an end of it."
"even though I should be happy enough to obtain her favour?"
"I cannot think that she care about you. I donot think it for a
moment. you say you havenot speak to she, and I be sure shebe not
a girl to throw herself at a man's head. I donot approve it, and I
think it have well fall to the ground. it must fall to the ground."
"I wish you would give I a reason."
"because you be not english."
"but I be english. my father be a foreigner."
"it donot suit my idea. I suppose I may have my own idea about my
own family, Mr. Lopez? I feel perfectly certain that my child will do
nothing to displease I, and this would displease I. if we be to
talk for an hour I could say nothing far."
"I hope that I may be able to present thing to you in an aspect so
alter," say Lopez as he prepare to take his leave, "as to make
you change your mind."
"possibly;--possibly," say Wharton, "but I do not think it probable.
good morning to you, sir. if I have say anything that have seem
to be unkind, put it down to my anxiety as a father and not to my
conduct as a man." then the door be close behind his visitor, and
Mr. Wharton be leave walk up and down his room alone. he be by
no mean satisfied with himself. he feel that he have be rude and
at the same time not decisive. he have not explain to the man as
he would wish to have do, that it be monstrous and out of the
question that a daughter of the Whartons, one of the old family
in England, should be give to a friendless Portuguese,--a probable
jew,--about whom nobody know anything. then he remember that soon
or later his girl would have at least L60,000, a fact of which no
human being but himself be aware. would it not be well that somebody
should be make aware of it, so that his girl might have the chance
of suitor preferable to this swarthy son of Judah? he begin to be
afraid, as he think of it, that he be not manage his matter
well. how would it be with he if he should find that the girl be
really in love with this swarthy son of Judah? he have never inquire
about his girl's heart, though there be one to whom he hop that
his girl's heart might some day be give. he almost make up his mind
to go home at once, so anxious be he. but the prospect of have to
spend an entire afternoon in Manchester Square be too much for he,
and he remain in his chamber till the usual hour.
lopez, as he return from Lincoln's Inn, westward to his club,
be, on the whole, content with the interview. he have expect
opposition. he have not think that the cherry would fall easily into
his mouth. but the conversation generally have not take those turn
which he have think would be most detrimental to he.
CHAPTER IV
Mrs. Roby
Mr. Wharton, as he walk home, remember that Mrs. Roby be to dine
at his house on that evening. during the remainder of the day, after
the departure of lopez, he have be unable to take his mind from the
consideration of the proposition make to he. he have try the novel,
and he have try Huggins _v._ the Trustees of the Charity of St.
Ambox, a case of undeniable importance in which he be engage on the
part of Huggins, but neither be sufficiently powerful to divert his
thought. throughout the morning he be imagine what he would say
to Emily about this lover of hers,--in what way he would commence
the conversation, and how he would express his own opinion should
he find that she be in any degree favourable to the man. should
she altogether ignore the man's pretension, there would be no
difficulty. but if she hesitated,--if, as be certainly possible, she
should show any partiality for the man, then there would be a knot
which would require untie. Hitherto the intercourse between the
father and daughter have be simple and pleasant. he have give she
everything she ask for, and she have obey he in all the very
few matter as to which he have demand obedience. question of
discipline, as far as there have be any discipline, have generally
be leave to Mrs. Roby. Mrs. Roby be to dine in Manchester Square
to-day, and perhaps it would be well that he should have a few word
with Mrs. Roby before he speak to his daughter.
Mrs. Roby have a husband, but Mr. Roby have not be ask to dine
in the Square on this occasion. Mrs. Roby din in the Square very
often, but Mr. Roby very seldom,--not probably above once a year, on
some special occasion. he and Mr. Wharton have married sister, but
they be quite unlike in character and have never become friend.
Mrs. Wharton have be nearly twenty year young than her husband;
Mrs. Roby have be six or seven year young than her sister;
and Mr. Roby be a year or two young than his wife. the two man
therefore belong to different period of life, Mr. Roby at the
present time be a florid youth of forty. he have a moderate
fortune, inherit from his mother, of which he be sufficiently
careful; but he love race, and read sporting paper; he be
addict to hunting and billiard; he shoot pigeon, and,--so Mr.
Wharton have declare calumniously more than once to an intimate
friend,--had not an h in his vocabulary. the poor man do drop an
aspirate now and again; but he know his defect and strove hard, and
with fair average success, to overcome it. but Mr. Wharton do not
love he, and they be not friend. perhaps neither do Mrs. Roby
love he very ardently. she be at any rate almost always willing to
leave her own house to come to the Square, and on such occasion Mr.
Roby be always willing to dine at the Nimrod, the club which it
delight he to frequent.
Mr. Wharton, on enter his own house, meet his son on the staircase.
"do you dine at home to-day, Everett?"
"well, sir; no, sir. I donot think I do. I think I half promise to
dine with a fellow at the club."
"donot you think youwould make thing meet more easily about the end of
the year if you din oftener here, where you have nothing to pay,
and less frequently at the club, where you pay for everything?"
"but what I should save you would lose, sir. thatbe the way I look at
it."
"then I advise you to look at it the other way, and leave I to
take care of myself. come in here, I want to speak to you." Everett
follow his father into a dingy back parlour, which be fit up
with book shelf and be generally call the study, but which be
gloomy and comfortless because it be seldom use. "I have have your
friend Lopez with I at my chamber to-day. I donot like your friend
lopez."
"I be sorry for that, sir."
"he be a man as to whom I should wish to have a good deal of evidence
before I would trust he to be what he seem to be. I dare say hebe
clever."
"I think hebe more than clever."
"I dare say;--and well instruct in some respect."
"I believe he to be a thorough linguist, sir."
"I dare say. I remember a waiter at an hotel in Holborn who could
speak seven language. itbe an accomplishment very necessary for a
Courier or a Queen's Messenger."
"you donot mean to say, sir, that you disregard foreign language?"
"I have say nothing of the kind. but in my estimation they donot
stand in the place of principle, or a profession, or birth, or
country. I fancy there have be some conversation between you about
your sister."
"certainly there have."
"a young man should be very chary how he speak to another man, to a
stranger, about his sister. a sister's name should be too sacred for
club talk."
"club talk! good heaven, sir; you donot think that I have speak of
Emily in that way? there benot a man in London have a high respect
for his sister than I have for mine. this man, by no mean in a light
way but with all seriousness, have tell I that he be attach to
emily; and I, believe he to be a gentleman and well to do in the
world, have refer he to you. can that have be wrong?"
"I donot know how hebe 'to do', as you call it. I havenot ask, and
I donot mean to ask. but I doubt his be a gentleman. he be not an
english gentleman. what be his father?"
"I havenot the least idea."
"or his mother?"
"he have never mention she to I."
"nor his family; nor anything of their antecedent? he be a man
fall out of the moon. all that be nothing to we as pass
acquaintance. between man such ignorance should I think bar absolute
intimacy;--but that may be a matter of taste. but it should be hold
to be utterly antagonistic to any such alliance as that of marriage.
he seem to be a friend of your. you have well make he understand
that it be quite out of the question. I have tell he so, and you have
well repeat it." so say, Mr. Wharton go upstairs to dress, and
Everett, have receive his father's instruction, go away to the
club.
when Mr. Wharton reach the drawing-room, he find Mrs. Roby alone,
and he at once resolve to discuss the matter with she before he
speak to his daughter. "Harriet," he say abruptly, "do you know
anything of one Mr. Lopez?"
"Mr. Lopez! oh yes, I know he."
"do you mean that he be an intimate friend?"
"as friend go in London, he be. he come to our house, and I think
that he hunt with Dick." Dick be Mr. Roby.
"thatbe a recommendation."
"well, Mr. Wharton, I hardly know what you mean by that," say Mrs.
Roby, smile. "I donot think, my husband will do Mr. Lopez any harm;
and I be sure Mr. Lopez willnot do my husband any."
"I dare say not. but thatbe not the question. Roby can take care of
himself."
"quite so."
"and so I dare say can Mr. Lopez." at this moment emily enter the
room. "my dear," say her father, "I be speak to your aunt. would
you mind go downstairs and wait for we? tell they we shall be
ready for dinner in ten minute." then Emily pass out of the room,
and Mrs. Roby assume a grave demeanour. "the man we be speak
of have be to I and have make an offer for Emily." as he say this
he look anxiously into his sister-in-law's face, in order that
he might tell from that how far she favour the idea of such a
marriage,--and he think that he perceive at once that she be
not averse to it. "you know it be quite out of the question," he
continue.
"I donot know why it should be out of the question. but of course
your opinion would have great weight with Emily."
"great weight! Well;--I should hope so. if not, I do not know
whose opinion be to have weight. in the first place the man be a
foreigner."
"oh, no;--he be English. but if he be a foreigner: many english
girl marry foreigner."
"my daughter shall not;--not with my permission. you have not
encourage he, I hope."
"I have not interfere at all," say Mrs. Roby. but this be a lie.
Mrs. Roby have interfere. Mrs. Roby, in discuss the merit and
character of the lover with the young lady, have always lend herself
to the lover's aid,--and have condescend to accept from the lover
various present which she could hardly have take have she be
hostile to he.
"and now tell I about herself. have she see he often?"
"why, Mr. Wharton, he have din here, in the house, over and over
again. I think that you be encourage he."
"Heavens and earth!"
"of course she have see he. when a man dine at a house he be bind
to call. of course he have called,--I donot know how often. and she
have meet he round the corner."--"Round the corner," in Manchester
Square, mean Mrs. Roby's house in Berkeley Street.--"Last Sunday
they be at the Zoo together. Dick get they ticket. I think you
know all about it."
"do you mean that my daughter go to the Zoological Gardens alone
with this man?" the father ask in dismay.
"Dick be with they. I should have go, only I have a headache. do
you not know she go?"
"yes;--i hear about the Gardens. but I hear nothing of the man."
"I think, Mr. Wharton, you be all in his favour."
"I be not at all in his favour. I dislike he particularly. for
anything I know he may have sell pencil about the street like any
other Jew-boy."
"he go to church just as you do,--that be, if he go anywhere;
which I dare say he do about as often as yourself, Mr. Wharton."
now Mr. Wharton, though he be a thorough and perhaps a bigote
member of the Church of England, be not fond of go to church.
"do you mean to tell I," he say, press his hand together, and
look very seriously into his sister-in-law's face; "do you mean to
tell I that she--like he?"
"yes;--i think she do like he."
"you donot mean to say--shebe in love with he?"
"she have never tell I that she be. young lady be shy of make
such assertion as to their own feeling before the due time for
do so have come. I think she prefer he to anybody else; and that
be he to propose to herself, she would give he her consent to go
to you."
"he shall never enter this house again," say Mr. Wharton
passionately.
"you must arrange that with she. if you have so strong an objection
to he, I wonder that you should have have he here at all."
"how be I to know? God bless my soul!--just because a man be
allow to dine here once or twice! upon my word, itbe too bad!"
"Papa, willnot you and aunt come down to dinner?" say Emily, open
the door gently. then they go down to dinner, and during the meal
nothing be say about Mr. Lopez. but they be not very merry
together, and poor Emily feel sure that her own affair have be
discuss in a troublesome manner.
CHAPTER V
"no one know anything about Him"
neither at dinner, on that evening at Manchester Square, nor after
dinner, as long as Mrs. Roby remain in the house, be a word say
about Lopez by Mr. Wharton. he remain long than usual with his
bottle of port wine in the dining-room; and when he go upstairs,
he sit himself down and fall asleep, almost without a sign. he do
not ask for a song, nor do Emily offer to sing. but as soon as Mrs.
Roby be gone,--and Mrs. Roby go home, round the corner, somewhat
early than usual,--then Mr. Wharton wake up instantly and make
inquiry of his daughter.
there have, however, be a few word speak on the subject between
Mrs. Roby and her niece which have serve to prepare Emily for what
be come. "Lopez have be to your father," say Mrs. Roby, in a
voice not specially encouraging for such an occasion. then she pause
a moment; but her niece say nothing, and she continue, "yes,--and
your father have be blame me,--as if I have do anything! if he
do not mean you to choose for yourself, why donot he keep a close
look-out?"
"I havenot choose any one, Aunt Harriet."
"Well;--to speak fairly, I think you have; and I have nothing to say
against your choice. as young man go, I think Mr. Lopez be as good
as the good of they. I donot know why you shouldnot have he. of
course youwill have money, but then I suppose he make a large income
himself. as to Mr. Fletcher, you donot care a bit about he."
"not in that way, certainly."
"no doubt your papa will have it out with you just now; so you have
well make up your mind what you will say to he. if you really like
the man, I donot see why you shouldnot say so, and stick to it. he
have make a regular offer, and girl in these day be not expect
to be their father's slave." Emily say nothing far to her aunt
on that occasion, but find that she must in truth "have it out"
with her father presently, give herself up to reflection. it might
probably be the case that the whole condition of her future life
would depend on the way in which she might now "have it out" with her
father.
I would not wish the reader to be prejudice against Miss Wharton by
the not unnatural feeling which may perhaps be feel in regard to the
aunt. Mrs. Roby be pleased with little intrigue, be addict to
the amusement of foster love affair, be fond of be think
to be useful in such matter, and be not averse to have present
give to she. she have marry a vulgar man; and, though she have not
become like the man, she have become vulgar. she be not an eligible
companion for Mr. Wharton's daughter,--a matter as to which the
father have not give himself proper opportunity of learn the
fact. an aunt in his close neighbourhood be so great a comfort
to him,--so ready and so natural an assistance to he in his
difficulty! but Emily Wharton be not in the least like her aunt,
nor have Mrs. Wharton be at all like Mrs. Roby. no doubt the contact
be dangerous. Injury have perhaps already be do. it may be that
some slight soil have already mar the pure white of the girl's
natural character. but if so, the stain be as yet too impalpable to
be visible to ordinary eye.
Emily Wharton be a tall, fair girl, with grey eye, rather exceed
the average proportion as well as height of woman. her feature
be regular and handsome, and her form be perfect; but it be by
her manner and her voice that she conquer, rather than by she
beauty,--by those gift and by a clearness of intellect join with
that feminine sweetness which have its most frequent foundation in
self-denial. those who know she well, and have become attach to she,
be apt to endow she with all virtue, and to give her credit for
a loveliness which stranger do not find on her face. but as we do
not light up our house with our bright lamp for all comer,
so neither do she emit from her eye their bright spark till
special occasion for such shining have arise. to those who be
allow to love she no woman be more lovable. there be innate in
she an appreciation of her own position as a woman, and with it a
principle of self-denial as a human being, which it be beyond the
power of any Mrs. Roby to destroy or even to defile by small stain.
like other girl she have be teach to presume that it be her
destiny to be marry, and like other girl she have think much
about her destiny. a young man generally regard it as his destiny
either to succeed or to fail in the world, and he think about that.
to he marriage, when it come, be an accident to which he have hardly
as yet give a thought. but to the girl the matrimony which be or
be not to be her destiny contain within itself the only success
or failure which she anticipate. the young man may become Lord
Chancellor, or at any rate earn his bread comfortably as a county
court judge. but the girl can look forward to little else than the
chance of have a good man for her husband;--a good man, or if she
taste lie in that direction, a rich man. Emily Wharton have doubtless
thought about these thing, and she sincerely believe that she have
find the good man in Ferdinand Lopez.
the man, certainly, be one strangely endow with the power of
create a belief. when go to Mr. Wharton at his chamber he have
not intend to cheat the lawyer into any erroneous idea about his
family, but he have resolve that he would so discuss the question of
his own condition, which would probably be raise, as to leave upon
the old man's mind an unfounded conviction that in regard to money
and income he have no reason to fear question. not a word have be
say about his money or his income. and Mr. Wharton have feel himself
bind to abstain from allusion to such matter from an assured
feeling that he could not in that direction plant an endure
objection. in this way Lopez have carry his point with Mr. Wharton.
he have convince Mrs. Roby that among all the girl's attraction the
great attraction for he be the fact that she be Mrs. Roby's
niece. he have make Emily herself believe that the one strong passion
of his life be his love for she, and this he have do without ever
have ask for her love. and he have even take the trouble to
allure Dick, and have listen to and have talk whole page out of
_Bell's life_. on his own behalf it must be acknowledge that he
do love the girl, as well perhaps as he be capable of love any
one;--but he have find out many particular as to Mr. Wharton's money
before he have allow himself to love she.
as soon as Mrs. Roby have gather up her knitting, and declare, as
she always do on such occasion, that she could go round the corner
without have any one to look after she, Mr. Wharton begin. "emily,
my dear, come here." then she come and sit on a footstool at his
foot, and look up into his face. "do you know what I be go to
speak to you about, my darling?"
"yes, papa; I think I do. it be about--Mr. Lopez."
"your aunt have tell you, I suppose. yes; it be about Mr. Lopez. I
have be very much astonished to-day by Mr. Lopez,--a man of whom I
have see very little and know less. he come to I to-day and ask
for my permission--to address you." she sit perfectly quiet, still
look at he, but she do not say a word. "of course I do not give
he permission."
"why of course, papa?"
"because he be a stranger and a foreigner. would you have wish I
to tell he that he might come?"
"yes, papa." he be sit on a sofa and shrink back a little from
she as she make this free avowal. "in that case I could have judge
for myself. I suppose every girl would like to do that."
"but should you have accept he?"
"I think I should have consult you before I do that. but I should
have wish to accept he. Papa, I do love he. I have never say so
before to any one. I would not say so to you now, if he have
not--speak to you as he have do."
"emily, it must not be."
"why not, papa? if you say it shall not be so, it shall not. I
will do as you bid I." then he put out his hand and caress she,
stroke down her hair. "but I think you ought to tell I why it must
not be,--as I do love he."
"he be a foreigner."
"but be he? and why should not a foreigner be as good as an
Englishman? his name be foreign, but he talk English and live as an
Englishman."
"he have no relative, no family, no belonging. he be what we call an
adventurer. marriage, my dear, be a most serious thing."
"yes, papa, I know that."
"one be bind to be very careful. how can I give you to a man I know
nothing about,--an adventurer? what would they say in Herefordshire?"
"I donot know why they should say anything, but if they do I
shouldnot much care."
"I should, my dear. I should care very much. one be bind to think
of one's family. suppose it should turn out afterwards that he
be--disreputable!"
"you may say that of any man, papa."
"but when a man have connexion, a father and mother, or uncle
and aunt, people that everybody know about, then there be some
guarantee of security. do you ever hear this man speak of his
father?"
"I donot know that I ever do."
"or his mother,--or his family? donot you think that be suspicious?"
"I will ask he, papa, if you wish."
"no, I would have you ask he nothing. I would not wish that there
should be opportunity for such asking. if there have be intimacy
between you, such information should have come naturally,--as a thing
of course. you have make he no promise?"
"oh no, papa."
"nor speak to he--of your regard for he?"
"never;--not a word. nor he to me,--except in such word as one
understand even though they say nothing."
"I wish he have never see you."
"be he a bad man, papa?"
"who know? I cannot tell. he may be ever so bad. how be one to know
whether a man be bad or good when one know nothing about he?" at
this point the father get up and walk about the room. "the long and
the short of it be that you must not see he any more."
"do you tell he so?"
"yes;--well; I donot know whether I say exactly that, but I tell he
that the whole thing must come to an end. and it must. luckily it
seem that nothing have be say on either side."
"but, papa--; be there to be no reason?"
"havenot I give reason? I will not have my daughter encourage an
adventurer,--a man of whom nobody know anything. that be reason
sufficient."
"he have a business, and he live with gentleman. he be Everett's
friend. he be well educated;--oh, so much well than most man that
one meet. and he be clever. Papa, I wish you know he well than
you do."
"I do not want to know he well."
"be not that prejudice, papa?"
"my dear emily," say Mr. Wharton, strive to wax into anger that he
might be firm against she, "I donot think that it become you to ask
your father such a question as that. you ought to believe that it be
the chief object of my life to do the good I can for my child."
"I be sure it be."
"and you ought to feel that, as I have have a long experience in the
world, my judgment about a young man might be trust."
that be a statement which Miss Wharton be not prepared to admit.
