she have already profess herself willing to submit to her father's
judgment, and do not now by any mean contemplate rebellion against
parental authority. but she do feel that on a matter so vital to she
she have a right to plead her cause before judgment should be give,
and she be not slow to assure herself, even as this interview go
on, that her love for the man be strong enough to entitle she to
assure her father that her happiness depend on his reversal of the
sentence already pronounce. "you know, papa, that I trust you," she
say. "and I have promise you that I will not disobey you. if you
tell I that I be never to see Mr. Lopez again, I will not see he."
"you be a good girl. you be always a good girl."
"but I think that you ought to hear I." then he stand still with his
hand in his trowser pocket look at she. he do not want to hear
a word, but he feel that he would be a tyrant if he refuse. "if you
tell I that I be not to see he, I shall not see he. but I shall be
very unhappy. I do love he, and I shall never love any one else in
the same way."
"that be nonsense, Emily. there be Arthur Fletcher."
"I be sure you will never ask I to marry a man I do not love, and
I shall never love Arthur Fletcher. if this be to be as you say, it
will make I very, very wretched. it be right that you should know
the truth. if it be only because Mr. Lopez have a foreign name--"
"it benot only that; no one know anything about he, or where to
inquire even."
"I think you should inquire, papa, and be quite certain before you
pronounce such a sentence against I. it will be a crush blow."
he look at she, and see that there be a fix purpose in her
countenance of which he have never before see similar sign. "you
claim a right to my obedience, and I acknowledge it. I be sure you
believe I when I promise not to see he without your permission."
"I do believe you. of course I believe you."
"but if I do that for you, papa, I think that you ought to be very
sure, on my account, that I havenot to bear such unhappiness for
nothing. youwill think about it, papa,--will you not, before you quite
decide?" she lean against he as she speak, and he kiss she.
"good night, now, papa. you will think about it?"
"I will. I will. of course I will."
and he begin the process of think about it immediately,--before
the door be close behind she. but what be there to think about?
nothing that she have say alter in the least his idea about the
man. he be as convince as ever that unless there be much to
conceal there would not be so much concealment. but a feeling begin
to grow upon he already that his daughter have a mode of plead
with he which he would not ultimately be able to resist. he have the
power, he know, of put an end to the thing altogether. he have
only to say resolutely and unchangeably that the thing shouldnot
be, and it wouldnot be. if he could steel his heart against his
daughter's sorrow for, say, a twelvemonth, the victory would be win.
but he already begin to fear that he lack the power to steel his
heart against his daughter.
CHAPTER VI
An Old Friend go to Windsor
"and what be they go to make you now?"
this question be ask of her husband by a lady with whom perhaps
the reader of this volume may have already form some acquaintance.
chronicle of her early life have be write, at any rate
copiously. the lady be the Duchess of Omnium, and her husband be of
course the Duke. in order that the nature of the question ask by
the duchess may be explain, it must be state that just at this
time the political affair of the nation have get themselves tie up
into one of those truly desperate knot from which even the wisdom
and experience of septuagenarian statesman can see no unravelment.
the head of party be at a standstill. in the House of Commons
there be, so to say, no majority on either side. the mind of
member be so astray that, accord to the good calculation that
could be make, there would be a majority of about ten against any
possible Cabinet. there would certainly be a majority against either
of those well-try but, at this moment, little-trust Prime
Ministers, Mr. Gresham and Mr. Daubeny. there be certain man,
nominally belong to this or to the other party, who would
certainly within a week of the nomination of a Cabinet in the House,
oppose the Cabinet which they ought to support. Mr. Daubeny have
be in power,--nay, be in power, though he have twice resign. Mr.
Gresham have be twice send for to Windsor, and have on one occasion
undertake and on another have refuse to undertake to form a
Ministry. Mr. Daubeny have try two or three combination, and have
be at his wit' end. he be no doubt still in power,--could appoint
bishop, and make peer, and give away ribbon. but he couldnot pass
a law, and certainly continue to hold his present uncomfortable
position by no will of his own. but a Prime Minister cannot escape
till he have succeed in find a successor; and though the
successor be find and consent to make an attempt, the old
unfortunate cannot be allow to go free when that attempt be show
to be a failure. he have not absolutely give up the key of his
box, and no one will take they from he. even a sovereign can
abdicate; but the Prime Minister of a constitutional government
be in bond. the reader may therefore understand that the Duchess
be ask her husband what place among the political ruler of
the country have be offer to he by the last aspirant to the
leadership of the Government.
but the reader should understand more than this, and may perhaps do
so, if he have ever see those former chronicle to which allusion
have be make. the Duke, before he become a duke, have hold very
high office, have be Chancellor of the Exchequer. when he be
transfer, perforce, to the House of Lords, he had,--as be not
uncommon in such cases,--accepte a low political station. this have
displease the Duchess, who be ambitious both on her own behalf and
that of her lord,--and who think that a Duke of Omnium should be
nothing in the government if not at any rate near the top. but after
that, with the simple and single object of do some special piece
of work for the nation,--something which he fancy that nobody else
would do if he donot do it,--his Grace, of his own motion, at his
own solicitation, have encounter further official degradation, very
much to the disgust of the Duchess. and it be not the way with her
Grace to hide such sorrow in the depth of her bosom. when affront
she would speak out, whether to her husband, or to another,--use
irony rather than argument to support her cause and to vindicate she

regard to his voluntary abasement have be many and sharp. they sting

he, they do not even anger he. it be her nature to say such
things,--and he know that they come rather from her uncontrolle
spirit than from any malice. she be his wife too, and he have an
idea that of little injury of that sort there should be no end of
bear on the part of a husband. sometimes he would endeavour to

fear that they be and must ever be unintelligible to she. but he

her own fashion, do what she conceive to be her own work in
endeavour to create within his bosom a desire for high thing.
"surely," she say to herself, "if a man of his rank be to be a
minister he should be a great minister;--at any rate as great as his
circumstance will make he. a man never can save his country by
degrade himself." in this he would probably have agree; but his
idea of degradation and hers hardly tally.
when therefore she ask he what they be go to make he, it
be as though some sarcastic housekeeper in a great establishment

now?"
but he do not answer the question in his usual manner. he would
customarily smile gently at her badinage, and perhaps say a word
intend to show that he be not in the least move by her raillery.

always value by other as they value themselves."
"I wish it be so," he reply. "I should sleep easy to-night."
"what be it, Plantagenet?" she exclaim, jump up from her chair.
"I never care for your ridicule hitherto, Cora; but now I feel that
I want your sympathy."
"if you be go to do anything,--to do really anything, you shall
have it. oh, how you shall have it!"
"I have receive her Majesty's order to go down to Windsor at once.
I must start within half-an-hour."
"you be go to be Prime Minister!" she exclaim. as she speak
she throw her arm up, and then rush into his embrace. never since
their first union have she be so demonstrative either of love or

I will slave for you." as he put his arm round her waist he already

certainly avoid it."
"oh no! and there would be cowardice; of course there would," say

the Duke of St. Bungay.

"he will retire. that be a matter of course. he will intend to

cloud arise, one know not why or whence, and create darkness when

"you must keep a hold of it now, Plantagenet," say the Duchess,
clench her own fist.
"I will not even close a finger on it with any personal ambition,"

it would be an ease to my heart. I remember once," he said,--and as

"I remember that too," she say, speak very gently and look up
at he.

well,--because the office then suggest to I be one which I

then as far as that work go. but for this attempt I have no belief
in myself. I doubt whether I have any gift for govern man."
"it will come."
"it may be that I must try;--and it may be that I must break my heart
because I fail. but I shall make the attempt if I be direct to do
so in any manner that shall seem feasible. I must be off now. the
Duke be to be here this evening. they have well have dinner ready
for I whenever I may be able to eat it." then he take his departure
before she could say another word.
when the Duchess be alone she take to thinking of the whole thing in
a manner which they who well know she would have think to be very

could give she, and together with those good thing a peculiar

kingdom in the world.
but she feel in regard to he somewhat as do Lady Macbeth towards
her lord.
"what thou would'st highly,
that would'st thou holily."
she know he to be full of scruple, unable to bend when aught be to

would never be within the power of her husband. he could never be

but the other lesson be easy and might she think be learn.
power be so pleasant that man quickly learn to be greedy in the

make he understand that his duty to his country require he to be
in very truth its chief ruler. and then with some knowledge of thing

that have never yet be know even among the nobility of England.
both in town and country those great mansion should be keep open

true to his own greatness.
the Duke have leave she about two. she do not stir out of the house

DEAR M.,
come to I at once. I be too excited to go to you.
your,
G.
this be address to one Mrs. Finn, a lady as to whom chronicle
also have be write, and who have be know to the reader of such

"be the Duke to be Prime Minister?"
"how on earth should you have guess that?"

"he have fritter away his political position by such meaningless

"I own it to you;--and I must teach it to he."
"you never will do that, Lady Glen."

any Prime Minister who have become sick of his power. they become sick

love of power be a kind of feeling which come to a man as he grow

"Mr. Finn will come in, of course," say the Duchess.

"no, indeed! why should I? he would be more often at the House,

CHAPTER VII
Another Old Friend
at about nine the Duke have return, and be eat his very simple

of sherry and Apollinaris water. no man more easily satisfied as to
what he eat and drink live in London in those day. as regard the

"then you be Prime Minister."
"not at all. Mr. Daubeny be Prime Minister. I have undertake to form
a ministry, if I find it practicable, with the assistance of such

"lean on yourself only. be enough for yourself."
"those be empty word, cora;--word that be quite empty. in one
sense a man should always be enough for himself. he should have
enough of principle and enough of conscience to restrain he from
do what he know to be wrong. but can a ship-builder build

then her manner change altogether, and she become serious and almost

the Duke remain an hour alone before he be join by the other

when the Duke be announce he rise to greet his old friend almost

ought to have go to you."
"not at all. it be always the rule in these case that the man who

he have say something about the Queen, express gracious wish for

"if I could only escape from it!"

future Prime Minister have not his name at his finger' end, the
future President of the Council have they. eight man be soon name
whom it be think well that the Duke of Omnium should consult early
in the morning as to their willingness to fill certain place.
"each one of they may have some other one or some two whom he may
insist on bring with he," say the eld Duke; "and though of
course you cannot yield to the pressure in every such case, it will
be wise to allow yourself scope for some amount of concession. youwill
find theywill shake down after the usual amount of resistance and
compliance. no;--don't you leave your house to-morrow to see anybody
unless it be Mr. Daubeny or her Majesty. Iwill come to you at two, and
if her grace will give I luncheon, Iwill lunch with she. good night,
and donot think too much of the bigness of the thing. I remember dear
old Lord Brock tell I how much more difficult it be to find a
good coachman than a good Secretary of State." the Duke of Omnium, as
he sit think of thing for the next hour in his chair, succeed
only in prove to himself that Lord Brock never ought to have be
Prime Minister of England after have venture to make so poor a
joke on so solemn a subject.
CHAPTER VIII
the beginning of a New Career
by the time that the Easter holiday be over,--holiday which have
be use so conveniently for the making of a new government,--the
work of get a team together have be accomplish by the united
energy of the two duke and other friend. the filling up of the
great place have be by no mean so difficult or so tedious,--nor
indeed the cause of half so many heartburns,--as the completion of
the list of the subordinate. _Noblesse oblige._ the Secretaries of
State, and the Chancellors, and the First Lords, select from this
or the other party, feel that the eye of mankind be upon they, and
that it behove they to assume a virtue if they have it not. they be
habitually indifferent to self-exaltation, and allow themselves
to be thrust into this or that unfit role, profess that the
Queen's Government and the good of the country be their only
consideration. Lord Thrift make way for Sir Orlando Drought at the
Admiralty, because it be feel on all side that Sir Orlando could
not join the new composite party without high place. and the same
grace be show in regard to Lord Drummond, who remain at the
colony, keep the office to which he have be lately transfer
under Mr. Daubeny. and Sir Gregory Grogram say not a word, whatever
he may have think, when he be tell that Mr. Daubeny's Lord
Chancellor, Lord Ramsden, be to keep the seal. Sir Gregory do,
no doubt, think very much about it; for legal office have a
signification differ much from that which attach itself to
place simply political. a Lord Chancellor become a peer, and on
go out of office enjoy a large pension. when the woolsack have
be reach there come an end of doubt, and a beginning of ease.
Sir Gregory be not a young man, and this be a terrible blow. but he
bear it manfully, say not a word when the Duke speak to he; but
he become convinced from that moment that no more inefficient lawyer
ever sit upon the english bench, or a more presumptuous politician in
the British Parliament, than Lord Ramsden.
the real struggle, however, lie in the appropriate distribution of
the Rattlers and the Robys, the Fitzgibbons and the Macphersons among
the subordinate office of State. Mr. Macpherson and Mr. Roby, with
a host of other who have belong to Mr. Daubeny, be prepare, as
they declare from the first, to lend their assistance to the Duke.
they have consult Mr. Daubeny on the subject, and Mr. Daubeny tell
they that their duty lie in that direction. at the first blush of
the matter the arrangement take the form of a gracious tender from
themselves to a statesman call upon to act in very difficult
circumstances,--and they be thank accordingly by the Duke, with
something of real cordial gratitude. but when the actual adjustment
of thing be in hand, the Duke, have but little power of assume
a soft countenance and use soft word while his heart be bitter,
feel on more than one occasion incline to withdraw his thank. he
be astounded not so much by the pretension as by the unblushing
assertion of these pretension in reference to place which he have
be innocent enough to think be always bestow at any rate
without direct application. he have measure himself rightly when he
tell the old duke in one of those anxious conversation which have
be hold before the attempt be make, that long as he have be in
office himself he do not know what be the way of bestow office.
"two gentleman have be here this morning," he say one day to the
Duke of St. Bungay, "one on the heel of the other, each assure I
not only that the whole stability of the enterprise depend on my
give a certain office to him,--but actually tell I to my face
that I have promise it to he!" the old statesman laugh. "to be
tell within the same half-hour by two man that I have make promise to
each of they inconsistent with each other!"
"who be the two man?"
"Mr. Rattler and Mr. Roby."
"I be assure that they be inseparable since the work be begin.
they always have a lean to each other, and now I hear they pass
their time between the step of the Carlton and Reform Clubs."
"but what be I to do? one must be Patronage Secretary, no doubt."
"theybe both good, man in their way, you know."
"but why do they come to I with their mouth open, like dog crave
a bone? it use not to be so. of course man be always anxious for
office as they be now."
"well; yes. wehave hear of that before to-day, I think."
"but I donot think any man ever venture to ask Mr. Mildmay."
"Time have do much for he in consolidate his authority, and
perhaps the present world be less reticent in its eagerness than
it be in his young day. I doubt, however, whether it be more
dishonest, and whether struggle be not make quite as disgraceful
to the struggler as anything that be do now. you cannot alter the
man, and you must use they." the young Duke sit down and sigh
over the degenerate patriotism of the age.
but at last even the Rattlers and Robys be fix, if not satisfied,
and a complete list of the ministry appear in all the newspaper.
though the thing have be long a-doing, still it have come
suddenly,--so that at the first proposition to form a coalition
ministry, the newspaper have hardly know whether to assist or to
oppose the scheme. there be no doubt, in the mind of all these
editor and contributor, the teaching of a tradition that coalition
of this kind have be generally feeble, sometimes disastrous, and
on occasion even disgraceful. when a man, perhaps through a long
political life, have bind himself to a certain code of opinion, how
can he change that code at a moment? and when at the same moment,
together with the change, he secure power, patronage, and pay, how
shall the public voice absolve he? but then again man, who have
by the work of their life grow into a certain position in the
country, and have unconsciously but not therefore less actually make
themselves indispensable either to this side in politic or to that,
cannot free themselves altogether from the responsibility of manage
they when a period come such as that now reach. this also the
newspaper perceive; and have, since the commencement of the
Session, be very loud in expose the disgraceful collapse of
government affair, could hardly refuse their support to any attempt
at a feasible arrangement. when it be first know that the Duke of
Omnium have consent to make the attempt, they have both on one side
and the other be loud in his praise, go so far as to say that he
be the only man in England who could do the work. it be probably
this encouragement which have enable the new Premier to go on with an
undertaking which be personally distasteful to he, and for which
from day to day he believe himself to be less and less fit. but when
the newspaper tell he that he be the only man for the occasion,
how could he be justify in credit himself in preference to they?
the work in Parliament begin under the new auspex with great
tranquillity. that there would soon come cause of hot blood,--the
English Church, the county suffrage, the income tax, and far
education questions,--all man know who know anything. but for the
moment, for the month even, perhaps for the session, there be to
be peace, with full latitude for the performance of routine duty.
there be so to say no opposition, and at first it seem that one
special bench in the House of Commons would remain unoccupied. but
after a day or two,--on one of which Mr. Daubeny have be see
sit just below the gangway,--that gentleman return to the place
usually hold by the Prime Minister's rival, say with a smile that
it might be for the convenience of the House that the seat should be
utilise. Mr. Gresham at this time have, with declare purpose, ask
and obtain the Speaker's leave of absence and be abroad. who
should lead the House? that have be a great question, cause by the
fact that the Prime Minister be in the House of Lords;--and what
office should the Leader hold? Mr. Monk have consent to take the
Exchequer, but the right to sit opposite to the Treasury Box and to
consider himself for the time the principal spirit in that chamber
be at last assign to Sir Orlando Drought. "it will never do,"
say Mr. Rattler to Mr. Roby. "I donot mean to say anything against
Drought, who have always be a very useful man to your party;--but he
lack something of the position."
"the fact be," say Roby, "that wehave trust to two man so long that
we donot know how to suppose any one else big enough to fill their
place. Monk wouldnot have do. the House donot care about Monk."
"I always think it should be Wilson, and so I tell the Duke. he have
an idea that it should be one of your man."
"I think hebe right there," say Roby. "there ought to be something
like a fair division. individual might be content, but the party
would be dissatisfy. for myself, Iwould have soon stay out as an
independent member, but Daubeny say that he think I be bind to
make myself useful."
"I tell the Duke from the beginning," say Rattler, "that I donot
think that I could be of any service to he. of course, I would
support he, but I have be too thoroughly a party man for a new

I be bind to join he. I ask Gresham, and when Gresham say so
too, of course I have no help for it."
neither of these excellent public servant have tell a lie in this.
some such conversation as those report have passed;--but a man
donot lie when he exaggerate an emphasis, or even when he give
by a tone a meaning to a man's word exactly opposite to that which
another tone would convey. or, if he do lie in do so, he do
not know that he lie. Mr. Rattler have go back to his old office at
the Treasury and Mr. Roby have be force to content himself with the
Secretaryship at the Admiralty. but, as the old Duke have say, they
be close friend, and prepare to fight together any battle which
might keep they in their present position.
many of the care of office the Prime Minister do succeed in
shuffle off altogether on to the shoulder of his eld friend.
he would not concern himself with the appointment of lady, about
whom he say he know nothing, and as to whose fitness and claim he
profess himself to be as ignorant as the office messenger. the
offer be of course make in the usual form, as though come direct
from the Queen, through the Prime minister;--but the selection be
in truth effect by the old Duke in council with--an illustrious
personage. the matter affect our duke,--only in so far that he
could not get out of his mind that strange application from his own
wife. "that she should have even dream of it!" he would say to
himself, not yet have acquire sufficient experience of his fellow
creature to be aware how wonderfully temptation will affect even
those who appear to be least subject to they. the town horse, use to
gaudy trapping, no doubt despise the work of his country brother;
but yet, now and again, there come upon he a sudden desire to
plough. the desire for ploughing have come upon the Duchess, but the
Duke could not understand it.
he perceive, however, in spite of the multiplicity of his official
work, that his refusal sit heavily on his wife's breast, and that,
though she speak no further word, she brood over her injury. and
his heart be sad within he when he think that he have vex
her,--love she as he do with all his heart, but with a heart
that be never demonstrative. when she be unhappy he be miserable,
though he would hardly know the cause of his misery. her ridicule and
raillery he could bear, though they sting he; but her sorrow, if
ever she be sorrowful, or her sullenness, if ever she be sullen,
upset he altogether. he be in truth so soft of heart that he could
not bear the discomfort of the one person in the world who seem to
he to be near to he. he have expressly ask she for her sympathy
in the business he have on hand,--thereby go much beyond his usual
coldness of manner. she, with an eagerness which might have be
expect from she, have promise that she would slave for he, if
slavery be necessary. then she have make her request, have be
refuse, and be now moody. "the Duchess of ---- be to be mistress
of the Robes," he say to her one day. he have go to she, up to her
own room, before he dress for dinner, have devote much more time
than as Prime Minister he ought to have do to a resolution that he
would make thing straight with she, and to the good way of do it.
"so I be tell. she ought to know her way about the place, as I
remember she be at the same work when I be a girl of eleven."
"thatbe not so very long ago, Cora."
"Silverbridge be old now than I be then, and I think that make it
a very long time ago." Lord Silverbridge be the Duke's eld son.
"but what do it matter? if she begin her career in the time of
George the Fourth, what be it to you?"
"nothing on earth,--only that she do in truth begin her career
in the time of George the Third. Ibe sure shebe nearer sixty than
fifty."
"Ibe glad to see you remember your date so well."
"itbe a pity she should not remember hers in the way she dress,"
say the Duchess.
this be marvellous to him,--that his wife, who as Lady Glencora
Palliser have be so conspicuous for a wild disregard of social rule
as to be look upon by many as an enemy of her own class, should be
so depressed by not be allow to be the Queen's head servant as
to descend to personal invective! "Ibe afraid," say he, attempt
to smile, "that it willnot come within the compass of my office to
effect or even to propose any radical change in her grace's apparel.
but donot you think that you and I can afford to ignore all that?"
"I can certainly. she may be an antiquated Eve for I."
"I hope, Cora, you be not still disappoint because I do not agree
with you when you speak about the place for yourself."
"not because you do not agree with me,--but because you do not
think I fit to be trust with any judgment of my own. I donot know
why Ibe always to be look upon as different from other women,--as
though I be half a savage."
"you be what you have make yourself, and I have always rejoice that
you be as you be, fresh, untrammelle, without many prejudice
which afflict other lady, and free from bond by which they be

to certain danger of its own."
"there be no doubt about the danger. the chance be that when I see
her grace I shall tell she what I think about she."
"you will I am sure say nothing unkind to a lady who be suppose to
be in the place she now fill by my authority. but do not let we
quarrel about an old woman."
"I willnot quarrel with you even about a young one."
"I cannot be at ease within myself while I think you be resent my
refusal. you do not know how constantly I carry you about with I."
"you carry a very unnecessary burden then," she say. but he could
tell at once from the altered tone of her voice, and from the light
of her eye as he glance into her face, that her anger about "The
Robes" be appease.
"I have do as you ask about a friend of your," he say. this
occur just before the final and perfect list of the new man have
appear in all the newspaper.
"what friend?"
"Mr. Finn be to go to Ireland."
"go to Ireland!--How do you mean?"
"it be look upon as be very great promotion. indeed I be tell
that he be consider to be the lucky man in all the scramble."
"you donot mean as Chief Secretary?"
"yes, I do. he certainly couldnot go as Lord Lieutenant."
"but they say that Barrington Erle be go to Ireland."
"well; yes. I donot know that youwould be interested by all the in and
out of it. but Mr. Erle decline. it seem that Mr. Erle be after
all the one man in Parliament modest enough not to consider himself
to be fit for any place that can be offer to he."
"Poor Barrington! he do not like the idea of cross the Channel
so often. I quite sympathise with he. and so Phineas be to be
Secretary for Ireland! not in the Cabinet?"
"no;--not in the Cabinet. it be not by any mean usual that he should
be."
"that be promotion, and I be glad! poor Phineas! I hope they willnot
murder he, or anything of that kind. they do murder people, you
know, sometimes."
"hebe an Irishman himself."
"thatbe just the reason why they should. he must put up with that of
course. I wonder whether shewill like go. theywill be able to spend
money, which they always like, over there. he come backwards and
forwards every week,--doesn't he?"
"not quite that, I believe."
"I shall miss she, if she have to stay away long. I know you donot
like she."
"I do like she. she have always behave well, both to I and to my
uncle."
"she be an angel to him,--and to you too, if you only know it. I
dare say yoube send he to Ireland so as to get she away from

"I have ask he to undertake the office," say the Duke solemnly,

pleasure in propose it to he because I think that it would

"it do please I, and I willnot be cross any more, and the Duchess
of ---- may wear her clothe just as she please, or go without they.
and as for Mrs. Finn, I donot see why she should be with he always
when he go. you can quite understand how necessary she be to I.
but she be in truth the only woman in London to whom I can say what I
think. and it be a comfort, you know, to have some one."
in this way the domestic peace of the Prime Minister be readjust,
and that sympathy and co-operation for which he have first ask
be accord to he. it may be a question whether on the whole the
Duchess do not work hard than he do. she do not at first dare to
expound to he those grand idea which she have conceive in regard to
magnificence and hospitality. she say nothing of any extraordinary
expenditure of money. but she set herself to work after her own
fashion, make to he suggestion as to dinner and evening
reception, to which he object only on the score of time. "you must
eat your dinner somewhere," she say, "and you need only come in just
before we sit down, and go into your own room if you please without
come upstairs at all. I can at any rate do that part of it for
you." and she do do that part of it with marvellous energy all
through the month of May,--so that by the end of the month, within
six week of the time at which she first hear of the Coalition
Ministry, all the world have begin to talk of the Prime Minister's
dinner, and of the reception give by the Prime Minister's wife.
CHAPTER IX
Mrs. Dick's Dinner Party.--No. I
our reader must not forget the trouble of poor Emily Wharton
amidst the gorgeous festivity of the new Prime Minister. throughout
April and May she do not see Ferdinand Lopez. it may be remember
that on the night when the matter be discuss between she and
her father, she promise he that she would not do so without his
permission,--saying, however, at the same time very openly that her
happiness depend on such permission be give to she. for two or
three week not a word far be say between she and her father on
the subject, and he have endeavour to banish the subject from his
mind,--feeling no doubt that if nothing far be ever say it
would be so much the well. but then his daughter refer to the
matter,--very plainly, with a simple question, and without disguise
of her own feeling, but still in a manner which he could not bring
himself to rebuke. "Aunt Harriet have ask I once or twice to go
there of an evening, when you have be out. I have decline because
I think Mr. Lopez would be there. must I tell she that I be not to
meet Mr. Lopez, papa?"
"if she have he there on purpose to throw he in your way, I shall
think very badly of she."
"but he have be in the habit of be there, papa. of course if you
be decide about this, it be well that I should not see he."
"do I not tell you that I be decide?"
"you say you would make some further inquiry and speak to I again."
now Mr. Wharton have make inquiry, but have learn nothing to reassure
himself;--neither have he be able to learn any fact, put his
finger on which he could point out to his daughter clearly that
the marriage would be unsuitable for she. of the man's ability and
position, as certainly also of his manner, the world at large seem
to speak well. he have be blackball at two club, but apparently
without any define reason. he live as though he possess a
handsome income, and yet be in no degree fast or flashy. he be
suppose to be an intimate friend of Mr. Mills Happerton, one of
the partner in the world-famous commercial house of Hunky and Sons,
which deal in million. indeed there have be at one time a rumour
that he be go to be take into the house of Hunky and Sons as a
junior partner. it be evident that many people have be favourably
impress by his outward demeanour, by his mode of talk, and by his
way of living. but no one know anything about he. with regard to his
material position Mr. Wharton could of course ask direct question if
he please, and require evidence as to alleged property. but he feel
that by do so he would abandon his right to object to the man as
be a portuguese stranger, and he do not wish to have Ferdinand
Lopez as a son-in-law, even though he should be a partner in Hunky
and son, and able to maintain a gorgeous palace at South Kensington.
"I have make inquiry."
"well, papa?"
"I donot know anything about he. nobody know anything about he."
"could you not ask himself anything you want to know? if I might see
he I would ask he."
"that would not do at all."
"it come to this, papa, that I be to sever myself from a man to whom
I be attach, and whom you must admit that I have be allow to
meet from day to day with no caution that his intimacy be unpleasant
to you, because he be call--lopez."
"it benot that at all. there be english people of that name; but he
benot an Englishman."
"of course, if you say so, papa, it must be so. I have tell Aunt
Harriet that I consider myself to be prohibit from meet
Mr. Lopez by what you have say; but I think, papa, you be a
little--cruel to I."
"cruel to you!" say Mr. Wharton, almost burst into tear.
"I be as ready to obey as a child;--but, not be a child, I think
I ought to have a reason." to this Mr. Wharton make no further
immediate answer, but pull his hair, and shuffle his foot about,
and then escape out of the room.
a few day afterwards his sister-in-law attack he. "be we to
understand, Mr. Wharton, that Emily be not to meet Mr. Lopez again?
it make it very unpleasant, because he have be intimate at our
house."
"I never say a word about she not meet he. of course I do not
wish that any meeting should be contrive between they."
"as it stand now it be prejudicial to she. of course it cannot but
be observe, and it be so odd that a young lady should be forbid
to meet a certain man. it look so unpleasant for her,--as though she
have misbehave herself."
"I have never think so for a moment."
"of course you have not. how could you have think so, Mr. Wharton?"
"I say that I never do."
"what must he think when he knows,--as of course he do know,--that
she have be forbid to meet he? it must make he fancy that he be
make very much of. all that be so very bad for a girl! indeed it be,
Mr. Wharton." of course there be absolute dishonesty in all this on
the part of Mrs. Roby. she be true enough to Emily's lover,--too
true to he; but she be false to Emily's father. if Emily would
have yield to she she would have arrange meeting at her own
house between the lover altogether in opposition to the father.
nevertheless there be a show of reason about what she say which
Mr. Wharton be unable to overcome. and at the same time there be
a reality about his girl's sorrow which overcome he. he have never
hitherto consult any one about anything in his family, have
always find his own information and intellect sufficient for his own
affair. but now he feel grievously in want of some pillar,--some
female pillar,--on which he could lean. he do not know all Mrs.
Roby's iniquity; but still he feel that she be not the pillar
of which he be in need. there be no such pillar for his use, and
he be drive to acknowledge to himself that in this distressing
position he must be guide by his own strength, and his own light.
he think it all out as well as he could in his own chamber,
allow his book or his brief to lie idle beside he for many a
half-hour. but he be much puzzled both as to the extent of his own
authority and the manner in which it should be use. he certainly have
not desire his daughter not to meet the man. he could understand
that unless some affront have be offer such an edict enforce as
to the conduct of a young lady would induce all her acquaintance to
suppose that she be either very much in love or else very prone to
misbehave herself. he fear, indeed, that she be very much in love,
but it would not be prudent to tell her secret to all the world.
perhaps it would be well that she should meet him,--alway with
the understanding that she be not to accept from he any peculiar
attention. if she would be obedient in one particular, she would
probably be so in the other;--and, indeed, he do not at all doubt
her obedience. she would obey, but would take care to show he that
she be make miserable by obey. he begin to foresee that he have a
bad time before he.
and then as he still sit idle, think of it all, his mind wander
off to another view of the subject. could he be happy, or even
comfortable, if she be unhappy? of course he endeavour to
convince himself that if he be bold, determine, and dictatorial
with she, it would only be in order that her future happiness might
be secure. a parent be often bind to disregard the immediate
comfort of a child. but then be he sure that he be right? he of
course have his own way of look at life, but be it reasonable that
he should force his girl to look at thing with his eye? the man be
distasteful to he as be unlike his idea of an english gentleman,
and as be without those far-reach fibre and root by which he
think that the solidity and stability of a human tree should be
assure. but the world be change around he every day. Royalty be
marry out of its degree. peer' son be look only for money.
and, more than that, peer' daughter be bestow themselves on
Jews and shopkeeper. have he not well make the usual inquiry about
the man's mean, and, if satisfied on that head, let the girl do as
she would? add to all this there be grow on he a feeling that
ultimately youth would as usual triumph over age, and that he would
be beat. if that be so, why worry himself, or why worry she?
on the day after Mrs. Roby's attack upon he he again see that lady,
have on this occasion send round to ask she to come to he. "I want
you to understand that I put no embargo on Emily as to meet Mr.
Lopez. I can trust she fully. I do not wish she to encourage his
attention, but I by no mean wish she to avoid he."
"be I to tell Emily what you say?"
"I will tell she myself. I think it well to say as much to you, as
you seem to be embarrass by the fear that they might happen to
see each other in your drawing-room."
"it be rather awkward;--wasn't it?"
"I have speak now because you seem to think so." his manner to she
be not very pleasant, but Mrs. Roby have know he for many year,
and do not care very much for his manner. she have an object to gain,
and could put up with a good deal for the sake of her object.
"very well. then I shall know how to act. but, Mr. Wharton, I must
say this, you know Emily have a will of her own, and you must not hold
I responsible for anything that may occur." as soon as he hear this
he almost resolve to withdraw the concession he have made;--but he
do not do so.
very soon after this there come a special invitation from Mr. and
Mrs. Roby, ask the Whartons, father and daughter, to dine with
they round the corner. it be quite a special invitation, because
it come in the form of a card,--which be unusual between the
two family. but the dinner be too, in some degree, a special
dinner,--as Emily be enable to explain to her father, the whole
speciality have be fully detailed to herself by her aunt. Mr.
Roby, whose belonging be not generally aristocratic, have one great
connexion with whom, after many year of quarrelling, he have lately
come into amity. this be his half-brother, considerably old than
himself, and be no other than that Mr. Roby who be now Secretary to
the Admiralty, and who in the last Conservative Government have be
one of the Secretaries to the Treasury. the old Mr. Roby of all,
now long since gather to his father, have have two wife and two
son. the eld son have not be leave as well off as friend, or
perhaps as he himself, could have wish. but he have rise in the
world by his wit, have make his way into Parliament, and have become,
as all reader of these chronicle know, a staff of great strength to
his party. but he have always be a poor man. his period of office
have be much short than those of his friend Rattler, and his other
source of income have not be certain. his young half-brother,
who, as far as the great world be concern, have none of his eld
brother's advantage, have be endow with some fortune from his

"I donot particularly want to meet Mr. Thomas Roby," the old

"I suppose it may as well be do," say Mr. Wharton.

CHAPTER X
Mrs. Dick's Dinner Party.--No. II

lopez, though he talk throughout the whole of dinner,--turning

but Lord Mongrober's dinner have not yet have the effect of mollify

there be two drawing-room up-stair, open into each other, but

CHAPTER XI
Carlton Terrace
certainly the thing be do very well by Lady Glen,--as many in the
political world persist in call she even in these day. she have
not as yet quite carry out her plan,--the doing of which would have
require she to reconcile her husband to some excessive abnormal
expenditure, and to have obtain from he a deliberate sanction for
appropriation and probable sale of property. she never could find the
proper moment for do this, have, with all her courage,--low down
in some corner of her heart,--a wholesome fear of a certain quiet
power which her husband possess. she could not bring herself to
make she proposition;--but she almost act as though it have be
make and approve. her house be always gorgeous with flower. of
course there would be the bill;--and he, when he see the exotic, and
the whole place turn into a bower of ever fresh bloom floral
glory, must know that there would be the bill. and when he find
that there be an archducal dinner-party every week, and an almost
imperial reception twice a week; that at these reception a banquet
be always provide; when he be ask whether she might buy a
magnificent pair of bay carriage-horse, as to which she assure he
that nothing so lovely have ever as yet be see step in the
street of London,--of course he must know that the bill would come.
it be well, perhaps, to do it in this way, than to make any direct
proposition. and then, early in June, she speak to he as to the
guest to be invite to Gatherum Castle in August. "do you want to
go to Gatherum in August?" he ask in surprise. for she hat the
place, and have hardly be content to spend ten day there every year
at Christmas.
"I think it should be do," she say solemnly. "one cannot quite
consider just now what one like oneself."
"why not?"
"you would hardly go to a small place like matching in your present
position. there be so many people whom you should entertain! you
would probably have two or three of the foreign minister down for a
time."
"we always use to find plenty of room at Matching."
"but you do not always use to be Prime Minister. it be only for such
a time as this that such a house as Gatherum be serviceable."
he be silent for a moment, think about it, and then give way
without another word. she be probably right. there be the huge pile
of magnificent building; and somebody, at any rate, have think that

be do at any time, it ought to be do now. in that his wife have
be right. "very well. then let we go there."
"Iwill manage it all," say the Duchess,--"I and Locock." Locock be
the house-steward.
"I remember once," say the Duke, and he smile as he speak with a

say that his place entail upon he expense high than his
stipend would defray. I begin to think that my experience will be the
same."
"do that fret you?"
"no, Cora;--it certainly do not fret I, or I should not allow it.
but I think there should be a limit. no man be ever rich enough to
squander."
though they be to squander she fortune,--the money which she
have brought,--for the next ten year at a much great rate than

he have look at she, and read it all in her face. "God know," he

"for your sake!" then he stoop down and kiss she twice, and

then there be solemn cabinet hold, at which she preside, and

"indeed, no;--very far from that. I havenot a shilling."

"that must be nonsense. but donot you think woman be fool to marry

"and how well they do for you!"
"Pas si mal.--He's Prime Minister, which be a great thing, and I

our old friend Phineas Finn, who have now reach a high place in
politic than even his political dream have assign to he, though

commit to his care? who do not know the gentleman at the Home

something might be do to prove to his susceptible countryman that
at the present moment no curse could be lay upon they so heavy as
that of have to rule themselves apart from England; and he think

for Tipperary." Phineas Finn, have see, or think that he have

an honest and patriotic man might not compass some remedy for the

the Duchess be on the whole very successful with her party. there

"who be that man? Ihave see he here before. the Duchess be talk

Lopez be there for the third time, about the middle of June, and have

Mr. Boffin be a gentleman who have belong to the late Ministry, but

"I think it be so lovely!" say Mrs. Boffin. "you look down through

another old friend, of whom former chronicle be not silent, be at

Phineas Finn and Barrington Erle have go through some political
struggle together, and have be very intimate.
"I hope not very much," say the lady.
"why so? because hebe away so much?"
"no;--not that. I should not grudge his absence if the work satisfy
he. but I know he so well. the more he take to it now,--the more
sanguine he be as to some special thing to be done,--the more bitter
will be the disappointment when he be disappoint. for there never
really be anything special to be done;--is there, Mr. Erle?"
"I think there be always a little too much zeal about Finn."
"of course there be. and then with zeal there always go a thin
skin,--and unjustifiable expectation, and bite despair, and
contempt of other, and all the element of unhappiness."
"that be a sad programme for your husband."
"he have recuperative faculty which bring he round at last:--but I
really doubt whether he be make for a politician in this country.
you remember Lord Brock?"
"dear old Brock;--of course I do. how should I not, if you remember
he?"
"young man be boy at college, row in boat, when woman have be
ever so long out in the world. he be the very model of an English
statesman. he love his country dearly, and wish she to be, as he
believe she to be, first among nation. but he have no belief in
perpetuate her greatness by any grand improvement. let thing take
their way naturally,--with a slight direction hither or thither as
thing might require. that be his method of ruling. he believe in
man rather than measure. as long as he have loyalty around he, he
could be personally happy, and quite confident as to the country. he
never break his heart because he could not carry this or that reform.
what would have hurt he would have be to be worst in personal
conflict. but he could always hold his own, and he be always
happy. your man with a thin skin, a vehement ambition, a scrupulous
conscience, and a sanguine desire for rapid improvement, be never a
happy, and seldom a fortunate politician."
"Mrs. Finn, you understand it all well than any one else that I
ever know."
"I have be watch it a long time, and of course very closely
since I have be marry."
"but you have an eye train to see it all. what a useful member you
would have be in a government!"
"but I should never have have patience to sit all night upon that
bench in the House of Commons. how man can do it! they mustnot read.
they cannot think because of the speaking. it donot do for they to
talk. I donot believe they ever listen. it benot in human nature to
listen hour after hour to such platitude. I believe they fall into a
habit of half-wakeful sleeping, which carry they through the hour;
but even that cannot be pleasant. I look upon the Treasury Bench in
July as a sort of casual-ward which we know to be necessary, but be
almost too horrid to be contemplate."
"man do get bread and skilly there certainly; but, Mrs. Finn, we can
go into the library and smoking-room."
"oh, yes;--and a clerk in an office can read the newspaper instead
of do his duty. but there be a certain surveillance exercise, and
a certain quantity of work exact. I have meet Lords of the Treasury
out at dinner on Mondays and Thursdays, but we all regard they as
boy who have shirk out of school. I think upon the whole, Mr.
Erle, we woman have the good of it."
"I donot suppose you will go in for your 'right'."
"not by Act of Parliament, or by platform meeting. I have a great
idea of a woman's right; but that be the way, I think, to throw they
away. what do you think of the Duchess's evening?"
"Lady Glen be in her way as great a woman as you are;--perhap
great, because nothing ever stop she."
"whereas I have scruple."
"her grace have none. she have feeling and conviction which keep she
straight, but no scruple. look at she now talk to Sir Orlando
Drought, a man whom she both hat and despise. I be sure she be
look forward to some happy time in which the Duke may pitch
Sir Orlando overboard, and rule supreme, with I or some other
subordinate lead the House of Commons simply as lieutenant. such a
time will never come, but that be her idea. but she be talk to Sir
Orlando now as if she be pour her full confidence into his ear,
and Sir Orlando be believe she. Sir Orlando be in a seventh heaven,
and she be measure his credulity inch by inch."
"she make the place very bright."
"and be spend an enormous deal of money," say Barrington Erle.
"what do it matter?"
"well, no;--if the Duke like it. I have an idea that the Duke would

do he? no one would think he be the master of everything here. he
have get himself hide almost behind the screen. Ibe sure he donot
like it."
"he try to like whatever she like," say Mrs. Finn.
as her husband be away in Ireland, Mrs. Finn be stay in the
house in Carlton Gardens. the Duchess at present require so much of
her time that this be find to be convenient. when, therefore, the
guest on the present occasion have all go, the Duchess and Mrs.

"who be hopeless?"
"Heavens and earth! Plantagenet;--who else? be there another man in
the world would come into his own house, among his own guest, and

the staff on which alone Ministers can lean in this country with
security."

"you know as well as I do that the two go together. wehave see enough

he have stay away people might have think that he be read

"he talk to the Duke of St. Bungay because there be no one he trust
so much. a few year ago it would have be Mr. Mildmay."
"my dear," say the Duchess angrily, "you treat I as though I be
