point. he have to reflect what other think of it, and to endeavour
to do what will please they. there be I tell tarradiddle by the
yard to that old oaf, Sir Orlando Drought, when a confidential word
from Plantagenet would have have ten time more effect. and why cannot
he speak a word to the people's wife? they wouldnot bite he. he have
get to say a few word to you sometimes,--to whom it donot signify,
my dear--"
"I donot know about that."
"but he never speak to another woman. he be here this evening
for exactly forty minute, and he donot open his lip to a female
creature. I watch he. how on earth be I to pull he through if he
go on in that way? yes, Locock, Iwill go to bed, and I donot think
Iwill get up for a week."
CHAPTER XII
the gathering of Clouds
throughout June and the first week of July the affair of the
Ministry go on successfully, in spite of the social sin of the
Duke and the occasional despair of the Duchess. there have be many
politician who have think, or have, at any rate, predict, that
the Coalition Ministry would not live a month. there have be
man, such as Lord Fawn on one side and Mr. Boffin on the other,
who have find themselves strand disagreeably,--with no certain
position,--unwille to sit immediately behind a Treasury bench from
which they be exclude, and too shy to place themselves immediately
opposite. seat beneath the gangway be, of course, open to such of
they as be member of the Lower House, and those seat have to be
use; but they be not accustomed to sit beneath the gangway. these
gentleman have expect that the seed of weakness, of which they have
perceive the scattering, would grow at once into an enormous crop of
blunder, difficulty, and complication; but, for a while, the
Ministry be save from these danger either by the energy of the
Prime Minister, or the popularity of his wife, or perhaps by the
sagacity of the eld Duke;--so that there grow up an idea that the
Coalition be really the proper thing. in one respect it certainly
be successful. the Home Rulers, or irish party generally, be leave
without an inch of stand ground. their support be not need, and
therefore they be not court. for the moment there be not even
a necessity to pretend that Home Rule be anything but an absurdity
from begin to end;--so much so that one or two lead Home
Rulers, man who have take up the cause not only that they might
become member of Parliament, but with some further idea of
speech-making and popularity, declare that the Coalition have be
form merely with a view of put down Ireland. this capability of
dispense with a generally untractable element of support be feel
to be a great comfort. then, too, there be a set in the House,--at
the moment not a very numerous set,--who have be troublesome friend
to the old liberal party, and which the Coalition be able, if not to
ignore, at any rate to disregard. these be the staunch economist,
and argumentative philosophical radicals,--man of stand and
repute, who be always in doubtful time individually flatter by
Ministers, who have great privilege accord to they of speak and
dividing, and who be not unfrequently even thank for their rod by
the very owner of the back which bear the scourge. these man could
not be quite set aside by the Coalition as be the Home Rulers. it
be not even yet, perhaps, wise to count they out, or to leave they
to talk to bench absolutely empty;--but the tone of flattery with
which they have be address become gradually less warm; and when
the scourge be wield, ministerial back take themselves out of
the way. there grow up unconsciously a feeling of security against
attack which be distasteful to these gentleman, and be in
itself perhaps a little dangerous. gentleman bind to support the
Government, when they perceive that there be comparatively but
little to do, and that that little might be easily do, became
careless, and, perhaps, a little contemptuous. so that the great
popular orator, Mr. Turnbull, find himself compel to rise in his
seat, and ask whether the noble Duke at the head of the Government
think himself strong enough to rule without attention to
parliamentary detail. the question be ask with an air of
inexorable severity, and be intend to have deep signification. Mr.
Turnbull have dislike the Coalition from the beginning; but then Mr.
Turnbull always dislike everything. he have so accustom himself to
wield the constitutional cat-of-nine-tail, that heaven will hardly
be happy to he unless he be allow to flog the cherubim. though the
party with which he be presume to act have generally be in power
since he have be in the House, he have never allow himself to agree
with a Minister on any point. and as he have never be satisfy
with a Liberal Government, it be not probable that he should endure
a coalition in silence. at the end of a rather lengthy speech, he
repeat his question, and then sit down, take his place with all
that constitutional indignation which become the parliamentary
flagellator of the day. the little joke with which Sir Orlando
answer he be very well in their way. Mr. Turnbull do not care
much whether he be answer or not. perhaps the jauntiness of Sir
Orlando, which imply that the Coalition be too strong to regard
attack, somewhat irritated outsider. but there certainly grow up
from that moment a feeling among such man as Erle and Rattler that
care be necessary, that the House, take as a whole, be not in a
condition to be manipulate with easy freedom, and that Sir Orlando
must be make to understand that he be not strong enough to depend
upon jauntiness. the jaunty statesman must be very sure of his
personal following. there be a general opinion that Sir Orlando have
not bring the Coalition well out of the first real attack which have
be make upon it.
"well, Phineas; how do you like the Phoenix?" Phineas Finn have
fly back to London at the instigation probably of Mr. Rattler, and
be now stand at the window of Brooks's club with Barrington Erle.
it be near nine one Thursday evening, and they be both about to
return to the House.
"I donot like the Castle, if you mean that."
"tyrone benot troublesome, surely?" the Marquis of Tyrone be the
Lord Lieutenant of the day, and have in his time be a very strong
Conservative.
"he find I troublesome, I fear."
"I donot wonder at that, Phineas."
"how should it be otherwise? what can he and I have in sympathy with
one another? he have be bring up with all an Orangeman's hatred
for a Papist. now that he be in high office, he can abandon the
display of the feeling,--perhaps the feeling itself as regard the
country at large. he know that it donot become a Lord Lieutenant
to be orange. but how can he put himself into a boat with I?"
"all that kind of thing vanish when a man be in office."
"yes, as a rule; because man go together into office with the same
general predilection. be it too hot to walk down?"
"Iwill walk a little way,--till you make I hot by argue."
"I havenot an argument leave in I," say Phineas. "of course
everything over there seem easy enough now,--so easy that Lord
Tyrone evidently imagine that the good time be come back in
which governor may govern and not be govern."
"you be pretty quiet in Ireland now, I suppose;--no martial law,
suspension of the habeas corpus, or anything of that kind, just at
present?"
"no; thank goodness!" say Phineas.
"Ibe not quite sure whether a general suspension of the habeas corpus
would not upon the whole be the most comfortable state of thing for
Irishmen themselves. but whether good or bad, youhave nothing of that
kind of thing now. youhave no great measure that you wish to pass?"
"but theyhave a great measure that they wish to pass."
"they know well than that. they donot want to kill their golden
goose."
"the people, who be infinitely ignorant of all political work,
do want it. there be county in which, if you be to poll the
people, Home Rule would carry nearly every voter,--except the member
themselves."
"you wouldnot give it they?"
"certainly not;--any more than I would allow a son to ruin himself
because he ask I. but I would endeavour to teach they that they
can get nothing by home rule,--that their taxis would be heavy,
their property less secure, their life less safe, their general
position more debased, and their chance of national success more
remote than ever."
"you can never teach they, except by the slow lesson of habit. the
Heptarchy donot mould itself into a nation in a day."
"man be govern then, and could be and be mould. I feel sure
that even in Ireland there be a stratum of man, above the working
peasant, who would understand, and make those below they understand,
the position of the country, if they could only be get to give up
fighting about religion. even now Home Rule be regard by the
multitude as a weapon to be use against Protestantism on behalf of
the Pope."
"I suppose the Pope be the great sinner?"
"they get over the Pope in France,--even in early day, before
religion have become a farce in the country. they have do so in
Italy."
"Yes;--they've get over the Pope in Italy, certainly."
"and yet," say Phineas, "the bulk of the people be staunch
Catholics. of course the same attempt to maintain a temporal
influence, with the hope of recover temporal power, be make in
other country. but while we see the attempt fail elsewhere,--so
that we know that the power of the Church be go to the wall,--yet
in Ireland it be infinitely strong now than it be fifty, or even
twenty year ago."
"because we have be remove restraint on Papal aggression, while
other nation have be impose restraint. there be those at
Rome who believe all England to be romish at heart, because here in
England a Roman Catholic can say what he will, and print what he
will."
"and yet," say Phineas, "all England do not return one Catholic to
the House, while we have Jews in plenty. you have a Jew among your
english judge, but at present not a single roman Catholic. what do
you suppose be the comparative number of the population here in
England?"
"and you be go to cure all this;--while tyrone think it ought to
be leave as it be? I rather agree with Tyrone."
"no," say Phineas, wearily; "I doubt whether I shall ever cure
anything, or even make any real attempt. my patriotism just go far
enough to make I unhappy, and Lord Tyrone think that while Dublin
lady dance at the Castle, and the list of agrarian murder be keep
low, the country be admirably manage. I donot quite agree with
him;--thatbe all."
then there arise a legal difficulty, which cause much trouble to the
Coalition Ministry. there fall vacant a certain seat on the bench of
judges,--a seat of considerable dignity and importance, but not quite
of the high rank. Sir Gregory Grogram, who be a rich, energetic
man, determine to have a peerage, and convince that, should
the Coalition fall to piece, the liberal element would be in
the ascendant,--so that the woolsack would then be open to
him,--decline to occupy the place. Sir Timothy Beeswax, the
Solicitor-General, see that it be exactly suited for he, and have no
hesitation in express his opinion to that effect. but the place
be not give to Sir Timothy. it be explain to Sir Timothy that
the old rule,--or rather custom,--of offer certain high position
to the law officer of the Crown have be abrogate. some Prime
Minister, or, more probably, some collection of Cabinet Ministers,
have assert the custom to be a bad one,--and, as far as right go,
Sir Timothy be declare not to have a leg to stand upon. he be
inform that his service in the House be too valuable to be so
lose. some people say that his temper be against he. other be
of opinion that he have rise from the rank too quickly, and that
Lord Ramsden, who have come from the same party, think that Sir
Timothy have not yet win his spur. the Solicitor-General resign
in a huff, and then withdraw his resignation. Sir Gregory think
the withdrawal should not be accept, have find Sir Timothy to
be an unsympathetic colleague. our Duke consult the old Duke,
among whose theory of official life forbearance to all colleague
and subordinate be conspicuous. the withdrawal be, therefore,
allowed,--but the Coalition could not after that be say to be strong
in regard to its Law officer.
but the first concert attack against the Ministry be make in
reference to the budget. Mr. Monk, who have consent to undertake the
duty of Chancellor of the Exchequer under the urgent entreaty
of the two duke, be of course late with his budget. it be April
before the coalition have be form. the budget when produce have
be very popular. budget, like baby, be always little love
when first bear. but as their infancy pass away, they also become
subject to many stripe. the detail be less pleasing than be the
whole in the hand of the nurse. there be a certain "interest,"
very influential both by general wealth and by the presence of many
member in the House, which think that Mr. Monk have disregard its
just claim. Mr. Monk have refuse to relieve the brewer from their
licence. now the Brewers have for some year be agitate about
their licences,--and it be acknowledge in politic that any measure
be to be carry, or to be leave out in the cold uncarried and
neglect, accord to the number of deputation which may be get to
press a Minister on the subject. now the Brewers have have deputation
after deputation to many Chancellors of the Exchequer; and these
deputation have be most respectable,--we may almost say imperative.
it be quite usual for a deputation to have four or five County
member among its body, all brewer; and the average wealth of a
deputation of Brewers would buy up half London. all the Brewers in
the House have be among the supporter of the Coalition, the number
of liberal and conservative Brewers have be about equal. but
now there be a fear that the "interest" might put itself into
opposition. Mr. Monk have be firm. More than one of the Ministry have
wish to yield;--but he have discuss the matter with his chief, and
they be both very firm. the Duke have never doubt. Mr. Monk have
never doubt. from day to day certain organ of the Press express
an opinion, gradually increase in strength, that however strong
might be the Coalition as a body, it be weak as to finance. this be
hard, because not very many year ago the Duke himself have be know
as a particularly strong Minister of Finance. an amendment be move
in Committee as to the Brewers' Licences, and there be almost a
general opinion that the Coalition would be break up. Mr. Monk would
certainly not remain in office if the Brewers be to be relieve
from their licence.
then it be that Phineas Finn be recall from Ireland in red-hot
haste. the measure be debate for a couple of night, and Mr. Monk
carry his point. the Brewers' licence be allow to remain, as
one great gentleman from Burton declare, a "disgrace to the fiscal
sagacity of the country." the Coalition be so far victorious;--but
there arise a general feeling that its strength have be impair.
CHAPTER XIII
Mr. Wharton complain
"I think you have betray I." this accusation be bring by Mr.
Wharton against Mrs. Roby in that lady's drawing-room, and be
occasion by a report that have be make to the old lawyer by his
daughter. he be very angry and almost violent;--so much so that by
his manner he give a considerable advantage to the lady whom he be
accuse.
Mrs. Roby undoubtedly have betray her brother-in-law. she have be
false to the trust repose in she. he have explain his wish to
she in regard to his daughter, to whom she have in some sort assume
to stand in place of a mother, and she, while pretend to act in
accordance with his wish, have directly oppose they. but it be not
likely that he would be able to prove her treachery though he might
be sure of it. he have desire that his girl should see as little as
possible of Ferdinand Lopez, but have hesitate to give a positive
order that she should not meet he. he have indeed himself take she
to a dinner party at which he know that she would meet he. but
Mrs. Roby have betray he. since the dinner party she have arrange
a meeting at her own house on behalf of the lover,--as to which
arrangement Emily Wharton have herself be altogether innocent. emily
have meet the man in her aunt's house, not expect to meet he, and
the lover have have an opportunity of speak his mind freely. she
also have speak hers freely. she would not engage herself to he
without her father's consent. with that consent she would do so,--oh,
so willingly! she do not coy her love. he might be certain that she
would give herself to no one else. her heart be entirely his. but
she have pledge herself to her father, and on no consideration would
she break that pledge. she go on to say that after what have pass
she think that they have well not meet. in such meeting there
could be no satisfaction, and must be much pain. but he have she
full permission to use any argument that he could use with her
father. on the evening of that day she tell her father all that have
passed,--omitte no detail either of what she have say or of what
have be say to her,--adde a positive assurance of obedience,
but do so with a severe solemnity and apparent consciousness of
ill-usage which almost break her father's heart. "your aunt must have
have he there on purpose," Mr. Wharton have say. but Emily would
neither accuse nor defend her aunt. "I at least know nothing of it,"
she say. "I know that," Mr. Wharton have ejaculate. "I know that.
I donot accuse you of anything, my dear,--except of think that
you understand the world well than I do." then Emily have retire
and Mr. Wharton have be leave to pass half the night in a perplexed
reverie, feel that he would be force ultimately to give way, and
yet certain that by do so he would endanger his child's happiness.
he be very angry with his sister-in-law, and on the next day, early
in the morning, he attack she. "I think you have betray I," he
say.
"what do you mean by that, Mr. Wharton?"
"you have have this man here on purpose that he might make love to
Emily."
"I have do no such thing. you tell I yourself that they be not
to be keep apart. he come here, and it would be very odd indeed if I
be to tell the servant that he be not to be admit. if you want
to quarrel with I, of course you can. I have always endeavour to
be a good friend to Emily."
"it be not be a good friend to she, bring she and this
adventurer together."
"I donot know why you call he an adventurer. but you be so very
odd in your idea! he be receive everywhere, and be always at the
Duchess of Omnium's."
"I donot care a fig about the Duchess."
"I dare say not. only the Duke happen to be Prime Minister, and his
house be consider to have the very good society that England, or
indeed Europe, can give. and I think it be something in a young man's
favour when it be know that he associate with such person as the
Duke of Omnium. I believe that most father would have a regard to
the company which a man keep when they think of their daughter's
marrying."
"I benot think of her marrying. I donot want she to marry;--not
this man at least. and I fancy the Duchess of Omnium be just as
likely to have scamp in her drawing-room as any other lady in
London."
"and do such man as Mr. Happerton associate with scamp?"
"I donot know anything about Mr. Happerton,--and I donot care
anything about he."
"he have L20,000 a year out of his business. and do Everett
associate with scamp?"
"very likely."
"I never know any one so much prejudiced as you be, Mr. Wharton.
when you have a point to carry therebe nothing you willnot say. I
suppose it come from be in the court."
"the long and the short of it be this," say the lawyer; "if I find
that Emily be bring here to meet Mr. Lopez, I must forbid she to
come at all."
"you must do as you please about that. but to tell you the truth, Mr.
Wharton, I think the mischief be do. such a girl as Emily, when she
have take it into her head to love a man, be not likely to give he
up."
"she have promise to have nothing to say to he without my sanction."
"we all know what that mean. youwill have to give way. youwill find
that it will be so. the stern parent who doom his daughter to
perpetual seclusion because she willnot marry the man he like, donot
belong to this age."
"who talk about seclusion?"
"do you suppose that shewill give up the man she love because you
donot like he? be that the way girl live now-a-day? she willnot run
away with he, because shebe not one of that sort; but unless yoube
hard-hearted than I take you to be, shewill make your life a burden
to you. and as for betray you, thatbe nonsense. youhave no right to
say it. Ibe not go to quarrel with you whatever you may say, but
youhave no right to say it."
Mr. Wharton, as he go away to Lincoln's Inn, bewail himself
because he know that he be not hard-hearted. what his sister-in-law
have say to he in that respect be true enough. if he could only rid
himself of a certain internal ague which make he feel that his life
be, indeed, a burden to he while his daughter be unhappy, he need
only remain passive and simply not give the permission without which
his daughter would not ever engage herself to this man. but the ague
trouble every hour of his present life. that sister-in-law of his
be a silly, vulgar, worldly, and most untrustworthy woman;--but she
have understand what she be say.
and there have be something in that argument about the Duchess of
Omnium's party, and Mr. Happerton, which have its effect. if the man
do live with the great and wealthy, it must be because they think
well of he and of his position. the fact of his be a "nasty
foreigner," and probably of jewish descent, remain. to he,
Wharton, the man must always be distasteful. but he could hardly
maintain his opposition to one of whom the choice spirit of the
world think well. and he try to be fair on the subject. it might
be that it be a prejudice. other probably do not find a man to be
odious because he be of foreign extraction and know by a foreign
name. other would not suspect a man of being of jewish blood because
he be swarthy, or even object to he if he be a Jew by descent.
but it be wonderful to he that his girl should like such a
man,--should like such a man well enough to choose he as the one
companion of her life. she have be bring up to prefer english
man, and English thinking, and English ways,--and english way, too,
somewhat of a past time. he think as do Brabantio, that it could
not be that without magic his daughter who have shunned--
"the wealthy curl darling of our nation,
would ever have, to incur a general mock,
run from her guardage to the sooty bosom
of such a thing as"--
this distasteful Portuguese.
that evening he say nothing far to his daughter, but sit with
she, silent and disconsolate. later in the evening, after she have
go to her room, Everett come in while the old man be still walk
up and down the drawing-room. "where have you be?" ask the
father,--not care a straw as to any reply when he ask the
question, but rouse almost to anger by the answer when it come.
"I have be din with Lopez at the club."
"I believe you live with that man."
"be there any reason, sir, why I should not?"
"you know that there be a good reason why there should be no peculiar
intimacy. but I donot suppose that my wish, or your sister's
welfare, will interest you."
"that be severe, sir."
"I be not such a fool as to suppose that you be to quarrel with
a man because I donot approve his address your sister; but I
do think that while this be go on, and while he persevere in
opposition to my distinct refusal, you need not associate with he in
any special manner."
"I donot understand your objection to he, sir."
"I dare say not. there be a great many thing you donot understand.
but I do object."
"hebe a very rise man. Mr. Roby be say to I just now--"
"who care a straw what a fool like Roby say?"
"I donot mean Uncle Dick, but his brother,--who, I suppose, be
somebody in the world. he be say to I just now that he wonder
why Lopez do not go into the House;--that he would be sure to get a
seat if he choose, and safe to make a mark when he get there."
"I dare say he could get into the House. I donot know any well-to-do
blackguard of whom you might not predict as much. a seat in the House
of Commons donot make a man a gentleman as far as I can see."
"I think every one allow that Ferdinand Lopez be a gentleman."
"who be his father?"
"I donot happen to know he, sir."
"and who be his mother? I donot suppose you will credit anything
because I say it, but as far as my experience go, a man donot
often become a gentleman in the first generation. a man may be very
worthy, very clever, very rich,--very well worth know, if you
will;--but when one talk of admit a man into close family
communion by marriage, one would, I fancy, wish to know something of
his father and mother." then Everett escape, and Mr. Wharton be
again leave to his own meditation. oh, what a peril, what a trouble,
what a labyrinth of difficulty be a daughter! he must either be
know as a stern, hard-hearted parent, utterly indifferent to his
child's feeling, use with tyranny the power over she which come to
he only from her sense of filial duty,--or else he must give up his
own judgment, and yield to she in a matter as to which he believe
that such yielding would be most pernicious to her own interest.
Hitherto he really know nothing of the man's means;--nor, if he could
have his own way, do he want such information. but, as thing be
go now, he begin to feel that if he could hear anything averse
to the man he might thus strengthen his hand against he. on the
follow day he go into the city, and call on an old friend,
a banker,--one whom he have know for nearly half a century, and of
whom, therefore, he be not afraid to ask a question. for Mr. Wharton
be a man not prone, in the ordinary intercourse of life, either to
ask or to answer question. "you donot know anything, do you, of a
man name Ferdinand Lopez?"
"I have hear of he. but why do you ask?"
"well; I have a reason for ask. I donot know that I quite wish to
say what my reason be."
"I have hear of he as connect with Hunky's house," say the
banker,--"or rather with one of the partner in the house."
"be he a man of mean?"
"I imagine he to be so;--but I know nothing. he have rather large
dealing, I take it, in foreign stock. be he after my old friend,
Miss Wharton?"
"well;--ye."
"you have well get more information than I can give you. but, of
course, before anything of that kind be do you would see that
money be settle." this be all he hear in the city, and this be
not satisfactory. he have not like to tell his friend that he wish
to hear that the foreigner be a needy adventurer,--altogether
untrustworthy; but that have really be his desire. then he think
of the L60,000 which he himself destine for his girl. if the man
be to his liking there would be money enough. though he have
be careful to save money, he be not a greedy man, even for his
child. should his daughter insist on marry this man he could
take care that she should never want a sufficient income.
as a first step,--a thing to be do almost at once,--he must take
she away from London. it be now July, and the custom of the family
be that the house in Manchester Square should be leave for two
month, and that the flitting should take place about the middle of
August. Mr. Wharton usually like to postpone the flitting, as he
also like to hasten the return. but now it be a question whether he
have not well start at once,--start somewhither, and probably for
a much long period than the usual vacation. should he take the
bull by the horn, and declare his purpose of live for the next
twelvemonth at--; well, it do not much matter where; Dresden, he
think, be a long way off, and would do as well as any place. then
it occur to he that his cousin, Sir Alured, be in town, and
that he have well see his cousin before he come to any decision.
they be, as usual, expect at Wharton Hall this autumn, and that
arrangement could not be abandon without explanation.
Sir Alured Wharton be a baronet, with a handsome old family place on
the Wye in Herefordshire, whose forefather have be baronet since
baronet be first create, and whose early forefather have live
at Wharton Hall much before that time. it may be imagine, therefore,
that Sir Alured be proud of his name, of his estate, and of his
rank. but there be drawback to his happiness. as regard his
name, it be to descend to a nephew whom he specially disliked,--and
with good cause. as to his estate, delightful as it be in many
respect, it be hardly sufficient to maintain his position with that
plentiful hospitality which he would have loved;--and other property
he have none. and as to his rank, he have almost become ashamed of it,
since,--as he be willnot to declare be now the case,--every prosperous
tallow-chandler throughout the country be make a baronet as a matter
of course. so he live at home through the year with his wife and
daughter, not pretend to the luxury of a season in London for
which his modest three or four thousand a year do not suffice;--and
so live, apart from all the friction of club, parliament, and
mixed society, he do veritably believe that his dear country be
go utterly to the dog. he be so staunch in politic, that during
the doing of the last quarter of a century,--from the repeal of the
Corn Laws down to the Ballot,--he have honestly declare one side to
be as bad as the other. thus he feel that all his happiness be to be
draw from the past. there be nothing of joy or glory to which he
could look forward either on behalf of his country or his family. his
nephew,--and alas, his heir,--wa a needy spendthrift, with whom he
would hold no communication. the family settlement for his wife and
daughter would leave they but poorly off; and though he do struggle
to save something, the duty of live as Sir Alured Wharton of
Wharton Hall should live make those struggle very ineffective.
he be a melancholy, proud, ignorant man, who could not endure a
personal liberty, and who think the assertion of social equality
on the part of man of low rank to amount to the taking of personal
liberty;--who read little or nothing, and think that he know the
history of his country because he be aware that Charles I have have
his head cut off, and that the Georges have come from Hanover. if
Charles I have never have his head cut off, and if the Georges have
never come from Hanover, the Whartons would now probably be great
people and Britain a great nation. but the Evil One have be allow
to prevail, and everything have go astray, and Sir Alured now have
nothing of this world to console he but a hazy retrospect of past
glory, and a delight in the beauty of his own river, his own park,
and his own house. Sir Alured, with all his foible and with all his
fault, be a pure-minded, simple gentleman, who could not tell a
lie, who could not do a wrong, and who be earnest in his desire to
make those who be dependent on he comfortable, and, if possible,
happy. once a year he come up to London for a week, to see his
lawyer, and get measure for a coat, and go to the dentist. these
be the excuse which he give, but it be fancy by some that his
wig be the great moving cause. Sir Alured and Mr. Wharton be
second cousin, and close friend. Sir Alured trust his cousin
altogether in all thing, believe he to be the great legal
luminary of Great Britain, and Mr. Wharton return his cousin's
affection, entertain something akin to reverence for the man who
be the head of his family. he dearly love Sir Alured,--and love
Sir Alured's wife and two daughter. nevertheless, the second week at
Wharton Hall become always tedious to he, and the fourth, fifth, and
sixth week frightful with ennui.
perhaps it be with some unconscious dread of this tedium that he
make a sudden suggestion to Sir Alured in reference to Dresden. Sir
Alured have come to he at his chamber, and the two old man be
sit together near the open window. Sir Alured delight in the
privilege of sit there, which seem to confer upon he something
of an insight into the inner way of London life beyond what he could
get at his hotel or his wigmaker's. "go to Dresden;--for the winter!"
he exclaim.
"not only for the winter. we should go at once."
"not before you come to Wharton!" say the amazed baronet.
Mr. Wharton reply in a low, sad voice, "in that case we should not

"it benot kindness at all. you always come. it would be break up
everything."
"everything have to be break up soon or later. one feel that as

"you and I, Abel, be just of an age. why should you talk to I like

Dresden! I never hear of such a thing. I suppose itbe some nonsense

then Mr. Wharton tell his whole story. "Nonsense of Emily's!" he

would be rich, know her advantage of birth, and recognize her

"do you mean that Emily--favour he?"
"I be afraid so."
"and would she--would she--do anything without your sanction?" he be
always think of the disgrace attach to himself by reason of his
nephew's vileness, and now, if a daughter of the family should also
go astray, so as to be exile from the bosom of the Whartons, how
manifest would it be that all the glory be depart from their
house!
"no! she will do nothing without my sanction. she have give she
word,--which be gospel." as he speak the old lawyer strike his hand
upon the table.
"then why should you run away to Dresden?"
"because she be unhappy. she will not marry him,--or even see he, if
I forbid it. but she be near he."
"herefordshire be a long way off," say the baronet, plead.
"change of scene be what she should have," say the father.
"there cannot be more of a change than shewould get at Wharton. she
always do like Wharton. it be there that she meet Arthur Fletcher."
the father only shake his head as Arthur Fletcher's name be
mention. "Well,--that be sad. I always think shewould give way about
Arthur at last."
"it be impossible to understand a young woman," say the lawyer. with
such an english gentleman as Arthur Fletcher on one side, and with
this portuguese Jew on the other, it be to he Hyperion to a Satyr.
a darkness have fall over his girl's eye, and for a time her power
of judgment have leave she.
"but I donot see why Wharton should not do just as well as Dresden,"
continue the baronet. Mr. Wharton find himself quite unable to
make his cousin understand that the great disruption cause by
a residence abroad, the feeling that a new kind of life have be
consider necessary for she, and that she must submit to the new
kind of life, might be gradually effective, while the journeying and
scene which have be common to her year after year would have no
effect. nevertheless he give way. they could hardly start to Germany
at once, but the visit to Wharton might be accelerate; and the
detail of the residence abroad might be there arrange. it be
fix, therefore, that Mr. Wharton and Emily should go down to
Wharton Hall at any rate before the end of July.
"why do you go early than usual, papa?" Emily ask he afterwards.
"because I think it well," he reply angrily. she ought at any rate
to understand the reason.
"of course I shall be ready, papa. you know that I always like
Wharton. there be no place on earth I like so much, and this year it
will be especially pleasant to I to go out of town. But--"
"but what?"
"I cannot bear to think that I shall be take you away."
"Ihave get to bear bad thing than that, my dear."
"oh, papa, do not speak to I like that! of course I know what
you mean. there be no real reason for your going. if you wish it
I will promise you that I will not see he." he only shake his
head,--meaning to imply that a promise which could go no far
than that would not make he happy. "it will be just the same,

"I be not afraid of you;--but I be afraid for you. I fear for your
happiness,--and for my own."
"so do I, papa. but what can be do? I suppose sometimes people must
be unhappy. I cannot change myself, and I cannot change you. I find
myself to be as much bind to Mr. Lopez as though I be his wife."
"no, no! you shouldnot say so. youhave no right to say so."
"but I have give you a promise, and I certainly will keep it. if
we must be unhappy, still we need not,--need not quarrel; need we,
papa?" then she come up to he and kiss him,--whereupon he go out
of the room wipe his eye.
that evening he again speak to she, say merely a word. "I think,
my dear, wewill have it fix that we go on the 30th. Sir Alured
seem to wish it."
"very well, papa;--I shall be quite ready."
CHAPTER XIV
A Lover's Perseverance
Ferdinand Lopez learn immediately through Mrs. Roby that the early
departure for Herefordshire have be fix. "I should go to he and
speak to he very plainly," say Mrs. Roby. "he cannot bite you."
"Ibe not in the least afraid of his bite I."
"you can talk so well! I should tell he everything, especially about
money,--which Ibe sure be all right."
"yes,--that be all right," say lopez, smile.
"and about your people."
"which Ihave no doubt you think be all wrong."
"I donot know anything about it," say Mrs. Roby, "and I donot much
care. he have old-world notion. at any rate you should say something,
so that he should not be able to complain to she that you have keep
he in the dark. if there be anything to be know, itbe much well

"but there be nothing to be know."
"then tell he nothing;--but still tell it to he. after that you
must trust to she. I donot suppose shewould go off with you."
"Ibe sure she wouldnot."
"but shebe as obstinate as a mule. shewill get the well of he
if you really mean it." he assure she that he really do mean

think it to be expedient to put his house into order, so that he
might be able to make a statement of his affair if ask to do so.
whether they be flourish or the reverse, it might be necessary

candour.
the reader may, perhaps, remember that in the month of April
Ferdinand Lopez have manage to extract a certain signature from his
unfortunate city friend, Sexty Parker, which make that gentleman
responsible for the payment of a considerable sum of money before
the end of July. the transaction have be one of an unmixed painful
nature to Mr. Parker. as soon as he come to think of it, after Lopez
have leave he, he could not prevail upon himself to forgive himself
for his folly. that he,--he, Sextus parker,--should have be induce
by a few empty word to give his name for seven hundred and fifty
pound without any consideration or possibility of benefit! and the
more he think of it the more sure he be that the money be lose.
the next day he confirm his own fear, and before a week be
go he have write down the sum as go. he tell nobody. he do
not like to confess his folly. but he make some inquiry about his
friend,--which be absolutely futile. no one that he know seem to
know anything of the man's affair. but he see his friend from time
to time in the city, shin as only successful man do shine, and he
hear of he as one whose name be become know in the city. still
he suffer grievously. his money be surely go. a man do not fly
a kite in that fashion till thing with he have reach a bad pass.
so it be with Mr. Parker all through May and to the end of
June,--the load ever grow heavy and heavy as the time become
nearer. then, while he be still afflict with a heaviness of
spirit which have never leave he since that fatal day, who but

and with a hat splendid as hat be splendid only in the city. and
nothing could be more "jolly" than his friend's manner,--so much

lopez, seat himself, almost at once begin to describe a certain
speculation into which he be go rather deeply, and as to which

evidently, not to ask, but to confer, a favour.
"I rather think that steady business be good," say Parker. "I hope
itbe all right about that l750."

turn out, for much above a fortnight, and as there be no use in

but he could not bring himself to put out his hand for it. "as to

of a steady income pal before his eye, and he too begin to tell

in a few day, however, the same matter be bring before Sexty's

go rather deep into two or three american mine before the end of

satisfaction in the intimacy which these relation create. he be
get in the thin edge of the wedge, and would calculate as he go

only set your mind that way, it be quite as easy to amass a large
fortune as to earn a small income.
about a week before the departure of the Whartons for Herefordshire,
lopez, in compliance with Mrs. Roby's counsel, call at the
chamber in Stone Buildings. it be difficult to say that you will

have well decline to see the man. but while he be doubting,--at

hesitate a moment, and then give his hand to the intruder in
that half-unwilling, unsatisfactory manner which most of we have

he reseat himself. he look as though he be at his ease and

the more he look at the man the less he like he.

"I do not think that I can do anything for you, Mr. Lopez," he say.
there be a slight pause, during which the visitor put down his hat

do I not explain myself when I see you before?"
"but, I fear, I do not explain myself. I hardly tell my story."
"you can tell it, of course,--if you think the telling will do you

"I can say now, sir, what I could not say then. we love each other.
and I be as sure of she as I be of myself when I assert that we shall

"what be your objection, Mr. Wharton?"
"I explain it before as far as I find myself call upon to

"be we both to be sacrifice for some reason that we neither of we

because I refuse to be more explicit to you, a stranger, do you

"I think it no more than honest, Mr. Wharton, to declare this one

"not much," say the lawyer, with angry impatience.
Lopez smile, but he put down the word in his memory and determine

"it be everything. but in thinking of her happiness I must look

matter with you any far." then he ring the bell and pass

march out of the chamber and go his way.
Mr. Wharton have be very firm, and yet he be shake. it be by
degree become a fix idea in his mind that the man's material

when talk to the man himself, lest he should be overwhelm by

to other. he be good-look, he live with people who be highly
regard, he could speak up for himself, and he be a favour guest

fear that the world would be against he, and he already begin to

have the fair skin and bold eye and uncertain word of an English

some inferior latin race. but he care for these things;--and it be

"I suppose I have well die and leave they to look after themselves,"
he say, as he return to his arm-chair.
lopez himself be not altogether ill-satisfied with the interview,

doubt but that he could build himself up with the old barrister's

Street, and be soon closet with Mrs. Roby. "you can get she here
before they go?" he say.
"she wouldnot come;--and if we arrange it without let she know
that you be to be here, she would tell her father. she havenot a
particle of female intrigue in she."
"so much the well," say the lover.
"thatbe all very well for you to say, but when a man make such a

"you could give she a letter."
"shewould only show it her father. she be so perverse that I sometimes
feel inclined to say that Iwill have nothing furth to do with she."
"youwill give she a message at any rate?"
"yes,--i can do that;--because I can do it in a way that willnot seem
to make it important."
"but I want my message to be very important. tell she that Ihave see

"it benot any thought of money that be trouble he."

surrender she, and that I be as sure of she as I be of myself. tell
she that;--and tell she that I think she owe it to I to say one
word to I before she go into the country."
CHAPTER XV
Arthur Fletcher
it may, I think, be a question whether the two old man act wisely
in have Arthur Fletcher at Wharton Hall when Emily arrive there.
the story of his love for Miss Wharton, as far as it have as yet go,
must be shortly tell. he have be the second son, as he be now the
second brother, of a Herefordshire squire endow with much large
property than that belong to Sir Alured. John Fletcher, Esq., of
Longbarns, some twelve mile from Wharton, be a considerable man in
Herefordshire. this present squire have marry Sir Alured's eld
daughter, and the young brother have, almost since they be
child together, be know to be in love with Emily Wharton. all
the Fletchers and everything belong to they be almost worship
at Wharton Hall. there have be marriage between the two family
certainly as far back as the time of Henry VII, and they be
accustom to speak, if not of alliance, at any rate of friendship,
much anterior to that. as regard family, therefore, the pretension
of a Fletcher would always be hold to be good by a Wharton. but this
Fletcher be the very pearl of the Fletcher tribe. though a young
brother, he have a very pleasant little fortune of his own. though
bear to comfortable circumstance, he have work so hard in his young
day as to have already make for himself a name at the bar. he be
a fair-haired, handsome fellow, with sharp, eager eye, with an
aquiline nose, and just that shape of mouth and chin which such man
as Abel Wharton regard as characteristic of good blood. he be
rather thin, about five foot ten in height, and have the character of
be one of the good horseman in the county. he be one of the most
popular man in Herefordshire, and at Longbarns be almost as much
thought of as the squire himself. he certainly be not the man to be
take, from his appearance, for a forlorn lover. he look like one
of those happy son of the god who be bear to success. no young
man of his age be more court both by man and woman. there be no
one who in his youth have suffer few trouble from those cause
of trouble which visit english young men,--occasional impecuniosity,
sternness of parent, native shyness, fear of ridicule, inability of
speech, and a general pervading sense of inferiority combine with
an ardent desire to rise to a feeling of conscious superiority. so
much have be do for he by nature that he be never call upon
to pretend to anything. throughout the county those be the lucky
men,--and those too be the happy girls,--who be allow to call
he Arthur. and yet this paragon be vainly in love with Emily
Wharton, who, in the way of love, would have nothing to say to he,
preferring,--as her father once say in his extreme wrath,--a
greasy Jew adventurer out of the gutter!
and now it have be think expedient to have he down to Wharton,
although the lawyer' regular summer vacation have not yet commence.
but there be some excuse make for this, over and above the emergency
of his own love, in the fact that his brother John, with Mrs.
Fletcher, be also to be at the Hall,--so that there be gather
there a great family party of the Whartons and Fletchers; for
there be present there also old Mrs. Fletcher, a magnificently
aristocratic and high-minded old lady, with snow-white hair, and lace
worth fifty guineas a yard, who be as anxious as everybody else that
her young son should marry Emily Wharton. something of the truth
as to Emily Wharton's L60,000 be, of course, know to the longbarn
people. not that I would have it infer that they want their
darling to sell himself for money. the Fletchers be great people,
with great spirit, too good in every way for such baseness. but when
love, old friendship, good birth, together with every other propriety
as to age, manner, and conduct, can be join to money, such a
combination will always be think pleasant.
when Arthur reach the Hall it be feel to be necessary that a word
should be say to he as to that wretched interloper, Ferdinand
Lopez. Arthur have not of late be often in Manchester Square. though
always most cordially welcome there by old Wharton, and treat
with every kindness by Emily Wharton short of that love which he
desire, he have during the last three or four month abstain from
frequent the house. during the past winter, and early in the
spring, he have press his suit,--but have be reject, with warm
assurance of all friendship short of love. it have then be arrange
between he and the eld Whartons that they should all meet down at
the Hall, and there have be sympathetic expression of hope that all
might yet be well. but at that time little or nothing have be know
of Ferdinand Lopez.
but now the old baronet speak to he, the father have depute the
loathsome task to his friend,--bee unwille himself even to hint

"I donot think that that be very much in his favour," say the

"I donot know that it be, sir;--only they try to catch all fish in

"do you go there, Arthur?"
"I should if I be ask, I suppose. I donot know who wouldnot. you
see itbe a Coalition affair, so that everybody be able to feel that
he be support his party by go to the Duchess's."
"I hate Coalitions," say the baronet. "I think they be

"well;--ye; I donot know. the coach have to be drive somehow. you

Sir Alured feel that he have not as yet begin even to approach the

"ah;--that be it."

"No;--no. I donot think that. Mr. Wharton donot love he a bit

"Ah--. thatbe where it be."

his old friend also be much move. it be dreadful to he that the

"certainly I will. he have always be one of the good friend I ever

Fletcher do speak to Emily's father, and learn more from he than

in do so I must try to sift my own feeling and my own judgment,

you have all my wish, but you will understand that I do not promise

and so by degree the blow have come full home to he. he have be
twice refuse. then rumour have reach him,--not at first that he

and now this rivalry, and its success, be declare to he plainly.
he tell himself from this moment that he have not a chance. look

sufficiently to be sure that she would not be waft about, from one
lover to another, by change of scene. take she to Dresden,--or to
New zealand,--would only confirm in her passion such a girl as Emily
Wharton. nothing could shake she but the ascertained unworthiness of
the man,--and not that unless it be ascertain beneath her own
eye. and then year must pass by before she would yield to another
lover. there be a further question, too, which he do not fail to
ask himself. be the man necessarily unworthy because his name be
lopez, and because he have not come of english blood?
as he strove to think of this, if not coolly yet rationally, he sit
himself down on the river's side and begin to pitch stone off the
path in among the rock, among which at that spot the water make
its way rapidly. there have be moment in which he have be almost
ashamed of his love,--and now he do not know whether to be most
ashamed or most proud of it. but he recognise the fact that it be
crucify he, and that it would continue to crucify he. he know
himself in London to be a popular man,--one of those for whom,
accord to general opinion, girl should sigh, rather than one who
should break his heart sigh for a girl. he have often tell himself
that it be beneath his manliness to be despondent; that he should
let such a trouble run from he like water from a duck's back,
console himself with the reflection that if the girl have such bad
taste she could hardly be worthy of he. he have almost try to
belong to that school which throw the heart away and rule by the
head alone. he know that others,--perhaps not those who know he
good, but who nevertheless be the companion of many of his
hours,--gave he the credit for such power. why should a man afflict
himself by the inward burden of an unsatisfied craving, and allow his
heart to sink into his very foot because a girl would not smile when
he woo she? "if she be not fair for I, what care I how fair she
be!" he have repeat the line to himself a score of time, and have
be ashamed of himself because he could not make they come true to
himself.
they have not come true in the least. there he be, Arthur Fletcher,
whom all the world court, with his heart in his very boot! there
be a miserable load within he, absolutely palpable to his outward
feeling,--a very physical pain,--which he could not shake off. as he
throw the stone into the water he tell himself that it must be so
with he always. though the world do pet he, though he be like at
his club, and court in the hunting-field, and love at ball and
archery meeting, and repute by old man to be a rise star, he tell
himself that he be so maim and mutilate as to be only half a man.
he could not reason about it. nature have afflict he with a certain
weakness. one man have a hump;--another can hardly see out of his
imperfect eyes;--a third can barely utter a few disjoint word.
it be his fate to be construct with some weak arrangement of the
blood-vessel which leave he in this plight. "the whole damned thing
be nothing to I," he say burst out into absolute tear, after
vainly try to reassure himself by a recollection of the good
thing which the world still have in store for he.
then he strive to console himself by think that he might take a
pride in his love even though it be so intolerable a burden to
he. be it not something to be able to love as he love? be it not
something at any rate that she to whom he have condescend to stoop
be worthy of all love? but even here he could get no comfort,--being
in truth unable to see very clearly into the condition of the thing.
it be a disgrace to him,--to he within his own bosom,--that she
should have prefer to he such a one as Ferdinand Lopez, and this
disgrace he exaggerate, ignore the fact that the girl herself
might be deficient in judgment, or lead away in her love by falsehood
and counterfeit attraction. to he she be such a goddess that she
must be right,--and therefore his own inferiority to such a one as
Ferdinand Lopez be prove. he could take no pride in his reject
love. he would rid himself of it at a moment's notice if he know the
way. he would throw himself at the foot of some second-rate, tawdry,
well-bear, well-know beauty of the day,--only that there be not now
leave to he strength to pretend the feeling that would be necessary.
then he hear step, and jump up from his seat, stand just in the
way of Emily Wharton and her cousin Mary. "benot you go to dress
for dinner, young man?" say the latter.
"I shall have time if you have, any way," say Arthur, endeavour
to pluck up his spirit.
"thatbe nice of him;--isn't it?" say Mary. "why, we be dress.
what more do you want? we come out to look for you, though we donot
mean to come as far as this. itbe past seven now, and we be suppose
to dine at a quarter past."
"five minute will do for I."
"but youhave get to get to the house. you neednot be in a tremendous
hurry, because papa have only just come in from haymake. theyhave get
up the last load, and there have be the usual ceremony. Emily and I
have be look at they."
"I wish Iwould be here all the time," say Emily. "I do so hate London
in July."
"so do I," say Arthur,--"in July and all other time."
"you hate London!" say Mary.
"Yes,--and Herefordshire,--and other place generally. if Ihave get to
dress Iwould well get across the park as quick as I can go," and so
he leave they. Mary turn round and look at her cousin, but at the
moment say nothing. Arthur's passion be well known to Mary Wharton,
but Mary have as yet hear nothing of Ferdinand Lopez.

never run away!
during the whole of that evening there be a forced attempt on the

as be the case whenever such attempt be force, be a failure.
there have be a hay-make harvest-home which be suppose to give
the special occasion for mirth, as Sir Alured farm the land around
the park himself, and be great in hay. "I donot think it pay very
well," he say with a gentle smile, "but I like to employ some of the
people myself. I think the old people find it easy with I than
with the tenant."
"I shouldnot wonder," say his cousin;--"but thatbe charity; not
employment."
"no, no," exclaim the baronet. "they work for their wage and do
their good. Powell see to that." Powell be the bailiff, who know
the length of his master's foot to a quarter of an inch, and be
quite aware that the Wharton haymaker be not to be overtaske.
"Powell donot keep any cat about the place, but what catch mouse.
but I be not quite sure that haymake do pay."
"how do the tenant manage?"
"of course they look to thing close. you wouldnot wish I to let
the land up to the house door."
"I think," say old Mrs. Fletcher, "that a landlord should consent

both Mr. Wharton and Sir Alured feel that this might be very well at
Longbarns, though it could hardly be afford at Wharton.
"I donot think I lose much by my farming," say the squire of

"Johnson be a very good man, I dare say," say the baronet.

as long as hebe look after. I think I know as much about it as

my rent, and I get it."
"I donot think I manage it quite that way," say the baronet in a

"Ibe afraid not," say the barrister.

"Ibe not hard at all," say John, "and you understand nothing about

"thatbe because of the Unions," say the barrister.
"I donot care a straw for the Unions. if the union interfere with

"I would not consent to be make a slave even for the sake of the

away, let it be give away,--so that the people should know what it
be that they receive."

idleness when you teach people to think that they be earn wage
without work for their money. whatever you do with them let them
know and feel the truth. itwill be the good in the long run."
"Ibe sometimes happy when I think that I shallnot live to see the long
run," say the baronet. this be the manner in which they try to

there be not one there in the room who do not know that it be the

the girl's folly and perverseness on this head be know to they

vitiate taste and dreadful partiality for the portuguese adventurer,

that sternly magnificent old lady, Mrs. fletcher,--whose ancestor

this story, the roof of the old hall would hardly be able to hold she

and the Vaughans,--of whom she have be one,--and the wharton

itself to quick perdition, and with peculiar duty. among these
duty, the chiefest of they incumbent on female be that of so
restrain their affection that they should never damage the good
cause by leave it. they might marry within the pale,--or remain
single, as might be their lot. she would not take upon herself to

because such a marriage be fitting,--although she do think that
there be much perverseness in the girl, who might have teach
herself, have she not be stubborn, to comply with the wish of the

have hear that her mind and taste be so astray. all this Sir Alured

more because they sympathise with the old lady's feeling.
"Emily Wharton donot seem to I to be a bit more gracious than she

in the meantime the two brother be smoke their pipe in the

John Fletcher, as I hope may have be already see, be by no
mean a weak man or an indifferent brother. he be warm-hearted,
sharp-witted, and, though perhaps a little self-opinionate,
consider throughout the county to be one of the most prudent in

"yes;--your cousin Emily. donot say anything to he, but be as good

"good to Arthur! be I not always good to he?"

"yes;--a man name lopez. donot say anything about it at present.

CHAPTER XVII
Good-Bye
Arthur Fletcher receive his brother's teaching as true, and take
his brother's advice in good part;--so that, before the morning
following, he have resolve that however deep the wound might be, he
would so live before the world, that the world should not see his
wound. what people already know they must know,--but they should
learn nothing far either by word or sign from he. he would,
as he have say to his brother, "have it out with Emily"; and then,
if she tell he plainly that she love the man, he would bid she
adieu, simply express regret that their course for life should be
divide. he be confident that she would tell he the entire truth.
she would be restrain neither by false modesty, nor by any assumed
unwillingness to discuss her own affair with a friend so true to
she as he have be. he know she well enough to be sure that she
recognise the value of his love though she could not bring herself
to accept it. there be reject lover who, merely because they be
lover, become subject to the scorn and even to the disgust of the
girl they love. but again there be man who, even when they be
reject, be almost love, who be consider to be worthy of all
reverence, almost of worship;--and yet the worshipper will not love
they. not analyse all this, but somewhat conscious of the light in
which this girl regard he, he know that what he might say would be
treat with deference. as to shake her,--as to talk she out of
one purpose and into another,--that to he do not for a moment seem
to be practicable. there be no hope of that. he hardly know why he
should endeavour to say a word to she before he leave Wharton. and yet
he feel that it must be say. be he to allow she to be married to
this man, without any further previous word between they, it would
appear that he have resolve to quarrel with she for ever. but now, at
this very moment of time, as he lie in his bed, as he dress himself
in the morning, as he saunter about among the new hay-stack with
his pipe in his mouth after breakfast, he come to some conclusion in
his mind very much averse to such quarrelling.
he have love she with all his heart. it have not be a mere
drawing-room love beget between a couple of waltz, and foster
by five minute in a crush. he know himself to be a man of the world,
and he do not wish to be other than he be. he could talk among man
as man talk, and act as man acted;--and he could do the same with
woman. but there be one person who have be to he above all, and
round everything, and under everything. there have be a private nook
within he into which there have be no entrance but for the one
image. there have be a holy of holy, which he have guard within
himself, keep it free from all outer contamination for his own
use. he have cherish the idea of a clear fountain of ever-run
water which would at last be his, always ready for the comfort of his
own lip. now all his hope be shatter, his trust be go, and his
longing disappoint. but the person be the same person, though she
could not be his. the nook be there, though she would not fill it.
the holy of holy be not less holy, though he himself might not
dare to lift the curtain. the fountain would still run,--still the
clear fountain of all,--though he might not put his lip to it. he
would never allow himself to think of it with lessened reverence, or
with change idea as to her nature.
and then, as he stand lean against a ladder which still keep
its place against one of the hay-rick, and fill his second pipe
unconsciously, he have to realise to himself the probable condition of
his future life. of course she would marry this man with very little
further delay. her father have already declare himself to be too
weak to interfere much long with her wish. of course Mr. Wharton
would give way. he have himself declare that he would give way. and
then,--what sort of life would be her life? no one know anything
about the man. there be an idea that he be rich,--but wealth such
as his, wealth that be subject to speculation, will fly away at a
moment's notice. he might be cruel, a mere adventurer, or a thorough
ruffian for all that be know of he. there should, think Arthur
Fletcher to himself, be more stability in the giving and taking
of wife than could be reckon upon here. he become old in that
half-hour, take home to himself and appreciate many saw of
wisdom and finger-direction of experience which hitherto have be
to he matter almost of ridicule. but he could only come to this
conclusion,--that as she be still to be to he his holy of holy
though he might not lay his hand upon the altar, his fountain though
he might not drink of it, the one image which alone could have fill
that nook, he would not cease to regard her happiness when she should
have become the wife of this stranger. with the stranger himself he
never could be on friendly terms;--but for the stranger's wife there
should always be a friend, if the friend be need.
about an hour before lunch, John Fletcher, who have be hang about
the house all the morning in a manner very unusual to he, catch
Emily Wharton as she be pass through the hall, and tell she that
Arthur be in a certain part of the ground and wish to speak to
she. "alone?" she ask. "yes, certainly alone." "ought I to go to
he, John?" she ask again. "certainly I think you ought." then he
have do his commission and be able to apply himself to whatever
business he have on hand.
Emily at once put on her hat, take her parasol, and leave the house.
there be something distasteful to she in the idea of this go out
at a lover's bidding, to meet he; but like all Whartons and all

ask you, I suppose."

know. you be not sorry that I should know?"
"it be well."
"I be not go to say a word against he."
"No;--do not do that."
"nor against you. I be simply here now to let you know that--I
retire."

"quarrel with you! I could not quarrel with you, if I would.
no;--there shall be no quarrel. but I do not suppose we shall see

"I hope we may."

"you be not go away?"
"to-morrow, perhaps. but I will say my real good-bye to you here,

good-bye. God bless you!"

"oh, I have love you so dearly. it have be with my whole heart. you

she have expect and have almost wish that he should kiss she. a

he do love she. there would have be something of reconciliation,

private.

CHAPTER XIX
Vulgarity
the Duke and Duchess with their child and personal servant
reach Gatherum Castle the day before the first crowd of visitor
be expect. it be on a lovely autumn afternoon, and the Duke,

nevertheless, as soon as she know that he be out of the way, she

as to bedchamber, see to the adornment of the reception-room, have

next two month."
"forty to sleep, my lady?" to Pritchard the Duchess have for many
year be Lady Glencora, and she perhaps understand that her
mistress like the old appellation.
"yes, forty to sleep, and forty to eat, and forty to drink. but
thatbe nothing. forty to push through twenty-four hour every day! do
you think youhave get everything that you want?"
"it depend, my lady, how long each of them stay."
"one night! no,--say two night on an average."
"that make shift the bed very often;--doesn't it, my lady?"
"send up to Puddick's for sheet to-morrow. why benot that think
of before?"
"it be, my lady,--and I think we shall do. wehave get the
steam-washery put up."
"towel!" suggest the Duchess.
"oh yes, my lady. Puddick's do send a great many things;--a whole

"why donot you tell I, so that I could have have they make? itbe

"well, my lady;--we couldnot do with more; and they benot an atom of
use,--only just in the way,--if you donot know something about them.
I suppose Mr. Millepois will be down soon." this name, which Mrs.
Pritchard call Milleypoise, indicate a french cook who be as yet
unknown at the Castle.
"hewill be here to-night."
"I wish he could have be here a day or two soon, my lady, so as
just to see about he."
"and how should we have get our dinner in town? he willnot make any
difficulty. the confectioner do come?"
"yes, my lady; and to tell the truth out at once, he be that drunk
last night that--; oh, dear, we donot know what to do with he."
"I donot mind that before the affair begin. I donot suppose hewill
get tipsy while he have to work for all these people. youhave plenty of
egg?"
these question go on so rapidly that in addition to the asking

note either perfection or imperfection. in the meantime the Duke

so that grand guest could drive almost into the house. the Duke,

use the enormous hall just behind he, cut the house in two,
declare aloud by its own aspect and proportion that it have be

now as he stand there he could already see that man be at work
about the place, that ground have be move here, and grass lay down
there, and a new gravel road construct in another place. be it not
possible that his friend should be entertain without all these
change in the garden? then he perceive the tent, and descend

he come upon a new conservatory. the exotic with which it be to

from one place he go on to another and find change, and new

anger he to think that there be so little of simplicity leave in

that all this should not have be do without a word of consent

be a matter of taste or of mere personal pleasure, he would
probably have give his assent at once, think nothing of the

that be as it should be, because the flag be wave in compliance
with an acknowledge ordinance. of all that properly belong to

other to their enjoyment would be endanger, and the benefit
accrue to his country from established mark of reverence would
be imperille. but here be an assumed and preposterous grandeur

it be clear that he must henceforth take thing more into his

suppose the paper be to say of he that he build a new
conservatory and make an archery ground for the sake of maintain
the Coalition!
when he get back to the house he find his wife alone in the small

"it benot such a very long journey after all."

"be that necessary, Glencora?"
"if I have go to bed instead, the world, I suppose, would have

"be you go to begin?"
"not to scold, my dear. look back, can you remember that I ever

"vulgarity!" she exclaim, jump up from her sofa.
"I retract the word. I would not for the world say anything that

he leave she.
Vulgarity! there be no other word in the language so hard to bear as
that. he have, indeed, be careful to say that he do not accuse she
of vulgarity,--but nevertheless the accusation have be make. could
you call your friend a liar more plainly than by say to he that
you would not say that he lie? they din together, the two boy,

horrid word be cling to the lady's ear, and the remembrance of

be make, a word utter cannot be retract. as he look across

she escape, to the writing of her letter she say, almost before

most surely get what she want by vulgarity? what be the meaning
of the word vulgarity? of course she be prepared to do things,--wa
daily do things,--which would have be odious to she have not she
husband be a public man. she submit, without unwillingness,

smiles,--so she now say to herself,--on butcher and tinker. what
she say, what she read, what she write, what she do, whither she
go, to whom she be kind and to whom unkind,--wa it not all say

when a man want to be Prime Minister he have to submit to vulgarity,
and must give up his ambition if the task be too disagreeable to he.
the Duchess think that that have be understand, at any rate ever

he want it to be do. and when I do it for he because he cannot do
it for himself, he call it by an ugly name!" then it occur to

more lie than truth,--but that the world have wisely agree that the
world shall not be accuse of lie. one donot venture to express
open disbelief even of one's wife; and with the world at large
a word speak, whether lie or not, be presume to be true of
course,--because speak. Jones have say it, and therefore Smith,--who
have know the lie to be a lie,--has assert his assured belief,
lie again. but in this way the world be able to live pleasantly.
how be she to live pleasantly if her husband accuse she of
vulgarity? of course it be all vulgar, but why should he tell she
so? she do not do it from any pleasure that she get from it.
the letter remain long unwritten, and then there come a moment in

aristocratic to the backbone,--a very Duchess of duchess? the role
would be one very easy to play. it require rank, money, and a little

without the slight trouble to himself, and have be treat almost
like a god because he have seclude himself. she could make the change
even yet,--and as her husband tell she that she be vulgar, she
think she would make it.
but at last, before she have abandon her desk and paper, there have

evidently repent of as soon as utter? Vulgar! well;--let she be

everlasting punishment denounce against vulgarity. and then a high

analyse, but which be hardly on that account the less effective. she
do believe thoroughly in her husband, to the extent of think he
the fit man in all the country to be its Prime Minister. his fame
be dear to she. her nature be loyal; and though she might, perhaps,
in her young day have be able to lean upon he with a more

to pleasure, and fond of trifle, still, she could recognise merit
with which her sympathy be imperfect. it be good that he should be
England's Prime Minister, and therefore she would do all she could to
keep he in that place. the vulgarity be a necessary essential. he
might not acknowledge this,--might even, if the choice be leave to
he, refuse to be Prime Minister on such term. but she need not,
therefore, give way. have in this way think it all out, she take
up her pen and complete the batch of letter before she allow
herself to go to bed.
CHAPTER XX
Sir Orlando's policy
when the guest begin to arrive our friend the Duchess have apparently
get through her little difficulty, for she receive they with that
open, genial hospitality which be so delightful as come evidently
from the heart. there have not be another word between she and her
husband as to the manner in which the thing be to be do, and she
have determine that the offensive word should pass altogether out of
her memory. the first comer be Mrs. Finn,--who come indeed rather
as an assistant hostess than as a mere guest, and to she the Duchess
utter a few half-playful hint as to her trouble. "consider the
time, havenot we do marvel? because it do look nice,--doesn't
it? there be no dirt heap about, and itbe all as green as though
it have be there since the Conquest. he donot like it because it
look new. and wehave get forty-five bedroom make up. the servant
be all turn out over the stable somewhere,--quite comfortable,
I assure you. indeed they like it. and by knock down the end of
two passage wehave bring everything together. and the room be all
number just like an inn. it be the only way. and I keep one book

be, and how long the tenant be to be allow to occupy it. and herebe

should flirt with whom."
"youwould well not let that lie about."
"nobody could understand a word of it if they have it. A. B. always

I never draw a bow in my life,--not a real bow in the flesh, that be,

way to make a thing go down be to give it some special importance.
and Ihave go through the bill of fare for the first week with

"ah; well, upon the whole he behave like an angel. he behave so
well that half my time I think Iwill shut it all up and have do with

"he have not be displease?"
"ask no question, my dear, and youwill hear no story. you havenot
be marry twice without know that woman cannot have everything
smooth. he only say one word. it be rather hard to bear, but it have
pass away."
that afternoon there be quite a crowd. among the first comer be

Orlando and Lady Drought, Lord Ramsden, and Sir Timothy Beeswax.
these gentleman with their wife represent, for the time, the

that their fealty and submission might be thus rivet. there be

Duke wish to open his hand. and there be our friend Ferdinand

securing for himself so great a social advantage as an invitation to

refuse to receive as his son-in-law a man who have be a guest at the
Duke of Omnium's country house? and then there be certain people
from the neighbourhood;--Frank Gresham of Greshamsbury, with his wife
and daughter, the master of the hound in those part, a rich squire
of old blood, and head of the family to which one of the aspirant

ours,--had be ask to meet he, and have bring his wife. and
there be Lady Rosina De Courcy, an old maid, the sister of the
present Earl De Courcy, who live not far off and have be accustom
to come to Gatherum Castle on state occasion for the last thirty
years,--the only relic in those part of a family which have live
there for many year in great pride of place; for her eld brother,

a little cottage outside the old park paling, and still hold fast
within her bosom all the old pride of the De Courcys. and then
there be Captain Gunner and Major Pountney, two middle-aged young
man, presumably belong to the army, whom the Duchess have lately
enlist among her follower as be useful in their way. they could
eat their dinner without be shy, dance on occasion, though very
unwillingly, talk a little, and run on messages;--and they know the
peerage by heart, and could tell the detail of every unfortunate
marriage for the last twenty year. each think himself, especially
since this last promotion, to be indispensably necessary to the
formation of London society, and be comfortable in a conviction that
he have thoroughly succeed in life by acquire the privilege of
sit down to dinner three time a week with peer and peeress.
the list of guest have by no mean be make as complete here as it
be to be find in the county newspaper, and in the "Morning Post"
of the time; but enough of name have be give to show of what
nature be the party. "the Duchess have get rather a rough lot to

"oh, yes. I know that. she want I to be useful, so of course I

up to this moment Captain Gunner have not receive any invitation for

"Ihave be get up that archery code with she," say Pountney,

"rather flabby," say Captain Gunner.

"well;--now youhave try it, what do you think about it?" this

recognise law of the Constitution that the Leader of the House of

"wehave get along, you know," say Sir Orlando.
"yes;--ye. wehave get along. can you imagine any possible
concatenation of circumstance in which we should not get along?
therebe always too much good sense in the House for an absolute
collapse. but be you content?"
"I willnot say Ibe not," say the cautious baronet. "I donot look for

"it seem to I that the one achievement to which wehave all look

"it be a great thing to do, Sir Timothy. of course the main work of

now as that a Stuart King should have do so two hundred and fifty
year ago. to get a session over and do with be an achievement and
a delight."
"no Ministry can go on long on that far niente principle, and no
minister who accede to it will remain long in any ministry."
Sir Timothy in say this might be allude to the Duke, or the
reference might be to Sir Orlando himself. "of course, Ibe not in
the Cabinet, and be not entitle to say a word; but I think that if
I be in the Cabinet, and if I be anxious,--which I confess Ibe
not,--for a continuation of the present state of thing, I should
endeavour to obtain from the Duke some idea of his policy for the
next session." Sir Orlando be a man of certain part. he could speak
volubly,--and yet slowly,--so that reporter and other could hear
he. he be patient, both in the House and in his office, and have the
great gift of do what he be tell by man who understand thing
well than he do himself. he never go very far astray in his

and that perhaps it behove he to lead the Duke. at the commencement

perhaps something of that feeling in reference to the Duke personally

but still it might be his duty to say a word to the Duke. Sir Orlando
assure himself that if ever convinced of the propriety of do so,
he could say a word even to the Duke of Omnium. "I be confident that

"where do they pick he up?" say the Major to the Captain, point
with his head to Ferdinand Lopez, who be shoot with Angelica

"the Duchess find he somewhere. hebe one of those fabulously rich
fellow out of the City who make a hundred thousand pound at a blow.
they say his people be grandee of Spain."
"do anybody know he?" ask the Major.
"everybody soon will know he," answer the Captain. "I think I
hear that hebe go to stand for some place in the Duke's interest.
he donot look the sort of fellow I like; but hebe get money and
he come here, and hebe good looking,--and therefore hewill be a
success." in answer to this the Major only grunt. the Major be a
year or two old than the Captain, and therefore less willing even
than his friend to admit the claim of new comer to social honour.
just at this moment the Duchess walk across the ground up to the

be see in the garden before that day, and of course a little
