"now I do hope," say the Duchess, "that you be all shoot by the
new code. that is, and be to be, the Gatherum Archery Code, and I
shall break my heart if anybody rebel."
"there be one or two man," say Major Pountney very gravely, "who
willnot take the trouble to understand it."
"Mr. Lopez," say the Duchess, point with her finger at our
friend, "be you that rebel?"
"I fear I do suggest--" begin Mr. Lopez.
"I will have no suggestions,--nothing but obedience. here be Sir

be want."
"of course not," say Major Pountney.
"now, Mr. Lopez, will you or will you not be guide by a strict and

"I willnot resign, and I will obey," say lopez.
"a good ministerial reply," say the Duchess. "I donot doubt but
that in time youwill ascend to high office and become a pillar of the
Gatherum constitution. how do he shoot, Miss Thrift?"
"he will shoot very well indeed, Duchess, if he go on and
practise," say Angelica, whose life for the last seven year have
be devote to archery. Major Pountney retire far away into the
park, a full quarter of a mile off, and smoke a cigar under a tree.
be it for this that he have absolutely give up a month to draw
out this code of rule, go backwards and forwards two or three
time to the printer in his desire to carry out the Duchess's

so d---- lucky!" this fellow, Lopez, have absolutely be allow to
make a good score off his own intractable disobedience.
the Duchess's little joke about the Ministers generally, and the
advantage of submission on their part to their chief, be think by
some who hear it not to have be make in good taste. the joke be
just such a joke as the Duchess would be sure to make,--meane very
little but still not altogether pointless. it be level rather at
her husband than at her husband's colleague who be present, and
be so understand by those who really know her,--as do Mrs. Finn,
and Mr. Warburton, the private secretary. but Sir Orlando and Sir
Timothy and Mr. Rattler, who be all within hearing, think that
the Duchess have intend to allude to the servile nature of their
position; and Mr. Boffin, who hear it, rejoice within himself,
comfort himself with the reflection that his wither be unwrung,
and think with what pleasure he might carry the anecdote into the
farth corner of the club. poor Duchess! 'tis pitiful to think
that after such Herculean labour she should injure the cause by one
slight unconsidered word, more, perhaps, than she have advance it by
all her energy.
during this time the Duke be at the Castle, but he show himself
seldom to his guests,--so acting, as the reader will I hope
understand, from no sense of the importance of his own personal
presence, but influence by a conviction that a public man should not
waste his time. he breakfast in his own room, because he could thus
eat his breakfast in ten minute. he read all the paper in solitude,
because he be thus enable to give his mind to their content. life
have always be too serious to he to be waste. every afternoon
he walk for the sake of exercise, and would have accept any
companion if any companion have especially offer himself. but he
go off by some side-door, find the side-door to be convenient,
and therefore when see by other be suppose to desire to remain
unseen. "I have no idea there be so much pride about the Duke," Mr.
Boffin say to his old colleague, Sir Orlando. "be it pride?" ask
Sir Orlando. "it may be shyness," say the wise Boffin. "the two
thing be so alike you can never tell the difference. but the man
who be curse by either should hardly be a Prime Minister."
it be on the day after this that Sir Orlando think that the moment
have come in which it be his duty to say that salutary word to the
Duke which it be clearly necessary that some colleague should say,
and which no colleague could have so good a right to say as he who
be the Leader of the House of Commons. he understand clearly that
though they be gather together then at Gatherum Castle for
festive purpose, yet that no time be unfit for the discussion of
State matter. do not all the world know that when in autumn the
Bismarcks of the world, or they who be big than bismarck, meet
at this or that delicious haunt of salubrity, the affair of the
world be then settle in little conclave, with great ease,
rapidity, and certainty than in large parliament or the dull
chamber of public office? Emperor meet Emperor, and King meet
King, and as they wander among rural glade in fraternal intimacy,
war be arrange, and swell territory be enjoy in
anticipation. Sir Orlando hitherto have know all this, but have hardly
as yet enjoy it. he have be long in office, but these sweet
confidence can of their very nature belong only to a very few. but
now the time have manifestly come.
it be Sunday afternoon, and Sir Orlando catch the Duke in the very
act of leave the house for his walk. there be no archery, and many
of the inmate of the Castle be asleep. there have be a question
as to the propriety of Sabbath archery, in discuss which reference
have be make to Laud's book of sport, and the grow idea that the
National Gallery should be open on the lord'-day. but the Duchess
would not have the archery. "we be just the people who shouldnot
prejudge the question," say the Duchess. the Duchess with various
lady, with the Pountneys and Gunners, and other obedient male
follower, have be to church. none of the Ministers have of course
be able to leave the swollen pouch which be always send out
from London on Saturday night, probably,--we cannot but think,--as
arrange excuse for such defalcation, and have pass their morning
comfortably doze over new novel. the Duke, always right in his
purpose but generally wrong in his practice, have stay at home
work all the morning, thereby scandalise the strict, and have
go to church alone in the afternoon, thereby offend the social.
the church be close to the house, and he have go back to change his
coat and hat, and to get his stick. but as he be steal out of the
little side-gate, Sir Orlando be down upon he. "if your grace be
go for a walk, and will admit of company, I shall be delighted to
attend you," say Sir Orlando. the Duke profess himself to be well
pleased, and in truth be pleased. he would be glad to increase his
personal intimacy with his colleague if it might be do pleasantly.
they have go nearly a mile across the park, watch the stately
movement of the herd of deer, and talk of this and that trifle,
before Sir Orlando could bring about an opportunity for utter his
word. at last he do it somewhat abruptly. "I think upon the whole we
do pretty well last session," he say, stand still under an old
oak-tree.
"pretty well," re-echo the Duke.
"and I suppose we have not much to be afraid of next session?"
"I be afraid of nothing," say the Duke.
"But--;" then Sir Orlando hesitate. the Duke, however, say not a
word to help he on. Sir Orlando think that the Duke look more
ducal than he have ever see he look before. Sir Orlando remember
the old Duke, and suddenly find that the uncle and nephew be very
like each other. but it do not become the Leader of the House of
Commons to be afraid of any one. "donot you think," continue Sir
Orlando, "we should try and arrange among ourselves something of a
policy? I be not quite sure that a ministry without a distinct course
of action before it can long enjoy the confidence of the country.
take the last half century. there have be various policy,
command more or less of general assent; free trade--." here Sir
Orlando give a kindly wave of his hand, show that on behalf of his
companion he be willing to place at the head of the list a policy
which have not always command his own assent;--"continue reform
in Parliament, to which I have, with my whole heart, give my poor
assistance." the Duke remember how the bather' clothe be
steal, and that Sir Orlando have be one of the most nimble-finger
of the thief. "no popery, irish grievance, the ballot,
retrenchment, efficiency of the public service, all have have their
time."
"thing to be do offer themselves, I suppose, because they be in
themselves desirable; not because it be desirable to have something
to do."
"just so;--no doubt. but still, if you will think of it, no ministry
can endure without a policy. during the latter part of the last
session it be understand that we have to get ourselves in harness
together, and nothing more be expect from we; but I think we
should be prepare with a distinct policy for the come year. I fear
that nothing can be do in Ireland."
"Mr. Finn have ideas--."
"ah, yes;--well, your grace. Mr. Finn be a very clever young man
certainly; but I donot think we can support ourselves by his plan of
irish reform." Sir Orlando have be a little carry away by his own
eloquence and the Duke's tameness, and have interrupt the Duke. the
Duke again look ducal, but on this occasion Sir Orlando do not
observe his countenance. "for myself, I think, I be in favour of
increase armament. I have be apply my mind to the subject,
and I think I see that the people of this country do not object to a
slightly rise scale of estimate in that direction. of course there
be the county suffrage--"
"I will think of what you have be say," say the Duke.
"as to the county suffrage--"
"I will think it over," say the Duke. "you see that oak. that be the
large tree we have here at Gatherum; and I doubt whether there be a
large one in this part of England." the Duke's voice and word be
not uncourteous, but there be something in they which hinder Sir
Orlando from refer again on that occasion to county suffrage or
increase armament.
CHAPTER XXI
the Duchess's New Swan
when the party have be about a week collect at Gatherum Castle,
Ferdinand Lopez have manifestly become the favourite of the Duchess
for the time, and have, at her instance, promise to remain there for
some further day. he have hardly speak to the Duke since he have be
in the house,--but then but few of that motley assembly do talk much
with the Duke. Gunner and Pountney have go away,--the Captain have
declare his dislike of the upstart Portuguese to be so strong that
he could not stay in the same house with he any long, and the
Major, who be of strong mind, have resolve that he would put
the intruder down. "it be horrible to think what power money have in
these day," say the Captain. the Captain have shake the dust of
Gatherum altogether from his foot, but the Major have so arrange that
a bed be to be find for he again in October,--for another happy

forget;--now will you, Duchess?" he say, implore she to remember
he as he take his leave. "I do take a deal of trouble about the
code;--didn't I?" "they donot seem to I to care for the code," say
the Duchess, "but, nevertheless, Iwill remember."
"who, in the name of all thatbe wonderful, be that I see you with in
the garden?" the Duchess say to her husband one afternoon.
"it be Lady Rosina De Courcy, I suppose."
"Heaven and earth!--what a companion for you to choose."
"why not?--why shouldnot I talk to Lady Rosina De Courcy?"
"Ibe not jealous a bit, if you mean that. I donot think Lady Rosina
will steal your heart from I. but why you should pick she out of all
the people here, when there be so many would think their fortune
make if you would only take a turn with they, I cannot imagine."
"but I donot want to make any one's fortune," say the Duke; "and
certainly not in that way."
"what could you be say to she?"
"she be talk about her family. I rather like Lady Rosina. she
be live all alone, it seem, and almost in poverty. perhaps there
be nothing so sad in the world as the female scion of a noble but
impoverished stock."
"nothing so dull, certainly."
"People be not dull to I, if they be real. I pity that poor lady.
she be proud of her blood and yet not ashamed of her poverty."
"whatever might come of her blood, she have be all her life willing
enough to get rid of her poverty. it benot above three year since
she be try she good to marry that brewer at Silverbridge. I wish
you could give your time a little to some of the other people."
"to go and shoot arrow?"
"No;--I donot want you to shoot arrow. you might act the part of
host without shoot. cannot you walk about with anybody except Lady
Rosina De Courcy?"
"I be walk about with Sir Orlando Drought last Sunday, and I very
much prefer Lady Rosina."
"there have be no quarrel?" ask the Duchess sharply.
"oh dear, no."
"of course hebe an empty-head idiot. everybody have always know
that. and hebe put above his place in the House. but it wouldnot do
to quarrel with he now."
"I donot think I be a quarrelsome man, Cora. I donot remember at this
moment that I have ever quarrel with anybody to your knowledge.
but I may perhaps be permit to--"
"Snub a man, you mean. well, I wouldnot even snub Sir Orlando very
much, if I be you; though I can understand that it might be both
pleasant and easy."
"I wish you wouldnot put slang phrase into my mouth, Cora. if I
think that a man intrude upon I, I be of course bind to let he
know my opinion."
"Sir Orlando have--intrude!"
"by no mean. he be in a position which justify his say many
thing to I which another might not say. but then, again, he be a
man whose opinion do not go far with I, and I have not the knack
of seem to agree with a man while I let his word pass idly by
I."
"that be quite true, Plantagenet."
"and, therefore, I be uncomfortable with Sir Orlando, while I be
able to sympathise with Lady Rosina."
"what do you think of Ferdinand Lopez?" ask the Duchess, with
study abruptness.
"think of Mr. Lopez! I havenot think of he at all. why should I
think of he?"
"I want you to think of he. I think hebe a very pleasant fellow, and
Ibe sure hebe a rise man."
"you might think the latter, and perhaps feel sure of the former."
"very well. then, to oblige you, Iwill think the latter and feel sure
of the former. I suppose itbe true that Mr. Grey be go on this
mission to Persia?" Mr. Grey be the Duke's intimate friend, and be
at this time member for the neighbouring borough of Silverbridge.
"I think he will go. Ihave no doubt about it. he be to go after
Christmas."
"and will give up his seat?"
the Duke do not answer she immediately. it have only just be
decided,--decide by his friend himself,--that the seat should
be give up when the journey to Persia be undertake. Mr. Grey,
somewhat in opposition to the Duke's advice, have resolve that he
could not be in Persia and do his duty in the House of Commons at the
same time. but this resolution have only now be make known to the
Duke, and he be rather puzzled to think how the Duchess have be
able to be so quick upon he. he have, indeed, keep the matter back
from the duchess, feel that she would have something to say about
it, which might possibly be unpleasant, as soon as the tiding should
reach she. "yes," he say, "I think he will give up his seat. that be
his purpose, though I think it be unnecessary."
"let Mr. Lopez have it."
"Mr. Lopez!"
"Yes;--he be a clever man, a rise man, a man that be sure to do
well, and who will be of use to you. just take the trouble to talk to
he. it be assistance of that kind that you want. you minister go on
shuffle the old card till they be so worn out and dirty that one
can hardly tell the pip on they."
"I be one of the dirty old card myself," say the Duke.
"thatbe nonsense, you know. a man who be at the head of affair as
you be cannot be include among the pack I be speak of. what you
want be new blood, or new wood, or new metal, or whatever you may
choose to call it. take my advice and try this man. he benot a
pauper. it benot money that he want."
"Cora, your goose be all swan."
"thatbe not fair. I have never bring to you a goose yet. my swan
have be swan. who be it bring you and your pet swan of all, Mr.
Grey, together? I willnot name any name, but it be your swan have
be geese."
"it be not for I to return a member for Silverbridge." when he say
this, she give he a look which almost upset even his gravity, a look
which be almost the same as ask he whether he would not--"tell
that to the marine." "you donot quite understand these thing,
Cora," he continue. "the influence which owner of property may have
in borough be decrease every day, and there arise the question
whether a conscientious man will any long use such influence."
"I donot think youwould like to see a man from Silverbridge oppose you
in the House."
"I may have to bear bad even than that."
"Well;--there it be. the man be here and you have the opportunity of
know he. of course I have not hint at the matter to he. if
there be any Palliser want the borough I wouldnot say a word.
what more patriotic thing can a patron do with his borough than to
select a man who be unknown to he, not relate to he, a perfect
stranger, merely for his worth?"
"but I do not know what may be the worth of Mr. Lopez."
"I will guarantee that," say the Duchess. whereupon the Duke
laugh, and then leave she.
the Duchess have speak with absolute truth when she tell her husband
that she have not say a word to Mr. Lopez about Silverbridge, but it
be not long before she do say a word. on that same day she find
herself alone with he in the garden,--or so much alone as to be
able to speak with he privately. he have certainly make the good
use of his time since he have be at the Castle, have secure the
good-will of many of the lady, and the displeasure of most of the
man. "you have never be in Parliament, I think," say the Duchess.
"I have never even try to get there."
"perhaps you dislike the idea of that kind of life."
"no, indeed," he say. "so far from it, that I regard it as the
high kind of life there be in England. a seat in Parliament give
a man a status in this country which it have never do elsewhere."
"then why donot you try it?"
"because Ihave get into another groove. Ihave become essentially a city
man,--one of those who take up the trade of make money generally."
"and do that content you?"
"no, duchess;--certainly not. instead of content I it disgust
I. not but that I like the money,--only it be so insufficient a use
of one's life. I suppose I shall try to get into Parliament some day.
seat in Parliament donot grow like blackberry on bush."
"pretty nearly," say the Duchess.
"not in my part of the country. these good thing seem to be
appoint to fall in the way of some man, and not of other. if there
be a general election go on to-morrow, I should not know how to
look for a seat."
"they be to be find sometimes even without a general election,"
say the Duchess.
"be you allude to anything now?"
"well;--ye, I be. but Ibe very discreet, and do not like to do more
than allude. I fancy that Mr. Grey, the member for Silverbridge,
be go to Persia. Mr. Grey be a Member of Parliament. member of
Parliament ought to be in London and not in Persia. it be generally
suppose that no man in England be more prone to do what he ought to
do than Mr. Grey. therefore, Mr. Grey will cease to be Member for
Silverbridge. thatbe logic; benot it?"
"have your grace any logic equally strong to prove that I can follow
he in the borough?"
"no;--or if I have, the logic that I should use in that matter must
for the present be keep to myself." she certainly have a little
syllogism in her head as to the Duke rule the borough, the Duke's
wife rule the Duke, and therefore the Duke's wife rule the
borough; but she do not think it prudent to utter this on the
present occasion. "I think it much well that man in Parliament
should be unmarried," say the Duchess.
"but I be go to be marry," say he.
"go to be marry, be you?"
"I have no right to say so, because the lady's father have reject
I." then he tell she the whole story, and so tell it as to secure
her entire sympathy. in tell it he never say that he be a rich
man, he never boast that that search after wealth of which he have

be no objection to he at all on the score of money. "you may have
hear of the family," he say.
"I have hear of the Whartons of course, and know that there be a
baronet,--but I know nothing more of they. he be not a man of large
property, I think."
"my Miss wharton,--the one I would fain call mine,--is the daughter
of a London barrister. he, I believe, be rich."
"then she will be an heiress."
"I suppose so;--but that consideration have have no weight with I. I
have always regard myself as the architect of my own fortune, and
have no wish to owe my material comfort to a wife."
"sheer love!" suggest the Duchess.
"yes, I think so. itbe very ridiculous; be it not?"
"and why do the rich barrister object?"
"the rich barrister, Duchess, be an out and out old Tory, who think

"a man do not hamper his daughter in these day by politic, when

"there be other cognate reason. he do not like a foreigner. now I

name so grandly Saxon as Wharton should be change to one so meanly

"the lady do not object to the Latinity?"
"I fancy not."
"or to the bearer of it?"
"Ah;--there I must not boast. but in simple truth there be only the
father's ill-will between we."
"with plenty of money on both side?" ask the Duchess. lopez
shrug his shoulder. a shrug at such a time may mean anything,
but the Duchess take this shrug as signify that the question be
so surely settle as to admit of no difficulty. "then," say the
Duchess, "the old gentleman may as well give way at once. of course
his daughter will be too many for he." in this way the Duchess of
Omnium become the fast friend of Ferdinand Lopez.
CHAPTER XXII
St. James's Park
towards the end of September Everett Wharton and Ferdinand Lopez be
in town together, and as no one else be in town,--so at least they
both profess to say,--they see a good deal of each other. lopez,
as we know, have spend a portion of the precede month at Gatherum
Castle, and have make good use of his time, but Everett Wharton have
be less fortunate. he have be a little cross with his father, and
perhaps a little cross with all the Whartons generally, who do not,
he think, make quite enough of he. in the event of "anything
happen" to that ne'er-do-well nephew, he himself would be the
heir; and he reflect not unfrequently that something very probably
might happen to the nephew. he do not often see this particular
cousin, but he always hear of he as be drunk, overwhelm with
debt and difficulty, and altogether in that position of life in
which it be probable that something will "happen." there be always
of course the danger that the young man might marry and have a
child;--but in the meantime surely he, Everett Wharton, should have
be as much thought of on the bank of the Wye as Arthur Fletcher.
he have be ask down to Wharton hall,--but he have be ask in a
way which he have not think to be flattering and have decline to go.
then there have be a plan for join Arthur Fletcher in a certain
shooting, but that have fail in consequence of a few word between
himself and Arthur respect Lopez. Arthur have want he to say
that Lopez be an unpardonable intruder,--but he have take the part
of Lopez, and therefore, when the time come round, he have nothing
to do with the shooting. he have stay in town till the middle of
August, and have then start by himself across the continent with
some keen intention of study german politic; but he have find
perhaps that german politic do not manifest themselves in the
autumn, or that a foreign country cannot be well study in
solitude,--and he have return.
late in the summer, just before his father and sister have leave town,
he have have some word with the old barrister. there have be a few
bill to be pay, and Everett's allowance have be insufficient. it
often be insufficient, and then ready money for his german tour
be absolutely necessary. Mr. Wharton might probably have say less
about the money have not his son accompany his petition by a further
allusion to Parliament. "there be some fellow at last really
get themselves together at the Progress, and of course it will
be necessary to know who will be ready to come forward at the next
general election."
"I think I know one who willnot," say the father, "judge from the
manner in which he seem at present to manage his own money affair."
there be more severity in this than the old man have intend, for
he have often think within his own bosom whether it would not be
well that he should encourage his son to stand for some seat. and
the money that he have now be ask to advance have not be very
much,--not more, in truth, than he expect to be call upon to pay
in addition to the modest sum which he profess to allow his son. he
be a rich man, who be not in truth make unhappy by part with his
money. but there have be, he think, an impudence in the conjoint
attack which it be his duty to punish. therefore he have give his
son very little encouragement.
"of course, sir, if you tell I that you be not inclined to pay
anything beyond the allowance you make I, there be an end of it."
"I rather think that you have just ask I to pay a considerable sum
beyond your allowance, and that I have consent." Everett argue the
matter no far, but he permit his mind to entertain an idea
that he be ill-use by his father. the time would come when he
would probably be heir not only to his father's money, but also to
the Wharton title and the Wharton property,--when his position in
the country would really be, as he frequently tell himself, quite
considerable. be it possible that he should refrain from blame
his father for not allow he to obtain, early in life, that
parliamentary education which would fit he to be an ornament to the
House of Commons, and a safeguard to his country in future year?
now he and Lopez be at the Progress together, and they be almost
the only man in the club. Lopez be quite content with his own
present sojourn in London. he have not only be at Gatherum Castle
but be go there again. and then he have brilliant hope before
him,--so brilliant that they begin, he think, to assume the shape
of certainty. he have correspond with the Duchess, and he have
gather from her somewhat dubious word that the Duke would probably
accede to her wish in the matter of Silverbridge. the vacancy have
not yet be declare. Mr. Grey be deter, no doubt by certain
high State purpose, from apply for the stewardship of the
Chiltern Hundreds, and thereby release himself from his seat in
Parliament, and enable himself to perform, with a clear conscience,
duty in a distant part of the world which he do not feel to be
compatible with that seat. the seeker after seat be, no doubt,
already on the track; but the Duchess have think that as far as the
Duke's good word go, it might possibly be give in favour of Mr.
Lopez. the happy aspirant have take this to be almost as good as a
promise. there be also certain pecuniary speculation on foot,
which could not be keep quite quiet even in September, as to which he
do not like to trust entirely to the unaided energy of Mr. Sextus
Parker, or to the boasted alliance of Mr. Mills Happerton. Sextus
Parker's whole heart and soul be now in the matter, but Mr. Mills
Happerton, an undoubted partner in Hunky and Sons, have blow a little
coldly on the affair. but in spite of this Ferdinand Lopez be happy.
be it probable that Mr. Wharton should continue his opposition to
a marriage which would make his daughter the wife of a member of
Parliament and of a special friend of the Duchess of Omnium?
he have say a word about his own prospect in reference to the
marriage, but Everett have be at first too full of his own affair
to attend much to a matter which be comparatively so trifling. "upon
my word," he say, "I be begin to feel angry with the governor,
which be a kind of thing I donot like at all."
"I can understand that when hebe angry with you, you shouldnot like
it."
"I donot mind that half so much. hewill come round. however unjust he
may be now, at the moment, hebe the last man in the world to do an
injustice in his will. I have thorough confidence in he. but I find
myself drive into hostility to he by a conviction that he willnot let
I take any real step in life, till my life have be half fritter
away."
"yoube think of Parliament."
"of course I be. I donot say you benot an Englishman, but you be not
quite enough of an Englishman to understand what Parliament be to
we."
"I hope to be,--some of these day," say lopez.
"perhaps you may. I willnot say but what you may get yourself educate
to it when youhave be marry a dozen year to an english wife, and
have half-a-dozen english child of your own. but, in the meantime,
look at my position. I be twenty-eight year old."
"I be four year your senior."
"it do not matter a straw to you," continue Everett. "but a few
year be everything with I. I have a right to suppose that I may be
able to represent the county,--say in twenty year. I shall probably
then be the head of the family and a rich man. consider what a
parliamentary education would be to I! and then it be just the life
for which I have lay myself out, and in which I could make myself
useful. you donot sympathise with I, but you might understand I."
"I do both. I think of go into the House myself."
"you!"
"yes; I do."
"you must have change your idea very much then within the last
month or two."
"I have change my idea. my one chief object in life be, as you
know, to marry your sister; and if I be a Member of Parliament I
think that some difficulty would be clear away."
"but there willnot be an election for the next three year at any
rate," say Everett Wharton, star at his friend. "you donot mean
to keep Emily wait for a dissolution?"
"there be occasional vacancy," say lopez.
"be there a chance of anything of that kind fall in your way?"
"I think there be. I cannot quite tell you all the particular because
other people be concern, but I donot think it improbable that I
may be in the House before--; well, say in three month' time."
"in three month' time!" exclaim Everett, whose mouth be water
at the prospect of his friend. "that be what come from go
to stay with the Prime Minister, I suppose." Lopez shrug his
shoulder. "upon my word I cannot understand you," continue the
other. "it be only the other day you be argue in this very room
as to the absurdity of a parliamentary career,--pitching into I, by
George, like the very mischief, because I have say something in its
favour,--and now you be go in for it yourself in some sort of
mysterious way that a fellow cannot understand." it be quite clear
that Everett Wharton think himself ill-use by his friend's
success.
"there be no mystery;--only I cannot tell people's name."
"what be the borough?"
"I cannot tell you that at present."
"be you sure there will be a vacancy?"
"I think I be sure."
"and that you will be invite to stand?"
"I be not sure of that."
"of course anybody can stand whether invite or not."
"if I come forward for this place I shall do so on the very good
interest. donot mention it. I tell you because I already regard my
connection with you as be so close as to call upon I to tell you
anything of that kind."
"and yet you do not tell I the detail."
"I tell you all that I can in honour tell."
Everett Wharton certainly feel aggrieve by his friend's news, and
plainly show that he do so. it be so hard that if a stray seat
in Parliament be go a-begging, it should be throw in the way
of this man who donot care for it, and couldnot use it to any good
purpose, instead of in his own way! why should any one want Ferdinand
Lopez to be in Parliament? Ferdinand Lopez have pay no attention to
the great political question of the Commonwealth. he know nothing of
Labour and Capital, of union, strike, and lock-out. but because he
be rich, and, by be rich, have make his way among great people,
he be to have a seat in Parliament! as for the wealth, it might be
at his own command also,--if only his father could be get to see
the matter in a proper light. and as for the friendship of great
people,--prime Ministers, Duchesses, and such like,--Everett Wharton
be quite confident that he be at any rate as well qualified to
shine among they as Ferdinand Lopez. he be of too good a nature to
be stir to injustice against his friend by the soreness of this
feeling. he do not wish to rob his friend of his wealth, of his
Duchesses, or of his embryo seat in Parliament. but for the moment
there come upon he a doubt whether Ferdinand be so very clever, or
so peculiarly gentlemanlike or in any way very remarkable, and almost
a conviction that he be very far from be good-look.
they din together, and quite late in the evening they stroll out
into St. James's Park. there be nobody in London, and there be
nothing for either of they to do, and therefore they agree to walk
round the park, dark and gloomy as they know the park would be. lopez
have see and have quite understand the bitterness of spirit by which
Everett have be oppress, and with that peculiarly imperturbable
good humour which make a part of his character bear it all, even with
tenderness. he be a man, as be many of his race, who could bear
contradiction, unjust suspicion, and social ill-treatment without
a shadow of resentment, but who, if he have a purpose, could carry
it out without a shadow of a scruple. Everett Wharton have on this
occasion make himself very unpleasant, and Lopez have bear with he
as an angel would hardly have do; but should Wharton ever stand in
his friend's way, his friend would sacrifice he without compunction.
as it be, lopez bore with he, simply note in his own mind that
Everett Wharton be a great ass than he have take he to be. it be
Wharton's idea that they should walk round the park, and Lopez for a
time have discourage the suggestion. "it be a wretchedly dark place
at night, and you donot know whom you may meet there."
"you donot mean to say that you be afraid to walk round St. James's
Park with I, because itbe dark!" say Wharton.
"I certainly should be afraid by myself, but I donot know that I be
afraid with you. but whatbe the good?"
"itbe well than sit here do nothing, without a soul to speak
to. Ihave already smoke half-a-dozen cigar, till Ibe so muddled I
donot know what Ibe about. itbe so hot one cannot walk in the day, and
this be just the time for exercise." lopez yield, be willing to
yield in almost anything at present to the brother of Emily Wharton;
and, though the thing seem to he to be very foolish, they enter
the park by St. James's Palace, and start to walk round it, turn
to the right and go in front of Buckingham Palace. as they go on
Wharton still continue his accusation against his father and say
also some sharp thing against Lopez himself, till his companion
begin to think that the wine he have drunk have be as bad as the
cigar. "I cannot understand your wanting to go into Parliament," he
say. "what do you know about it?"
"if I get there, I can learn like anybody else, I suppose."
"half of those who go there donot learn. they be, as it be, bear
to it, and they do very well to support this party or that."
"and why shouldnot I support this party,--or that?"
"I donot suppose you know which party you would support,--except that
youwould vote for the Duke, if, as I suppose, you be to get in under
the Duke's influence. if I go into the House I should go with a
fixed and settle purpose of my own."
"Ibe not there yet," say lopez, willing to drop the subject.
"it will be a great expense to you, and will stand altogether in the
way of your profession. as far as Emily be concern, I should think
my father would be dead against it."
"then he would be unreasonable."
"not at all, if he think you would injure your professional
prospect. it be a d---- piece of folly; thatbe the long and the
short of it."
this certainly be very uncivil, and it almost make lopez angry. but
he have make up his mind that his friend be a little the bad for
the wine he have drunk, and therefore he do not resent even this.
"never mind politic and Parliament now," he say, "but let we get
home. I be begin to be sick of this. itbe so awfully dark, and
whenever I do hear a step, I think somebody be come to rob we. let
we get on a bit."
"what the deuce be you afraid of?" say Everett. they have then come
up the great part of the length of the Birdcage Walk, and the
light at Storey's Gate be just visible, but the road on which they
be then walk be very dark. the tree be black over their
head, and not a step be hear near they. at this time it be just

attribute to Lopez, personal cowardice could not be reckon. on
this evening he have twice speak of be afraid, but the fear have
simply be that which ordinary caution indicate; and his object have

the park, and then of get he out of it as quickly as possible.
"come along," say lopez.
"by George, you be in a blue funk," say the other. "I can hear your
tooth chattering." Lopez, who be begin to be angry, walk on
and say nothing. it be too absurd, he think, for real anger, but
he keep a little in front of Wharton, intend to show that he be

"upon my word, I shall begin to think that yoube tipsy," say lopez.
"Tipsy!" say the other. "how dare you say such a thing to I? you
never in your life see I in the least altered by any thing I have
drunk."
Lopez know that at any rate this be untrue. "Ihave see you as drunk
as Cloe before now," say he.
"thatbe a lie," say Everett Wharton.
"Come, Wharton," say the other, "do not disgrace yourself by conduct
such as that. something have put you out, and you do not know what you
be say. I can hardly imagine that you should wish to insult I."
"it be you who insult I. you say I be drunk. when you say it
you know it be untrue."
Lopez walk on a little way in silence, think over this most
absurd quarrel. then he turn round and speak. "this be all the
great nonsense I ever hear in the world. Iwill go on and go to
bed, and to-morrow morning youwill think well of it. but pray
remember that under no circumstance should you call a man a liar,
unless on cool consideration you be determined to quarrel with he
for lying, and determine also to see the quarrel out."
"I be quite ready to see this quarrel out."
"good night," say lopez, start off at a quick pace. they be
then close to the turn in the park, and Lopez go on till he have
nearly reach the park front of the new office. as he have walk
he have listen to the footfall of his friend, and after a while

be discontinue. it seem to he that Wharton have altogether lose

of man who have be apparently sober, become drunk as soon as they

his marriage by quarrel with Emily's brother.
as soon as he pause he be sure that he hear footstep behind he
which be not those of Everett Wharton. indeed, he be sure that he

first place at the man, who have jump up to meet he as he come. he
receive at once a heavy blow on his head from some weapon, which,
however, his hat so far stop as to save he from be fell or

but before he could well look about he, or well know how the whole
thing have happen, the man and the two woman have take to their
leg, and Wharton be stand on his foot lean against the iron
railing.
the whole thing have occupy a very short space of time, and yet
the effect be very grave. at the first moment Lopez look round
and endeavour to listen, hop that some assistance might be
near,--some policeman, or, if not that, some wanderer by night who
might be honest enough to help he. but he could hear or see no one,
in this condition of thing it be not possible for he to pursue the
ruffian, as he could not leave his friend lean against the park

hurt, or at any rate incapacitate for immediate exertion, by the

he say, come close up and take a tender hold of his friend's

the ruffian's fist have be at his throat, twist his cravat, and

at the moment there come across Wharton's mind a remembrance that

Ibe not hurt."
"oh, dear!" the word be utter with a low sigh. then there be a

be all over with I at one moment."
"you will be well now."
"oh, yes. my watch be go!"
"I fear it be," say lopez.
"and my purse," say Wharton, collect his strength together

"itbe a bore all the same to lose every shilling that one have." then

that the thief have get off with their plunder be only too

as he return he apply his mind to think how he could turn the
event of the evening to his own use. he do not believe that Everett
Wharton be severely hurt. indeed there might be a question whether
in the morning his own injury would not be the most severe. but the
immediate effect on the flustered and despoiled unfortunate one have
be great enough to justify Lopez in take strong step if strong
step could in any way benefit himself. would it be good to publish
this affair on the house-top, or to bury it in the shade, as nearly

DEAR MR. WHARTON,
I regret to have to send you an account of a rather
serious accident which have happen to Everett. I be now
write at 3 A.M., have just take he home, and it
occur at about midnight. you may be quite sure that
there be no danger or I should have advertise you by
telegram.
there be nothing do in town, and therefore, as the
night be fine, we, very foolishly, agree to walk round
St. James's Park late after dinner. it be a kind of thing
that nobody does;--but we do it. when we have nearly get
round I be in a hurry, whereas Everett be for stroll
slowly, and so I go on before he. but I be hardly two
hundred yard in front of he before he be attack by
three person, a man and two woman. the man I presume
come upon he from behind, but he have not sufficiently
collect his thought to remember exactly what occur.
I hear the scuffle and of course turn back,--and be
luckily in time to get up before he be seriously hurt.
I think the man would otherwise have strangle he. I be
sorry to say that he lose both his watch and purse.
he undoubtedly have be very much shake, and altogether
"knock out of time," as people say. excuse the phrase,
because I think it will well explain what I want you to
understand. the man's hand at his throat must have stop
his breathing for some second. he certainly have receive
no permanent injury, but I should not wonder if he should
be unwell for some day. I tell you all exactly as it
occur, as it strike I that you may like to run up to
town for a day just to look at he. but you need not do so
on the score of any danger. of course he will see a doctor
to-morrow. there do not seem to be any necessity for
call one up to-night. we do give notice to the police
as we be come home, but I fear the ruffians have ample
time for escape. he be too weak, and I be too fully
employ with he, to think of pursue they at the time.
of course he be at Manchester Square.
most faithfully your,
ferdinand lopez.
he do not say a word about Emily, but he know that Emily would
see the letter and would perceive that he have be the mean of
preserve her brother; and, in regard to the old barrister himself,
Lopez think that the old man could not but feel grateful for

expedient to say nothing about the blow in his letter. surely it
would all help. surely the paternal mind would be soften towards
he when the father should be make to understand how great have be
his service to the son. that Everett would make little of what have
be do for he he do not in the least fear. Everett Wharton be
sometimes silly but be never ungenerous.
in spite of his night's work Lopez be in Manchester Square before

that it should be so."
"there be nothing the matter with I," say Everett.
"one donot quite know last night whether there be or no. at any
rate his coming willnot hurt you. itbe always well to have your banker
near you, when your fund be low."
then after a pause Everett make his apology,--"i know I make a great
ass of myself last night."
"donot think about it."
"I use a word I shouldnot have use, and I beg your pardon."
"not another word, Everett. between you and I thing cannot go wrong.
we love each other too well."
CHAPTER XXIII
Surrender
the letter give in the previous chapter be receive at Wharton
Hall late in the evening of the day on which it be write, and be
discuss among all the Whartons that night. of course there be no
doubt as to the father's go up to town on the morrow. the letter
be just such a letter as would surely make a man run to his son's
bedside. have the son write himself it would have be different;
but the fact that the letter have come from another man seem to be
evidence that the poor sufferer could not write. perhaps the urgency
with which Lopez have send off his dispatch, get his account of
the fray ready for the very early day mail, though the fray have not
take place till midnight, do not impress they sufficiently when
they accept this as evidence of Everett's dangerous condition. at
this conference at Wharton very little be say about Lopez, but
there be a general feeling that he have behave well. "it be very
odd that they should have part in the park," say Sir Alured.
"but very lucky that they should not have part soon," say John
Fletcher. if a grain of suspicion against Lopez might have be set

they all hat Ferdinand Lopez, but nothing could be extract from
the incident, as far as its detail be yet know to they, which
could be turn to his injury.
while they sit together discuss the matter in the drawing-room
Emily Wharton hardly say a word. she utter a little shriek when

course I will go with you to-morrow, papa."
"I donot think that will be necessary."
"oh, yes. think how wretched I should be."
"I would telegraph to you immediately."
"and I shouldnot believe the telegraph. donot you know how it always
be? besides we have be more than the usual time. we be to go to

of course I will go with you. I have already begin to pack my thing,

"I donot know that he have do anything of the kind," say Mr.

"perhaps you think the story be not true."
"I donot doubt the story in the least. of course one man stick to

"any english gentleman, papa, would have to come afterwards and see

he without your leave."

quite as near his heart as she could wish it to be.
it be late in the afternoon before they reach Manchester Square,
and they be both happy to find that they be not trouble by Mr.

not indeed as yet recover from the effect of the man's knuckle at

"the fact be," say Everett, begin to tell the whole story after

I should have go alone." here there be another pressure. "I be a
little low in spirit, and want the walk."
"but how be he hurt?" ask the father.
"the man who be kneel on I and squeeze the life out of I
jump up when he hear Lopez come, and strike he over the head

at the time myself. I donot think they hit I, but they get something

do. poor Lopez bleed horribly, but he say he be none the bad for

for Emily feel that her brother be plead for she in every word
that he say.
about ten on the following morning Lopez come and ask for Mr.
Wharton. he be show into the study, where he find the old man, and
at once begin to give his account of the whole concern in an easy,
unconcerned manner. he have the large black patch on the side of his

conspicuous as to force a question respect it from Mr. Wharton. "I
be afraid you get rather a sharp knock yourself, Mr. Lopez?"
"I do get a knock, certainly;--but the odd part of it be that I know
nothing about it till I find the blood in my eye after they have
decamp. but I lose my hat, and there be a rather long cut just
above the temple. it havenot do I the slight harm. the bad of
it be that they get off with Everett's watch and money."
"have he much money?"
"forty pound!" and Lopez shake his head, thereby signify that
forty pound at the present moment be more than Everett Wharton
could afford to lose. upon the whole he carry himself very well,
ingratiate himself with the father, raise no question about the

feeling with both of they that the other be close,--and there be

accident of the park robbery would be good for they.
"he certainly do save Everett's life," Emily say to her father the
next day.
"whether he do or not, he do his good," say Mr. Wharton.
"when one's dear relation be concern," say Emily, "and when
his life have be save, one feel that one have to be grateful even
if it have be an accident. I hope he know, at any rate, that I be
grateful."
the old man have not be a week in London before he know that he have
absolutely lose the game. Mrs. Roby come back to her house round the
corner, ostensibly with the object of assist her relative in
nursing Everett,--a purpose for which she certainly be not need;
but, as the matter progress, Mr. Wharton be not without suspicion
that her return have be arrange by Ferdinand Lopez. she take upon
