should be loud in his praise. in a little time all London have hear
of the affair, and it have be discuss out of London. down at
Gatherum Castle the matter have be know, or partly known,--but the
telling of it have always be to the great honour and glory of the
hero. Major Pountney have almost break his heart over it, and Captain
Gunner, write to his friend from the Curragh, have assert his
knowledge that it be all a "get-up thing" between the two man. the
"Breakfast table" and the "Evening Pulpit" have be loud in praise of
lopez; but the "People's Banner," under the management of Mr. Quintus
Slide, have naturally throw much suspicion on the incident when it
become know to the Editor that Ferdinand Lopez have be entertain
by the Duke and Duchess of Omnium. "we have always feel some slight
doubt as to the detail of the affair say to have happen about a
fortnight ago, just at midnight, in St. James's Park. we should be
glad to know whether the policeman have succeed in trace any of
the steal property, or whether any real attempt to trace it have be
make." this be one of the paragraph, and it be hint still more
plainly afterwards that Everett Wharton, be short of money, have
arrange the plan with the view of open his father's purse. but
the general effect be certainly serviceable to lopez. Emily Wharton
do believe he to be a hero. Everett be beyond measure grateful to
him,--not only for have save he from the thief, but also for
have tell nothing of his previous folly. Mrs. Roby always allude
to the matter as if, for all come age, every Wharton ought to
acknowledge that gratitude to a Lopez be the very first duty of
life. the old man feel the absurdity of much of this, but still it
affect he. when Lopez come he could not be rough to the man who
have do a service to his son. and then he find himself compel
to do something. he must either take his daughter away, or he must
yield. but his power of take his daughter away seem to be less
than it have be. there be an air of quiet, unmerited suffering
about she, which quell he. and so he yield.
it be after this fashion. whether affect by the violence of the
attack make on he, or from other cause, Everett have be unwell
after the affair, and have keep his room for a fortnight. during this
time Lopez come to see he daily, and daily Emily Wharton have to take
herself out of the man's way, and hide herself from the man's sight.
this she do with much tact and with lady-like quietness, but not
without an air of martyrdom, which cut her father to the quick. "my
dear," he say to her one evening, as she be prepare to leave the
drawing-room on hear his knock, "stop and see he if you like it."
"Papa!"
"I donot want to make you wretched. if I could have die first, and
get out of the way, perhaps it would have be well."
"Papa, you will kill I if you speak in that way! if there be
anything to say to he, do you say it." and then she escape.
well! it be an add bitterness, but no doubt it be his duty. if he
do intend to consent to the marriage, it certainly be for he to
signify that consent to the man. it would not be sufficient that he
should get out of the way and leave his girl to act for herself as
though she have no friend in the world. the surrender which he have
make to his daughter have come from a sudden impulse at the moment,
but it could not now be withdraw. so he stand out on the staircase,
and when Lopez come up on his way to Everett's bedroom, he take
he by the arm and lead he into the drawing-room. "Mr. Lopez," he
say, "you know that I have not be willing to welcome you into
my house as a son-in-law. there be reason on my mind,--perhaps
prejudices,--which be strong against it. they be as strong now
as ever. but she wish it, and I have the utmost reliance on her
constancy."
"so have I," say lopez.
"stop a moment, if you please, sir. in such a position a father's
thought be only as to his daughter's happiness and prosperity. it be
not his own that he should consider. I hear you well speak of in
the outer world, and I do not know that I have a right to demand
of my daughter that she should tear you from her affection,
because--because you be not just such as I would have her husband to
be. you have my permission to see she." then, before Lopez could say
a word, he leave the room, and take his hat and hurry away to his
club.
as he go he be aware that he have make no term at all;--but then
he be inclined to think that no term should be make. there seem
to be a general understanding that Lopez be do well in the
world,--in a profession of the working of which Mr. Wharton
himself know absolutely nothing. he have a large fortune at his
own bestowal,--intende for his daughter,--which would have be
forthcoming at the moment and pay down on the nail, have she
marry Arthur Fletcher. the very way in which the money should be
invest and tie up and make to be safe and comfortable to the
Fletcher-cum-Wharton interest generally, have be fully settle
among they. but now this other man, this stranger, this Portuguese,
have enter in upon the inheritance. but the stranger, the
Portuguese, must wait. Mr. Wharton know himself to be an old man.
she be his child, and he would not wrong she. but she should have
her money closely settle upon herself on his death,--and on her
child, should she then have any. it should be do by his will. he
would say nothing about money to Lopez, and if Lopez should, as be
probable, ask after his daughter's fortune, he would answer to this
effect. thus he almost resolve that he would give his daughter to
the man without any inquiry as to the man's mean. the thing have to
be do, and he would take no further trouble about it. the comfort
of his life be go. his home would no long be a home to he. his
daughter could not now be his companion. the soon that death come
to he the well, but till death should come he must console himself
as well as he could by play whist at the Eldon. it be after this
fashion that Mr. Wharton think of the come marriage between his
daughter and her lover.
"I have your father's consent to marry your sister," say Ferdinand
immediately on enter Everett's room.
"I know it must come soon," say the invalid.
"I cannot say that it have be give in the most gracious
manner,--but it have be give very clearly. I have his express
permission to see she. those be his last word."
then there be a sending of note between the sick-room and the sick
man's sister's room. Everett write and Ferdinand write, and emily
wrote,--short line each of them,--a few word scrawl. the last
from Emily be as follows:--"Let he go into the drawing-room. E. W."
and so Ferdinand go down, to meet his love,--to encounter she for
the first time as she recognise future husband and engage lover.
Passionate, declare, and thorough as be her love for this man, the
familiar intercourse between they have hitherto be very limited.
there have be little,--we may perhaps say none,--of that dalliance
between they which be so delightful to the man and so wondrous to the
girl till custom have stale the edge of it. he have never sit with his
arm round her waist. he have rarely hold even her hand in his for a
happy recognise pause of a few second. he have never kiss even
her brow. and there she be now, stand before he, all his own,
absolutely give to he, with the full assurance of love on her
part, and with the declare consent of her father. even he have be
a little confused as he open the door,--even he, as he pause to
close it behind he, have have to think how he would address she, and
perhaps have think in vain. but he have not a moment for any thought
after enter the room. whether it be his doing or hers he hardly
know; but she be in his arm, and her lip be press to his, and
his arm be tight round her waist, hold she close to his breast.
it seem as though all that be want have be understand in a
moment, and as though they have live together and love for the last
twelve month with the full mutual confidence. and she be the
first to speak:--
"Ferdinand, I be so happy! be you happy?"
"my love; my darling!"
"you have never doubt I, I know,--since you first know it."
"Doubted you, my girl!"
"that I would be firm! and now papa have be good to I, and how
quickly one's sorrow be over. I be your, my love, for ever and ever.
you know it before, but I like to tell you. I will be true to you in
everything! oh, my love!"
he have but little to say to she, but we know that for "lover lack
matter, the cleanly shift be to kiss." in such moment silence
charm, and almost any word be unsuitable except those soft,
bird-like murmuring of love which, sweet as they be to the ear, can
hardly be so write as to be sweet to the reader.
CHAPTER XXIV
the Marriage
the engagement be make in October, and the marriage take place
in the latter part of November. when Lopez press for an early
day,--which he do very strongly,--emily raise no difficulty in
the way of his wish. the father, foolishly enough, would at first
have postpone it, and make himself so unpleasant to Lopez by his
manner of do this, that the bride be drive to take her lover's
part. as the thing be to be do, what be to be gain by delay?
it could not be make a joy to he; nor, look at the matter as he
look at it, could he make a joy even of her presence during the few
intervene week. Lopez propose to take his bride into Italy for
the winter month, and to stay there at any rate through December
and January, allege that he must be back in town by the beginning
of February;--and this be take as a fair plea for hasten the
marriage.
when the matter be settle, he go back to Gatherum Castle, as he
have arrange to do with the Duchess, and manage to interest her
Grace in all his proceeding. she promise that she would call on his
bride in town, and even go so far as to send she a costly wedding
present. "you be sure she have get money?" say the Duchess.
"I be not sure of anything," say Lopez,--"except this, that I do
not mean to ask a single question about it. if he say nothing to
I about money, I certainly shall say nothing to he. my feeling be
this, Duchess; I be not marry Miss Wharton for her money. the
money, if there be any, have have nothing to do with it. but of course
it will be a pleasure add if it be there." the Duchess compliment
he, and tell he that this be exactly as it should be.
but there be some delay as to the seat for Silverbridge. Mr. Grey's
departure for Persia have be postponed,--the Duchess think only
for a month or six week. the Duke, however, be of opinion that
Mr. Grey should not vacate his seat till the day of his going be
at any rate fix. the Duke, moreover, have not make any promise of
support his wife's favourite. "donot set your heart upon it too
much, Mr. Lopez," the Duchess have say; "but you may be sure I will
not forget you." then it have be settle between they that the
marriage should not be postpone, or the propose trip to Italy
abandon, because of the probable vacancy at Silverbridge. should
the vacancy occur during his absence, and should the Duke consent,
he could return at once. all this occur in the last week or two
before his marriage.
there be various little incident which do not tend to make the
happiness of Emily Wharton complete. she write to her cousin Mary
Wharton, and also to Lady Wharton;--and her father write to Sir
Alured; but the folk at Wharton Hall do not give in their adherence.
Old Mrs. Fletcher be still there, but John Fletcher have go home to
longbarn. the obduracy of the Whartons might probably be owe to
these two accident. Mrs. Fletcher declare aloud, as soon as the
tiding reach she, that she never wish to see or hear anything
more of Emily Wharton. "she must be a girl," say Mrs. Fletcher,
"of an ingrain vulgar taste." Sir Alured, whose letter from Mr.
Wharton have be very short, reply as shortly to his cousin. "dear
abel,--we all hope that Emily will be happy, though of course we
regret the marriage." the father, though he have not himself write
triumphantly, or even hopefully,--as father be willnot to write when
their daughter be give away in marriage,--wa wound by the
curtness and unkindness of the baronet's reply, and at the moment
declare to himself that he would never go to Herefordshire any more.
but on the follow day there come a bad blow than Sir Alured's
single line. emily, not in the least doubting but that her request
would be receive with the usual ready assent, have ask Mary Wharton
to be one of her bridesmaid. it must be suppose that the answer to
this be write, if not under the dictation, at any rate under the
inspiration, of Mrs. Fletcher. it be as follows:--
DEAR EMILY,
of course we all wish you to be very happy in your
marriage, but equally of course we be all disappointed.
we have teach ourselves to think that you would have bind
yourself close with we down here, instead of separate
yourself entirely from we.
under all the circumstance mamma think it would not be
wise for I to go up to London as one of your bridesmaid.
your affectionate Cousin,
MARY WHARTON.
this letter make poor Emily very angry for a day or two. "it be as
unreasonable as it be ill-natured," she say to her brother.
"what else could you expect from a stiff-neck, prejudiced set of
provincial ignoramus?"
"what Mary say be not true. she do not think that I be go to
bind myself close with they, as she call it. I have be quite
open with she, and have always tell she that I could not be Arthur
Fletcher's wife."
"why on earth should you marry to please they?"
"because they donot know Ferdinand they be determined to insult he.
it be an insult never to mention even his name. and to refuse to come
to my marriage! the world be wide and there be room for we and they;
but it make I unhappy,--very unhappy,--that I should have to break
with they." and then the tear come into her eye. it be intend,
no doubt, to be a complete breach, for not a single wedding present
be send from Wharton Hall to the bride. but from Longbarns,--from
John Fletcher himself,--there do come an elaborate coffee-pot,
which, in spite of its inutility and ugliness, be very valuable to
Emily.
but there be one other of her old Herefordshire friend who receive
the tiding of her marriage without quarrel with she. she herself
have write to her old lover.
MY DEAR arthur,
there have be so much true friendship and affection
between we that I do not like that you should hear
from any one but myself the news that I be go to be
married to Mr. Lopez. we be to be marry on the 28th of
november,--this day month.
your affectionately,
EMILY WHARTON.
to this she receive a very short reply;--
DEAR EMILY,
I be as I always have be.
your,
A. F.
he send she no present, nor do he say a word to she beyond this;
but in her anger against the Herefordshire people she never include
Arthur Fletcher. she pore over the little note a score of time, and
weep over it, and treasure it up among her inmost treasure, and
tell herself that it be a thousand pity. she could talk, and do
talk, to ferdinand about the Whartons, and about old Mrs. Fletcher,
and describe to he the arrogance and the stiffness and the
ignorance of the Herefordshire squirearchy generally; but she never
speak to he of Arthur Fletcher,--except in that one narrative of she
past life, in which, girl-like, she tell her lover of the one other
lover who have love she.
but these thing of course give a certain melancholy to the occasion
which perhaps be increase by the season of the year,--by the
November fog, and by the emptiness and general sadness of the town.
and add to this be the melancholy of old Mr. Wharton himself.
after he have give his consent to the marriage he admit a certain
amount of intimacy with his son-in-law, ask he to dinner, and
discuss with he matter of general interest,--but never, in
truth, open his heart to he. indeed, how can any man open his
heart to one whom he dislike? at good he can only pretend to open
his heart, and even this Mr. Wharton would not do. and very soon
after the engagement Lopez leave London and go to the Duke's place
in the country. his object in do this and his aspiration in
regard to a seat in Parliament be all make known to his future
wife,--but he say not a word on the subject to her father; and she,
act under his instruction, be equally reticent. "he will get to
know I in time," he say to she, "and his manner will be soften
towards I. but till that time shall come, I can hardly expect he to
take a real interest in my welfare."
when Lopez leave London not a word have be say between he and his
father-in-law as to money. Mr. Wharton be content with such silence,
not wish to make any promise as to immediate income from himself,
pretend to look at the matter as though he should say that, as his
daughter have make for herself her own bed, she must lie on it, such
as it might be. and this silence certainly suit Ferdinand Lopez at
the time. to tell the truth of him,--though he be not absolutely
penniless, he be altogether propertyless. he have be speculate
in money without capital, and though he have now and again be
successful, he have also now and again fail. he have contrive
that his name should be mention here and there with the name
of well-know wealthy commercial man, and have for the last twelve
month make up a somewhat intimate alliance with that very sound
commercial man, Mr. Mills Happerton. but his dealing with Mr. Sextus
Parker be in truth much more confidential than those with Mr.
Mills Happerton, and at the present moment poor Sexty Parker be
alternately between triumph and despair as thing go this way or
that.
it be not, therefore, surprising that Ferdinand Lopez should
volunteer no statement to the old lawyer about money, and that he
should make no inquiry. he be quite confident that Mr. Wharton have
the wealth which be suppose to belong to he, and be willing to
trust to his power of obtain a fair portion of it as soon as he
should in truth be Mr. Wharton's son-in-law. situate as he be, of
course he must run some risk. and then, too, he have speak of himself
with a grain of truth when he have tell the Duchess that he be not
marry for money. Ferdinand Lopez be not an honest man or a good
man. he be a self-seeking, intriguing adventurer, who do not know
honesty from dishonesty when he see they together. but he have at any
rate this good about he, that he do love the girl whom he be about
to marry. he be willing to cheat all the world,--so that he might
succeed, and make a fortune, and become a big and a rich man; but
he do not wish to cheat she. it be his ambition now to carry she
up with he, and he think how he might well teach she to assist
he in do so,--how he might win she to help he in his cheating,
especially in regard to her own father. for to himself, to his own
thinking, that which we call cheating be not dishonesty. to his
thinking there be something bold, grand, picturesque, and almost
beautiful in the battle which such a one as himself must wage with
the world before he could make his way up in it. he would not pick
a pocket, or turn a false card, or, as he think, forge a name.
that which he do, and desire to do, take with he the name of
speculation. when he persuade poor Sexty Parker to hazard his all,
know well that he induce the unfortunate man to believe what
be false, and to trust what be utterly untrustworthy, he do not
himself think that he be go beyond the line of fair enterprise.
now, in his marriage, he have in truth join himself to real wealth.
could he only command at once that which he think ought to be his
wife's share of the lawyer's money, he do not doubt but that he
could make a rapid fortune. it would not do for he to seem to be
desirous of the money a day before the time;--but, when the time
should come, would not his wife help he in his great career? but
before she could do so she must be make to understand something of
the nature of that career, and of the need of such aid.
of course there arise the question where they should live. but he be
ready with an immediate answer to this question. he have be to look
at a flat,--a set of rooms,--in the Belgrave Mansions, in Pimlico,
or Belgravia you ought more probably to call it. he propose to take
they furnish till they could look about at their leisure and get a
house that should suit they. would she like a flat? she would have
like a cellar with he, and so she tell he. then they go to look
at the flat, and old Mr. Wharton condescend to go with they. though
his heart be not in the business, still he think that he be bind
to look after his daughter's comfort. "they be very handsome room,"
say Mr. Wharton, look round upon the rather gorgeous furniture.
"oh, Ferdinand, be they not too grand?" say Emily.
"perhaps they be a little more than we quite want just at present,"
he say. "but Iwill tell you, sir, just how it have happen. a man I
know want to let they for one year, just as they be, and offer
they to I for L450,--if I could pay the money in advance, at the
moment. and so I pay it."
"you have take they, then?" say Mr. Wharton.
"be it all settle?" say Emily, almost with disappointment.
"I have pay the money, and I have so far take they. but it be by
no mean settle. you have only to say you donot like they, and you
shall never be ask to put your foot in they again."
"but I do like they," she whisper to he.
"the truth be, sir, that there be not the slight difficulty in
parting with they. so that when the chance come in my way I think
it good to secure the thing. it have all to be do, so to say, in an
hour. my friend,--as far as he be a friend, for I donot know much
about him,--wante the money and want to be off. so here they be,
and Emily can do as she like." of course the room be regard
from that moment as the home for the next twelve month of Mr. and
Mrs. Ferdinand Lopez.
and then they be marry. the marriage be by no mean a gay
affair, the chief management of it fall into the hand of Mrs.
Dick Roby. Mrs. Dick indeed provide not only the breakfast,--or
see rather that it be provide, for of course Mr. Wharton pay the
bill,--but the four bridesmaid also, and all the company. they be
marry in the church in Vere Street, then go back to the house in
Manchester Square, and within a couple of hour be on their road to
Dover. through it all not a word be say about money. at the last
moment,--when he be free from fear as to any question about his own
affairs,--lopez have hop that the old man would say something. "you
will find so many thousand pound at your bankers';"--or, "you may
look to I for so many hundred a year." but there be not a word.
the girl have come to he without the assurance of a single shilling.
in his great endeavour to get she he have be successful. as he
think of this in the carriage, he press his arm close round her
waist. if the bad be to come to the bad, he would fight the
world for she. but if this old man should be stubborn, close-fiste,
and absolutely resolve to bestow all his money upon his son because
of this marriage,--ah!--how should he be able to bear such a wrong as
that?
half-a-dozen time during that journey to Dover he resolve to think
nothing further about it, at any rate for a fortnight; and yet,
before he reach Dover, he have say a word to she. "I wonder what
your father mean to do about money? he never tell you?"
"not a word."
"it be very odd that he should never have say anything."
"do it matter, dear?"
"not in the least. but of course I have to talk about everything to
you;--and it be odd."
CHAPTER XXV
the beginning of the Honeymoon
on the morning of his marriage, before he go to the altar, Lopez
make one or two resolution as to his future conduct. the first be
that he would give himself a fortnight from his marriage day in which
he would not even think of money. he have make certain arrangement,
in the course of which he have cause Sextus Parker to stare with
surprise and to sweat with dismay, but which nevertheless be
successfully conclude. bill be draw to run over to February,
and ready money to a moderate extent be forthcoming, and fiscal
tranquillity be insure for a certain short period. the confidence
which Sextus Parker have once feel in his friend's own resource be
somewhat on the decline, but he still believe in his friend's skill
and genius, and, after due inquiry, he believe entirely in his
friend's father-in-law. Sextus Parker still think that thing
would come round. Ferdinand,--he always now call his friend by his
Christian name,--Ferdinand be beautifully, seraphically confident.
and Sexty, who have be in a manner magnetise by Ferdinand, be
confident too--at certain period of the day. he be very confident
when he have have his two or three glass of sherry at luncheon,
and he be often delightfully confident with his cigar and
brandy-and-water at night. but there be period in the morning in
which he would shake with fear and sweat with dismay.
but Lopez himself, have with his friend's assistance arrange his
affair comfortably for a month or two, have, as a first resolution,
promise himself a fortnight's freedom from all carking care. his
second resolution have be that at the end of the fortnight he would
commence his operation on Mr. Wharton. up to the last moment he have
hoped,--had almost expected,--that a sum of money would have be
pay to he. even a couple of thousand pound for the time would have
be of great use to him;--but no tender of any kind have be make.
not a word have be say. thing could not of course go on in that
way. he be not go to play the coward with his father-in-law.
then he bethink himself how he would act if his father-in-law be
sternly to refuse to do anything for he, and he assure himself that
in such circumstance he would make himself very disagreeable to his
father-in-law. and then his third resolution have reference to his
wife. she must be instruct in his way. she must learn to look at
the world with his eye. she must be teach the great importance
of money,--not in a griping, hard-fiste, prosaic spirit; but that
she might participate in that feeling of his own which have in it so
much that be grand, so much that be delightful, so much that be
picturesque. he would never ask she to be parsimonious,--never even
to be economical. he would take a glory in see she well dress
and well attend, with her own carriage and her own jewel. but she
must learn that the enjoyment of these thing must be build upon
a conviction that the most important pursuit in the world be the
acquiring of money. and she must be make to understand, first of all,
that she have a right to at any rate a half of her father's fortune.
he have perceive that she have much influence with her father, and she
must be teach to use this influence unscrupulously on her husband's
behalf.
we have already see that under the pressure of his thought he do
break his first resolution within an hour or two of his marriage. it
be easy for a man to say that he will banish care, so that he may
enjoy to the full the delight of the moment. but this be a power
which none but a savage possesses,--or perhaps an Irishman. we have
learn the lesson from the divine, the philosopher, and the poet.
Post equitem sedet atra cura. thus be Ferdinand Lopez mount high
on his horse,--for he have triumph greatly in his marriage, and
really feel that the world could give he no delight so great as to
have she beside he, and she as his own. but the inky devil sit close
upon his shoulder. where would he be at the end of three month if
Mr. Wharton would do nothing for him,--and if a certain venture in
guano, to which he have tempt Sexty Parker, should not turn out the
right way? he believe in the guano and he believe in Mr. Wharton,
but it be a terrible thing to have one's whole position in the world
hang upon either an unwilling father-in-law or a probable rise in
the value of manure! and then how would he reconcile himself to she
if both father-in-law and guano should go against he, and how should
he endure her misery?
the inky devil have force he to ask the question even before they
have reach Dover. "do it matter?" she have ask. then for the time
he have repudiate his solicitude, and have declare that no question
of money be of much consequence to him,--thereby make his future
task with she so much the more difficult. after that he say nothing
to she on the subject on that their wedding day,--but he could not
prevent himself from think of it. have he go to the depth of ruin
without a wife, what would it have matter? for year past he have
be at the same kind of work,--but while he be unmarried there
have be a charm in the very danger. and as a single man he have
succeed, be sometimes utterly impecunious, but still with a
capacity of living. now he have lade himself with a burden of which
the very intensity of his love immensely increase the weight. as for
not think of it, that be impossible. of course she must help he.
of course she must be teach how imperative it be that she should
help he at once. "be there anything trouble you?" she say, as she
sit lean against he after their dinner in the hotel at Dover.
"what should trouble I on such a day as this?"
"if there be anything, tell it I. I do not mean to say now, at this
moment,--unless you wish it. whatever may be your trouble, it shall
be my great happiness, as it be my first duty, to lessen they if I
can."
the promise be very well. it all go in the right direction. it
show he that she be at any rate prepare to take a part in the
joint work of their life. but, nevertheless, she should be spar for
the moment. "when there be trouble, you shall be tell everything," he
say, press his lip to her brow, "but there be nothing that need
trouble you yet." he smile as he say this, but there be something
in the tone of his voice which tell she that there would be trouble.
when he be in Paris he receive a letter from Parker, to whom he
have be oblige to intrust a running address, but from whom he have
enforce a promise that there should be no letter-writing unless
under very pressing circumstance. the circumstance have not be
press. the letter contain only one paragraph of any importance,
and that be due to what Lopez try to regard as fidgety cowardice
on the part of his ally. "please to bear in mind that I cannot and
willnot arrange for the bill for L1500 due 3rd February." that be
the paragraph. who have ask he to arrange for these bill? and
yet Lopez be well aware that he intend that poor Sexty should
"arrange" for they, in the event of his failure to make arrangement
with Mr. Wharton.
at last he be quite unable to let the fortnight pass by without
begin the lesson which his wife have to learn. as for that first
intention as to drive his care out of his own mind for that time,
he have long since abandon even the attempt. it be necessary to
he that a considerable sum of money should be extract from the
father-in-law, at any rate before the end of January, and a week or
even a day might be of importance. they have hurry on southward
from Paris, and before the end of the first week have pass over the
Simplon, and be at a pleasant inn on the shore of Como. everything
in their travel have be as yet delightful to Emily. this man, of
whom she know in truth so little, have certain good gifts,--gift of
intellect, gift of temper, gift of voice and manner and outward
appearance,--which have hitherto satisfy she. a husband who be also
an eager lover must be delightful to a young bride. and hitherto no
lover could have be more tender than Lopez. every word and every
act, every look and every touch, have be love. have she know
the world well she might have feel, perhaps, that something be
expect where so much be give. perhaps a rougher manner, with some
little touch of marital self-assertion, might be a safe commencement
of married life,--safer to the wife as come from her husband.
Arthur Fletcher by this time would have ask she to bring he his
slipper, take infinite pride in have his little behest obey
by so sweet a servitor. that also would have be pleasant to she have
her heart in the first instance follow his image; but now also the
idolatry of Ferdinand Lopez have be very pleasant.
but the moment for the first lesson have come. "your father have not
write to you since you start?" he say.
"not a line. he have not know our address. he be never very good at
letter-writing. I do write to he from Paris, and I scribble a few
word to Everett yesterday."
"it be very odd that he should never have write to I."
"do you expect he to write?"
"to tell you the truth, I rather do. not that I should have dream
of his corresponding with I have he speak to I on a certain
subject. but as, on that subject, he never open his mouth to I, I
almost think he would write."
"do you mean about money?" she ask in a very low voice.
"well;--ye; I do mean about money. thing hitherto have go so very
strangely between we. sit down, dear, till we have a real domestic
talk."
"tell I everything," she say, as she nestle herself close to his
side.
"you know how it be at first between he and I. he object to I
violently,--i mean openly, to my face. but he base his objection
solely on my nationality,--nationality and blood. as to my condition
in the world, fortune, or income, he never ask a word. that be
strange."
"I suppose he think he know."
"he could not have think he know, dearest. but it be not for I to
force the subject upon he. you can see that."
"I be sure whatever you do be right, Ferdinand."
"he be indisputably a rich man,--one who might be suppose to be able
and willing to give an only daughter a considerable fortune. now I
certainly have never think of marry for money." here she rub
her face upon his arm. "I feel that it be not for I to speak of
money. if he choose to be reticent, I could be so equally. have he
ask I, I should have tell he that I have no fortune, but be
make a large though precarious income. it would then be for he
to declare what he intend to do. that would, I think, have be
preferable. as it be we be all in doubt. in my position a knowledge
of what your father intend to do would be most valuable to I."
"should you not ask he?"
"I believe there have always be a perfect confidence between you and
he?"
"certainly,--as to all our way of living. but he never say a word
to I about money in his life."
"and yet, my darling, money be most important."
"of course it be. I know that, Ferdinand."
"would you mind ask?" she do not answer he at once, but sit
thinking. and he also pause before he go on with his lesson.
but, in order that the lesson should be efficacious, it would be as
well that he should tell she as much as he could even at this first
lecture. "to tell you the truth, this be quite essential to I at
present,--very much more than I have think it would be when we fix
the day for our marriage." her mind within she recoil at this,
though she be very careful that he should not feel any such motion
in her body. "my business be precarious."
"what be your business, Ferdinand?" poor girl! that she should have
be allow to marry a man, and then have to ask such a question!
"it be generally commercial. I buy and sell on speculation. the
world, which be shy of new word, have not yet give it a name. I be
a good deal at present in the south american trade." she listen,
but receive no glimmering of an idea from his word. "when we be
engage everything be as bright as rose with I."
"why do you not tell I this before,--so that we might have be
more prudent?"
"such prudence would have be horrid to I. but the fact be that
I should not now have speak to you at all, but that since we leave
England I have have letter from a sort of partner of mine. in our
business thing will go astray sometimes. it would be of great
service to I if I could learn what be your father's intention."
"you want he to give you some money at once."
"it would not be unusual, dear,--when there be money to be give. but
I want you specially to ask he what he himself would propose to do.
he know already that I have take a home for you and pay for it,
and he knows--. but it do not signify go into that."
"tell I everything."
"he be aware that there be many expense. of course if he be a
poor man there would not be a word about it. I can with absolute
truth declare that have he be penniless it would have make no
difference as to my suit to you. but it would possibly have make some
difference as to our after plan. he be a thorough man of the world,
and he must know all that. I be sure he must feel that something be
due to you,--and to I as your husband. but he be odd-temper, and,
as I have not speak to he, he choose to be silent to I. now, my
darling, you and I cannot afford to wait to see who can be silent the
long."
"what do you want I to do?"
"to write to he."
"and ask he for money?"
"not exactly in that way. I think you should say that we should be
glad to know what he intend to do, also say that a certain sum of
money would at present be of use to I."
"would it not be well from you? I only ask, Ferdinand. I never have
even speak to he about money, and of course he would know that you
have dictate what I say."
"no doubt he would. it be natural that I should do so. I hope the
time may come when I may write quite freely to your father myself,
but hitherto he have hardly be courteous to I. I would rather that
you should write,--if you do not mind it. write your own letter, and
show it I. if there be anything too much or anything too little I
will tell you."
and so the first lesson be teach. the poor young wife do not at
all like the lesson. even within her own bosom she find no fault
with her husband. but she begin to understand that the life before
she be not to be a life of rose. the first word speak to she in
the train, before it reach Dover, have explain something of this
to she. she have feel at once that there would be trouble about money.
and now, though she do not at all understand what might be the
nature of those trouble, though she have derive no information
whatever from her husband's hint about the south american trade,
though she be as ignorant as ever of his affair, yet she feel that
the trouble would come soon. but never for a moment do it seem to
she that he have be unjust in bring she into troubled water.
they have love each other, and therefore, whatever might be the
trouble, it be right that they should marry each other. there be
not a spark of anger against he in she bosom;--but she be unhappy.
he demand from she the writing of the letter almost immediately
after the conversation which have be give above, and of course the
letter be written,--written and recopy, for the paragraph about
the money be, of course, at last of his wording. and she could not
make the remainder of the letter pleasant. the feeling that she be
make a demand for money on her father run through it all. but the
reader need only see the passage in which Ferdinand Lopez make his
demand,--through her hand.
"Ferdinand have be speak to I about my fortune." it have go
much against the grain with she to write these word, "my fortune."
"but I have no fortune," she say. he insist however, explain
to she that she be entitle to use these word by her father's
undoubted wealth. and so, with an aching heart, she write they.
"Ferdinand have be speak to I about my fortune. of course, I
tell he that I know nothing, and that as he have never speak to
I about money before our marriage, I have never ask about it. he
say that it would be of great service to he to know what be your
intention; and also that he hop you may find it convenient to
allow he to draw upon you for some portion of it at present. he say
that L3000 would be of great use to he in his business." that be
the paragraph, and the work of write it be so distasteful to she
that she could hardly bring herself to form the letter. it seem
as though she be seize the advantage of the first moment of her
freedom to take a violent liberty with her father.
"it be altogether his own fault, my pet," he say to she. "I have the
great respect in the world for your father, but he have allow
himself to fall into the habit of keep all his affair secret from
his child; and, of course, as they go out into the world, this
secrecy must in some degree be invade. there be precisely the same
thing go on between he and Everett; only Everett be a great deal
rougher to he than you be likely to be. he never will let Everett
know whether he be to regard himself as a rich man or a poor man."
"he give he an allowance."
"because he cannot help himself. to you he do not do even as much
as that, because he can help himself. I have choose to leave it to
he and he have do nothing. but this be not quite fair, and he must
be tell so. I donot think he could be tell in more dutiful language."
Emily do not like the idea of tell her father anything which he
might not like to hear; but her husband's behest be to she in
these, her early married day, quite imperative.
CHAPTER XXVI
the end of the Honeymoon
Mrs. Lopez have beg her father to address his reply to she at
Florence, where,--as she explain to him,--they expect to find
themselves within a fortnight from the date of her writing. they
have reach the lake about the end of November, when the weather
have still be fine, but they intend to pass the winter month
of December and January within the warmth of the city. that
intervene fortnight be to she a period of painful anticipation.
she fear to see her father's handwriting, feel almost sure that
he would be bitterly angry with she. during this time her husband
frequently speak to she about the letter,--about her own letter and
her father's expect reply. it be necessary that she should learn
her lesson, and she could only do so by have the subject of money
make familiar to her ear. it be not a part of his plan to tell she
anything of the mean by which he hop to make himself a wealthy
man. the less she know of that the well. but the fact that her
father absolutely owe to he a large amount of money as her fortune
could not be make too clear to she. he be very desirous to do this
in such a manner as not to make she think that he be accuse
her,--or that he would accuse she if the money be not forthcoming.
but she must learn the fact, and must be imbue with the conviction
that her husband would be the most ill-treat of man unless the
money be forthcoming. "I be a little nervous about it too," say
he, allude to the expected letter;--"not so much as to the money
itself, though that be important; but as to his conduct. if he
choose simply to ignore we after our marriage he will be behave
very badly." she have no answer to make to this. she could not defend
her father, because by do so she would offend her husband. and yet
her whole life-long trust in her father could not allow she to think
it possible that he should behave ill to they.
on their arrival at Florence he go at once to the post-office, but
there be as yet no letter. the fortnight, however, which have be
name have only just run itself out. they go on from day to day
inspect building, look at picture, make for themselves
a taste in marble and bronze, visit the lovely village which
cluster on the hill round the city,--doing precisely in this respect
as do all young married couple who devote a part of their honeymoon
to florence;--but in all their little journeying and in all their
work of pleasure the inky devil sit not only behind he but behind
she also. the heavy care of life be already begin to work
furrow on her face. she would already sit, knit her brow, as she
think of come trouble. would not her father certainly refuse?
and would not her husband then begin to be less loving and less
gracious to herself?
every day for a week he call at the post-office when he go out
with she, and still the letter do not come. "it can hardly be
possible," he say at last to she, "that he should decline to answer
his own daughter's letter."
"perhaps he be ill," she reply.
"if there be anything of that kind Everett would tell we."
"perhaps he have go back to Herefordshire?"
"of course his letter would go after he. I own it be very singular
to I that he should not write. it look as though he be determine
to cast you off from he altogether because you have marry against
his wish."
"not that, Ferdinand;--do not say that!"
"well; we shall see."
and on the next day they do see. he go to the post-office before
breakfast, and on this day he return with a letter in his hand.
she be sit wait for he with a book in her lap, and see the
letter at once. "be it from papa?" she say. he nod his head as
he hand it to she. "open it and read it, Ferdinand. I have get
to be so nervous about it, that I cannot do it. it seem to be so
important."
"Yes;--it be important," he say with a grim smile, and then he
open the letter. she watch his face closely as he read it, and at
first she could tell nothing from it. then, in that moment, it first
occur to she that he have a wonderful command of his feature. all
this, however, last but half a minute. then he chuck the letter,
lightly, in among the tea-cup, and come to she take she closely in
his arm and almost hurt she by the violence of his repeat kiss.
"have he write kindly?" she say, as soon as she could find she
breath to speak.
"by George, hebe a brick after all. I own I do not think it. my
darling, how much I owe you for all the trouble I have give you."
"oh, Ferdinand! if he have be good to you I shall be so happy."
"he have be awfully good. ha, ha, ha!" and then he begin walk

a pity we donot say four thousand, or five. think of his take I
just at my word. itbe a great deal well than I expect; thatbe all
I can say. and at the present moment it be of the utmost importance
to I."
all this do not take above a minute or two, but during that minute

find that his joy be true enough. the letter be very short, and
be as follows:--
MY DEAR EMILY,
what you have say under your husband's instruction about
money, I find upon consideration to be fair enough. I
think he should have speak to I before his marriage; but
then again perhaps I ought to have speak to he. as it
be, I be willing to give he the sum he require, and I
will pay L3000 to his account, if he would tell I where
he would have it lodge. then I shall think I have do
my duty by he. what I shall do with the remainder of any
money that I may have, I do not think he be entitle to
ask.
Everett be well again, and as idle as ever. your aunt Roby
be make a fool of herself at Harrogate. I have hear
nothing from Herefordshire. everything be very quiet and
lonely here.
your affectionate father,
A. WHARTON.
as he have din at the Eldon every day since his daughter have leave
he, and have play on an average a dozen rubber of whist daily, he
be not justify in complain of the loneliness of London.
the letter seem to Emily herself to be very cold, and have not her
husband rejoice over it so warmly she would have consider it to be
unsatisfactory. no doubt the l3000 would be give; but that, as far
as she could understand her father's word, be to be the whole of
her fortune. she have never know anything of her father's affair or
of his intention, but she have certainly suppose that her fortune
would be very much more than this. she have learn in some indirect

buy because of the expense. "pray donot spend your money for I,"
she say to he. "it be nice to have you give I thing, but it
would be nice to I even than that to think that I could save you

but he be not in a mood to be deny. "you donot understand," he

sort. owe to circumstance, your father's money be at this moment
of importance to me;--but he have answer to the whip and the money
be there, and that trouble be over. we can enjoy ourselves now. other
trouble will spring up, no doubt, before long."
she do not quite like be tell that her father have "answer to
the whip,"--but she be willing to believe that it be a phrase
common among man to which it would be prudish to make objection.

"I have be think of that," he say. "you can write if you like,
and of course you will. but I also will write, and have well do so
a post or two before you. as he have come round I suppose I ought to
show myself civil. what he say about the rest of his money be of

bit he will make permanent arrangement." everything in the business
