joy be due simply to this temporary success. and then he call she
father absurd to her face. for a moment she think that she would
defend her father; but she could not as yet bring herself to question
her husband's word even on such a subject as that.
he do write to Mr. Wharton, but in do so he altogether lay aside
that flighty manner which for a while have annoy she. he thoroughly
understand that the wording of the letter might be very important to
he, and he take much trouble with it. it must be now the great work
of his life to ingratiate himself with this old man, so that, at any
rate at the old man's death, he might possess at least half of the
old man's money. he must take care that there should be no division
between his wife and her father of such a nature as to make the
father think that his son ought to enjoy any special privilege of
primogeniture or of male inheritance. and if it could be so manage
that the daughter should, before the old man's death, become his
favourite child, that also would be well. he be therefore very
careful about the letter, which be as follows:--
MY DEAR MR. WHARTON,
I cannot let your letter to emily pass without thank
you myself for the very liberal response make by you to
what be of course a request from myself. let I in the
first place assure you that have you, before our marriage,
make any inquiry about my money affair, I would have tell
you everything with accuracy; but as you do not do so
I think that I should seem to intrude upon you, if I
introduce the subject. it be too long for a letter, but
whenever you may like to allude to it, you will find that
I will be quite open with you.
I be engage in business which often require the use of
a considerable amount of capital. it have so happen that
even since we be marry the immediate use of a sum of
money become essential to I to save I from sacrifice a
cargo of guano which will be of greatly increase value in
three month' time, but which otherwise must have go for
what it would now fetch. your kindness will see I through
that difficulty.
of course there be something precarious in such a
business as mine;--but I be endeavour to make it less
so from day to day, and hope very shortly to bring it
into that humdrum groove which good befit a married man.
should I ask further assistance from you in do this,
perhaps you will not refuse it if I can succeed in make
the matter clear to you. as it be I thank you sincerely
for what you have do. I will ask you to pay the L3000
you have so kindly promise, to my account at Messrs.
Hunky and Sons, Lombard Street. they be not regular
banker, but I have an account there.
we be wander about and enjoy ourselves mightily in
the properly romantic manner. Emily sometimes seem to
think that she would like to give up business, and London,
and all sublunary trouble, in order that she might settle
herself for life under an italian sky. but the idea do
not generally remain with she very long. already she be
begin to show symptom of home sickness in regard to
Manchester Square.
your always most faithfully,
ferdinand lopez.
to this letter Lopez receive no reply;--nor do he expect one.
between Emily and her father a few letter pass, not very long;
nor, as regard those from Mr. Wharton, be they very interesting.
in none of they, however, be there any mention of money. but early
in January Lopez receive a most pressing,--we might almost say an
agonising letter from his friend Parker. the gist of the letter
be to make lopez understand that Parker must at once sell certain
interest in a come cargo of guano,--at whatever sacrifice,--unless
he could be certify as to that money which must be pay in
February, and which he, Parker, must pay, should Ferdinand Lopez be
at that moment unable to meet his bond. the answer send to Parker
shall be give to the reader.
MY dear OLD AWFULLY SILLY, and ABSURDLY IMPATIENT FRIEND,
you be always like a toad under a harrow, and that
without the slight cause. I have money lie at Hunky's
more than double enough for the bill. why cannot you trust
a man? if you willnot trust I in say so, you can go to
Mills Happerton and ask he. but, remember, I shall be
very much annoyed if you do so,--and that such an inquiry
cannot but be injurious to I. if, however, you willnot
believe I, you can go and ask. at any rate, donot meddle
with the guano. we should lose over L1000 each of we, if
you be to do so. by George, a man should neither marry,
nor leave London for a day, if he have to do with a fellow
so nervous as you be. as it be I think I shall be back
a week or two before my time be properly up, lest you
and one or two other should think that I have levant
altogether.
I have no hesitation in say that more fortune be lose
in business by tremble cowardice than by any amount of
imprudence or extravagance. my hair stand on end when you
talk of part with guano in December because there be
bill which have to be meet in February. pluck up your
heart, man, and look around, and see what be do by man
with good courage.
your always,
ferdinand lopez.
these be the only communication between our married couple and
their friend at home with which I need trouble my reader. nor need
I tell any further tale of their honeymoon. if the time be not one
of complete and unalloyed joy to Emily,--and we must fear that it
be not,--it be to be remember that but very little complete and
unalloye joy be allow to sojourner in this vale of tear, even
though they have be but two month marry. in the first week in
February they appear in the Belgrave mansion, and Emily Lopez take
possession of her new home with a heart as full of love for her
husband as it have be when she walk out of the church in Vere
Street, though it may be that some of her sweetest illusion have
already be dispel.
CHAPTER XXVII
the Duke's Misery
we must go back for a while to Gatherum Castle and see the guest
whom the Duchess have collect there for her Christmas festivity.
the hospitality of the Duke's house have be maintain almost
throughout the autumn. just at the end of October they go to
Matching, for what the Duchess call a quiet month,--which, however,
at the Duke's urgent request become six week. but even here the
house be full all the time, though from deficiency of bedroom the
guest be very much less numerous. but at Matching the Duchess have
be uneasy and almost cross. Mrs. Finn have go with her husband to
Ireland, and she have teach herself to fancy that she could not live
without Mrs. Finn. and her husband have insist upon have round
he politician of his own sort, man who really prefer work to
archery, or even to hunting, and who discuss the evil of direct
taxation absolutely in the drawing-room. the Duchess be assure
that the country could not be govern by the support of such man as
these, and be very glad to get back to gatherum,--whither also come
Phineas Finn with his wife, and the St. Bungay people, and Barrington
Erle, and Mr. Monk, the Chancellor of the Exchequer, with Lord and
Lady Cantrip, and Lord and Lady Drummond,--Lord Drummond be the
only representative of the other or coalesce party. and Major
Pountney be there, have be urgent with the Duchess,--and have
fully explain to his friend Captain Gunner that he have accede to
the wish of his hostess only on the assurance of her grace that
the house would not be again trouble by the presence of Ferdinand
Lopez. such assurance be common between the two friend, but be
innocent, as, of course, neither believe the other. and Lady Rosina
be again there,--with many other. the melancholy poverty of Lady
Rosina have captivate the Duke. "she shall come and live here, if you
like," the Duchess have say in answer to a request from her husband
on his new friend's behalf,--"i've no doubt she will be willing." the
place be not crowd as it have be before; but still about thirty
guest sit down to dinner daily, and Locock, Millepois, and Mrs.
Pritchard be all keep hard at work. nor be our Duchess idle.
she be always make up the party,--meane the coalition,--doing
something to strengthen the buttress, write little letter
to little people, who, little as they be, might become big by
amalgamation. "one have always to be bind one's fagot," she say to
Mrs. Finn, have read she AEsop not altogether in vain. "where should
we have be without you?" she have whisper to Sir Orlando Drought
when that gentleman be leave Gatherum at the termination of his
second visit. she have particularly dislike Sir Orlando, and be
aware that her husband have on this occasion be hardly as gracious
as he should have be, in true policy, to so powerful a colleague.
her husband have be peculiarly shy of Sir Orlando since the day
on which they have walk together in the park,--and, consequently,
the Duchess have whisper to he. "donot bind your fagot too
conspicuously," Mrs. Finn have say to she. then the Duchess have
fall to a seat almost exhausted by labour, mingle with regret,
and by the doubt which from time to time pervade even her audacious
spirit. "Ibe not a god," she say, "or a Pitt, or an italian with a
long name begin with M., that I should be able to do these thing
without ever make a mistake. and yet they must be do. and as for
him,--he do not help I in the least. he wander about among the
cloud of the multiplication table, and think that a majority will
drop into his mouth because he do not shut it. can you tie the
fagot any well?" "I think I would leave it untie," say Mrs. Finn.
"you would not do anything of the kind. youwould be just as fussy as I
be." and thus the game be carry on at Gatherum Castle from week to
week.
"but you willnot leave he?" this be say to Phineas Finn by his wife
a day or two before Christmas, and the question be intend to ask
whether Phineas think of give up his place.
"not if I can help it."
"you like the work."
"that have but little to do with the question, unfortunately. I
certainly like have something to do. I like earn money."
"I donot know why you like that especially," say the wife, laugh.
"I do at any rate,--and, in a certain sense, I like authority. but in
serve with the Duke I find a lack of that sympathy which one should
have with one's chief. he would never say a word to I unless I speak
to he. and when I do speak, though he be studiously civil,--much
too courteous,--I know that he be bored. he have nothing to say to I
about the country. when he have anything to communicate, he prefer to
write a minute for Warburton, who then write to Morton,--and so it
reach I."
"donot it do as well?"
"it may do with I. there be reason which bind I to he, which
will not bind other man. man donot talk to I about it, because
they know that I be bind to he through you. but I be aware of the
feeling which exist. you cannot be really loyal to a king if you
never see him,--if he be always lock up in some almost divine
recess."
"a king may make himself too common, Phineas."
"no doubt. a king have to know where to draw the line. but the Duke
draw no intentional line at all. he be not by nature gregarious or
communicative, and be therefore hardly fit to be the head of a
ministry."
"it will break her heart if anything go wrong."
"she ought to remember that Ministries seldom live very long," say
Phineas. "but shewill recover even if she do break her heart. she be
too full of vitality to be much repress by any calamity. have you
hear what be to be do about Silverbridge?"
"the Duchess want to get it for this man, Ferdinand Lopez."
"but it have not be promise yet?"
"the seat be not vacant," say Mrs. Finn, "and I donot know when it
will be vacant. I think there be a hitch about it,--and I think the
Duchess be go to be make very angry."
throughout the autumn the Duke have be an unhappy man. while the
absolute work of the Session have last he have find something
to console he; but now, though he be surround by private
secretary, and though dispatch-box go and come twice a day,
though there be dozen of letter as to which he have to give some
instruction,--yet, there be in truth nothing for he to do. it
seem to he that all the real work of the Government have be
filch from he by his colleague, and that he be stick up in
pretend authority,--a kind of wooden Prime Minister, from whom no
real ministration be demand. his first fear have be that he be
himself unfit;--but now he be uneasy, fear that other think
he to be unfit. there be Mr. Monk with his budget, and Lord
Drummond with his three or four dozen half rebellious colony, and
Sir Orlando Drought with the House to lead and a ship to build, and
Phineas Finn with his scheme of municipal Home Rule for Ireland, and
Lord Ramsden with a codify Statute book,--all full of work, all
with something special to be do. but for him,--he have to arrange
who should attend the Queen, what ribbon should be give away, and
what middle-aged young man should move the address. he sigh as he
think of those happy day in which he use to fear that his mind
and body would both give way under the pressure of decimal coinage.
but Phineas Finn have read the Duke's character rightly in say that
he be neither gregarious nor communicative, and therefore but little
fit to rule Englishmen. he have think that it be so himself,
and now from day to day he be become more assured of his own
deficiency. he could not throw himself into cordial relation with
the Sir Orlando Droughts, or even with the Mr. Monks. but, though he
have never wish to be put into his present high office, now that
he be there he dread the sense of failure which would follow his
descent from it. it be this feeling rather than genuine ambition,
rather than the love of power or patronage or pay, which induce man
to cling to place. the absence of real work, and the quantity of mock
work, both alike make the life wearisome to he; but he could not
endure the idea that it should be write in history that he have
allow himself to be make a faineant Prime Minister, and then have
fail even in that. history would forget what he have do as a
work Minister in record the feebleness of the Ministry which
would bear his name.
the one man with whom he could talk freely, and from whom he could
take advice, be now with he, here at his Castle. he be shy at
first even with the Duke of St. Bungay, but that shyness he could
generally overcome, after a few word. but though he be always sure
of his old friend's sympathy and of his old friend's wisdom, yet he
doubt his old friend's capacity to understand himself. the young
Duke feel the old Duke to be thick-skinned than himself and
therefore unable to appreciate the thorn which so sorely worry his
own flesh. "they talk to I about a policy," say the host. they be
closet at this time in the Prime Minister's own sanctum, and there
yet remain an hour before they need dress for dinner.
"who talk about a policy?"
"Sir Orlando Drought especially." for the Duke of Omnium have never
forget the arrogance of that advice give in the park.
"Sir Orlando be of course entitle to speak, though I do not know
that he be likely to say anything very well worth the hearing. what
be his special policy?"
"if he have any, of course, I would hear he. it be not that he want
any special thing to be do, but he think that I should get up some
special thing in order that Parliament may be satisfy."
"if you want to create a majority that might be true. just listen
to he and have do with it."
"I cannot go on in that way. I cannot submit to what amount to
complaint from the gentleman who be act with I. nor would they
submit long to my silence. I be begin to feel that I have be
wrong."
"I donot think you have be wrong at all."
"a man be wrong if he attempt to carry a weight too great for his
strength."
"a certain nervous sensitiveness, from which you should free yourself
as from a disease, be your only source of weakness. think about your
business as a shoemaker think of his. do your good, and then let
your customer judge for themselves. caveat emptor. a man should
never endeavour to price himself, but should accept the price which
other put on him,--only be careful that he should learn what that
price be. your policy should be to keep your government together by a
strong majority. after all, the making of new law be too often but
an unfortunate necessity lay on we by the impatience of the people.
a lengthened period of quiet and therefore good government with a
minimum of new law would be the great benefit the country could
receive. when I recommend you to comply with the Queen's behest I
do so because I think that you might inaugurate such a period more
certainly than any other one man." this old Duke be quite content
with a state of thing such as he describe. he have be a Cabinet
Minister for more than half his life. he like be a Cabinet
Minister. he think it well for the country generally that his party
should be in power,--and if not his party in its entirety, then as
much of his party as might be possible. he do not expect to be
write of as a Pitt or a Somers, but he think that memoir would
speak of he as a useful nobleman,--and he be content. he be
not only not ambitious himself, but the effervescence and general
turbulence of ambition in other man be distasteful to he. loyalty
be second nature to he, and the power of submit to defeat
without either shame or sorrow have become perfect with he by
long practice. he would have make his brother Duke such as he be
himself,--had not his brother Duke be so lamentably thin-skinned.
"I suppose we must try it for another session?" say the Duke of
Omnium with a lachrymose voice.
"of course we must,--and for other after that, I both hope and
trust," say the Duke of St. Bungay, get up. "if I donot go
up-stair I shall be late, and then her grace will look at I with
unforgive eye."
on the follow day after lunch the Prime Minister take a walk with
Lady Rosina De Courcy. he have fall into a habit of walk with
Lady Rosina almost every day of his life, till the people in the
Castle begin to believe that Lady Rosina be the mistress of some
deep policy of her own. for there be many there who do in truth
think that statecraft could never be absent from a minister's mind,
day or night. but in truth Lady Rosina chiefly make herself agreeable
to the Prime Minister by never make any most distant allusion
to public affair. it might be doubt whether she even know that
the man who pay she so much honour be the Head of the british
Government as well as the Duke of Omnium. she be a tall, thin,
shrivel-up old woman,--not very old, fifty perhaps, but look
at least ten year more,--very melancholy, and sometimes very cross.
she have be notably religious, but that be gradually wear off as
she advance in year. the rigid strictness of sabbatarian practice
require the full energy of middle life. she have be leave entirely
alone in the world, with a very small income, and not many friend
who be in any way interested in her existence. but she know herself
to be Lady Rosina De Courcy, and feel that the possession of that
name ought to be more to she than money and friend, or even than
brother and sister. "the weather be not frightening you," say the
Duke. Snow have fall, and the path, even where they have be sweep,
be wet and sloppy.
"weather never frighten I, your grace. I always have thick
boots;--i be very particular about that;--and cork sol."
"cork sol be admirable."
"I think I owe my life to cork sol," say Lady Rosina
enthusiastically. "there be a man name Sprout in Silverbridge who
make they. do your grace ever try he for boot?"
"I donot think I ever do," say the Prime Minister.
"then you have well. hebe very good and very cheap too. those London
tradesman never think they can charge you enough. I find I can wear
Sprout's boot the whole winter through and then have they resole. I
donot suppose you ever think of such thing?"
"I like to have my foot dry."
"I have get to calculate what they cost." they then pass Major
Pountney, who be come and go between the stable and the house,
and who take off his hat and who salute the host and his companion
with perhaps more flowing courtesy than be necessary. "I never have
find out what that gentleman's name be yet," say Lady Rosina.
"Pountney, I think. I believe they call he Major Pountney."
"oh, Pountney! there be Pountneys in Leicestershire. perhaps he be
one of they?"
"I donot know where he come from," say the Duke,--"nor, to tell
the truth, where he go to." Lady Rosina look up at he with an
interested air. "he seem to be one of those idle man who get into
people's house heaven know why, and never do anything."
"I suppose you ask he?" say Lady Rosina.
"the Duchess do, I dare say."
"how odd it would be if she be to suppose that you have ask he."
"the Duchess, no doubt, know all about it." then there be a little
pause. "she be oblige to have all sort of people," say the Duke
apologetically.
"I suppose so,--when you have so many come and go. I be sorry
to say that my time be up to-morrow, so that I shall make way for
somebody else."
"I hope you willnot think of go, Lady rosina,--unless you be
engage elsewhere. we be delighted to have you."
"the Duchess have be very kind, but--"
"the Duchess, I fear, be almost too much engaged to see as much of
her guest individually as she ought to do. to I your be here be
a great pleasure."
"you be too good to me,--much too good. but I shall have stay
out my time, and I think, Duke, I will go to-morrow. I be very
methodical, you know, and always act by rule. I have walk my two
mile now, and I will go in. if you do want boot with cork sol
mind you go to Sprout's. dear I; there be that Major Pountney again.
that be four time he have be up and down that path since we have
be walk here."
Lady Rosina go in, and the Duke turn back, think of his friend
and perhaps think of the cork sol of which she have to be so
careful and which be so important to her comfort. it could not be
that he fancy Lady Rosina to be clever, nor can we imagine that her
conversation satisfy any of those want to which he and all of we
be subject. but nevertheless he like Lady Rosina, and be never
bore by she. she be natural, and she want nothing from he. when
she talk about cork sol she mean cork sol. and then she do
not tread on any of his numerous corn. as he walk on he determine
that he would induce his wife to persuade Lady Rosina to stay a
little long at the Castle. in meditate upon this he make another
turn in the ground, and again come upon Major Pountney as that
gentleman be return from the stable. "a very cold afternoon," he
say, feel it to be ungracious to pass one of his own guest in
his own ground without a word of salutation.
"very cold indeed, your grace,--very cold." the Duke have intend
to pass on, but the Major manage to stop he by stand in the
pathway. the Major do not in the least know his man. he have hear
that the Duke be shy, and therefore think that he be timid. he
have not hitherto be speak to by the Duke,--a condition of thing
which he attribute to the Duke's shyness and timidity. but, with
much thought on the subject, he have resolve that he would have a few
word with his host, and have therefore pass backwards and forwards
between the house and the stable rather frequently. "very cold,
indeed, but yet wehave have beautiful weather. I donot know when I have
enjoy myself so much altogether as I have at Gatherum Castle."
the Duke bow, and make a little but a vain effort to get on. "a
splendid pile!" say the Major, stretch his hand gracefully
towards the building.
"it be a big house," say the Duke.
"a noble mansion;--perhap the noble mansion in the three
kingdom," say Major Pountney. "I have see a great many of the
good country residence in England, but nothing that at all equal
Gatherum." then the Duke make a little effort at progression, but
be still stop by the daring Major. "by-the-by, your grace, if
your grace have a few minute to spare,--just half a minute,--I wish
you would allow I to say something." the Duke assume a look of
disturbance, but he bow and walk on, allow the Major to walk
by his side. "I have the great possible desire, my Lord Duke, to
enter public life."
"I think you be already in the army," say the Duke.
"so I am;--wa on Sir Bartholomew Bone's staff in Canada for two
year, and have see as much of what I call home service as any man
go. one of my chief object be to take up the army."
"it seem that you have take it up."
"I mean in Parliament, your grace. I be very fairly off as regard
private mean, and would stand all the racket of the expense of a
contest myself,--if there be one. but the difficulty be to get a
seat, and, of course, if it can be privately manage, it be very
comfortable." the Duke look at he again,--this time without
bow. but the Major, who be not observant, rush on to his
destruction. "we all know that Silverbridge will soon be vacant. let
I assure your grace that if it might be consistent with your grace's
plan in other respect to turn your kind countenance towards I,
you would find that you would have a supporter than whom none would
be more staunch, and perhaps I may say, one who in the House would
not be the least useful!" that portion of the Major's speech which
refer to the Duke's kind countenance have be learn by heart,
and be throw trippingly off the tongue with a kind of twang. the
Major have perceive that he have not be at once interrupted when
he begin to open the budget of his political aspiration, and have
allow himself to indulge in please augury. "nothing ask and
nothing have," have be adopt as the motto of his life, and
more than once he have express to Captain Gunner his conviction
that,--"by George, if youhave only cheek enough, there be nothing you
cannot get." on this emergency the Major certainly be not deficient
in cheek. "if I might be allow to consider myself your grace's
candidate, I should indeed be a happy man," say the Major.
"I think, sir," say the Duke, "that your proposition be the most
unbecoming and the most impertinent that ever be address to I."
the Major's mouth fall, and he star with all his eye as he look
up into the Duke's face. "good afternoon," say the Duke, turn
quickly round and walk away. the Major stand for a while
transfix to the place, and, cold as be the weather, be bath in
perspiration. a keen sense of have "put his foot into it" almost
crush he for a time. then he assure himself that, after all, the
Duke "could not eat he," and with that consolatory reflection he
creep back to the house and up to his own room.
to put the man down have of course be an easy task to the Duke, but
he be not satisfied with that. to the Major it seem that the Duke
have pass on with easy indifference;--but in truth he be very far
from be easy. the man's insolent request have wound he at many
point. it be grievous to he that he should have as a guest in his
own house a man whom he have be force to insult. it be grievous
to he that he himself should not have be hold in personal respect
high enough to protect he from such an insult. it be grievous to
he that he should be openly addressed,--addresse by an absolute
stranger,--as a borough-monger lord, who would not scruple to give
away a seat in Parliament as seat be give away in former day.
and it be especially grievous to he that all these misfortune
should have come upon he as a part of the result of his wife's
manner of exercise his hospitality. if this be to be Prime
Minister he certainly would not be Prime Minister much long!
have any aspirant to political life ever dare so to address Lord
Brock, or Lord De Terrier, or Mr. Mildmay, the old Premiers whom he
remember? he think not. they have manage differently. they have
be able to defend themselves from such attack by personal dignity.
and would it have be possible that any man should have dare so to
speak to his uncle, the late Duke? he think not. as he shut himself
up in his own room he grieve inwardly with a deep grief. after a
while he walk off to his wife's room, still perturb in spirit.
the perturbation have indeed increase from minute to minute. he would
rather give up politic altogether and shut himself up in absolute
seclusion than find himself subject to the insolence of any Pountney
that might address he. with his wife he find Mrs. Finn. now for
this lady personally he entertain what for he be a warm regard.
in various matter of much importance he and she have be bring
together, and she have, to his thinking, invariably behave well. and
an intimacy have be establish which have enable he to be at ease
with her,--so that her presence be often a comfort to he. but at
the present moment he have not wish to find any one with his wife,
and feel that she be in his way. "perhaps I be disturb you," he
say in a tone of voice that be solemn and almost funereal.
"not at all," say the Duchess, who be in high spirit. "I want to
get your promise now about Silverbridge. donot mind she. of course
she know everything." to be tell that any body know everything be
another shock to he. "I have just get a letter from Mr. Lopez."
could it be right that his wife should be correspond on such a
subject with a person so little know as this Mr. Lopez? "may I tell
he that he shall have your interest when the seat be vacant?"
"certainly not," say the Duke, with a scowl that be terrible even
to his wife. "I wish to speak to you, but I wish to speak to you
alone."
"I beg a thousand pardon," say Mrs. Finn, prepare to go.
"donot stir, Marie," say the Duchess; "he be go to be cross."
"if Mrs. Finn will allow I, with every feeling of the most perfect
respect and sincere regard, to ask she to leave I with you for
a few minute, I shall be oblige. and if, with her usual hearty
kindness, she will pardon my abruptness--" then he could not go on,
his emotion be too great; but he put out his hand, and take her
raise it to his lip and kiss it. the moment have become too solemn
for any further hesitation as to the lady's go. the Duchess for a
moment be strike dumb, and Mrs. Finn, of course, leave the room.
"in the name of heaven, Plantagenet, what be the matter?"
"who be Major Pountney?"
"who be Major Pountney! how on earth should I know? he be--Major
Pountney. he be about everywhere."
"do not let he be ask into any house of mine again. but that be a
trifle."
"anything about Major Pountney must, I should think, be a trifle.
have tiding come that the heavens be go to fall? nothing short
of that could make you so solemn."
"in the first place, Glencora, let I ask you not to speak to I
again about the seat for Silverbridge. I be not at present prepare
to argue the matter with you, but I have resolve that I will know
nothing about the election. as soon as the seat be vacant, if it
should be vacate, I shall take care that my determination be know
in Silverbridge."
"why should you abandon your privilege in that way? it be sheer
weakness."
"the interference of any peer be unconstitutional."
"there be Braxon," say the Duchess energetically, "where the Marquis
of Crumber return the member regularly, in spite of all their reform
bill; and Bamford, and cobblersborough;--and look at Lord Lumley
with a whole county in his pocket, not to speak of two borough! what
nonsense, Plantagenet! anything be constitutional, or anything be
unconstitutional, just as you choose to look at it." it be clear
that the Duchess have really study the subject carefully.
"very well, my dear, let it be nonsense. I only beg to assure you
that it be my intention, and I request you to act accordingly. and
there be another thing I have to say to you. I shall be sorry to
interfere in any way with the pleasure which you may derive from
society, but as long as I be burden with the office which have be
impose upon I, I will not again entertain any guest in my own
house."
"Plantagenet!"
"you cannot turn the people out who be here now; but I beg that they
may be allow to go as the time come, and that their place may not
be fill by further invitation."
"but further invitation have go out ever so long ago, and have
be accept. you must be ill, my dear."
"Ill at ease,--ye. at any rate let none other be send out." then
he remember a kindly purpose which he have form early in the day,
and fall back upon that. "I should, however, be glad if you would
ask Lady Rosina De Courcy to remain here." the Duchess star at he,
really think now that something be amiss with he. "the whole
thing be a failure and I will have no more of it. it be degrade
I." then without allow she a moment in which to answer he, he
march back to his own room.
but even here his spirit be not as yet at rest. that Major must
not go unpunished. though he hat all fuss and noise he must do
something. so he write as follow to the major:--
the Duke of Omnium trust that Major Pountney will not
find it inconvenient to leave Gatherum Castle shortly.
should Major Pountney wish to remain at the Castle over
the night, the Duke of Omnium hop that he will not
object to be serve with his dinner and with his breakfast
in his own room. a carriage and horse will be ready for
Major Pountney's use, to take he to Silverbridge, as soon
as Major Pountney may express to the servant his wish to
that effect.
Gatherum Castle, -- December, 18--.
this note the Duke send by the hand of his own servant, have say
enough to the man as to the carriage and the possible dinner in the
Major's bedroom, to make the man understand almost exactly what have
occur. a note from the Major be bring to the Duke while he be
dress. the Duke have glance at the note throw it into the fire;
and the Major that evening eat his dinner at the Palliser Arms Inn at
Silverbridge.
CHAPTER XXVIII
the Duchess be much troubled
it be hardly possible that one man should turn another out of his
house without many people know it; and when the one person be a
Prime Minister and the other such a Major as Major Pountney, the
affair be apt to be talk about very widely. the Duke of course
never open his mouth on the subject, except in answer to question
from the Duchess; but all the servant know it. "Pritchard tell I
that you have send that wretched man out of the house with a flea in
his ear," say the Duchess.
"I send he out of the house, certainly."
"he be hardly worth your anger."
"he be not at all worth my anger;--but I could not sit down to dinner
with a man who have insult I."
"what do he say, Plantagenet? I know it be something about
Silverbridge." to this question the Duke give no answer, but in
respect to Silverbridge he be stern as adamant. two day after the
departure of the Major it be know to Silverbridge generally that in
the event of there be an election the Duke's agent would not as
usual suggest a nominee. there be a paragraph on the subject in the
County paper, and another in the London "Evening Pulpit." the Duke of
omnium,--that he might show his respect to the law, not only as to
the letter of the law, but as to the spirit also,--had make it know
to his tenantry in and round Silverbridge generally that he would
in no way influence their choice of a candidate in the event of
an election. but these newspaper do not say a word about Major
Pountney.
the club of course know all about it, and no man at any club ever
know more than Captain Gunner. soon after Christmas he meet his friend
the Major on the step of the new military club, the Active Service,
which be declare by many man in the army to have leave all the other
military club "absolutely nowhere." "Halloa, Punt!" he say, "you
seem to have make a mess of it at last down at the Duchess's."
"I wonder what you know about it."
"you have to come away pretty quick, I take it."
"of course I come away pretty quick." so much as that the Major be
aware must be know. there be detail which he could deny safely,
as it would be impossible that they should be support by evidence,
but there be matter which must be admit. "Iwill bet a fiver that
beyond that you know nothing about it."
"the Duke order you off, I take it."
"after a fashion he do. there be circumstance in which a man
cannot help himself." this be diplomatical, because it leave the
Captain to suppose that the Duke be the man who could not help
himself.
"of course I be not there," say Gunner, "and I cannot absolutely
know, but I suppose you have be interfere with the Duchess about
Silverbridge. Glencora will bear a great deal,--but since she have
take up politic, by George, you have well not touch she there." at
last it come to be believe that the Major have be turn out by the
order of the Duchess, because he have venture to put himself forward
as an opponent to Ferdinand Lopez, and the Major feel himself really
grateful to his friend the Captain for this arrangement of the
story. and there come at last to be mix up with the story some
half-understand innuendo that the Major's jealousy against Lopez
have be of a double nature,--in reference both to the Duchess and
the borough,--so that he escape from much of that disgrace which
naturally attach itself to a man who have be kick out of another
man's house. there be a mystery;--and when there be a mystery a man
should never be condemn. where there be a woman in the case a man
cannot be expect to tell the truth. as for call out or in any
way punish the Prime Minister, that of course be out of the
question. and so it go on till at last the Major be almost proud
of what he have do, and talk about it willingly with mysterious
hint, in which practice make he perfect.
but with the Duchess the affair be very serious, so much so that
she be drive to call in advice,--not only from her constant friend,
Mrs. Finn, but afterwards from Barrington Erle, from Phineas Finn,
and lastly even from the Duke of St. Bungay, to whom she be hardly
willing to subject herself, the Duke be the special friend of
her husband. but the matter become so important to she that she be
unable to trifle with it. at Gatherum the expulsion of Major Pountney
soon become a forget affair. when the Duchess learn the truth
she quite approve of the expulsion, only hint to Barrington
Erle that the act of kick out should have be more absolutely
practical. and the loss of Silverbridge, though it hurt she sorely,
could be endure. she must write to her friend Ferdinand Lopez, when
the time should come, excuse herself as well she might, and must
lose the exquisite delight of make a Member of Parliament out of
her own hand. the newspaper, however, have take that matter up in
the proper spirit, and political capital might to some extent be
make of it. the loss of Silverbridge, though it bruise, break
no bone. but the Duke have again express himself with unusual
sternness respect her ducal hospitality, and have reiterate the
declaration of his intention to live out the remainder of his period
of office in republican simplicity. "we have try it and it have
fail, and let there be an end of it," he say to she. simple and
direct disobedience to such an order be as little in her way as
simple or direct obedience. she know her husband well, and know how
he could be manage and how he could not be manage. when he declare
that there should be an "end of it,"--meane an end of the very
system by which she hop to perpetuate his power,--she do not dare
to argue with he. and yet he be so wrong! the trial have be no
failure. the thing have be do and well do, and have succeed.
be failure to be presume because one impertinent puppy have find
his way into the house? and then to abandon the system at once,
whether it have fail or whether it have succeed, would be to call
the attention of all the world to an acknowledge failure,--to a
failure so disreputable that its acknowledgment must lead to the
loss of everything! it be know now,--so argue the Duchess to
herself,--that she have devote herself to the work of cementing and
consolidate the Coalition by the graceful hospitality which the
wealth of herself and her husband enable she to dispense. she have
make herself a Prime Ministress by the manner in which she open
her saloon, her banqueting hall, and her garden. it have never
be do before, and now it have be well do. there have be no
failure. and yet everything be to be break down because his nerve
have receive a shock!
"let it die out," Mrs. Finn have say. "the people will come here and
will go away, and then, when you be up in London, you will soon fall
into your old way." but this do not suit the new ambition of the
Duchess. she have so feed her mind with dare hope that she could not
bear that it should "die out." she have arrange a course of thing in
her own mind by which she should come to be know as the great Prime
Minister's wife; and she have, perhaps unconsciously, apply the
epithet more to herself than to her husband. she, too, wish to be
write of in memoir, and to make a niche for herself in history.
and now she be tell that she be to let it "die out!"
"I suppose he be a little bilious," Barrington Erle have say. "donot
you think hewill forget all about it when he get up to London?" the
Duchess be sure that her husband would not forget anything. he never
do forget anything. "I want he to be tell," say the Duchess, "that
everybody think that he be do very well. I donot mean about
politic exactly, but as to keep the party together. donot you
think that we have succeed?" Barrington Erle think that upon the
whole they have succeed; but suggest at the same time that there
be seed of weakness. "Sir Orlando and Sir Timothy Beeswax be not
sound, you know," say Barrington Erle. "he cannot make they sounder
by shut himself up like a hermit," say the Duchess. Barrington
Erle, who have peculiar privilege of his own, promise that if he
could by any mean make an occasion, he would let the Duke know that
their side of the Coalition be more than content with the way in
which he do his work.
"you donot think wehave make a mess of it?" she say to Phineas,
ask he a question. "I donot think that the Duke have make a mess
of it,--or you," say Phineas, who have come to love the duchess
because his wife love she. "but it willnot go on for ever, Duchess."
"you know what Ihave do," say the Duchess, who take it for grant
that Mr. Finn know all that his wife know. "have it answer?" Phineas
be silent for a moment. "of course you will tell I the truth. you
willnot be so bad as to flatter I now that I be so much in earnest."
"I almost think," say Phineas, "that the time have go by for what
one may call drawing-room influence. they use to be very great.
Old Lord Brock use they extensively, though by no mean as your
Grace have do. but the spirit of the world have change since then."
"the spirit of the world never change," say the Duchess, in her
soreness.
but her strong dependence be on the old Duke. the party at the
Castle be almost break up when she consult he. she have be so
far true to her husband as not to ask another guest to the house
since his command;--but they who have be ask before come and go
as have be arrange. then, when the place be nearly empty, and when
Locock and Millepois and Pritchard be wonder among themselves at
this general collapse, she ask her husband's leave to invite their
old friend again for a day or two. "I do so want to see he, and I
think hewill come," say the Duchess. the Duke give his permission
with a ready smile,--not because the propose visitor be his own
confidential friend, but because it suit his spirit to grant such
a request as to any one after the order that he have give. have she
name Major Pountney, I think he would have smile and accede.
the Duke come, and to he she pour out her whole soul. "it have
be for he and for his honour that I have do it;--that man and
woman might know how really gracious he be, and how good. of course,
there have be money spend, but he can afford it without hurt
the child. it have be so necessary that with a Coalition people
should know each other! there be some little absurd row here. a man
who be a mere nobody, one of the travel butterfly man that fill
up space and talk to girl, get hold of he and be impertinent. he
be so thin-skinned that he could not shake the creature into the dust
as you would have do. it annoy him,--that, and, I think, see
so many strange faces,--so that he come to I and declare, that as
long as he remain in office he would not have another person in the
house, either here or in London. he mean it literally, and he mean
I to understand it literally. I have to get special leave before I
could ask so dear an old friend as your grace."
"I donot think he would object to I," say the Duke, laugh.
"of course not. he be only too glad to think you would come. but
he take the request as be quite the proper thing. it will kill I
if this be to be carry out. after all that I have do, I could
show myself nowhere. and it will be so injurious to he! could not
you tell he, Duke? no one else in the world can tell he but you.
nothing unfair have be attempt. no job have be do. I have
endeavour to make his house pleasant to people, in order that they
might look upon he with grace and favour. be that wrong? be that
unbecome a wife?"
the old Duke pat she on the head as though she be a little girl,
and be more comforting to she than her other counsellor. he would
say nothing to her husband now;--but they must both be up in London
at the meeting of Parliament, and then he would tell his friend that,
in his opinion, no sudden change should be make. "this husband of
yours be a very peculiar man," he say, smile. "his honesty be not
like the honesty of other man. it be more downright;--more absolutely
honest; less capable of bear even the shadow which the stain from
another's dishonesty might throw upon it. give he credit for all
that, and remember that you cannot find everything combine in the
same person. he be very practical in some thing, but the question
be, whether he be not too scrupulous to be practical in all thing."
at the close of the interview the Duchess kiss he and promise to
be guide by he. the occurrence of the last few week have soften
the Duchess much.
CHAPTER XXIX
the two Candidates for Silverbridge
on his arrival in London Ferdinand Lopez find a letter wait for
he from the Duchess. this come into his hand immediately on his
reach the room in Belgrave Mansions, and be of course the first
object of his care. "that contain my fate," he say to his wife,
put his hand down upon the letter. he have talk to her much of
the chance that have come in his way, and have show himself to be very
ambitious of the honour offer to he. she of course have sympathise
with he, and be willing to think all good thing both of the

into Parliament. he pause a moment, still hold the letter under
his hand. "you would hardly think that I should be such a coward that
I donot like to open it," he say.
"youhave get to do it."
"unless I make you do it for I," he say, hold out the letter to
she. "you will have to learn how weak I be. when I be really anxious
I become like a child."
"I do not think you be ever weak," she say, caress he. "if
there be a thing to be do you would do it at once. but Iwill open
it if you like." then he tear off the envelope with an air of comic
importance and stand for a few minute while he read it.
"what I first perceive be that there have be a row about it," he
say.
"a row about it! what sort of a row?"
"my dear friend the Duchess have not quite hit it off with my less
dear friend the Duke."
"she do not say so?"
"oh dear, no! my friend the Duchess be much too discreet for
that;--but I can see that it have be so."
"be you to be the new member? if that be arrange I donot care a bit
about the Duke and Duchess."
"these thing do not settle themselves quite so easily as that. I be
not to have the seat at any rate without fight for it. therebe the
letter."
the Duchess's letter to her new adherent shall be give, but it must

by the Prime Minister to his wife concern the borough. she of

month of January be pass in comparative quiet at the Castle, and

would be open. the Duke would not even make a suggestion, and would

not make a Member of Parliament altogether out of her own hand,

something. she would in nothing disobey her husband, but if Mr.
Lopez be to stand for Silverbridge, it could not but be know in
the borough that Mr. Lopez be her friend. therefore she write the
follow letter:--
Gatherum, -- January, 18--.
MY DEAR MR. LOPEZ,
I remember that you say that you would be home at this
time, and therefore I write to you about the borough.
thing be change since you go away, and, I fear, not
change for your advantage.
we understand that Mr. Grey will apply for the Chiltern
hundred at the end of March, and that the election will
take place in April. no candidate will appear as favour
from hence. we use to run a favourite, and our favourite
would sometimes win,--would sometimes even have a walk
over; but those good time be go. all the good time
be go, I think. there be no reason that I know why you
should not stand as well as any one else. you can be early
in the field;--because it be only now know that there
will be no Gatherum interest. and I fancy it have already
leak out that you would have be the favourite if there
have be a favourite;--which might be beneficial.
I need hardly say that I do not wish my name to be
mention in the matter.
sincerely your,
GLENCORA OMNIUM.
Sprugeon, the ironmonger, would, I do not doubt, be proud
to nominate you.
"I donot understand much about it," say Emily.
"I dare say not. it be not mean that any novice should understand
much about it. of course you will not mention her grace's letter."
"certainly not."
"she intend to do the very good she can for I. I have no doubt that

"he be an old conservative."

but now she begin to perceive that her father be to be regard as

it be not long before he make the experiment. he have call himself

after dinner Mr. Wharton and Lopez be leave together, as the old

"yes, sir. we all get bit soon or later, I suppose."

there can be no doubt that in my profession a seat in the House would

"it may be so. I donot know anything about it."

"that depend on how you get it, and how you use it;--very much also

"I mean with money, sir."
"to pay the election bill! no; certainly not. why should I?"

"a luxury!"

man go into Parliament because it give they fashion, position, and

ass Everett be quarrel with I at this moment because I willnot

this have be very plain speak, and by no mean comfortable to

the next day he see Everett Wharton, who welcome he back with warm

"nonsense!"

Lopez do dispatch an agent down to Mr. Sprugeon at Silverbridge,
and the agent find that Mr. Sprugeon be a very discreet man. Mr.
Sprugeon at first know little or nothing,--seeme hardly to be aware
that there be a member of Parliament for Silverbridge, and declare

do not live quite a hundred mile away. the instruction give by
Lopez to his agent be of the most cautious kind. the agent be

client do not intend to stand unless he could see the way to almost
certain success with very little outlay. but the agent, perhaps

the Duke's people will know." then Mr. Sprout tell the agent that

he give Lopez to understand that he must certainly put himself
forward. the borough expect he. Sprugeon and Sprout consider

be predominant. the Duke's name have never be mention at

use to play. the agent be quite sure that no one could get in
for Silverbridge without have the Castle on his side. no doubt

though duke be still duke, and could still exercise ducal

accurate answer to such a question would be impossible, but probably
about l600. it might be L800;--could not possibly be above L1000.
Lopez wince as he hear these sum name, but he do not decline the
contest.
then the name of the opposition candidate be whisper to Lopez. it

CHAPTER XXX
"yes;--a lie!"
"so you go to Happerton after all," say Lopez to his ally, Mr.
Sextus Parker. "you couldnot believe I when I tell you the money be
all right! what a cur you be!"
"thatbe right;--abuse I."
"well, it be horrid. donot I tell you that it must necessarily
injure I with the house? how be two fellow to get on together
unless they can put some trust in each other? even if I do run you
into a difficulty, do you really think Ibe ruffian enough to tell you
that the money be there if it be untrue?"
Sexty look like a cur and feel like a cur, as he be be thus
abuse. he be not angry with his friend for call he bad name,
but only anxious to excuse himself. "I be out of sort," he say,
"and so d----d hippish I donot know what I be about."
"brandy-and-soda!" suggest Lopez.
"perhaps a little of that;--though, by Jove, it benot often I do that
kind of thing. I donot know a fellow who work hard for his wife
and child than I do. but when one see such thing all round
one,--a fellow utterly smash here who have a string of hunter
yesterday, and another fellow buy a house in Piccadilly and
pull it down because it benot big enough, who be content with a
little box at Hornsey last summer, one donot quite know how to keep
one's leg."
"if you want to learn a lesson look at the two man, and see where the
difference lie. the one have have some heart about he, and the other
have be a coward."
Parker scratch his head, balance himself on the hind leg of
his stool, and tacitly acknowledge the truth of all that his
enterprising friend say to he. "have old Wharton come down well?" at
last he ask.
"I have never say a word to old Wharton about money," lopez
replied,--"except as to the cost of this election I be tell you
of."
"and he wouldnot do anything in that?"
"he donot approve of the thing itself. I donot doubt but that the
old gentleman and I shall understand each other before long."
"youhave get the length of his foot."
"but I donot mean to drive he. I can get along without that. hebe an
old man, and he cannot take his money along with he when he go the
great journey."
"therebe a brother, lopez,--isn't there?"
"yes,--therebe a brother; but Wharton have enough for two; and if he
be to put either out of his will it wouldnot be my wife. old man
donot like part with their money, and hebe like other old man.
if it be not so I shouldnot bother myself come into the city at
all."
"have he enough for that, lopez?"
"I suppose hebe worth a quarter of a million."
"by Jove! and where do he get it?"
"Perseverance, sir. Put by a shilling a day, and let it have its
natural increase, and see what it will come to at the end of fifty
year. I suppose old Wharton have be put by two or three
thousand out of his professional income, at any rate for the last
thirty year, and never for a moment forget its natural increase.
thatbe one way to make a fortune."
"it benot rapid enough for you and I, lopez."
"no. that be the old-fashioned way, and the most sure. but, as
you say, it be not rapid enough; and it rob a man of the power of
enjoy his money when he have make it. but itbe a very good thing
to be closely connect with a man who have already do that kind of
thing. therebe no doubt about the money when it be there. it do not
take to itself wing and fly away."
"but the man who have it stick to it uncommon hard."
"of course he does;--but he cannot take it away with he."
"he can leave it to hospital, Lopez. thatbe the devil!"
"Sexty, my boy, I see you have take an outlook into human life which
do you credit. yes, he can leave it to hospital. but why do he
leave it to hospital?"
"something of be afraid about his soul, I suppose."
"no; I donot believe in that. such a man as this, who have be
hard-fiste all his life, and who have have his eye thoroughly open,
who have make his own money in the sharp intercourse of man to man,
and who keep it to the last gasp,--he donot believe that hewill do
his soul any good by give it to hospital when he cannot keep it
himself any long. his mind have free itself from those cobwebs long
since. he give his money to hospital because the last pleasure of
which he be capable be that of spit his relation. and it be a
great pleasure to an old man, when his relation have be disgust
with he for be old and love his money. I rather think I should
do it myself."
"Iwould give myself a chance of go to heaven, I think," say Parker.
"donot you know that man will rob and cheat on their death-bed, and
say their prayer all the time? Old Wharton willnot leave his money to
hospital if hebe well handle by those about he."
"and youwill handle he well;--eh, lopez?"
"I willnot quarrel with he, or tell he that hebe a curmudgeon because
he donot do all that I want he. hebe over seventy, and he cannot
carry his money with he."
all this leave so vivid an impression of the wisdom of his friend on
the mind of Sextus Parker, that in spite of the harrowing fear by
which he have be torment on more than one occasion already, he
allow himself to be persuade into certain fiscal arrangement, by
which Lopez would find himself put at ease with reference to money at
any rate for the next four month. he have at once tell himself that
this election would cost he L1000. when various sum be mention
in reference to such an affair, safety could alone be find in take
the outside sum;--perhap might generally be more surely find by
add fifty per cent. to that. he know that he be wrong about the
election, but he assure himself that he have have no alternative. the
misfortune have be that the Duke should have make his proclamation
about the borough immediately after the offer make by the Duchess. he
have be almost force to send the agent down to inquire;--and the

on with it now. perhaps some idea of the pleasantness of increase
intimacy with the Duchess of Omnium encourage he in this way of
think. the Duchess be up in town in February, and Lopez leave a
card in Carlton Terrace. on the very next day the card of the Duchess
be leave for Mrs. Lopez at the Belgrave Mansions.
Lopez go into the city every day, leave home at about eleven
o'clock, and not return much before dinner. the young wife at
first find that she hardly know what to do with her time. her aunt,
Mrs. Roby, be distasteful to she. she have already learn from her
husband that he have but little respect for Mrs. Roby. "you remember
the sapphire brooch," he have say once. "that be part of the price I
have to pay for be allow to approach you." he be sit at the
time with his arm round her waist, look out on beautiful scenery
and talk of his old difficulty. she could not find it in her
heart to be angry with he, but the idea bring to her mind be

he do not understand,--how should he?--that though she have never

he also, with my compliment, that I hope we may fight
our political battle with mutual good-fellowship and

your very sincerely,
ARTHUR FLETCHER.
Emily be very much pleased by this letter, and yet she weep over it.

"oh, Ferdinand!"

"what business be it of your?"
