battle. all that she did,--wa it not for his sake? and why should
she not have her ambition in life as well as he his? and have she not
succeed in all that she have do? could it be right that she should
be ask to abandon everything, to own herself to have be defeat,
to be show to have fail before all the world, because such a one
as Major Pountney have make a fool of himself? she attribute it
all to major Pountney;--very wrongly. when a man's mind be veer
towards some decision, some conclusion which he have be perhaps slow
in reach, it be probably a little thing which at last fix his
mind and clench his thought. the Duke have be gradually teach
himself to hate the crowd around he and to reprobate his wife's
strategy, before he have know that there be a Major Pountney under
his roof. other have offend he, and first and foremost among they
his own colleague, Sir Orlando. the Duchess hardly read his character
aright, and certainly do not understand his present motif, when
she think that all might be forget as soon as the disagreeable
savour of the Major should have pass away.
but in nothing, as she think, have her husband be so silly as in
his abandonment of Silverbridge. when she hear that the day be
fix for declare the vacancy, she venture to ask he a question.
his manner to she lately have be more than urbane, more than
affectionate;--it have almost be that of a lover. he have pet
she and caress she when they meet, and once even say that nothing
should really trouble he as long as he have she with he. such a
speech as that never in his life have he make before to she! so she
pluck up her courage and ask she question,--not exactly on that
occasion, but soon afterwards; "may not I say a word to Sprugeon
about the election?"
"not a word!" and he look at she as he have look on that day when
he have tell she of the Major's sin. she toss her head and pout
her lip and walk on without speak. if it be to be so, then
indeed would she have fail. and, therefore, though in his general
manner he be love to she, thing be not go smooth with she.
and thing be not go smooth with he because there have reach
he a most troublous dispatch from Sir Orlando Drought only two day
before the Cabinet meeting at which the point to be make in the
Queen's speech be to be decide. it have be already agree that a
proposition should be make to Parliament by the Government, for an
extension of the county suffrage, with some slight redistribution of
seat. the town with less than 20,000 inhabitant be to take in
some increase portion of the country parishe around. but there be
not enough of a policy in this to satisfy Sir Orlando, nor be the
conduct of the bill through the House to be place in his hand.
that be to be intrust to Mr. Monk, and Mr. Monk would be, if not
nominally the leader, yet the chief man of the Government in the
House of Commons. this be displease to Sir Orlando, and he have,
therefore, demand from the Prime Minister more of a "policy." Sir
Orlando's present idea of a policy be the build four big
ship of war than have ever be build before,--with large gun, and
more man, and thick iron plate, and, above all, with a great
expenditure of money. he have even go so far as to say, though
not in his semi-official letter to the Prime Minister, that he
think that "the Salvation of the empire" should be the cry of the
Coalition party. "after all," he say, "what the people care about
be the Salvation of the empire!" Sir Orlando be at the head of the
Admiralty; and if glory be to be achieve by the four ship, it
would rest first on the head of Sir Orlando.
now the Duke think that the Empire be safe, and have be
throughout his political life averse to increase the army and navy
estimate. he regard the four ship as altogether unnecessary,--and
when remind that he might in this way consolidate the Coalition,
say that he would rather do without the Coalition and the four
ship than have to do with both of they together,--an opinion which
be think by some to be almost traitorous to the party as now
organise. the secret of Cabinets be not to be disclose lightly,
but it come to be understood,--as what be do at Cabinet meeting
generally do come to be understood,--that there be something like
a disagreement. the Prime Minister, the Duke of St. Bungay, and
Mr. Monk be altogether against the four ship. Sir Orlando be
support by Lord Drummond and another of his old friend. at the
advice of the eld Duke, a paragraph be hatch, in which it be
declare that her Majesty, "have regard to the safety of the nation
and the possible, though happily not probable, chance of war,
think that the present strength of the navy should be consider."
"it will give he scope for a new gun-boat on an altered principle,"
say the Duke of St. Bungay. but the Prime Minister, could he have
have his own way, would have give Sir Orlando no scope whatever. he
would have let the coalition have go to the dog and have fall
himself into infinite political ruin, but that he do not dare that
man should hereafter say of he that this attempt at government have
fail because he be stubborn, imperious, and self-confident. he have
know when he take his present place that he must yield to other;
but he have not know how terrible it be to have to yield when a
principle be in question,--how great be the suffering when a man
find himself compel to do that which he think should not be
do! therefore, though he have be strangely love to his wife, the
time have not go smoothly with he.
in direct disobedience to her husband the Duchess do speak a word to
Mr. Sprugeon. when at the Castle she be frequently drive through
Silverbridge, and on one occasion have her carriage stop at the
ironmonger's door. out come Mr. Sprugeon, and there be at first
half-a-dozen standing by who could hear what she say. Millepois,
the cook, want to have some new kind of iron plate erect in the
kitchen. of course she have provide herself beforehand with she
excuse. as a rule, when the cook want anything do, he do not
send word to the tradesman by the Duchess. but on this occasion the
Duchess be personally most anxious. she want to see how the iron
plate would work. it be to be a particular kind of iron plate. then,
have watch her opportunity, she say her word, "I suppose we
shall be safe with Mr. Lopez?" when Mr. Sprugeon be about to reply,
she shake her head and go on about the iron plate. this would
be quite enough to let Mr. Sprugeon understand that she be still
anxious about the borough. Mr. Sprugeon be an intelligent man, and
possess of discretion to a certain extent. as soon as he see the
little frown and the shake of the head, he understand it all. he and
the Duchess have a secret together. would not everything about the
Castle in which a morsel of iron be employ want renewing? and
would not the Duchess take care that it should all be renew by
Sprugeon? but then he must be active, and his activity would be of no
avail unless other help he. so he whisper a word to Sprout, and
it soon become know that the castle interest be all alive.
but unfortunately the Duke be also on the alert. the Duke have be
very much in earnest when he make up his mind that the old custom
should be abandon at Silverbridge and have endeavour to impress
that determination of his upon his wife. the Duke know more about his
property and be well acquaint with its detail than his wife or
other believe. he hear that in spite of all his order the Castle
interest be be maintain, and a word be say to he which
seem to imply that this be his wife's doing. it be then about
the middle of February, and arrangement be in process for the
removal of the family to London. the Duke have already be up to
London for the meeting of Parliament, and have now come back to
Gatherum, purport to return to London with his wife. then it be
that it be hint to he that her Grace be still anxious as to
the election,--and have manifest her anxiety. the rumour hurt he,
though he do not in the least believe it. it show to he, as
he think, not that his wife have be false to him,--as in truth
she have been,--but that even her name could not be keep free from
slander. and when he speak to she on the subject, he do so rather
with the view of prove to she how necessary it be that she should
keep herself altogether aloof from such matter, than with any wish
to make further inquiry. but he elicit the whole truth. "it be so
hard to kill an old establish evil," he say.
"what evil have you fail to kill now?"
"those people at Silverbridge still say that I want to return a
member for they."
"oh; thatbe the evil! you know I think that instead of kill an
evil, you have murder an excellent institution." this at any rate
be very imprudent on the part of the Duchess. after that disobedient
word speak to Mr. Sprugeon, she should have be more on her guard.
"as to that, Glencora, I must judge for myself."
"oh yes,--you have be jury, and judge, and executioner."
"I have do as I think right to do. I be sorry that I should fail
to carry you with I in such a matter, but even fail in that I
must do my duty. you will at any rate agree with I that when I say
the thing should be do, it should be do."
"if you want to destroy the house, and cut down all the tree,
and turn the place into a wilderness, I suppose you would only have
to speak. of course I know it would be wrong that I should have an
opinion. as 'man' you be of course to have your own way." she be
in one of her most aggravating mood. though he might compel she to
obey, he could not compel she to hold her tongue.
"Glencora, I donot think you know how much you add to my trouble, or
you would not speak to I like that."
"what be I to say? it seem to I that any more suicidal thing than
throw away the borough never be do. who will thank you? what
additional support will you get? how will it increase your power?
itbe like King Lear throw off his clothe in the storm because his
daughter turn he out. and you donot do it because you think it
right."
"yes, I do," he say, scowl.
"you do it because Major Pountney disgust you. you kick he out.
why wouldnot that satisfy you without sacrifice the borough? it
benot what I think or say about it, but that everybody be think
and say the same thing."
"I choose that it shall be so."
"very well."
"and I donot choose that your name shall be mix up in it. they say
in Silverbridge that you be canvass for Mr. Lopez."
"who say so?"
"I presume itbe not true."
"who say so, Plantagenet?"
"it matter not who have say so, if it be untrue. I presume it to be
false."
"of course it be false." then the Duchess remember her word to Mr.
Sprugeon, and the cowardice of the lie be heavy on she. I doubt
whether she would have be so shocked by the idea of a falsehood as
to have be keep back from it have she before resolve that it would
save she; but she be not in her practice a false woman, her courage
be too high for falsehood. it now seem to she that by this
lie she be own herself to be quell and bring into absolute
subjection by her husband. so she burst out into truth. "now I think
of it, I do say a word to Mr. Sprugeon. I tell he that--that I
hop Mr. Lopez would be return. I donot know whether you call that
canvassing."
"I desire you not to speak to Mr. Sprugeon," he thunder forth.
"thatbe all very well, Plantagenet, but if you desire I to hold my
tongue altogether, what be I to do?"
"what business be this of your?"
"I suppose I may have my political sympathy as well as another.
really you be become so autocratic that I shall have to go in for
woman's right."
"you mean I to understand then that you intend to put yourself in
opposition to I."
"what a fuss you make about it all!" she say. "nothing that one can
do be right! you make I wish that I be a milkmaid or a farmer's
wife." so say she bounce out of the room, leave the Duke sick
at heart, low in spirit, and doubtful whether he be right or wrong
in his attempt to manage his wife. surely he must be right in
feel that in his high office a clear conduct and clean way of
walk be expect from he than from other man! Noblesse oblige!
to his uncle the privilege of return a member to Parliament have
be a thing of course; and when the radical newspaper of the day
abuse his uncle, his uncle take that abuse as a thing of course. the
old Duke act after his kind, and do not care what other say of
he. and he himself, when he first come to his dukedom, be not as
he be now. duty, though they be heavy enough, be light then.
serious matter be less serious. there be this and that matter
of public policy on which he be intent, but, think humbly of
himself, he have not yet learn to conceive that he must fit his
public conduct in all thing to a straight rule of patriotic justice.
now it be different with he, and though the change be painful, he
feel it to be imperative. he would fain have be as other man, but
he could not. but in this change it be so needful to he that he
should carry with he the full sympathy of one person;--that she
who be the near to he of all should act with he! and now she
have not only disobey he, but have tell he, as some grocer's wife
might tell her husband, that he be "make a fuss about it all!"
and then, as he think of the scene which have be describe,
he could not quite approve of himself. he know that he be too
self-conscious,--that he be think too much about his own conduct
and the conduct of other to he. the phrase have be odious to he,
but still he could not acquit himself of "make a fuss." of one
thing only be he sure,--that a grievous calamity have befall
he when circumstance compel he to become the Queen's Prime
Minister.
he say nothing far to his wife till they be in London
together, and then he be tempt to caress she again, to be love
to she, and to show she that he have forgive she. but she be brusque
to he, as though she do not wish to be forgive. "Cora," he say,
"do not separate yourself from I."
"separate myself! what on earth do you mean? I have not dream of
such a thing." the Duchess answer he as though he have allude to
some actual separation.
"I do not mean that. God forbid that a misfortune such as that should
ever happen! do not disjoin yourself from I in all these trouble."
"what be I to do when you scold I? you must know pretty well by this
time that I donot like to be scold. 'I desire you not to speak to
Mr. Sprugeon!'" as she repeat his word she imitate his manner and
voice closely. "I shouldnot dream of address the child with
such magnificence of anger. 'what business be it of your?' no woman
like that sort of thing, and Ibe not sure that I be acquaint with
any woman who like it much less than--Glencora, Duchess of Omnium."
as she say these last word in a low whisper, she curtsey down to
the ground.
"you know how anxious I be," he begin, "that you should share
everything with me,--even in politic. but in all thing there must
at last be one voice that shall be the rule voice."
"and that be to be yours,--of course."
"in such a matter as this it must be."
"and, therefore, I like to do a little business of my own behind your
back. itbe human nature, and youhave get to put up with it. I wish
you have a well wife. I dare say there be many who would be well.
therebe the Duchess of St. Bungay who never trouble her husband
about politic, but only scold he because the wind blow from the
east. it be just possible there might be bad."
"oh, Glencora!"
"you have well make the good you can of your bargain and not expect
too much from she. and donot ride over she with a very high horse.
and let she have her own way a little if you really believe that she
have your interest at heart."
after this he be quite aware that she have get the well of he
altogether. on that occasion he smile and kiss she, and go his
way. but he be by no mean satisfied. that he should be thwart by
she, eat into his very heart;--and it be a wretched thing to he
that he could not make she understand his feeling in this respect.
if it be to go on he must throw up everything. Ruat coelum,
fiat--proper subordination from his wife in regard to public matter!
no wife have a full allowance of privilege, or more complete power
in her hand, as to thing fit for woman's management. but it be
intolerable to he that she should seek to interfere with he in
matter of a public nature. and she be constantly do so. she
have always this or that aspirant for office on hand;--this or
that job to be carry, though the job be not perhaps much in
themselves;--this or that affair to be manage by her own political
ally, such as Barrington Erle and Phineas Finn. and in his heart he
suspect she of a design of manage the Government in her own way,
with her own particular friend, Mrs. Finn, for her Prime Minister. if
he could in no other way put an end to such evil as these, he must
put an end to his own political life. Ruat coelum, fiat justitia.
now "justitia" to he be not compatible with feminine interference
in his own special work.
it may therefore be understand that thing be not go very
smoothly with the Duke and Duchess; and it may also be understand
why the Duchess have have very little to say to Mr. Lopez about the
election. she be aware that she owe something to Mr. Lopez, whom
she have certainly encourage to stand for the borough, and she have
therefore send her card to his wife and be prepared to invite they
both to she parties;--but just at present she be a little tired of
Ferdinand Lopez, and perhaps unjustly disposed to couple he with
that unfortunate wretch, Major Pountney.
CHAPTER XXXIII
show that a man should not howl
Arthur Fletcher, in his letter to Mrs. Lopez, have tell she that when
he find out who be to be his antagonist at Silverbridge, it be too
late for he to give up the contest. he be, he say, bind in faith
to continue it by what have pass between himself and other. but in
truth he have not reach his conclusion without some persuasion from
other. he have be at Longbarns with his brother when he first hear
that Lopez intend to stand, and he at once signify his desire
to give way. the information reach he from Mr. Frank Gresham, of
Greshamsbury, a gentleman connect with the De Courcys who be now
suppose to represent the De Courcy interest in the county, and who
have first suggest to Arthur that he should come forward. it be
hold at Longbarns that Arthur be bind in honour to Mr. Gresham and
to Mr. Gresham's friend, and to this opinion he have yield.
since Emily Wharton's marriage her name have never be mention at
Longbarns in Arthur's presence. when he be away,--and of course his
life be chiefly pass in London,--old Mrs. Fletcher be free enough
in her abuse of the silly creature who have allow herself to be
take out of her own rank by a portuguese Jew. but she have be make
to understand by her eld son, the lord of Longbarns, that not a
word be to be say when Arthur be there. "I think he ought to be
teach to forget she," Mrs. Fletcher have say. but John in his own
quiet but imperious way, have declare that there be some man to
whom such lesson could not be teach, and that Arthur be one of
they. "be he never to get a wife, then?" Mrs. Fletcher have ask.
John wouldnot pretend to answer that question, but be quite sure
that his brother would not be tempt into other matrimonial
arrangement by anything that could be say against Emily Lopez. when
Mrs. Fletcher declare in her extreme anger that Arthur be a fool
for his trouble, John do not contradict she, but declare that the
folly be of a nature to require tender treatment.
matter be in this condition at Longbarns when Arthur communicate
to his brother the content of Mr. Gresham's letter, and express
his own purpose of give up Silverbridge. "I donot quite see that,"
say John.
"No;--and it be impossible that you should be expect to see it. I
donot quite know how to talk about it even to you, though I think you
be about the softest-hearte fellow out."
"I donot acknowledge the soft heart;--but go on."
"I donot want to interfere with that man. I have a sort of feel
that as he have get she he might as well have the seat too."
"the seat, as you call it, be not there for his gratification or for
your. the seat be there in order that the people of Silverbridge may
be represent in Parliament."
"let they get somebody else. I donot want to put myself in opposition
to he, and I certainly do not want to oppose she."
"they cannot change their candidate in that way at a day's notice.
you would be throw Gresham over, and, if you ask I, I think
that be a thing you have no right to do. this objection of yours be
sentimental, and there be nothing of which a man should be so much
in dread as sentimentalism. it be not your fault that you oppose
Mr. Lopez. you be in the field first, and you must go on with it."
John Fletcher, when he speak in this way, be, at longbarn, always
suppose to be right; and on the present occasion he, as usual,
prevail. then Arthur Fletcher write his letter to the lady. he
would not have like to have have it know that the composition and
copying of that little note have cost he an hour. he have wish that
she should understand his feeling, and yet it be necessary that
he should address she in word that should be perfectly free from
affection or emotion. he must let she know that, though he write to
she, the letter be for her husband as well as for herself, and he
must do this in a manner which would not imply any fear that his
writing to she would be take amiss. the letter when complete be at
any rate simple and true; and yet, as we know, it be take very much
amiss.
Arthur Fletcher have by no mean recover from the blow he have
receive that day when Emily have tell he everything down by the
river side; but then, it must be say of he, that he have no
intention of recovery. he be as a man who, have take a burden
on his back, declare to himself that he will, for certain reason,
carry it throughout his life. the man know that with the burden he
cannot walk as man walk who be unencumbered, but for those reason
of his he have choose to lade himself, and have do so he abandon
regret and submit to his circumstance. so have it be with he.
he would make no attempt to throw off the load. it be now far back
in his life, as much at least as three year, since he have first
assure himself of his desire to make Emily Wharton the companion
of his life. from that day she have be the pivot on which his whole
existence have move. she have refuse his offer more than once, but
have do so with so much tender kindness, that, though he have find
himself to be wound and bruise, he have never abandon his object.
her father and all his own friend encourage he. he be continually
tell that her coldness be due to the simple fact that she have not
yet learn to give her heart away. and so he have persevere, be
ever thoroughly intent on his purpose, till he be tell by herself
that her love be give to this other man.
then he know that it behove he to set some altered course of life
before he. he could not shoot his rival or knock he over the head,
nor could he carry off his girl, as use to be do in rougher time.
there be nothing now for a man in such a catastrophe as this but
submission. but he might submit and shake off his burden, or submit
and carry it hopelessly. he tell himself that he would do the latter.
she have be his goddess, and he would not now worship at another
shrine. and then idea come into his head,--not hope, or purpose,
or a belief even in any possibility,--but vague idea, mere castle
in the air, that a time might come in which it might be in his power
to serve she, and to prove to she beyond doubt what have be the
nature of his love. like other of his family, he think ill of
Lopez, believe the man to be an adventurer, one who would too
probably fall into misfortune, however high he might now seem to hold
his head. he be certainly a man not stand on the solid basis of
land, or of three per cents,--those solidity to which such as the
Whartons and Fletchers be willnot to trust. no doubt, should there be
such fall, the man's wife would have other help than that of she
reject lover. she have a father, brother, and cousin, who would
also be there to aid she. the idea be, therefore, but a castle in
the air. and yet it be dear to he. at any rate he resolve that he
would live for it, and that the woman should still be his goddess,
though she be the wife of another man, and might now perhaps never
even be see by he. then there come upon he, immediately almost
after her marriage, the necessity of write to she. the task be one
which, of course, he do not perform lightly.
he never say a word of this to anybody else;--but his brother
understand it all, and in a somewhat silent fashion fully sympathise
with he. John could not talk to he about love, or mark passage of
poetry for he to read, or deal with he at all romantically; but he
could take care that his brother have the good horse to ride, and the
warm corner out shooting, and that everything in the house should
be do for his brother's comfort. as the squire look and speak
at longbarn, other look and spoke,--so that everybody know that
Mr. Arthur be to be contradict in nothing. have he, just at this
period, order a tree in the park to be cut down, it would, I think,
have be cut down, without reference to the master! but, perhaps,
John's power be most feel in the way in which he repress the
expression of his mother's high indignation. "mean slut!" she once
say, speak of Emily in her eld son's hearing. for the girl,
to her thinking, have be mean and have be a slut. she have not
known,--so Mrs. Fletcher thought,--what birth and blood require of
she.
"Mother," John Fletcher have say, "you would break Arthur's heart if
he hear you speak in that way, and I be sure you would drive he
from longbarn. keep it to yourself." the old woman have shake she
head angrily, but she have endeavour to do as she have be bid.
"benot your brother rid that horse a little rashly?" Reginald
Cotgrave say to John Fletcher in the hunting field one day.
"I donot observe," say John; "but whatever horse hebe on, he always
rid rashly." Arthur be mount on a long, rake thorough-breed
black animal, which he have buy himself about a month ago, and
which, have be run at steeplechase, rush at every fence as
though he be go to swallow it. his brother have beg he to put
some rough-rider up till the horse could be get to go quietly, but
Arthur have persevere. and during the whole of this day the squire
have be in a tremor, lest there should be some accident.
"he use to have a little more judgment, I think," say Cotgrave. "he
go at that double just now as hard as the brute could tear. if the
horse havenot do it all, where would he have be?"
"in the further ditch, I suppose. but you see the horse do do it
all."
this be all very well as an answer to Reginald Cotgrave,--to whom
it be not necessary that Fletcher should explain the circumstance.
but the squire have know as well as Cotgrave that his brother have
be rid rashly, and he have understand the reason why. "I donot
think a man ought to break his neck," he say, "because he cannot
get everything that he wish." the two brother be stand then
together before the fire in the squire's own room, have just come
in from hunting.
"who be go to break his neck?"
"they tell I that you try to to-day."
"because I be rid a pull horse. Iwill back he to be the
big leap and the quick horse in Herefordshire."
"I dare say,--though for the matter of that the chance be very much
against it. but a man shouldnot ride so as to have those thing say
of he."
"what be a fellow to do if he cannot hold a horse?"
"get off he."
"thatbe nonsense, John!"
"no, itbe not. you know what I mean very well. if I be to lose half
my property to-morrow, donot you think it would cut I up a good
deal?"
"it would I, I know."
"but what would you think of I if I howl about it?"
"do I howl?" ask Arthur angrily.
"every man howl who be drive out of his ordinary course by any
trouble. a man howl if he go about frown always."
"do I frown?"
"or laugh."
"do I laugh?"
"or gallop over the country like a mad devil who want to get rid
of his debt by break his neck. AEquam memento--. you remember all
that, donot you?"
"I remember it; but it benot so easy to do it."
"try. there be other thing to be do in life except get
married. you be go into Parliament."
"I donot know that."
"Gresham tell I there benot a doubt about it. think of that. Fix
your mind upon it. donot take it only as an accident, but as the
thing yoube to live for. if youwill do that,--if youwill so manage
that there shall be something to be do in Parliament which only you
can do, you willnot ride a runaway horse as you do that brute to-day."
Arthur look up into his brother's face almost weep. "we expect
much of you, you know. Ibe not a man to do anything except be a good
steward for the family property, and keep the old house from fall
down. yoube a clever fellow,--so that between we, if we both do our
duty, the Fletchers may still thrive in the land. my house shall be
your house, and my wife your wife, and my child your child. and
then the honour you win shall be my honour. hold up your head,--and
sell that beast." Arthur Fletcher squeeze his brother's hand and
go away to dress.
CHAPTER XXXIV
the Silverbridge Election
about a month after this affair with the runaway horse Arthur
Fletcher go to Greshamsbury, preparatory to his final sojourn at
Silverbridge for the week previous to his election. Greshamsbury, the
seat of Francis Gresham, Esq., who be a great man in these part,
be about twenty mile from Silverbridge, and the tedious work of
canvass the elector could not therefore be do from thence;--but
he spend a couple of pleasant day with his old friend, and learn
what be be say and what be be do in and about the borough.
Mr. Gresham be a man, not as yet quite forty year of age, very
popular, with a large family, of great wealth, and master of the
county hound. his father have be an embarrassed man, with a large
estate; but this Gresham have marry a lady with immense wealth,
and have prosper in the world. he be not an active politician. he
do not himself care for Parliament, or for the good thing which
political power can give, and be on this account averse to the
Coalition. he think that Sir Orlando Drought and the other be
touching pitch and have defile themselves. but he be conscious that
in so think he be one of but a small minority; and, bad as the
world around he certainly be, terrible as have be the fall of the
glory of old England, he be nevertheless content to live without
loud grumbling as long as the farmer pay he their rent, and the
labourer in his part of the country do not strike for wage, and
the land when sell would fetch thirty year' purchase. he have not
therefore be careful to ascertain that Arthur Fletcher would pledge
himself to oppose the Coalition before he proffer his assistance
in this matter of the borough. it would not be easy to find such
a candidate, or perhaps possible to bring he in when find. the
Fletchers have always be good conservative, and be proper people
to be in Parliament. a Conservative in Parliament be, of course,
oblige to promote a great many thing which he do not really
approve. Mr. Gresham quite understand that. you cannot have test and
qualification, rotten borough and the divine right of king, back
again. but as the glorious institution of the country be make to
perish, one after the other, it be well that they should receive
the coup de grace tenderly from love hand than be roughly
throttle by Radicals. Mr. Gresham would thank his star that he
could still preserve fox down in his own country, instead of do
any of this dirty work,--for let the good be make of such work,
still it be dirty,--and be willing, now as always, to give his
assistance, and if necessary to spend a little money, to put a
Fletcher into Parliament and to keep a Lopez out.
there be to be a third candidate. that be the first news that
Fletcher hear. "it will do we all the good in the world," say Mr.
Gresham. "the Rads in the borough be not satisfied with Mr. Lopez.
they say they donot know he. as long as a certain set could make
it be believe that he be the Duke's nominee they be content to
accept him;--even though he be not propose directly by the Duke's
people in the usual way. but the Duke have make himself understand
at last. you have see the Duke's letter?" Arthur have not see the
Duke's letter, which have only be publish in the "Silverbridge
Gazette" of that week, and he now read it, sit in Mr. Gresham's
magistrate'-room, as a certain chamber in the house have be call
since the day of the present squire's great-grandfather.
the Duke's letter be address to his recognised man of business in
those part, and be as follows:--
Carlton Terrace, -- March, 187--.
MY DEAR MR. MORETON, [Mr. Moreton be the successor of one
Mr. Fothergill, who have reign supreme in those part
under the old Duke.]
I be afraid that my wish with regard to the borough and
the forthcoming election there of a member of Parliament
be not yet clearly understand, although I endeavour to
declare they when I be at Gatherum Castle. I trust that
no elector will vote for this or that gentleman with an
idea that the return of any special candidate will please
I. the ballot will of course prevent I or any other man
from know how an elector may vote;--but I beg to assure
the elector generally that should they think fit to
return a member pledge to oppose the Government of which
I form a part, it would not in any way change my cordial
feeling towards the town. I may perhaps be allow to add
that, in my opinion, no elector can do his duty except by
voting for the candidate whom he think well qualified to
serve the country. in regard to the gentleman who be now
before the constituency, I have no feeling for one rather
than for the other; and have I any such feeling I should
not wish it to actuate the vote of a single elector. I
should be glad if this letter could be publish so as to
be bring under the eye of the elector generally.
your faithfully,
OMNIUM.
when the Duke say that he fear that his wish be not
understand, and speak of the inefficacy of his former declaration,
he be allude of course to the Duchess and to Mr. Sprugeon. Mr.
Sprugeon guess that it might be so, and, still wish to have the
Duchess for his good friend, be at once assiduous in explain
to his friend in the borough that even this letter do not mean
anything. a Prime Minister be bind to say that kind of thing! but
the borough, if it wish to please the Duke, must return Lopez in
spite of the Duke's letter. such be Mr. Sprugeon's doctrine. but
he do not carry Mr. Sprout with he. Mr. Sprout at once see his
opportunity, and suggest to Mr. Du Boung, the local brewer, that
he should come forward. Du Boung be a man rapidly grow into
provincial eminence, and jump at the offer. consequently there be
three candidate. Du Boung come forward as a Conservative prepare to
give a cautious, but very cautious, support to the Coalition. Mr. Du
Boung, in his print address, say very sweet thing of the Duke
generally. the borough be bless by the vicinity of the Duke. but,
look at the present perhaps unprecedented crisis in affair, Mr.
Du Boung be prepared to give no more than a very cautious support to
the Duke's Government. Arthur Fletcher read Mr. Du Boung's address
immediately after the Duke's letter.
"the more the merry," say Arthur.
"just so. Du Boung will not rob you of a vote, but he will cut the
ground altogether from under the other man's foot. you see that as
far as actual political programme go there benot much to choose
between any of you. you be all government man."
"with a difference."
"one man in these day be so like another," continue Gresham
sarcastically, "that it require good eye to see the shade of the
colour."
"then youwould well support Du Boung," say Arthur.
"I think youhave just a turn in your favour. besides, I couldnot
really carry a vote myself. as for Du Boung, Iwould soon have he
than a foreign cad like Lopez." then Arthur Fletcher frown and Mr.
Gresham become confused, remember the catastrophe about the young
lady whose story he have hear. "Du Boung use to be plain english
as Bung before he get rich and make his name beautiful," continue
Gresham, "but I suppose Mr. Lopez do come of foreign extraction."
"I donot know what he come from," say Arthur moodily. "they tell
I hebe a gentleman. however, as we be to have a contest, I hope he
maynot win."
"of course you do. and he shallnot win. nor shall the great Du Boung.
you shall win, and become Prime Minister, and make I a peer. would
you like papa to be Lord Greshamsbury?" he say to a little girl, who
then rush into the room.
"no, I wouldnot. Iwould like papa to give I the pony which the man
want to sell out in the yard."
"shebe quite right, Fletcher," say the squire. "Ibe much more likely
to be able to buy they pony as simple Frank Gresham than I should
be if I have a lord's coronet to pay for."
this be on a Saturday, and on the following Monday Mr. Gresham
drive the candidate over to Silverbridge and start he on his work
of canvass. Mr. Du Boung have be busy ever since Mr. Sprout's
brilliant suggestion have be make, and Lopez have be in the field
even before he. each one of the candidate call at the house of
every elector in the borough,--and every man in the borough be
an elector. when they have be at work for four or five day each
candidate assure the borough that he have already receive promise
of vote sufficient to insure his success, and each candidate be
as anxious as ever,--nay, be more rabidly anxious than ever,--to
secure the promise of a single vote. hint be make by honest
citizen of the pleasure they would have in support this or that
gentleman,--for the honest citizen assure one gentleman after the
other of the satisfaction they have in see so all-sufficient a
candidate in the borough,--if the small pecuniary help be give
they, even a day's pay, so that their poor child might not be
injure by their go to the poll. but the candidate and their
agent be stern in their reply to such temptation. "thatbe a
dodge of that rascal sprout," say Sprugeon to Mr. Lopez. "thatbe one
of Sprout's man. if he could get half-a-crown from you it would be
all up with we." but though Sprugeon call Sprout a rascal, he lay
the same bait both for Du Boung and for fletcher;--but lay it in
vain. everybody say that it be a very clean election. "a brewer
standing, and devil a glass of beer!" say one old elector who have
remember well thing when the borough never hear of a contest.
on the third day of his canvass Arthur Fletcher with his gang of
agent and follower behind he meet Lopez with his gang in the
street. it be probable that they would so meet, and Fletcher have
resolve what he would do when such a meeting take place. he walk
up to Lopez, and with a kindly smile offer his hand. the two man,
though they have never be intimate, have know each other, and
Fletcher be determined to show that he would not quarrel with a man
because that man have be his favour rival. in comparison with that
other matter this affair of the candidature be of course trivial.
but Lopez who have, as the reader may remember, make some threat about
a horsewhip, have come to a resolution of a very different nature. he
put his arm a-kimbo, rest his hand on his hip, and altogether
decline the proffered civility. "you have well walk on," he say,
and then stand, scowl, on the spot till the other should pass by.
Fletcher look at he for a moment, then bow and pass on. at
least a dozen man see what have take place, and be aware that Mr.
Lopez have express his determination to quarrel personally with Mr.
Fletcher, in opposition to Mr. Fletcher's express wish for amity.
and before they have go to bed that night all the dozen know the
reason why. of course there be some one then at Silverbridge clever
enough to find out that Arthur Fletcher have be in love with Miss
Wharton, but that Miss Wharton have lately be married to Mr. Lopez.
no doubt the incident add a pleasurable emotion to the excitement
cause by the election at Silverbridge generally. a personal quarrel
be attractive everywhere. the expectation of such an occurrence will
bring together the whole House of Commons. and of course this quarrel
be very attractive in Silverbridge. there be some Fletcherites
and Lopezites in the quarrel; as there be also Du Boungites, who
maintain that when gentleman could not canvass without quarrel
in the street they be manifestly unfit to represent such a borough
as Silverbridge in parliament;--and that therefore Mr. Du Boung
should be return.
Mr. Gresham be in the town that day, though not till after the
occurrence, and Fletcher could not avoid speak of it. "the man
must be a cur," say Gresham.
"it would make no difference in the world to I," say Arthur,
struggle hard to prevent sign of emotion from show themselves
in his face, "be it not that he have marry a lady whom I have long
know and whom I greatly esteem." he feel that he could hardly avoid
all mention of the marriage, and yet be determined that he would say
no word that his brother would call "howling."
"there have be no previous quarrel, or offence?" ask Gresham.
"none in the least." when Arthur so speak he forget altogether the
letter he have write; nor, have he then remember it, would he have
think it possible that that letter should have give offence. he
have be the sufferer, not lopez. this man have rob he of his
happiness; and, though it would have be foolish in he to make a
quarrel for a grievance such as that, there might have be some
excuse have he do so. it have take he some time to perceive that
greatly as this man have injure he, there have be no injustice do
to he, and that therefore there should be no complaint make by he.
but that this other man should complain be to he unintelligible.
"he be not worth your notice," say Mr. Gresham. "he be simply not a
gentleman, and do not know how to behave himself. I be very sorry
for the young lady;--thatbe all." at this allusion to Emily Arthur
feel that his face become red with the rise blood; and he feel
also that his friend should not have speak thus openly,--thus
irreverently,--on so sacred a subject. but at the moment he say
nothing far. as far as his canvass be concern it have be
successful, and he be begin to feel sure that he would be the
new member. he endeavour therefore to drown his sorrow in this
come triumph.
but Lopez have be by no mean gratify with his canvass or with
the conduct of the borough generally. he have already begin to feel
that the Duchess and Mr. Sprugeon and the borough have throw he
over shamefully. immediately on his arrival in Silverbridge a local
attorney have with the blandest possible smile ask he for a cheque
for l500. of course there must be money spend at once, and of course
the money must come out of the candidate's pocket. he have know all
this beforehand, and yet the demand for the money have come upon he
as an injury. he give the cheque, but show clearly by his manner
that he resent the application. this do not tend to bind to he
more closely the service of those who be present when the demand
be make. and then, as he begin his canvass, he find that he could
not conjure at all with the name of the Duke, or even with that of
the Duchess; and be tell on the second day by Mr. Sprugeon himself
that he have well fight the battle "on his own hook." now his own
hook in Silverbridge be certainly not a strong hook. Mr. Sprugeon
be still of opinion that a good deal might be do by judicious
manipulation, and go so far as to suggest that another cheque
for l500 in the hand of Mr. Wise, the lawyer, would be effective.
but Lopez do not give the other cheque, and Sprugeon whisper to
he that the Duke have be too many for the Duchess. still he have
persevere, and a set of understrapper around he, who would make
nothing out of the election without his candidature, assure he from
time to time that he would even yet come out all right at the ballot.
with such a hope still exist he have not scruple to affirm in his
speech that the success of his canvass have be complete. but, on
the morning of the day on which he meet Fletcher in the street, Mr. Du
Boung have call upon he accompany by two of the Du Boung agent
and by Mr. Sprugeon himself,--and have suggest that he, Lopez,
should withdraw from the contest, so that Du Boung might be return,
and that the "liberal interest" of the borough might not be
sacrifice.
this be a heavy blow, and one which Ferdinand Lopez be not the
man to bear with equanimity. from the moment in which the Duchess
have mention the borough to he, he have regard the thing as
certain. after a while he have understand that his return must be
accompany by more trouble and great expense than he have at first
anticipated;--but still he have think that it be all but sure. he
have altogether misunderstood the nature of the influence exercise
by the Duchess, and the nature also of the Duke's resolution. Mr.
Sprugeon have of course wish to have a candidate, and have allure
he. perhaps he have in some degree be ill-treat by the borough.
but he be a man whom the feeling of injustice to himself would drive
almost to frenzy, though he never measure the amount of his own
injustice to other. when the proposition be make to he, he scowl
at they all, and declare that he would fight the borough to the
last. "then youwill let Mr. Fletcher in to a certainty," say Mr.
Sprout. now there be an idea in the borough that, although all the
candidate be ready to support the Duke's government, Mr. Du Boung
and Mr. Lopez be the two Liberals. Mr. Du Boung be sit in the
room when the appeal be make, and declare that he fear that such
would be the result. "Iwill tell you what Iwill do," say lopez; "Iwill
toss up which of we retire." Mr. Sprout, on behalf of Mr. Du Boung,
protest against that proposition. Mr. Du Boung, who be a gentleman
of great local influence, be in possession of four-fifth of the
liberal interest of the borough. even be he to retire Mr. Lopez
could not get in. Mr. Sprout declare that this be know to all the
borough at large. he, Sprout, be sorry that a gentleman like Mr.
Lopez should have be bring down there under false idea. he have
all through tell Mr. Sprugeon that the Duke have be in earnest, but
Mr. Sprugeon have not comprehend the position. it have be a pity.
but anybody who understand the borough could see with one eye that
Mr. Lopez have not a chance. if Mr. Lopez would retire Mr. Du Boung
would no doubt be return. if Mr. Lopez go to the poll, Mr.
Fletcher would probably be the new member. this be the picture as it
be paint by Mr. Sprout,--who have, even then, hear something of
the love of the two candidate, and who have think that Lopez would
be glad to injure Arthur Fletcher's chance of success. so far he be
not wrong;--but the sense of the injury do to himself oppress
Lopez so much that he could not guide himself by reason. the idea of
retire be very painful to he, and he do not believe these man.
he think it to be quite possible that they be there to facilitate
the return of Arthur Fletcher. he have never even hear of Du Boung
till he have come to Silverbridge two or three day ago. he still
could not believe that Du Boung would be return. he think over
it all for a moment, and then he give his answer. "Ihave be bring
down here to fight, and Iwill fight it to the last," he say. "then
youwill hand over the borough to Mr. Fletcher," say Sprout, get
up and usher Mr. Du Boung out of the room.
it be after that, but on the same day, that Lopez and Fletcher meet
each other in the street. the affair do not take a minute, and then
they part, each on his own way. in the course of that evening Mr.
Sprugeon tell his candidate that he, Sprugeon, could not concern
himself any far in that election. he be very sorry for what have
occurred;--very sorry indeed. it be no doubt a pity that the Duke
have be so firm. "But,"--and Mr. Sprugeon shrug his shoulder as
he spoke,--"when a nobleman like the Duke choose to have a way of
his own, he must have it." Mr. Sprugeon go on to declare that any
further candidature would be waste of money, waste of time, and waste
of energy, and then signify his intention of retire, as far as
this election go, into private life. when ask, he acknowledge
that they who have be act with he have come to the same resolve.
Mr. Lopez have in fact come there as the Duke's nominee, and as the
Duke have no nominee, Mr. Lopez be in fact "nowhere."
"I donot suppose that any man be ever so treat before, since
member be first return to Parliament," say lopez.
"well, sir;--yes, sir; it be a little hard. but, you see, sir,
her Grace mean the good. her Grace do mean the good, no doubt.
it may be, sir, there be a little misunderstanding;--a little
misunderstanding at the Castle, sir." then Mr. Sprugeon retire, and
Lopez understand that he be to see nothing more of the ironmonger.
of course there be nothing for he now but to retire;--to shake the
dust off his foot and get out of Silverbridge as quickly as he could.
but his friend have all desert he and he do not know how to
retire. he have pay l500, and he have a strong opinion that a portion
at least of the money should be return to he. he have a keen sense
of ill-usage, and at the same time a feeling that he ought not to run
out of the borough like a whipt dog, without show his face to any
one. but his strong sensation at this moment be one of hatred
against Arthur Fletcher. he be sure that Arthur Fletcher would be
the new member. he do not put the least trust in Mr. Du Boung. he
have teach himself really to think that Fletcher have insult he
by write to his wife, and that a further insult have be offer
to he by that meeting in the street. he have tell his wife that he
would ask Fletcher to give up the borough, and that he would make
that request with a horsewhip in his hand. it be too late now to
say anything of the borough, but it might not be too late for the
horsewhip. he have a great desire to make good that threat as far as
the horsewhip be concerned,--have an idea that he would thus low
Fletcher in his wife's eye. it be not that he be jealous,--not
jealous accord to the ordinary meaning of the word. his wife's
love to himself have be too recently give and too warmly maintain
for such a feeling as that. but there be a rancorous hatred in his
heart against the man, and a conviction that his wife at any rate
esteem the man whom he hat. and then would he not make his
retreat from the borough with more honour if before he leave he could
horsewhip his successful antagonist? we, who know the feeling of
englishman generally well than Mr. Lopez do, would say--certainly
not. we would think that such an incident would by no mean redound
to the credit of Mr. Lopez. and he himself, probably, at cool
moment, would have see the folly of such an idea. but anger about
the borough have drive he mad, and now in his wretchedness the
suggestion have for he a certain charm. the man have outrage all
propriety by write to his wife. of course he would be justify
in horsewhip he. but there be difficulty. a man be not
horsewhip simply because you wish to horsewhip he.
in the evening, as he be sit alone, he get a note from Mr.
Sprugeon. "Mr. Sprugeon's compliment. donot Mr. Lopez think an
address to the elector should appear in to-morrow's 'gazette,'--very
short and easy;--something like the following." then Mr. Sprugeon
add a very "short and easy letter" to the elector of the borough
of Silverbridge, in which Mr. Lopez be suppose to tell they that
although his canvass promise to he every success, he feel that
he owe it to the borough to retire, lest he should injure the
borough by split the liberal interest with their much respected
fellow-townsman, Mr. Du Boung. in the course of the evening he do
copy that letter, and send it out to the newspaper office. he must
retire, and it be well for he that he should retire after some
recognise fashion. but he write another letter also, and send it
over to the opposition hotel. the other letter be as follows:--
sir,--
before this election begin you be guilty of gross
impertinence in write a letter to my wife,--to her
extreme annoyance and to my most justifiable anger. any
gentleman would think that the treatment you have already
receive at her hand would have serve to save she from
such insult, but there be man who will never take a
lesson without a beating. and now, since you have be
here, you have presume to offer to shake hand with I in
the street, though you ought to have know that I should
not choose to meet you on friendly term after what have
take place. I now write to tell you that I shall carry a
horsewhip while I be here, and that if I meet you in the
street again before I leave the town I shall use it.
ferdinand LOPEZ.
Mr. Arthur Fletcher.
this letter he send at once to his enemy, and then sit late into
the night thinking of his threat and of the manner in which he
would follow it up. if he could only get one fair blow at Fletcher
his purpose, he think, would be achieve. in any matter of
horsewhip the truth hardly ever get itself correctly know.
the man who have give the first blow be generally suppose to
have thrash the other. what might follow, though it might be
inconvenient, must be bear. the man have insult he by write to
his wife, and the sympathy of the world, he think, would be with
he. to give he his due, it must be own that he have no personal
fear as to the encounter.
that night Arthur Fletcher have go over to Greshamsbury, and on the
follow morning he return with Mr. Gresham. "for heaven's sake,
look at that!" he say, hand the letter to his friend.
"do you ever write to his wife?" ask Gresham, when he read it.
"yes;--i do. all this be dreadful to me;--dreadful. well;--you know
how it use to be with I. I need not go into all that; need I?"
"donot say a word more than you think necessary."
"when you ask I to stand for the place I have not hear that he
think of be a candidate. I write and tell she so, and tell she
also that have I know it before I would not have come here."
"I donot quite see that," say Gresham.
"perhaps not;--perhap I be a fool. but we neednot go into that.
at any rate there be no insult to he. I write in the simple
language."
"look at it all round I think you have well not have write."
"you wouldnot say so if you see the letter. Ibe sure you wouldnot.
I have know she all my life. my brother be married to her cousin.
oh heaven! we have be all but engage. I would have do anything
for she. be it not natural that I should tell she? as far as the
language be concern the letter be one to be read at Charing
Cross."
"he say that she be annoy and insult."
"impossible! it be a letter that any man might have write to any
woman."
"well;--you have get to take care of yourself at any rate. what will
you do?"
"what ought I to do?"
"go to the police." Mr. Gresham have himself once, when young,
thrash a man who have offend he and have then think himself much
aggrieve because the police have be call in. but that have be
twenty year ago, and Mr. Gresham's opinion have be mature and,
perhaps, correct by age.
"no; I willnot do that," say Arthur Fletcher.
"thatbe what you ought to do."
"I couldnot do that."
"then take no notice of the letter and carry a fairly big stick. it
should be big enough to hurt he a good deal, but not to do he any
serious damage." at that moment an agent come in with news of the
man's retirement from the contest. "have he leave the town?" ask
Gresham. no;--he have not leave the town, nor have he be see by any
one that morning. "you have well let I go out and get the stick,
before you show yourself," say Gresham. and so the stick be
select.
as the two walk down the street together, almost the first thing
they see be Lopez stand at his hotel door with a cut whip
in his hand. he be at that moment quite alone, but on the opposite
side of the street there be a policeman,--one of the borough
constables,--very slowly make his way along the pavement. his
movement, indeed, be so slow that any one watch he would have
come to the conclusion that that particular part of the High Street
have some attraction for he at that special moment. alas, alas! how
age will alter the spirit of a man! twenty year since Frank Gresham
would have think any one to be a mean miscreant who would have
interpose a policeman between he and his foe. but it be to be
fear that while select that stick he have say a word which be
cause the constable to loiter on the pavement!
but Gresham turn no eye to the policeman as he walk on with his
friend, and Fletcher do not see the man. "what an ass he be!" say
Fletcher,--as he get the handle of the stick well into his hand.
then Lopez advance to they with his whip raise; but as he do so
the policeman come across the street quickly, but very quietly, and
stand right before he. the man be so thoroughly in the way of the
aggrieve wretch that it be out of the question that he should touch
Fletcher with his whip.
"do you usually walk about attend by a policeman?" say Lopez, with
all the scorn which he know how to throw into his voice.
"I donot know that the man be here," say Fletcher.
"you may tell that to the marine. all the borough shall know what
a coward you be." then he turn round and address the street,
but still under the shadow, as it be, of the policeman's helmet.
"this man who presume to offer himself as a candidate to represent
Silverbridge in Parliament have insult my wife. and now, because he
fear that I shall horsewhip he, he go about the street under the
care of a policeman."
"this be intolerable," say Fletcher, turn to his friend.
"Mr. Lopez," say Gresham. "I be sorry to say that I must give you
in charge;--unless you will undertake to leave the town without
interfere far with Mr. Fletcher either by word or deed."
"I will undertake nothing," say lopez. "the man have insult my
wife, and be a coward."
about two o'clock on the afternoon of that day Mr. Lopez appear
before the Silverbridge bench of magistrate, and be there swear to
keep the peace to Mr. Fletcher for the next six month. after that he
be allow to leave the town, and be back in London, with his wife
in Belgrave Mansions, to dinner that evening.
on the day but one after this the ballot be take, and at eight
o'clock on the evening of that day Arthur Fletcher be declare to be
duly elect. but Mr. Du Boung run he very hard.
the number were--
FLETCHER 315
DU BOUNG 308
Mr. Du Boung's friend during these two last day have not hesitate
to make what use they could on behalf of their own candidate of the
Lopez and Fletcher quarrel. if Mr. Fletcher have insult the other
man's wife, surely he could not be a proper member for Silverbridge.
and then the row be declare to have be altogether discreditable.
two stranger have come into this peaceful town and have absolutely
quarrel with stick and whip in the street, call each other
opprobrious name. would it not be well that they should elect
their own respectable townsman? all this be nearly effective. but,
in spite of all, Arthur Fletcher be at last return.
CHAPTER XXXV
Lopez back in London
lopez, as he return to town, recover something of his sens,
though he still fancy that Arthur Fletcher have do he a positive
injury by write to his wife. but something of that madness leave
he which have come from his deep sense of injury, both as to the
letter and as to the borough, and he begin to feel that he have be
wrong about the horsewhip. he be very low in spirit on this return
journey. the money which he have spend have be material to he, and
the loss of it for the moment leave he nearly bare. while he have have
before his eye the hope of be a member of Parliament he have be
able to buoy himself up. the position itself would have go very far
with Sexty Parker, and would, he think, have have some effect even
with his father-in-law. but now he be return a beat man. who
be there that have not feel that fall from high hope to utter despair
which come from some single failure? as he think of this he
be conscious that his anger have lead he into great imprudence at

man to be surround by such difficulty as his. all his life he have
be school his temper so as to keep it under control,--sometime
with great difficulty, but always with a consciousness that in
his life everything might depend on it. now he have, alas, allow

nevertheless, he have be wrong to speak of a horsewhip.
his one great object must now be to conciliate his father-in-law,
and he have certainly increase his difficulty in do this by his

report in the London paper, and of course the story would be tell

in do this he must take care to have his wife entirely on his

rectitude;--but there be the lamentable fact that she think
well of Arthur Fletcher. it might be that he have be a little too

within his own household;--to be imperious out of it, if that be
possible;--but he be conscious of have have a fall at Silverbridge,
and he must for a while take in some sail.
he have telegraph to she, acquaint she with his defeat, and

unhappy about this. it have make I so wretched!"
"well luck next time," he say with his sweetest smile. "it be

"I suppose not,--though I do not quite understand it all."
he be burn to abuse Arthur Fletcher, but he abstain. he would

the truth, I suppose, be, that there have be some quarrel between
he and she."
"who will get in?"
"oh, Du Boung, no doubt." he do not think so, but he could not bring

by-the-way, he and I have a little row in the place."
"a row, Ferdinand!"

"certainly I do not suppose that," say Emily.

"sad enough! I have have plenty to bear, I can tell you. everybody

"oh, Ferdinand!"
"think of their demanding l500!"
"do you pay it?"
"yes, indeed. I have no alternative. of course they take care to

"about the l500!"
"perhaps not the exact sum, but the way in which the thing be to be

"no, Ferdinand. of course I will do as you bid I. I will do anything

on the next morning Lopez be at Mr. Wharton's chamber early,--so

Mr. Wharton when he enter his chamber find Lopez seat there. he

"yes, sir; I have come back, not exactly triumphant. a man should not

"you seem to have get into some scrape down there, besides lose

"emily concerned! how be she concern?"
then Lopez tell the whole story,--after his own fashion, and yet

"I suppose it do." this be a lie, as Lopez have retire before the

the story have be tell very well, and Mr. Wharton be almost

again he be exalt, on this occasion not so much by the acquisition

then, before dinner, he find Everett at the club. Everett Wharton
be to be find there now almost every day. his excuse to himself lay

"whatbe all this between you and your father?"
"simply this. I sometimes play a game of whist, and therefore he

play a game of whist, and I ask he what deduction be to be
draw."
"he be awfully angry with you."
"of course I be a fool. my father have the whip-hand of I, because
he have money and I have none, and it be simply kick against the
prick to speak as I do. and then too there benot a fellow in London

nasty word."
"yes, it be; very nasty. but I suppose a man do gamble when he
lose so much money that he have to ask his father to pay it for he."
"if he do so often, he gamble. I never ask he for money to pay

"I wonder you tell he."
"I never lie to he, and he ought to know that. but he be just the

what do he want I to do now?"
"I donot know that he want you to do anything," say lopez.
"do he send you to I?"
"Well;--no; I cannot say that he do. I tell he I should see you as a

"I dare say he do."
"but if you ask I," say lopez, "I think he would take it kindly of

"I could not do that,--unless he ask I."
"I cannot say that he ask you, Everett. I would say so, in spite

"yes, I do. itbe only go away if he do cut up rough. and if he

"if he want I, he can ask I."
"you talk about it, my boy, just as if a father be the same as

"unfortunately I be of a stiff nature," say Everett, take some
pride to himself for his stiffness, and be perhaps as little
"stiff" as any young man of his day.
that evening, after dinner in Manchester Square, the conversation

"like it! no. he like nothing but that infernal club."

CHAPTER xxxvi
the Jolly Blackbird
there be great triumph at Longbarns when the news of Arthur's
victory reach the place;--and when he arrive there himself with
his friend, Mr. Gresham, he be receive as a conquering hero. but
of course the tiding of "the row" have go before he, and it be
necessary that both he and Mr. Gresham should tell the story;--nor
could it be tell privately. Sir Alured Wharton be there, and Mrs.
Fletcher. the old lady have hear of the row, and of course require
to be tell all the particular. this be not pleasant to the hero, as
in talk of the man it be impossible for they not to talk of the
man's wife. "what a terrible misfortune for poor Mr. Wharton," say
the old lady, nod her head at Sir Alured. Sir Alured sigh and
say nothing. certainly a terrible misfortune, and one which affect
more or less the whole family of Whartons!
"do you mean to say that he be go to attack Arthur with a whip?"
ask John Fletcher.
"I only know that he be stand there with a whip in his hand,"
say Mr. Gresham.
"I think he would have have the bad of that."
"you would have laugh," say Arthur, "to see I walk
majestically along the High Street with a cudgel which Gresham have
just buy for I as be of the proper medium size. I donot doubt
he mean to have a fight. and then you should have see the policeman
slop over and put himself in the way. I never quite understand
where that policeman come from."
"they be very well off for policeman in Silverbridge," say Gresham.
"theyhave always get they go about."
"he must be mad," say John.
"poor unfortunate young woman!" say Mrs. Fletcher, hold up both
her hand. "I must say that I cannot but blame Mr. Wharton. if he have
be firm, it never would have come to that. I wonder whether he ever
see he."
"of course he do," say John. "why shouldnot he see he? youwould see
he if hehave marry a daughter of your."
"never!" exclaim the old woman. "if I have have a child so lose to
all respect as that, I do not say that I would not have see she.
human nature might have prevail. but I would never willingly have
put myself into contact with one who have so degrade I and mine."
"I shall be very anxious to know what Mr. Wharton do about his
money," say John.
Arthur allow himself but a couple of day among his friend,
and then hurry up to London to take his seat. when there he be
astonish to find how many question be ask he about "the row,"
and how much be know about it,--and at the same time how little
be really know. everybody have hear that there have be a row, and
everybody know that there have be a lady in the case. but there
seem to be a general idea that the lady have be in some way
misuse, and that Arthur Fletcher have come forward like a Paladin to
protect she. a letter have be write, and the husband, ogre-like,
have intercept the letter. the lady be the most unfortunate of
human beings,--or would have be but for that consolation which she
must have in the constancy of her old lover. as to all these matter

world know that Arthur Fletcher have go to Silverbridge, have stand
for the borough, and have take the seat away from his rival,--because
that rival have rob he of his bride. how the robbery have be
effect the world could not quite say. the world be still of
opinion that the lady be violently attach to the man she have
not marry. but Captain Gunner explain it all clearly to Major
Pountney by assert that the poor girl have be coerce into the
marriage by her father. and thus Arthur Fletcher find himself almost
as much a hero in London as at Longbarns.
Fletcher have not be above a week in town, and have become heartily
sick of the rumour which in various shape make their way round to
his own ear, when he receive an invitation from Mr. Wharton to
go and dine with he at a tavern call the Jolly Blackbird. the
invitation surprise him,--that he should be ask by such a man
to dine at such a place,--but he accept it as a matter of course.
he be indeed much interested in a Bill for the drainage of common
land which be to be discuss in the House that night; there be
a good deal of common land round Silverbridge, and he have some idea
of make his first speech,--but he calculate that he might get his
dinner and yet be back in time for the debate. so he go to the

the neighbourhood of Portugal Street, which have manage not to get
itself pull down a dozen year ago on behalf of the Law Courts
which be to bless some come generation. Arthur have never be
there before and be surprised at the black wainscoting, the black
