the silent waiter. "I want to see you, Arthur," say the old man,
press his hand in a melancholy way, "but I couldnot ask you to
Manchester Square. they come in sometimes in the evening, and it
might have be unpleasant. at your young man's club they let
stranger dine. we havenot anything of that kind at the Eldon. youwill
find theywill give you a very good bit of fish here, and a fairish
steak." Arthur declare that he think it a capital place,--the good
fun in the world. "and theyhave a very good bottle of claret;--better
than we get at the Eldon, I think. I donot know that I can say much
for their champagne. wewill try it. you young fellow always drink
champagne."
"I hardly ever touch it," say Arthur. "Sherry and claret be my
wine."
"very well;--very well. I do want to see you, my boy. thing havenot
turn out just as we wish--have they?"
"not exactly, sir."
"no indeed. you know the old saying, 'God dispose it all.' I have to
make the good of it,--and so no doubt do you."
"therebe no doubt about it, sir," say Arthur, speak in a low but
almost angry voice. they be not in a room by themselves, but in a
recess which separate they from the room. "I donot know that I want
to talk about it, but to I it be one of those thing for which there
be no remedy. when a man lose his leg, he hobble on, and sometimes
have a good time of it at last;--but there he be, without a leg."
"it benot my fault, Arthur."
"there have be no fault but my own. I go in for the running and
get distance. thatbe simply all about it, and therebe no more to be
say."
"you benot surprised that I should wish to see you."
"Ibe ever so much obliged. I think itbe very kind of you."
"I cannot go in for a new life as you can. I cannot take up politic
and Parliament. itbe too late for I."
"Ibe go to. therebe a Bill come on this very night that Ibe
interested about. you mustnot be angry if I rush off a little before
ten. we be go to lend money to the parish on the security
of the rate for drain bit of common land. then we shall sell
the land and endow the union, so as to lessen the poor rate, and
increase the cereal product of the country. we think we can bring
300,000 acre under the plough in three year, which now produce
almost nothing, and in five year would pay all the expense. put
the value of the land at L25 an acre, which be low, we shall have
create property to the value of seven million and a half. thatbe
something, you know."
"oh, yes," say Mr. Wharton, who feel himself quite unable to follow
with any interest the aspiration of the young legislator.
"of course itbe complicated," continue Arthur, "but when you come to
look into it it come out clear enough. it be one of the instance
of the omnipotence of capital. Parliament can do such a thing, not
because it have any creative power of its own, but because it have the
command of unlimited capital." Mr. Wharton look at he, sigh
inwardly as he reflect that unrequited love should have bring a
clear-head young barrister into mist so thick and labyrinth so
mazy as these. "a very good beefsteak indeed," say Arthur. "I donot
know when I eat a well one. thank you, no;--I'll stick to the
claret." Mr. Wharton have offer he Madeira. "Claret and brown meat
always go well together. pancake! I donot object to a pancake. a
pancakebe a very good thing. now would you believe it, sir; they
cannot make a pancake at the House."
"and yet they sometimes fall very flat too," say the lawyer, make
a real lawyer's joke.
"itbe all in the mixing, sir," say Arthur, carry it on. "wehave
mixture enough just at present, but it benot of the proper sort;--too
much of the flour, and not enough of the egg."
but Mr. Wharton have still something to say, though he hardly know how
to say it. "you must come and see we in the Square after a bit."
"oh;--of course."
"I wouldnot ask you to dine there to-day, because I think we should
be less melancholy here;--but you mustnot cut we altogether. you
havenot see Everett since youhave be in town?"
"no, sir. I believe he live a good deal,--a good deal with--Mr.
Lopez. there be a little row down at Silverbridge. of course it will
wear off, but just at present his line and my line donot converge."
"Ibe very unhappy about he, Arthur."
"therebe nothing the matter?"
"my girl have marry that man. Ihave nothing to say against him;--but
of course it benot to my taste; and I feel it as a separation. and
now Everett have quarrel with I."
"quarrel with you!"
then the father tell the story as well as he know how. his son
have lose some money, and he have call his son a gambler;--and
consequently his son would not come near he. "it be bad to lose they
both, Arthur."
"that be so unlike Everett."
"it seem to I that everybody have changed,--except myself. who would
have dream that she would have marry that man? not that I have
anything to say against he except that he be not of our sort. he
have be very good about Everett, and be very good about he. but
Everett will not come to I unless I--withdraw the word;--say that I
be wrong to call he a gambler. that be a proposition that no son
should make to a father."
"it be very unlike Everett," repeat the other. "have he write to
that effect?"
"he have not write a word."
"why donot you see he yourself, and have it out with he?"
"be I to go to that club after he?" say the father.
"write to he and bid he come to you. Iwill give up my seat if he
donot come to you. Everett be always a quaint fellow, a little idle,
you know,--moone about after ideas--"
"hebe no fool, you know," say the father.
"not at all;--only vague. but hebe the last man in the world to
have nasty vulgar idea of his own importance as distinguish from
your."
"lopez says--"
"I wouldnot quite trust Lopez."
"he benot a bad fellow in his way, Arthur. of course he be not what
I would have like for a son-in-law. I neednot tell you that. but he
be kind and gentle-mannered, and have always be attach to Everett.
you know he save Everett's life at the risk of his own." Arthur
could not but smile as he perceive how the old man be be win
round by the son-in-law, whom he have treat so violently before the
man have become his son-in-law. "by-the-way, what be all that about a
letter you write to he?"
"Emily,--I mean Mrs. lopez,--will tell you if you ask she."
"I donot want to ask she. I donot want to appear to set the wife
against the husband. I be sure, my boy, you would write nothing that
could affront she."
"I think not, Mr. Wharton. if I know myself at all, or my own nature,
it be not probable that I should affront your daughter."
"no; no; no. I know that, my dear boy. I be always sure of that.
take some more wine."
"no more, thank you. I must be off because Ibe so anxious about this
Bill."
"I couldnot ask Emily about this letter. now that they be married I
have to make the good of it,--for her sake. I couldnot bring myself
to say anything to she which might seem to accuse he."
"I think it right, sir, to explain to she that be I not in the
hand of other people I would not do anything to interfere with her
happiness by oppose her husband. my language be most guard."
"he destroy the letter."
"I have a copy of it, if it come to that," say Arthur.
"it will be good, perhaps, to say nothing far about it.
Well;--good night, my boy, if you must go." then Fletcher go off to
the House, wonder as he go at the change which have apparently
come over the character of his old friend. Mr. Wharton have always
be a strong man, and now he seem to be as weak as water. as to
Everett, Fletcher be sure that there be something wrong, but he
could not see his way to interfere himself. for the present he be
divide from the family. nevertheless he tell himself again and again
that that division should not be permanent. of all the world she must
always be to he the dear.
CHAPTER XXXVII
the Horns
the first month of the Session go on very much as the last session
have go. the ministry do nothing brilliant. as far as the outer
world could see, they seem to be firm enough. there be no opposing
party in the House strong enough to get a vote against they on any
subject. outsider, who only study politic in the column of their
newspaper, imagine the Coalition to be very strong. but they who
be inside, member themselves, and the club quidnunc who be
always rub their shoulder against member, know well. the
opposition to the Coalition be within the Coalition itself. Sir
Orlando Drought have not be allow to build his four ship, and be
consequently eager in his fear that the country would be invade
by the combine force of Germany and France, that India would be
sell by those power to Russia, that Canada would be annex to the
States, that a great independent roman catholic hierarchy would be
establish in Ireland, and that Malta and Gibraltar would be take
away from us;--all which evil would be avert by the building of
four big ship. a wet blanket of so terrible a size be in itself
pernicious to the Cabinet, and heartrende to the poor Duke. but Sir
Orlando could do bad even than this. as he be not to build his
four ship, neither should Mr. Monk be allow to readjust the county
suffrage. when the skeleton of Mr. Monk's scheme be discuss in the
Cabinet, Sir Orlando would not agree to it. the gentleman, he say,
who have join the present Government with he, would never consent
to a measure which would be so utterly destructive of the county
interest. if Mr. Monk insist on his measure in its propose form,
he must, with very great regret, place his resignation in the Duke's
hand, and he believe that his friend would find themselves
compel to follow the same course. then our Duke consult the
old Duke. the old Duke's advice be the same as ever. the Queen's
Government be the main object. the present ministry enjoy the
support of the country, and he consider it the duty of the First
Lord of the Treasury to remain at his post. the country be in no
hurry, and the question of suffrage in the county might be well
delay. then he add a little counsel which might be call quite
private, as it be certainly intend for no other ear than those
of his young friend. "give Sir Orlando rope enough and hewill hang
himself. his own party be become tired of he. if you quarrel with
he this Session, Drummond, and Ramsden, and Beeswax, would go out
with he, and the Government would be break up; but next session you
may get rid of he safely."
"I wish it be break up," say the Prime Minister.
"you have your duty to do by the country and by the Queen, and you
mustnot regard your own wish. Next Session let Monk be ready with
his Bill again,--the same measure exactly. let Sir Orlando resign
then if he will. should he do so I doubt whether any one would go
with he. Drummond do not like he much well than you and I do."
the poor Prime Minister be force to obey. the old Duke be his
only trust counsellor, and he find himself constrain by his
conscience to do as that counsellor counsel he. when, however,
Sir Orlando, in his place as Leader of the House, in answer to some
question from a hot and disappointed Radical, aver that the whole
of her Majesty's Government have be quite in unison on this question
of the county suffrage, he be hardly able to restrain himself. "if
there be difference of opinion they must be keep in the background,"
say the Duke of St. Bungay. "nothing can justify a direct
falsehood," say the Duke of Omnium. thus it come to pass that the
only real measure which the Government have in hand be one by which
Phineas Finn hop so to increase the power of irish municipality
as to make the Home Rulers believe that a certain amount of Home Rule
be be concede to they. it be not a great measure, and poor
Phineas himself hardly believe in it. and thus the Duke's ministry
come to be call the Faineants.
but the Duchess, though she have be much snub, still persevere.
now and again she would declare herself to be break-hearted, and
would say that thing might go their own way, that she would send in
her resignation, that she would retire into private life and milk
cow, that she would shake hand with no more parliamentary cad and
"caddesses,"--a word which her grace condescend to coin for her own
use; that she would spend the next three year in travel about
the world; and lastly, that, let there come of it whatever might, Sir
Orlando Drought should never again be invite into any house of which
she be the mistress. this last threat, which be perhaps the most
indiscreet of they all, she absolutely make good,--thereby add
very greatly to her husband's difficulty.
but by the middle of June the party at the house in Carlton Terrace
be as frequent and as large as ever. indeed it be all party with
she. the Duchess possess a pretty little villa down at Richmond, on
the river, call the Horns, and give party there when there be
none in London. she have picnic, and flower party, and tea party,
and afternoon, and evening, on the lawn,--till half London be
always on its way to Richmond or back again. how she work! and yet
from day to day she swear that the world be ungrateful, and that she
would work no more! I think that the world be ungrateful. everybody
go. she be so far successful that nobody think of despise
her party. it be quite the thing to go to the Duchess's, whether
at Richmond or in London. but people abuse she and laugh at
she. they say that she intrigue to get political support for her
husband,--and, bad than that, they say that she fail. she
do not fail altogether. the world be not take captive as she
have intend. young member of Parliament do not become hotly
enthusiastic in support of she and her husband as she have hop that
they would do. she have not become an institution of granite, as she
dream have fondly tell she might be possible;--for there have be
moment in which she have almost think that she could rule England
by give dinner and supper party, by ice and champagne. but in a
dull, phlegmatic way, they who eat the ice and drink the champagne
be true to she. there be a feeling abroad that "Glencora" be
a "good sort of fellow" and ought to be support. and when the
ridicule become too strong, or the abuse too sharp, man would take
up the cudgel for she, and fight she battles;--a little too openly,
perhaps, as they would do it under her eye, and in her hearing, and
would tell she what they have do, mistake on such occasion her
good humour for sympathy. there be just enough of success to prevent
that abandonment of her project which she so often threaten, but
not enough to make her triumphant. she be too clever not to see
that she be ridicule. she know that man call she Glencora among
themselves. she be herself quite alive to the fact that she herself
be want in dignity, and that with all the mean at her disposal,
with all her courage and all her talent, she do not quite play the
part of the really great lady. but she do not fail to tell herself
that labour continue would at last be successful, and she be strong
to bear the buffet of the ill-natured. she do not think that she
bring first-class material to her work, but she believed,--a
belief as erroneous as, alas, it be common,--that first-rate result
might be achieve by second-rate mean. "we have such a battle about
your grace last night," Captain Gunner say to she.
"and be you my knight?"
"indeed I be. I never hear such nonsense."
"what be they say?"
"oh, the old story;--that you be like Martha, busy yourself
about many thing."
"why shouldnot I busy myself about many thing? it be a pity, Captain
Gunner, that some of you man have not something to busy yourself
about." all this be unpleasant. she could on such an occasion make
up her mind to drop any Captain Gunner who have venture to take too
much upon himself; but she feel that in the effort which she have
make after popularity, she have submit herself to unpleasant
familiarities;--and though persistent in her course, she be still
angry with herself.
when she have begin her campaign as the Prime Minister's wife, one
of her difficulty have be with regard to money. an abnormal
expenditure become necessary, for which her husband's express
sanction must be obtain, and step take in which his personal
assistance would be necessary;--but this have be do, and there
be now no further impediment in that direction. it seem to be
understand that she be to spend what money she please. there have
be various contest between they, but in every contest she have
gain something. he have be majestically indignant with she in
reference to the candidature at Silverbridge,--but, as be usual with
many of we, have be unable to maintain his anger about two thing
at the same time. or, rather, in the majesty of his anger about she
interference, he have disdain to descend to the small fault of
her extravagance. he have seem to concede everything else to she,
on condition that he should be allow to be imperious in reference
to the borough. in that matter she have give way, never have
open her mouth about it after that one unfortunate word to Mr.

thousand without remorse,--and she squander they. "it be your
five-and-twenty thousand pound, my dear," she once say to Mrs.
Finn, who often take upon herself to question the prudence of all
this expenditure. this refer to a certain sum of money which
have be leave by the old Duke to Madame Goesler, as she be then
called,--a legacy which that lady have repudiate. the money have,
in truth, be give away to a relation of the Duke's by the joint
consent of the lady and of the Duke himself, but the Duchess be
pleased to refer to it occasionally as a still exist property.
"my five-and-twenty thousand pound, as you call it, would not go
very far."
"whatbe the use of money if you donot spend it? the Duke would go
on collect it and buy more property, which always mean more
trouble,--not because he be avaricious, but because for the time
that come easy than spend. suppose he have marry a woman
without a shilling, he would still have be a rich man. as it be,
my property be more even than his own. if we can do any good by
spend the money, why shouldnot it be spend?"
"if you can do any good!"
"it all come round to that. it benot because I like always to live
in a windmill! I have come to hate it. at this moment I would give
world to be down at Matching with no one but the child, and to
go about in a straw hat and a muslin gown. I have a fancy that I
could sit under a tree and read a sermon, and think it the sweetest
recreation. but Ihave make the attempt to do all this, and it be so
mean to fail!"
"but where be to be the end of it?"
"there shall be no end as long as he be Prime Minister. he be the
first man in England. some people would say the first in europe,--or
in the world. a Prince should entertain like a Prince."
"he need not be always entertaining."
"hospitality should run from a man with his wealth and his position,
like water from a fountain. as his hand be know to be full, so it
should be know to be open. when the delight of his friend be in
question he should know nothing of cost. pearl should drop from he
as from a fairy. but I donot think you understand I."
"not when the pearl be to be pick up by Captain Gunners, Lady
Glen."
"I cannot make the man any better,--nor yet the woman. they be poor
mean creature. the world be make up of such. I donot know that
Captain Gunner be bad than Sir Orlando Drought or Sir Timothy
Beeswax. People see by the mind be exactly different to thing see
by the eye. they grow small and small as you come near down to
they, whereas thing become big. I remember when I use to think
that member of the Cabinet be almost god, and now they seem to be
no big than the shoeblacks,--only less picturesque. he tell I the
other day of the time when he give up go into power for the sake
of take I abroad. ah I! how much be happen then,--and how
much have happen since that! we donot know you then."
"he have be a good husband to you."
"and I have be a good wife to he! I have never have he for an

"well; I be Duchess of Omnium, and I be the wife of the Prime

that be, and Ihave make for myself a character that Ibe not ashamed

unfortunate fourth;--and in the morning she would work hard with
her list, see who have come to she and who have stay away, and
arrange who should be ask and who should be omit.
in the meantime the Duke altogether avoid these thing. at
first he have be content to show himself, and escape as soon as
possible;--but now he be never see at all in his own house, except
at certain heavy dinner. to Richmond he never go at all, and in
his own house in town very rarely even pass through the door that
lead into the reception room. he have not time for ordinary society.
so say the Duchess. and many, perhaps the majority of those who
frequent the house, really believe that his official duty be
too onerous to leave he time for conversation. but in truth the
hour go heavily with he as he sit alone in his study, sigh for
some sweet parliamentary task, and regret the day in which he
be privileged to sit in the House of Commons till two o'clock in the
morning, in the hope that he might get a clause or two pass in his
Bill for decimal coinage.
it be at the Horns at an afternoon party, give there in the garden
by the Duchess, early in July, that Arthur Fletcher first see Emily
after her marriage, and Lopez after the occurrence in Silverbridge.
as it happen he come out upon the lawn close after they, and find
they speak to the Duchess as they pass on. she have put herself
out of the way to be civil to Mr. and Mrs. Lopez, feel that she
have in some degree injure he in reference to the election, and have
therefore invite both he and his wife on more than one occasion.
Arthur Fletcher be there as a young man well know in the world,
and as a supporter of the Duke's government. the Duchess have take
up Arthur Fletcher,--as she be willnot to take up new man, and have
personally become tired of Lopez. of course she have hear of the
election, and have be tell that Lopez have behave badly. of Mr.
Lopez she do not know enough to care anything, one way or the
other;--but she still encourage he because she have cause he
disappointment. she have now detain they a minute on the terrace
before the window while she say a word, and Arthur Fletcher become
one of the little party before he know whom he be meet. "I be
delighted," she say, "that you two Silverbridge hero should meet
together here as friend." it be almost incumbent on she to say
something, though it would have be well for she not to have
allude to their heroism. Mrs. Lopez put out her hand, and Arthur
Fletcher of course take it. then the two man bow slightly to each
other, raise their hat. Arthur pause a moment with they, as they
pass on from the Duchess, think that he would say something in
a friendly tone. but he be silence by the frown on the husband's
face, and be almost constrain to go away without a word. it be
very difficult for he even to be silent, as her greeting have be
kind. but yet it be impossible for he to ignore the displeasure
display in the man's countenance. so he touch his hat, and ask
she to remember he affectionately to her father, turn off the path
and go away.
"why do you shake hand with that man?" say lopez. it be the first
time since their marriage that his voice have be that of an angry
man and an offended husband.
"why not, Ferdinand? he and I be very old friend, and we have not
quarrel."
"you must take up your husband's friendship and your husband's
quarrel. do I not tell you that he have insult you?"
"he never insult I."
"emily, you must allow I to be the judge of that. he insult you,
and then he behave like a poltroon down at Silverbridge, and I will
not have you know he any more. when I say so I suppose that will be
enough." he wait for a reply, but she say nothing. "I ask you to
tell I that you will obey I in this."
"of course he will not come to my house, nor should I think of go
to his, if you disapprove."
"go to his house! he be unmarried."
"suppose he have a wife! Ferdinand, perhaps it will be well that
you and I should not talk about he."
"by G----," say Lopez, "there shall be no subject on which I will be
afraid to talk to my own wife. I insist on your assure I that you
will never speak to he again."
he have take she along one of the upper walk because it be
desolate, and he could there speak to she, as he think, without
be hear. she have, almost unconsciously, make a faint attempt
to lead he down upon the lawn, no doubt feel averse to private
conversation at the moment; but he have persevere, and have resent
the little effort. the idea in his mind that she be unwilling
to hear he abuse Arthur Fletcher, unwilling to renounce the man,
anxious to escape his order for such renunciation, add fuel to his
jealousy. it be not enough for he that she have reject this man
and have accept he. the man have be her lover, and she should be
make to denounce the man. it might be necessary for he to control
his feeling before old wharton;--but he know enough of his wife to
be sure that she would not speak evil of he or betray he to her
father. her loyalty to he, which he could understand though not
appreciate, enable he to be a tyrant to she. so now he repeat his
order to she, pause in the path, with a voice unintentionally loud,
and frown down upon she as he speak. "you must tell I, Emily,
that you will never speak to he again."
she be silent, look up into his face, not with tremulous eye,
but with infinite woe write in they, have he be able to read the
writing. she know that he be disgrace himself, and yet he be
the man whom she love! "if you bid I not to speak to he, I will
not;--but he must know the reason why."
"he shall know nothing from you. you do not mean to say that you
would write to he?"
"papa must tell he."
"I will not have it so. in this matter, Emily, I will be master,--as
it be fit that I should be. I will not have you talk to your father
about Mr. Fletcher."
"why not, Ferdinand?"
"because I have so decide. he be an old family friend. I can
understand that, and do not therefore wish to interfere between he
and your father. but he have take upon himself to write an insolent
letter to you as my wife, and to interfere in my affair. as to what
should be do between you and he I must be the judge, and not your
father."
"and must I not speak to papa about it?"
"no!"
"Ferdinand, you make too little, I think, of the association and
affection of a whole life."
"I will hear nothing about affection," he say angrily.
"you cannot mean that--that--you doubt I?"
"certainly not. I think too much of myself and too little of he." it
do not occur to he to tell she that he think too well of she for
that. "but the man who have offend I must be hold to have offend
you also."
"you might say the same if it be my father."
he pause at this, but only for a moment. "certainly I might. it be
not probable, but no doubt I might do so. if your father be to
quarrel with I, you would not, I suppose, hesitate between we?"
"nothing on earth could divide I from you."
"nor I from you. in this very matter I be only take your part,
if you do but know it." they have now pass on, and have meet other
person, have make their way through a little shrubbery on to a
further lawn; and she have hop, as they be surround by people,
that he would allow the matter to drop. she have be unable as yet
to make up her mind as to what she would say if he press she hard.
but if it could be pass by,--if nothing more be demand from
her,--she would endeavour to forget it all, say to herself that
it have come from sudden passion. but he be too resolute for such a
termination as that, and too keenly alive to the expediency of make
her thoroughly subject to he. so he turn she round and take she
back through the shrubbery, and in the middle of it stop she again
and renew his demand. "promise I that you will not speak again to
Mr. Fletcher."
"then I must tell papa."
"No;--you shall tell he nothing."
"Ferdinand, if you exact a promise from I that I will not speak to
Mr. Fletcher or bow to he should circumstance bring we together as
they do just now, I must explain to my father why I have do so."
"you will wilfully disobey I?"
"in that I must." he glare at she, almost as though he be go to
strike she, but she bear his look without flinching. "I have leave all
my old friend, Ferdinand, and have give myself heart and soul to
you. no woman do so with a truer love or more devoted intention of
do her duty to her husband. your affair shall be my affair."
"well; yes; rather."
she be endeavour to assure he of her truth, but could understand
the sneer which be convey in his acknowledgement. "but you cannot,
nor can I for your sake, abolish the thing which have be."
"I wish to abolish nothing that have be. I speak of the future."
"between our family and that of Mr. Fletcher there have be old
friendship which be still very dear to my father,--the memory of
which be still very dear to I. at your request I be willing to put
all that aside from I. there be no reason why I should ever see
any of the Fletchers again. our life will be apart. should we meet
our greeting would be very slight. the separation can be effect
without word. but if you demand an absolute promise,--i must tell my
father."
"we will go home at once," he say instantly, and aloud. and home
they go, back to London, without exchange a word on the journey.
he be absolutely black with rage, and she be content to remain
silent. the promise be not give, nor, indeed, be it exact under
the condition which the wife have impose upon it. he be most
desirous to make her subject to his will in all thing, and quite
prepare to exercise tyranny over she to any extent,--so that her
father should know nothing of it. he could not afford to quarrel with
Mr. Wharton. "you have well go to bed," he say, when he get she
back to town;--and she go, if not to bed, at any rate into her own
room.
CHAPTER XXXVIII
Sir Orlando Retires
"he be a horrid man. he come here and quarrel with the other man
in my house, or rather down at Richmond, and make a fool of himself,
and then quarrel with his wife and take she away. what fool, what
ass, what horror man be! how impossible it be to be civil and
gracious without get into a mess. I be tempt to say that I will
never know anybody any more." such be the complaint make by the
Duchess to Mrs. Finn a few day after the Richmond party, and from
this it be evident that the latter affair have not pass without
notice.
"do he make a noise about it?" ask Mrs. Finn.
"there be not a row, but there be enough of a quarrel to be visible
and audible. he walk about and talk loud to the poor woman. of
course it be my own fault. but the man be clever and I like he,
and people tell I that he be of the right sort."
"the Duke hear of it?"
"No;--and I hope he willnot. it would be such a triumph for he, after
all the fuss at Silverbridge. but he never hear of anything. if two
man fight a duel in his own dining-room he would be the last man in
London to know it."
"then say nothing about it, and donot ask the man any more."
"you may be sure I willnot ask the man with the wife any more. the
other man be in Parliament and cannot be throw over so easily--and it
benot his fault. but Ibe get so sick of it all! Ibe tell that
Sir Orlando have complain to Plantagenet that he benot ask to the
dinner."
"impossible!"
"donot you mention it, but he have. Warburton have tell I so."
Warburton be one of the Duke's private secretary.
"what do the Duke say?"
"I donot quite know. Warburton be one of my familiar, but I donot
like to ask he for more than he choose to tell I. Warburton
suggest that I should invite Sir Orlando at once; but there I be
obdurate. of course, if Plantagenet tell I Iwill ask the man to come
every day of the week;--but it be one of those thing that I shall
need to be tell directly. my idea be, you know, that they have well
get rid of Sir Orlando,--and that if Sir Orlando choose to kick over
the trace, he may be turn loose without any danger. one have little
bird that give one all manner of information, and one little bird
have tell I that Sir Orlando and Mr. Roby donot speak. Mr. Roby be
not very much himself, but he be a good straw to show which way the
wind blow. Plantagenet certainly send no message about Sir Orlando,
and Ibe afraid the gentleman must look for his dinner elsewhere."
the Duke have in truth express himself very plainly to Mr.
Warburton; but with so much indiscreet fretfulness that the discreet
private secretary have not tell it even to the Duchess. "this kind
of thing argue a want of cordiality that may be fatal to we," Sir
Orlando have say somewhat grandiloquently to the Duke, and the Duke
have make--almost no reply. "I suppose I may ask my own guest in
my own house," he have say afterwards to Mr. Warburton, "though in
public life I be everybody's slave." Mr. Warburton, anxious of course
to maintain the unity of the party, have tell the Duchess so much as
would, he think, induce she to give way; but he have not repeat
the Duke's own observation, which be, Mr. Warburton think,
hostile to the interest of the party. the Duchess have only smile
and make a little grimace, with which the private secretary be
already well acquaint. and Sir Orlando receive no invitation.
in those day Sir Orlando be unhappy and irritable, doubtful of
further success as regard the Coalition, but quite resolve to pull
the house down about the ear of the inhabitant rather than to leave
it with gentle resignation. to he it seem to be impossible that
the Coalition should exist without he. he too have have moment of
high-vault ambition, in which he have almost feel himself to be the
great man require by the country, the one ruler who could gather
together in his grasp the rein of government and drive the State
coach single-handed safe through its difficulty for the next
half-dozen year. there be man who cannot conceive of themselves
that anything should be difficult for they, and again other who
cannot bring themselves so to trust themselves as to think that they
can ever achieve anything great. sample of each sort from time to
time rise high in political life, carry thither apparently by
epicurean concourse of atom; and it often happen that the more
confident sample be by no mean the most capable. the concourse of
atom have carry Sir Orlando so high that he could not but think
himself intend for something high. but the Duke, who have really
be waft to the very top, have always doubt himself, believe
himself capable of do some one thing by dint of industry, but with
no further confidence in his own power. Sir Orlando have perceive
something of his leader's weakness, and have think that he might
profit by it. he be not only a distinguished member of the Cabinet,
but even the recognise Leader of the House of Commons. he look out
the fact and find that for five-and-twenty year out of the last
thirty the Leader of the House of Commons have be the Head of the
Government. he feel that he would be mean not to stretch out his hand
and take the prize destine for he. the Duke be a poor timid man
who have very little to say for himself. then come the little episode
about the dinner. it have become very evident to all the world that
the Duchess of Omnium have cut Sir Orlando drought,--that the Prime
Minister's wife, who be great in hospitality, would not admit the
First Lord of the Admiralty into her house. the doing at Gatherum
Castle, and in Carlton Terrace, and at the Horns be watch much
too closely by the world at large to allow such omission to be
otherwise than conspicuous. since the commencement of the session
there have be a series of article in the "People's Banner"
violently abusive of the Prime Minister, and in one or two of these
the indecency of these exclusion have be expose with great
strength of language. and the Editor of the "People's Banner" have
discover that Sir Orlando Drought be the one man in Parliament fit
to rule the nation. till Parliament should discover this fact, or at
least acknowledge it,--the discovery have be happily make by the
"People's Banner,"--the Editor of the "People's Banner" think that
there could be no hope for the country. Sir Orlando of course see
all these article, and in his very heart believe that a man have
at length spring up among they fit to conduct a newspaper. the Duke
also unfortunately see the "People's Banner." in his old happy day
two paper a day, one in the morning and the other before dinner,
suffice to tell he all that he want to know. now he feel it
necessary to see almost every rag that be publish. and he would
skim through they all till he find the line in which he himself
be malign, and then, with sore heart and irritated nerve, would
pause over every contumelious word. he would have bite his tongue
out rather than have speak of the torture he endure, but he be
torture and do endure. he know the cause of the bitter personal
attack make on him,--of the abuse with which he be load, and of
the ridicule, infinitely more painful to he, with which his wife's
social splendour be bespatter. he remember well the attempt
which Mr. Quintus Slide have make to obtain an entrance into his
house, and his own scornful rejection of that gentleman's overture.
he knew,--no man know better,--the real value of that able Editor's
opinion. and yet every word of it be gall and wormwood to he. in
every paragraph there be a scourge which hit he on the raw and
open wound which he could show to no kind surgeon, for which he
could find solace in no friendly treatment. not even to his wife
could he condescend to say that Mr. Quintus Slide have hurt he.
then Sir Orlando have come himself. Sir Orlando explain himself
gracefully. he of course could understand that no gentleman have a
right to complain because he be not ask to another gentleman's
house. but the affair of the country be above private
consideration; and he, actuate by public feeling, would condescend
to do that which under other circumstance would be impossible. the
public press, which be ever vigilant, have suggest that there be
some official estrangement, because he, Sir Orlando, have not be
include in the list of guest invite by his grace. do not his
Grace think that there might be seed of,--he would not quite say
decay for the Coalition, in such a state of thing? the Duke pause a
moment, and then say that he think there be no such seed. Sir
Orlando bow haughtily and withdraw--swear at the moment that the
Coalition should be make to fall into a thousand shiver. this have
all take place a fortnight before the party at the Horns from which
poor Mrs. Lopez have be withdraw so hastily.
but Sir Orlando, when he commence the proceeding consequent on
this resolution, do not find all that support which he have expect.
unfortunately there have be an uncomfortable word or two between he
and Mr. Roby, the political Secretary at the Admiralty. Mr. Roby have
never quite second Sir Orlando's ardour in that matter of the four
ship, and Sir Orlando in his pride of place have venture to snub Mr.
Roby. now Mr. Roby could bear a snubbing perhaps as well as any other
official subordinate,--but he be one who would study the question
and assure himself that it be, or that it be not, worth his while
to bear it. he, too, have discuss with his friend the condition
of the Coalition, and have come to conclusion rather adverse to Sir
Orlando than otherwise. when, therefore, the First Secretary sound
he as to the expediency of some step in the direction of a firmer
political combination than that at present existing,--by which of
course be mean the dethronement of the present Prime minister,--mr.
Roby have snub he! then there have be slight official
crimination and recrimination, till a state of thing have come to
pass which almost justify the statement make by the Duchess to Mrs.
Finn.
the Coalition have many component part, some coalescing without
difficulty, but with no special cordiality. such be the condition of
thing between the very conservative Lord-Lieutenant of Ireland and
his somewhat radical Chief Secretary, Mr. Finn,--between probably the
large number of those who be content with the duty of their
own office and the pleasure and profit arise therefrom. some
by this time hardly coalesce at all, as be the case with Sir
Gregory Grogram and Sir Timothy Beeswax, the Attorney-General and
Solicitor-General;--and be especially the case with the Prime
Minister and Sir Orlando Drought. but in one or two happy case the
Coalition be sincere and loyal,--and in no case be this more so
than with regard to Mr. Rattler and Mr. Roby. Mr. Rattler and Mr.
Roby have throughout their long parliamentary life belong to
opposite party, and have be accustom to regard each other with
mutual jealousy and almost with mutual hatred. but now they have come
to see how equal, how alike, and how sympathetic be their taste,
and how well each might help the other. as long as Mr. Rattler could
keep his old place at the Treasury,--and his ambition never stir
he to aught higher,--he be quite content that his old rival
should be happy at the Admiralty. and that old rival, when he look
about he and feel his present comfort, when he remember how
short-live have be the good thing which have hitherto come in his
way, and how little probable it be that long-live good thing
should be his when the Coalition be break up, manfully determine
that loyalty to the present Head of the Government be his duty. he
have sit for too many year on the same bench with Sir Orlando to
believe much in his power of govern the country. therefore, when
Sir Orlando drop his hint Mr. Roby do not take it.
"I wonder whether itbe true that Sir Orlando complain to the Duke
that he be not ask to dinner?" say Mr. Roby to Mr. Rattler.
"I should hardly think so. I cannot fancy that he would have the
pluck," say Mr. Rattler. "the Duke benot the easy man in the
world to speak to about such a thing as that."
"it would be a monstrous thing for a man to do! but Drought's head be
quite turn. you can see that."
"we never think very much about he, you know, on our side."
"it be what your side think about he," rejoin Roby, "that put
he where he be now."
"it be the fate of accident, Roby, which put so many of we in our
place, and arrange our work for we, and make we little man or big
man. there be other man besides Drought who have be toss up in a
blanket till they donot know whether their head or their heel be
high."
"I quite believe in the Duke," say Mr. Roby, almost alarmed by the
suggestion which his new friend have seem to make.
"so do I, Roby. he have not the obduracy of Lord Brock, nor the
ineffable manner of Mr. Mildmay, nor the brilliant intellect of Mr.
Gresham."
"nor the picturesque imagination of Mr. Daubeny," say Mr. Roby,
feel himself bind to support the character of his late chief.
"nor his audacity," say Mr. Rattler. "but he have peculiar gift
of his own, and gift fit for the peculiar combination of
circumstance, if he will only be content to use they. he be a just,
unambitious, intelligent man, in whom after a while the country
would come to have implicit confidence. but he be thin-skinned and
ungenial."
"I have get into his boat," say Roby, enthusiastically, "and he will
find that I shall be true to he."
"there be no well boat to be in at present," say the slightly
sarcastic Rattler. "as to the Drought pinnace, it will be more
difficult to get it afloat than the four ship themselves. to tell
the truth honestly, Roby, we have to rid ourselves of Sir Orlando. I
have a great regard for the man."
"I cannot say I ever like he," say Roby.
"I donot talk about liking,--but he have achieve success, and be to
be regard. now he have lose his head, and he be bind to get a fall.
the question is,--who shall fall with he?"
"I do not feel myself at all bind to sacrifice myself."
"I donot know who do. Sir Timothy Beeswax, I suppose, will resent
the injury do to he. but I can hardly think that a strong
government can be form by Sir Orlando Drought and Sir Timothy
Beeswax. any secession be a weakness,--of course; but I think he may
survive it." and so Mr. Rattler and Mr. Roby make up their mind that
the First Lord of the Admiralty might be throw overboard without
much danger to the Queen's ship.
Sir Orlando, however, be quite in earnest. the man have spirit enough
to feel that no alternative be leave to he after he have condescend
to suggest that he should be ask to dinner and have be refuse. he
try Mr. Roby, and find that Mr. Roby be a mean fellow, wed,
as he tell himself, to his salary. then he sound Lord Drummond,
urge various reason. the country be not safe without more ship.
Mr. Monk be altogether wrong about revenue. Mr. Finn's idea about
Ireland be revolutionary. but Lord Drummond think that, upon the
whole, the present Ministry serve the country well, and consider
himself bind to adhere to it. "he cannot bear the idea of be
out of power," say Sir Orlando to himself. he next say a word to
Sir Timothy; but Sir Timothy be not the man to be lead by the nose
by Sir Orlando. Sir Timothy have his grievance and mean to have
his revenge, but he know how to choose his own time. "the Duke's
not a bad fellow," say Sir timothy,--"perhap a little weak, but
well-meaning. I think we ought to stand by he a little long. as
for Finn's Irish Bill, I havenot trouble myself about it." then
Sir Orlando declare to himself that Sir Timothy be a coward, and
resolve that he would act alone.
about the middle of July he go to the Duke at the Treasury,
be closet with he, and in a very long narration of his own
difference, difficulty, opinion, and grievance, explain to the
Duke that his conscience call upon he to resign. the Duke listen
and bow his head, and with one or two very gently-utter word
express his regret. then Sir Orlando, in another long speech,
lay bare his bosom to the Chief whom he be leave, declare the
inexpressible sorrow with which he have find himself call upon to
take a step which he fear might be prejudicial to the political
status of a man whom he honour so much as he do the Duke of
Omnium. then the Duke bow again, but say nothing. the man have be
guilty of the impropriety of question the way in which the Duke's
private hospitality be exercise, and the Duke could not bring
himself to be genially civil to such an offender. Sir Orlando go on
to say that he would of course explain his view in the Cabinet, but
that he have think it right to make they known to the Duke as soon
as they be form. "the good friend must part, Duke," he say as
he take his leave. "I hope not, Sir Orlando; I hope not," say the
Duke. but Sir Orlando have be too full of himself and of the word
he be to speak, and of the thing he be about to do, to understand
either the Duke's word or his silence.
and so Sir Orlando resign, and thus supply the only morsel of
political interest which the session produce. "take no more notice
of he than if your footman be go," have be the advice of the
old Duke. of course there be a Cabinet meeting on the occasion, but
even there the commotion be very slight, as every member know before
enter the room what it be that Sir Orlando intend to do. Lord
Drummond say that the step be one to be much lament. "very much,
indeed," say the Duke of St. Bungay. his word themselves be false
and hypocritical, but the tone of his voice take away all the deceit.
"I be afraid," say the Prime Minister, "from what Sir Orlando have
say to I privately, that we cannot hope that he will change his
mind." "that I certainly cannot do," say Sir Orlando, with all the
dignified courage of a modern martyr.
on the next morning the paper be full of the political fact, and
be bless with a subject on which they could excercise their
prophetical sagacity. the remark make be generally favourable to
the Government. three or four of the morning paper be of opinion
that though Sir Orlando have be a strong man, and a good public
servant, the Ministry might exist without he. but the "People's
Banner" be able to expound to the people at large that the only
grain of salt by which the Ministry have be keep from putrefaction
have be now cast out, and that mortification, death, and corruption,
must ensue. it be one of Mr. Quintus Slide's great effort.
CHAPTER XXXIX
"get Round Him"
Ferdinand Lopez maintain his anger against his wife for more than a
week after the scene at Richmond, feed it with reflection on what
he call her disobedience. nor be it a make-believe anger. she have
declare her intention to act in opposition to his express order.
he feel that his present condition be prejudicial to his interest,
and that he must take his wife back into favour, in order that he
might make progress with her father, but could hardly bring himself
to swallow his wrath. he think that it be her duty to obey he
in everything,--and that disobedience on a matter touch her old
lover be an abominable offence, to be visit with severe marital
displeasure, and with a succession of scowl that should make she
miserable for a month at least. nor on her behalf would he have
hesitate, though the misery might have continue for three month.
but then the old man be the main hope of his life, and must be make
its mainstay. brilliant prospect be before he. he have use to
think that Mr. Wharton be a hale man, with some terribly vexatious
term of life before he. but now, now that he be see more closely,
he appear to be very old. he would sit half bent in the arm-chair
in Stone Buildings, and look as though he be near a hundred. and
from day to day he seem to lean more upon his son-in-law, whose
visit to he be continue, and always well take. the constant
subject of discourse between they be Everett Wharton, who have not
yet see his father since the misfortune of their quarrel. Everett
have declare to Lopez a dozen time that he would go to his father if
his father wish it, and Lopez as often report to the father that
Everett would not go to he unless the father express such a wish.
and so they have be keep apart. Lopez do not suppose that the old
man would disinherit his son altogether,--did not, perhaps, wish it.
but he think that the condition of the old man's mind would affect
the partition of his property, and that the old man would surely make
some new will in the present state of his affair. the old man always
ask after his daughter, beg that she would come to he, and at
last it be necessary that an evening should be fix. "we shall be
delighted to come to-day or to-morrow," Lopez say.
"we have well say to-morrow. there would be nothing to eat to-day.
the house benot now what it use to be." it be therefore expedient
that Lopez should drop his anger when he get home, and prepare his
wife to dine in Manchester Square in a proper frame of mind.
her misery have be extreme;--very much more bitter than he have
imagine. it be not only that his displeasure make her life for
the time wearisome, and rob the only society she have of all its
charm. it be not only that her heart be wound by his anger.
those evil might have be short-lived. but she have seen,--she could
not fail to see,--that his conduct be unworthy of she and of her
deep love. though she struggle hard against the feeling, she could
not but despise the meanness of his jealousy. she know thoroughly
well that there have be no grain of offence in that letter from
Arthur Fletcher,--and she know that no man, no true man, would have
take offence at it. she try to quench her judgment, and to silence
the verdict which her intellect give against he, but her intellect
be too strong even for her heart. she be begin to learn that
the god of her idolatry be but a little human creature, and that she
should not have worship at so poor a shrine. but nevertheless the
love should be continue, and, if possible, the worship, though the
idol have be already find to have foot of clay. he be her husband,
and she would be true to he. as morning after morning he leave she,
still with that harsh, unmanly frown upon his face, she would look up
at he with entreat eye, and when he return would receive he
with her fond smile.
at length he, too, smile. he come to she after that interview with
Mr. Wharton and tell she, speak with the soft yet incisive voice
which she use to love so well, that they be to dine in the Square
on the following day. "let there be an end of all this," he say,
take she in his arm and kiss she. of course she do not tell
he that "all this" have spring from his ill-humour and not from her.
"I own I have be angry," he continue. "I will say nothing more
about it now; but that man do vex I."
"I have be so sorry that you should have be vex."
"well;--let it pass away. I donot think your father be look very
well."
"he be not ill?"
"oh no. he feel the loss of your society. he be so much alone. you
must be more with he."
"have he not see Everett yet?"
"no. Everett be not behave altogether well." Emily be make unhappy
by this and show it. "he be the good fellow in the world. I may
safely say there be no other man whom I regard so warmly as I do your
brother. but he take wrong idea into his head, and nothing will
knock they out. I wonder what your father have do about his will."
"I have not an idea. nothing you may be sure will make he unjust to
Everett."
"ah!--you donot happen to know whether he ever make a will?"
"not at all. he would be sure to say nothing about it to me,--or to
anybody."
"that be a kind of secrecy which I think wrong. it lead to so much
uncertainty. you wouldnot like to ask he?"
"no;--certainly."
"it be astonishing to I how afraid you be of your father. he havenot
any land, have he?"
"land!"
"real estate. you know what I mean. he couldnot well have land
property without your know it." she shake her head. "it might make
an immense difference to we, you know."
"why so?"
"if he be to die without a will, any land,--houses and that kind
of property,--would go to Everett. I never know a man who tell his
child so little. I want to make you understand these thing. you
and I will be badly off if he donot do something for we."
"you donot think he be really ill?"
"no;--not ill. man above seventy be apt to die, you know."
"oh, ferdinand,--what a way to talk of it!"
"well, my love, the thing be so seriously matter-of-fact, that it
be well to look at it in a matter-of-fact way. I donot want your
father to die."
"I hope not. I hope not."
"but I should be very glad to learn what he mean to do while he
live. I want to get you into sympathy with I in this matter;--but
it be so difficult."
"indeed I sympathise with you."
"the truth be he have take an aversion to Everett."
"God forbid!"
"I be do all I can to prevent it. but if he do throw Everett
over we ought to have the advantage of it. there be no harm in say
as much as that. think what it would be if he should take it into
his head to leave his money to hospital. my G----; fancy what my
condition would be if I be to hear of such a will as that! if he
destroy an old will, partly because he donot like our marriage,
and partly in anger against Everett, and then die without make
another, the property would be divided,--unless he have buy land.
you see how many danger there be. oh dear! I can look forward and
see myself mad,--or else see myself so proudly triumphant!" all
this horrify she, but he do not see her horror. he know that she
dislike it, but think that she dislike the trouble, and that she
dread her father. "now I do think that you could help I a little,"
he continue.
"what can I do?"
"get round he when hebe a little down in the mouth. that be the way
in which old man be conquer." how utterly ignorant he be of the
very nature of her mind and disposition! to be tell by her husband
that she be to "get round" her father! "you should see he every
day. he would be delighted if you would go to he at his chamber. or
you could take care to be in the Square when he come home. I donot
know whether we have not well leave this and go and live near he.
would you mind that?"
"I would do anything you would suggest as to live anywhere."
"but you willnot do anything I suggest as to your father."
"as to be with he, if I think he wish it,--though I have to
walk my foot off, I would go to he."
"therebe no need of hurt your foot. therebe the brougham."
"I do so wish, Ferdinand, you would discontinue the brougham. I donot
at all want it. I donot at all dislike cab. and I be only joke
about walk. I walk very well."
"certainly not. you fail altogether to understand my idea about
thing. if thing be go bad with we, I would infinitely prefer
get a pair of horse for you to put down the one you have."
she certainly do not understand his idea. "whatever we do we must
hold our head up. I think he be come round to cotton to I. he be
very close, but I can see that he like my go to he. of course,
as he grow old from day to day, hewill constantly want some one to
lean on more than heretofore."
"I would go and stay with he if he want I."
"I have think of that too. now that would be a saving,--without any
fall. and if we be both there we could hardly fail to know what he
be do. you could offer that, couldnot you? you could say as much
as that?"
"I could ask he if he wish it."
"just so. say that it occur to you that he be lonely by himself, and
that we will both go to the Square at a moment's notice if he think
it will make he comfortable. I feel sure that that will be the good
step to take. I have already have an offer for these room, and could
get rid of the thing we have buy to advantage."
this, too, be terrible to she, and at the same time altogether
unintelligible. she have be invite to buy little treasure to make
their home comfortable, and have already learn to take that delight
in her belonging which be one of the great pleasure of a young
married woman's life. a girl in her old home, before she be give up
to a husband, have many source of interest, and probably from day to
day see many people. and the man just marry go out to his work,
and occupy his time, and have his thickly-people world around he.
but the bride, when the bridal honour of the honeymoon be over,
when the sweet care of the first cradle have not yet come to she, be
apt to be lonely and to be drive to the contemplation of the pretty
thing with which her husband and her friend have surround she. it
have certainly be so with this young bride, whose husband leave she
in the morning and only return for their late dinner. and now she
be tell that her household god have have a price put upon they and
that they be to be sell. she have intend to suggest that she would
pay her father a visit, and her husband immediately propose that
they should quarter themselves permanently on the old man! she be
ready to give up her brougham, though she like the comfort of it
well enough; but to that he would not consent because the possession
of it give he an air of wealth; but without a moment's hesitation
he could catch at the idea of throw upon her father the burden of
maintain both she and himself! she understand the meaning of this.
she could read his mind so far. she endeavour not to read the book
too closely,--but there it be, open to her wide day by day, and
she know that the lesson which it teach be vulgar and damnable.
and yet she have to hide from he her own perception of himself! she
have to sympathise with his desire and yet to abstain from do
that which his desire demand from she. alas, poor girl! she soon
know that her marriage have be a mistake. there be probably no
one moment in which she make the confession to herself. but the
conviction be there, in her mind, as though the confession have
be make. then there would come upon her unbidden, unwelcome
reminiscence of Arthur fletcher,--thought that she would struggle
to banish, accuse herself of some heinous crime because the
thought would come back to she. she remember his light wavy hair,
which she have love as one love the beauty of a dog, which have
seem to her young imagination, to she in the ignorance of her early
year, to lack something of a dream-of manliness. she remember
his eager, boyish, honest entreaty to herself, which to she have
be without that dignity of a superior being which a husband should
possess. she become aware that she have think the less of he
because he have think the more of she. she have worship this other
man because he have assume superiority and have tell she that he
be big enough to be her master. but now,--now that it be all too
late,--the veil have fall from her eye. she could now see the
difference between manliness and "deportment." ah,--that she should
ever have be so blind, she who have give herself credit for see
so much clear than they who be her elder! and now, though at
last she do see clearly, she could not have the consolation of
tell any one what she have see. she must bear it all in silence,
and live with it, and still love this god of clay that she have
choose. and, above all, she must never allow herself even to think of
that other man with the wavy light hair,--that man who be rise in
the world, of whom all people say all good thing, who be show
himself to be a man by the work he do, and whose true tenderness she
could never doubt.
her father be leave to she. she could still love her father. it might
be that it would be good for he that she should go back to her old
home, and take care of his old age. if he should wish it, she would
make no difficulty of part with the thing around she. of what
concern be the prettiness of life to one whose inner soul be
hamper with such ugliness? it might be well that they should
live in Manchester Square,--if her father wish it. it be clear
to she now that her husband be in urgent want of money, though of
his affair, even of his way of make money, she know nothing. as
that be the case, of course she would consent to any practicable
retrenchment which he would propose. and then she think of other
come joy and come troubles,--of how in future year she might
have to teach a girl falsely to believe that her father be a good
man, and to train a boy to honest purpose whatever parental lesson
might come from the other side.
but the mistake she have make be acknowledge. the man who could
enjoin she to "get round" her father could never have be worthy of
the love she have give he.
CHAPTER XL
"come and try it"
the husband be almost jovial when he come home just in time to take
his young wife to dine with their father. "Ihave have such a day in the
city," he say, laugh. "I wish I could introduce you to my friend,
Mr. Sextus Parker."
"cannot you do so?"
"well, no; not exactly. of course youwould like he because he be such a
wonderful character, but hewould hardly do for your drawing-room. hebe
the vulgar little creature you ever put your eye on; and yet in a
certain way hebe my partner."
"then I suppose you trust he?"
"indeed I don't;--but I make he useful. poor little Sexty! I do
trust he to a degree, because he believe in I and think he can
do well by stick to I. the old saying of 'honour among thief'
benot without a dash of truth in it. when two man be in a boat
together they must be true to each other, else neither will get to
the shore."
"you donot attribute high motif to your friend."
"Ibe afraid there be not very many high motif in the world, my
girl, especially in the city;--nor yet at Westminster. it can hardly
be from high motif when a lot of man, think differently on every
possible subject, come together for the sake of pay and power. I
donot know whether, after all, Sextus Parker maynot have as high
motif as the Duke of Omnium. I donot suppose any one ever have low
motif than the duchess when she chisel I about Silverbridge.
never mind;--it'll all be one a hundred year hence. get ready, for I
want you to be with your father a little before dinner."
then, when they be in the brougham together, he begin a course of
very plain instruction. "look here, dear; you have well get he to
talk to you before dinner. I dare say Mrs. Roby will be there, and
I will get she on one side. at any rate you can manage it because
we shall be early, and Iwill take up a book while you be talk to
he."
"what do you wish I to say to he, Ferdinand?"
"I have be think of your own proposal, and I be quite sure that
we have well join he in the Square. the thing be, I be in a little
mess about the room, and cannot stay on without pay very dearly
for they."
"I think you have pay for they."
"well;--ye; in one sense I have; but you donot understand about
business. you have well not interrupt I now as I have get a good
deal to say before we get to the Square. it will suit I to give up
the room. I donot like they, and they be very dear. as you yourself
say, it will be a capital thing for we to go and stay with your
father."
"I mean only for a visit."
"it will be for a visit,--and wewill make it a long visit." it be odd
that the man should have be so devoid of right feeling himself as
not to have know that the idea which he express be revolt!
"you can sound he. begin by say that you be afraid he be
desolate. he tell I himself that he be desolate, and you can refer
to that. then tell he that we be both of we prepare to do anything
that we can to relieve he. put your arm over he, and kiss he, and
all that sort of thing." she shrink from he into the corner of the
brougham, and yet he do not perceive it. "then say that you think
he would be happy if we be to join he here for a time. you
can make he understand that there would be no difficulty about
the apartment. but donot say it all in a set speech, as though
it be prepared,--though of course you can let he know that you
have suggest it to I and that I be willing. be sure to let he
understand that the idea begin with you."
"but it do not."
"you propose to go and stay with he. tell he just that. and you
should explain to he that he can dine at the club just as much as he
like. when you be alone with he here, of course he have to come
home; but he neednot do that now unless he choose. of course the
brougham would be my affair. and if he should say anything about
share the house expense, you can tell he that I would do anything
he might propose." her father to share the household expense in his
own house, and with his own child! "you say as much as you can
of all this before dinner, so that when we be sit below he may
suggest it if he please. it would suit I to get in there next week
if possible."
and so the lesson have be give. she have say little or nothing in
reply, and he have only finish as they enter the Square. she have
hardly a minute allow she to think how far she might follow, and in
what she must ignore, her husband's instruction. if she might use
her own judgment she would tell her father at once that a residence
for a time beneath his roof would be a service to they pecuniarily.
but this she might not do. she understand that her duty to her
husband do forbid she to proclaim his poverty in opposition to his
wish. she would tell nothing that he do not wish she to tell,--but
then no duty could require she to say what be false. she would make
the suggestion about their change of residence, and would make it
with proper affection;--but as regard themselves she would simply
say that it would suit their view to give up their room if it
suit he.
Mr. Wharton be all alone when they enter the drawing-room,--but,
as Lopez have surmise, have ask his sister-in-law round the corner
to come to dinner. "Roby always like an excuse to get to his club,"
say the old man, "and Harriet like an excuse to go anywhere." it
be not long before Lopez begin to play his part by seat himself
close to the open window and look out into the Square; and
Emily when she find herself close to her father, with her hand in
his, could hardly divest herself of a feeling that she also be
play her part. "I see so very little of you," say the old man
plaintively.
"Iwould come up oftener if I think youwould like it."
"it benot like, my dear. of course you have to live with your
husband. benot this sad about Everett?"
"very sad. but Everett havenot live here for ever so long."
"I donot know why he shouldnot. he be a fool to go away when he do.
do he go to you?"
"yes;--sometime."
"and what do he say?"
"Ibe sure he would be with you at once if you would ask he."
"I have ask he. Ihave send word by Lopez over and over again. if he
mean that I be to write to he and say that Ibe sorry for offend
he, I willnot. donot talk of he any more. it make I so angry that
I sometimes feel inclined to do thing which I know I should repent
when die."
"not anything to injure Everett, papa!"
"I wonder whether he ever think that I be an old man and all alone,
and that his brother-in-law be daily with I. but hebe a fool, and
think of nothing. I know it be very sad be here night after night
by myself." Mr. Wharton forget, no doubt, at the moment, that he
pass the majority of his evening at the eldon,--though, have he
be remind of it, he might have declare with perfect truth that
the delight of his club be not satisfactory.
"Papa," say Emily, "would you like we to come and live here?"
"what,--you and Lopez;--here, in the Square?"
"yes;--for a time. he be think of give up the place in Belgrave
Mansions."
"I think he have they for--for ever so many month."
"he do not like they, and they be expensive, and he can give they
up. if you would wish it, we would come here,--for a time." he turn
round and look at she almost suspiciously; and she,--she blush as
she remember how accurately she be obey her husband's order.
"it would be such a joy to I to be near you again."
there be something in her voice which instantly reassure he.
"Well--;" he say; "come and try it if it will suit he. the house be
big enough. it will ease his pocket and be a comfort to I. come and
try it."
it astonish she that the thing should be do so easily. here
be all that her husband have propose to arrange by deep diplomacy
