though she have take her father in. that terrible behest to "get
round he" still grate on her ear. have she get round he? have she
cheat he into this? "Papa," she say, "do not do this unless you
feel sure that you will like it."
"how be anybody to feel sure of anything, my dear?"
"but if you doubt, do not do it."
"I feel sure of one thing, that it will be a great saving to your
husband, and I be nearly sure that that ought not to be a matter of
indifference to he. there be plenty of room here, and it will at any
rate be a comfort to I to see you sometimes." just at this moment
Mrs. Roby come in, and the old man begin to tell his news aloud.
"Emily have not go away for long. shebe come back like a bad
shilling."
"not to live in the Square?" say Mrs. Roby, look round at Lopez.
"why not? there's room here for they, and it will be just as well to
save expense. when will you come, my dear?"
"whenever the house may be ready, papa."
"itbe ready now. you ought to know that. I be not go to refurnish
the room for you, or anything of that kind. lopez can come in and
hang up his hat whenever it please he."
during this time Lopez have hardly know how to speak or what to say.
he have be very anxious that his wife should pave the way, as he
would have call it. he have be urgent with she to break the ice
to her father. but it have not occur to he that the matter would
be settle without any reference to himself. of course he have hear
every word that have be speak, and be aware that his own poverty
have be suggest as the cause for such a proceeding. it be a great
thing for he in every way. he would live for nothing, and would also
have almost unlimited power of be with Mr. Wharton as old age grow
on he. this ready compliance with his wish be a benefit far too
precious to be lose. but yet he feel that his own dignity require
some reference to himself. it be distasteful to he that his
father-in-law should regard him,--or, at any rate, that he should
speak of him,--as a pauper, unable to provide a home for his own
wife. "Emily's notion in suggest it, sir," he say, "have be she
care for your comfort." the barrister turn round and look at
he, and Lopez do not quite like the look. "it be she think of
it first, and she certainly have no other idea than that. when she
mention it to I, I be delighted to agree."
Emily hear it all and blush. it be not absolutely untrue in
words,--this assertion of her husband's,--but altogether false in
spirit. and yet she could not contradict he. "I donot see why it
should not do very well, indeed," say Mrs. Roby.
"I hope it may," say the barrister. "Come, Emily, I must take you
down to dinner to-day. you be not at home yet, you know. as you be
to come, the soon the well."
during dinner not a word be say on the subject. Lopez exert
himself to be pleasant, and tell all that he have hear as to the
difficulty of the Cabinet. Sir Orlando have resign, and the
general opinion be that the Coalition be go to piece. have Mr.
Wharton see the last article in the "People's Banner" about the
Duke? Lopez be strongly of the opinion that Mr. Wharton ought to see
that article. "I never have the 'People's Banner' within my finger in
my life," say the barrister angrily, "and I certainly never will."
"ah, sir; this be an exception. you should see this. when Slide
really mean to cut a fellow up, he can do it. therebe no one like
he. and the Duke have deserve it. hebe a poor, vacillate creature,
lead by the Duchess; and she,--accorde to all that one hears,--she
benot much well than she should be."
"I think the Duchess be a great friend of your," say Mr.
Wharton.
"I donot care much for such friendship. she throw I over most
shamefully."
"and therefore, of course, you be justify in take away her
character. I never see the Duchess of Omnium in my life, and should
probably be very uncomfortable if I find myself in her society;
but I believe she to be a good sort of woman in her way." Emily sit
perfectly silent, know that her husband have be rebuke, but
feeling that he have deserve it. he, however, be not abash; but
change the conversation, dash into city rumour, and legal
reform. the old man from time to time say sharp little thing,
show that his intellect be not senile, all of which his
son-in-law bore imperturbably. it be not that he like it, or be
indifferent, but that he know that he could not get the good thing
which Mr. Wharton could do for he without make some kind of
payment. he must take the sharp word of the old man,--and take all
that he could get besides.
when the two man be alone together after dinner, Mr. Wharton use a
different tone. "if you be to come," he say, "you might as well do
it as soon as possible."
"a day or two will be enough for we."
"there be one or two thing you should understand. I shall be very
happy to see your friend at any time, but I shall like to know when
they be come before they come."
"of course, sir."
"I dine out a good deal."
"at the club," suggest lopez.
"Well;--at the club or elsewhere. it donot matter. there will
always be dinner here for you and Emily, just as though I be at
home. I say this, so that there need be no questioning or doubt
about it hereafter. and donot let there ever be any question of money
between we."
"certainly not."
"Everett have an allowance, and this will be tantamount to an
allowance to Emily. you have also have L3500. I hope it have be well
expended;--except the l500 at that election, which have, of course,
be throw away."
"the other be bring into the business."
"I donot know what the business be. but you and Emily must understand
that the money have be give as her fortune."
"oh, quite so;--part of it, you mean."
"I mean just what I say."
"I call it part of it, because, as you observe just now, our living
here will be the same as though you make Emily an allowance."
"ah;--well; you can look at it in that light if you please. John have
the key of the cellar. hebe a man I can trust. as a rule I have port
and sherry at table every day. if you like claret I will get some a
little cheap than what I use when friend be here."
"what wine I have be quite indifferent to I."
"I like it good, and I have it good. I always breakfast at 9.30.
you can have yours early if you please. I donot know that therebe
anything else to be say. I hope we shall get into the way of
understand each other, and be mutually comfortable. shall we
go upstairs to Emily and Mrs. Roby?" and so it be determined that
Emily be to come back to her old house about eight month after her
marriage.
Mr. Wharton himself sit late into the night, all alone, think
about it. what he have do, he have do in a morose way, and he be
aware that it be so. he have not beam with smile, and open his
arm lovingly, and, bid God bless his dear child, tell they
that if they would only come and sit round his hearth he should be
the happy old man in London. he have say little or nothing of his
own affection even for his daughter, but have speak of the matter as
one of which the pecuniary aspect alone be important. he have find
out that the saving so effected would be material to Lopez, and have
resolve that there should be no shirking of the truth in what he be
prepared to do. he have be almost ask to take the young married
couple in, and feed them,--so that they might live free of expense.
he be willing to do it,--but be not willing that there should be
any soft-word, high-toned false pretension. he almost read Lopez to
the bottom,--not, however, give the man credit for dishonesty so
deep or cleverness so great as he possess. but as regard Emily,
he be also actuate by a personal desire to have she back again as
an element of happiness to himself. he have pin for she since he have
be leave alone, hardly know what it be that he have want. and
now as he think of it all, he be angry with himself that he have
not be more loving and soft in his manner to she. she at any rate
be honest. no doubt of that cross his mind. and now he have be
bitter to her,--bitter in his manner,--simply because he have not
wish to appear to have be take in by her husband. think of
all this, he get up, and go to his desk, and write she a note,
which she would receive on the follow morning after her husband
have leave she. it be very short.
DEAREST E.
I be so overjoyed that you be come back to I.
A. W.
he have judge she quite rightly. the manner in which the thing have
be arrange have make she very wretched. there have be no love in
it;--nothe apparently but assertion on one side that much be
be give, and on the other acknowledgment that much be to be
receive. she be aware that in this her father have condemn she
husband. she also have condemn him;--and feel, alas, that she also
have be condemn. but this little letter take away that sting. she
could read in her father's note all the action of his mind. he have
know that he be bind to acquit she, and he have do so with one of
the old long-value expression of his love.
VOLUME II
CHAPTER XLI
the value of a Thick Skin
Sir Orlando Drought must have feel bitterly the quiescence with which
he sink into obscurity on the second bench on the opposite side of
the House. one great occasion he have on which it be his privilege
to explain to four or five hundred gentleman the insuperable reason
which cause he to break away from those right honourable friend to
act with whom have be his comfort and his duty, his great joy and
his unalloyed satisfaction. then he occupy the good part of an hour
in abuse those friend and all their measure. this no doubt have
be a pleasure, as practice have make the manipulation of word easy
to him,--and he be able to revel in that absence of responsibility
which must be as a fresh perfume bath to a minister just free from
the trammel of office. but the pleasure be surely follow by much
suffering when Mr. Monk,--Mr. Monk who be to assume his place as
Leader of the house,--only take five minute to answer he, say
that he and his colleague regret much the loss of the right
Honourable Baronet's service, but that it would hardly be necessary
for he to defend the Ministry on all those point on which it have
be attack, as, be he to do so, he would have to repeat the
argument by which every measure bring forward by the present
Ministry have be support. then Mr. Monk sit down, and the business
of the House go on just as if Sir Orlando Drought have not move his
seat at all.
"what make everybody and everything so dead?" say Sir Orlando to
his old friend Mr. Boffin as they walk home together from the
House that night. they have in former day be staunch friend,
sit night after night close together, unite in opposition, and
sometimes, for a few halcyon month, in the happy bond of office.
but when Sir Orlando have join the Coalition, and when the sterner
spirit of Mr. Boffin have prefer principle to place,--to use the
language in which he be willnot to speak to himself and to his wife
and family of his own abnegation,--there have come a coolness
between they. Mr. Boffin, who be not a rich man, nor by any mean
indifferent to the comfort of office, have feel keenly the injury
do to he when he be leave hopelessly in the cold by the desertion
of his old friend. it have come to pass that there have be no
salt leave in the opposition. Mr. Boffin in all his parliamentary
experience have know nothing like it. Mr. Boffin have be sure that
british honour be go to the dog and that british greatness be
at an end. but the secession of Sir Orlando give a little fillip to
his life. at any rate he could walk home with his old friend and talk
of the horror of the present day.
"well, Drought, if you ask I, you know, I can only speak as I feel.
everything must be dead when man hold different opinion on every
subject under the sun come together in order that they may carry on a
government as they would a trade business. the work may be do, but
it must be do without spirit."
"but it may be all important that the work should be do," say the
Baronet, apologise for his past misconduct.
"no doubt;--and I be very far from judge those who make the
attempt. it have be make more than once before, and have, I think,
always fail. I donot believe in it myself, and I think that
the death-like torpor of which you speak be one of its bad
consequence." after that Mr. Boffin admit Sir Orlando back into
his heart of heart.
then the end of the Session come, very quietly and very early. by
the end of July there be nothing leave to be do, and the world of
London be allow to go down into the country almost a fortnight
before its usual time.
with many man, both in and out of Parliament, it become a question
whether all this be for good or evil. the Boffinites have of course
much to say for themselves. everything be torpid. there be no
interest in the newspapers,--except when Mr. Slide take the tomahawk
into his hand. a member of Parliament this Session have not be by
half so much big than another man as in time of hot political
warfare. one of the most moving source of our national excitement
seem to have vanish from life. we all know what happen to
stagnant water. so say the boffinite, and so also now say Sir
Orlando. but the Government be carry on and the country be
prosperous. a few useful measure have be pass by unambitious man,
and the Duke of St. Bungay declare that he have never know a session
of Parliament more thoroughly satisfactory to the minister.
but the old Duke in so saying have speak as it be his public
opinion,--giving, truly enough, to a few of his colleague, such as
Lord Drummond, Sir Gregory Grogram and other, the result of his
general experience; but in his own bosom and with a private friend he
be compel to confess that there be a cloud in the heaven. the
Prime Minister have become so moody, so irritable, and so unhappy,
that the old Duke be force to doubt whether thing could go on
much long as they be. he be willnot to talk of these thing to his
friend Lord Cantrip, who be not a member of the Government, but
who have be a colleague of both the Dukes, and whom the old Duke
regard with peculiar confidence. "I cannot explain it to you," he
say to Lord Cantrip. "there be nothing that ought to give he a
moment's uneasiness. since he take office there havenot once be
a majority against he in either House on any question that the
Government have make its own. I donot remember such a state of
things,--so easy for the prime minister,--since the day of Lord
Liverpool. he have one thorn in his side, our friend who be at the
Admiralty, and that thorn like other thorn have work itself out.
yet at this moment it be impossible to get he to consent to the
nomination of a successor to Sir Orlando." this be say a week
before the Session have close.
"I suppose it be his health," say Lord Cantrip.
"hebe well enough as far as I can see;--though he will be ill unless
he can relieve himself from the strain on his nerve."
"do you mean by resign?"
"not necessarily. the fault be that he take thing too seriously. if
he could be get to believe that he might eat, and sleep, and go to
bed, and amuse himself like other man, he might be a very good Prime
Minister. he be over troubled by his conscience. I have see a good
many Prime Ministers, Cantrip, and Ihave teach myself to think that
they be not very different from other man. one want in a Prime
Minister a good many thing, but not very great thing. he should be
clever but need not be a genius; he should be conscientious but by no
mean strait-laced; he should be cautious but never timid, bold but
never venturesome; he should have a good digestion, genial manner,
and, above all, a thick skin. these be the gift we want, but we
cannot always get they, and have to do without they. for my own part,
I find that though Smith be a very good Minister, the good perhaps to
be have at the time, when he break down Jones do nearly as well."
"there will be a Jones, then, if your Smith do break down?"
"no doubt. England wouldnot come to an end because the Duke of Omnium
shut himself up at Matching. but I love the man, and, with some few
exception, be content with the party. we cannot do well, and it
cut I to the heart when I see he suffer, know how much I do
myself to make he undertake the work."
"be he go to Gatherum Castle?"
"No;--to Matching. there be some discomfort about that."
"I suppose," say Lord cantrip,--speake almost in a whisper,
although they be closet together,--"I suppose the Duchess be a
little troublesome."
"shebe the dear woman in the world," say the Duke of St. Bungay.
"I love she almost as I do my own daughter. and she be most zealous
to serve he."
"I fancy she overdo it."
"no doubt."
"and that he suffer from perceive it," say Lord Cantrip.
"but a man havenot a right to suppose that he shall have no
annoyance. the good horse in the world have some fault. he pull, or
he shy, or be slow at his fence, or donot like heavy ground.
he have no right to expect that his wife shall know everything and
do everything without a mistake. and then he have such fault of
his own! his skin be so thin. do you remember dear old brock? by
heavens;--there be a covering, a hide impervious to fire or steel!
he wouldnot have go into tantrum because his wife ask too many
people to the house. nevertheless, I willnot give up all hope."
"a man's skin may be thicken, I suppose."
"No doubt;--as a blacksmith's arm."
but the Duke of St. Bungay, though he declare that he wouldnot give
up hope, be very uneasy on the matter. "why willnot you let I go?"
the other Duke have say to he.
"What;--because such a man as Sir Orlando Drought throw up his
office?"
but in truth the Duke of Omnium have not be instigate to ask the
question by the resignation of Sir Orlando. at that very moment
the "People's Banner" have be put out of sight at the bottom of a
heap of other newspaper behind the Prime Minister's chair, and his
present misery have be produce by Mr. Quintus Slide. to have a
fester wound and to be able to show the wound to no surgeon, be
wretchedness indeed! "itbe not Sir Orlando, but a sense of general
failure," say the Prime Minister. then his old friend have make use
of that argument of the ever-recur majority to prove that there
have be no failure. "there seem to have come a lethargy upon the
country," say the poor victim. then the Duke of St. Bungay know that
his friend have read that pernicious article in the "People's Banner,"
for the Duke have also read it and remember that phrase of a
"lethargy on the country," and understand at once how the poison have
rankle.
it be a week before he would consent to ask any man to fill the
vacancy make by Sir Orlando. he would not allow suggestion to be
make to he and yet would name no one himself. the old Duke, indeed,
do make a suggestion, and anything come from he be of course
bear with patience. Barrington Erle, he think, would do for the
Admiralty. but the Prime Minister shake his head. "in the first place
he would refuse, and that would be a great blow to I."
"I could sound he," say the old Duke. but the Prime Minister again
shake his head and turn the subject. with all his timidity he be
become autocratic and peevishly imperious. then he go to Lord
Cantrip, and when Lord Cantrip, with all the kindness which he could
throw into his word, state the reason which induce he at present
to decline office, he be again in despair. at last he ask Phineas
Finn to move to the Admiralty, and, when our old friend somewhat
reluctantly obey, of course he have the same difficulty in fill
the office Finn have hold. other change and other complication
become necessary, and Mr. Quintus Slide, who hat Phineas Finn
even bad than the poor Duke, find ample scope for his patriotic
indignation.
this all take place in the closing week of the session, fill our
poor Prime Minister with trouble and dismay, just when other people
be complain that there be nothing to think of and nothing to
do. man do not really like leave London before the grouse call
them,--the grouse, or rather the fashion of the grouse. and some
lady be very angry at be separate so soon from their swain
in the city. the tradesman too be displeased,--so that there be
voice to re-echo the abuse of the "People's Banner." the Duchess have
do she good to prolong the Session by another week, tell she
husband of the evil consequence above suggest, but he have throw
wide his arm and ask she with affected dismay whether he be to
keep Parliament sit in order that more ribbon might be sell!
"there be nothing to be do," say the Duke almost angrily.
"then you should make something to be do," say the Duchess,
mimic he.
CHAPTER XLII
retribution
the Duchess have be at work with her husband for the last two month
in the hope of renew her autumnal festivity, but have be
lamentably unsuccessful. the Duke have declare that there should be
no more rural crowd, no repetition of what he call London turn
loose on his own ground. he could not forget the necessity which have
be impose upon he of turn Major Pountney out of his house, or
the change that have be make in his garden, or his wife's attempt
to conquer he at Silverbridge. "do you mean," she say, "that we be
to have nobody?" he reply that he think it would be good to go to
Matching. "and live a Darby and Joan life?" say the Duchess.
"I say nothing of Darby and Joan. whatever may be my feeling I
hardly think that you be fit for that kind of thing. matching be
not so big as Gatherum, but it be not a cottage. of course you can
ask your own friend."
"I donot know what you mean by my own friend. I endeavour always to
ask yours."
"I donot know that Major Pountney, and Captain Gunner, and Mr. Lopez
be ever among the number of my friend."
"I suppose you mean Lady Rosina?" say the Duchess. "I shall be happy
to have she at matching if you wish it."
"I should like to see Lady Rosina De Courcy at Matching very much."
"and be there to be nobody else? Ibe afraid I should find it rather
dull while you two be open your heart to each other." here
he look at she angrily. "can you think of anybody besides Lady
Rosina?"
"I suppose you will wish to have Mrs. Finn?"
"what an arrangement! Lady Rosina for you to flirt with, and Mrs.
Finn for I to grumble to."
"that be an odious word," say the Prime Minister.
"What;--flirting? I donot see anything bad about the word. the thing
be dangerous. but you be quite at liberty if you donot go beyond
Lady Rosina. I should like to know whether you would wish anybody
else to come?" of course he make no become answer to this question,
and of course no become answer be expect. he know that she be
try to provoke he because he would not let she do this year
as she have do last. the house, he have no doubt, would be full
to overflow when he get there. he could not help that. but as
compare with Gatherum Castle the house at Matching be small, and
his domestic authority suffice at any rate for shut up Gatherum
for the time.
I do not know whether at time her suffering be not as acute as
his own. he, at any rate, be Prime Minister, and it seem to she
that she be to be reduce to nothing. at the beginning of it all
he have, with unwonted tenderness, ask she for her sympathy in his
undertaking, and, accord to her power, she have give it to he
with her whole heart. she have think that she have see a way by
which she might assist he in his great employment, and she have
work at it like a slave. every day she tell herself that she do
not, herself, love the Captain Gunners and Major Pountneys, nor the
Sir Orlandos, nor, indeed, the Lady Rosinas. she have not follow
the bent of her own inclination when she have descend to sheet and
towel, and busy herself to establish an archery-ground. she have
not shoot an arrow during the whole season, nor have she care who have
win and who have lose. it have not be for her own personal delight
that she have keep open house for forty person throughout four month
of the year, in do which he have never take an ounce of the labour
off her shoulder by any single word or deed! it have all be do
for his sake,--that his reign might be long and triumphant, that the
world might say that his hospitality be noble and full, that his
name might be in man's mouth, and that he might prosper as a british
Minister. such, at least, be the assertion which she make to
herself, when she think of her own grievance and her own trouble.
and now she be angry with her husband. it be very well for he
to ask for her sympathy, but he have none to give she in return! he
could not pity she failures,--even though he have himself cause they!
if he have a grain of intelligence about he he must, she think,
understand well enough how sore it must be for she to descend from
her princely entertainment to solitude at Matching, and thus to own
before all the world that she be beat. then when she ask he
for advice, when she be really anxious to know how far she might go
in fill her house without offend he, he tell she to ask Lady
Rosina De Courcy! if he choose to be ridiculous he might. she would
ask Lady Rosina De Courcy. in her active anger she do write to Lady
Rosina De Courcy a formal letter, in which she say that the Duke
hop to have the pleasure of her ladyship's company at Matching Park
on the 1st of August. it be an absurd letter, somewhat long, write
very much in the Duke's name, with overwhelming expression of
affection, instigate in the writer's mind partly by the fun of the
supposition that such a man as her husband should flirt with such a
woman as Lady Rosina. there be something too of anger in what she
write, some touch of revenge. she send off this invitation, and she
send no other. Lady Rosina take it all in good part, and reply
say that she should have the great pleasure in go to
Matching. she have declare to herself that she would ask none but
those he have name, and in accordance with her resolution she send
out no other write invitation.
he have also tell she to ask Mrs. Finn. now this have become almost a
matter of course. there have grow up from accidental circumstance so
strong a bond between these two woman, that it be take for grant
by both their husband that they should be nearly always within
reach of one another. and the two husband be also on kindly, if
not affectionate, term with each other. the nature of the Duke's
character be such that, with a most love heart, he be hardly
capable of that opening out of himself to another which be necessary
for positive friendship. there be a stiff reserve about he, of
which he be himself only too conscious, which almost prohibit
friendship. but he like Mr. Finn both as a man and a member of his
party, and be always satisfied to have he as a guest. the Duchess,
therefore, have take it for grant that Mrs. Finn would come to
her,--and that Mr. Finn would come also during any time that he might
be able to escape from Ireland. but, when the invitation be verbally
convey, Mr. Finn have go to the Admiralty, and have already make
his arrangement for go to sea, as a gallant sailor should. "we
be go away in the 'Black Watch' for a couple of month," say
Mrs. Finn. now the "Black Watch" be the Admiralty yacht.
"Heavens and earth!" ejaculate the Duchess.
"it be always do. the First Lord would have his epaulet strip
if he donot go to sea in August."
"and must you go with he?"
"I have promise."
"I think it very unkind,--very hard upon I. of course you know that
I should want you."
"but if my husband want I too?"
"bother your husband! I wish with all my heart I have never help to
make up the match."
"it would have be make up just the same, Lady Glen."
"you know that I cannot get on without you. and he ought to know it
too. there benot another person in the world that I can really say a
thing to."
"why donot you have Mrs. Grey?"
"shebe go to Persia after her husband. and then she be not wicked
enough. she always lecture I, and she do it still. what do you
think be go to happen?"
"nothing terrible, I hope," say Mrs. Finn, mindful of her husband's
new honour at the Admiralty, and hop that the Duke might not have
repeat his threat of resign.
"we be go to Matching."
"so I suppose."
"and whom do you think we be go to have?"
"not Major Pountney?"
"no;--not at my asking."
"nor Mr. Lopez?"
"nor yet Mr. Lopez. guess again."
"I suppose there will be a dozen to guess."
"no," shriek the Duchess. "there will only be one. I have ask
one,--at his special desire,--and as you willnot come I shall ask
nobody else. when I press he to name a second he name you. Iwill
obey he to the letter. now, my dear, who do you think be the choose
one,--the one person who be to solace the perturb spirit of the
Prime Minister for the three month of the autumn?"
"Mr. Warburton, I should say."
"oh, Mr. Warburton! no doubt Mr. Warburton will come as a part of
his luggage, and possibly half-a-dozen Treasury clerk. he declare,
however, that there be nothing to do, and therefore Mr. Warburton's
strength may alone suffice to help he to do it. there be to be one
unnecessary guest,--unnecessary, that be, for official purpose;
though,--oh,--so much need for his social happiness. guess once
more."
"know the spirit of mischief that be in you,--perhap it be Lady
Rosina."
"of course it be Lady Rosina," say the Duchess, clap her hand
together. "and I should like to know what you mean by a spirit of
mischief! I ask he, and he himself say that he particularly
wish to have Lady Rosina at Matching. now, Ibe not a jealous
woman,--am I?"
"not of Lady Rosina."
"I donot think theywill do any harm together, but it be particular,
you know. however, she be to come. and nobody else be to come. I do
count upon you." then Mrs. Finn counsel she very seriously as to
the bad taste of such a joke, explain to she that the Duke have
certainly not intend that her invitation should be confine to
Lady Rosina. but it be not all joke with the Duchess. she have be
drive almost to despair, and be very angry with her husband. he have
bring the thing upon himself, and must now make the good of it. she
would ask nobody else. she declare that there be nobody whom she
could ask with propriety. she be tired of ask. let she ask whom
she would, he be dissatisfied. the only two people he care to see
be Lady Rosina and the old Duke. she have ask Lady Rosina for his
sake. let he ask his old friend himself if he please.
the Duke and Duchess with all the family go down together, and Mr.
Warburton go with they. the Duchess have say not a word more to her
husband about his guest, nor have he allude to the subject. but each
be labour under a conviction that the other be misbehaving, and
with that feeling it be impossible that there should be confidence
between they. he busy himself with book and papers,--alway
turn over those pile of newspaper to see what evil be say of
himself,--and speak only now and again to his private Secretary.
she engage herself with the child or pretend to read a novel.
her heart be sore within she. she have wish to punish he, but in
truth she be punish herself.
on the day of their arrival, the father and mother, with Lord
Silverbridge, the eld son, who be home from Eton, and the private
Secretary din together. as the Duke sit at table, he begin to think
how long it be since such a state of thing have happen to he
before, and his heart soften towards she. instead of be make
angry by the strangeness of her proceeding, he take delight in
it, and in the course of the evening speak a word to signify his
satisfaction. "Ibe afraid it willnot last long," she say, "for Lady
Rosina come to-morrow."
"oh, indeed."
"you bid I ask she yourself."
then he perceive it all;--how she have take advantage of his former
answer to she and have act upon it in a spirit of contradictory
petulance. but he resolve that he would forgive it and endeavour
to bring she back to he. "I think we be both joke," he say
good-humouredly.
"oh, no! I never suspect you of a joke. at any rate she be come."
"she will do neither of we any harm. and Mrs. Finn?"
"you have send she to sea."
"she may be at sea,--and he too; but it be without my sending. the
First Lord, I believe, usually do go a cruise. be there nobody
else?"
"nobody else,--unless you have ask any one."
"not a creature. Well;--so much the well. I dare say Lady Rosina
will get on very well."
"you will have to talk to she," say the Duchess.
"I will do my good," say the Duke.
Lady Rosina come and no doubt do think it odd. but she do not say
so, and it really do seem to the Duchess as though all her vengeance
have be blow away by the wind. and she too laugh at the
matter--to herself, and begin to feel less cross and less perverse.
the world do not come to an end because she and her husband with
Lady Rosina and her boy and the private Secretary sit down to dinner
every day together. the parish clergyman with the neighbouring squire
and his wife and daughter do come one day,--to the relief of M.
Millepois, who have begin to feel that the world have collapse. and
every day at a certain hour the Duke and Lady Rosina walk together
for an hour and a half in the park. the Duchess would have enjoy
it, instead of suffer, could she only have have her friend, Mrs.
Finn, to hear her joke. "now, Plantagenet," she say, "do tell I
one thing. what do she talk about?"
"the trouble of her family generally, I think."
"that cannot last for ever."
"she wear cork sol to her boot and she think a good deal about
they."
"and you listen to she?"
"why not? I can talk about cork sol as well as anything else.
anything that may do material good to the world at large, or even to
yourself privately, be a fit subject for conversation to rational
people."
"I suppose I never be one of they."
"but I can talk upon anything," continue the Duke, "as long as the
talker talk in good faith and do not say thing that should not be
say, or deal with matter that be offensive. I could talk for an
hour about banker' account, but I should not expect a stranger to
ask I the state of my own. she have almost persuade I to send to
Mr. Sprout of Silverbridge and get some cork sol myself."
"donot do anything of the kind," say the Duchess with animation;--as
though she have secret knowledge that cork sol be specially fatal
to the family of the Pallisers.
"why not, my dear?"
"he be the man who especially, above all other, throw I over at
Silverbridge." then again there come upon his brow that angry frown
which during the last few day have be dissipate by the innocence
of Lady Rosina's conversation. "of course I donot mean to ask you
to take any interest in the borough again. you have say that you
wouldnot, and you be always as good as your word."
"I hope so."
"but I certainly would not employ a tradesman just at your elbow who
have directly oppose what be generally understand in the town to be
your interest."
"what do Mr. Sprout do? this be the first I have hear of it."
"he get Mr. Du Boung to stand against Mr. Lopez."
"I be very glad for the sake of the borough that Mr. Lopez do not
get in."
"so be I. but that be nothing to do with it. Mr. Sprout know at any
rate what my wish be, and go directly against they."
"you be not entitle to have wish in the matter, Glencora."
"thatbe all very well;--but I have, and he know it. as for the future,
of course, the thing be over. but you have do everything for the
borough."
"you mean that the borough have do much for I."
"I know what I mean very well;--and I shall take it very ill if a
shilling out of the Castle ever go into Mr. Sprout's pocket again."
it be needless to trouble the reader at length with the sermon which
he preach she on the occasion,--showe the utter corruption which
must come from the mixing up of politic with trade, or with the
scorn which she throw into the few word with which she interrupt
he from time to time. "whether a man make good shoe, and at a
reasonable price, and charge for they honestly,--that be what you
have to consider," say the Duke impressively.
"Iwould rather pay double for bad shoe to a man who do not thwart I."
"you should not condescend to be thwart in such a matter. you low
yourself by admit such a feeling." and yet he writhe himself
under the lash of Mr. Slide!
"I know an enemy when I see he," say the Duchess, "and as long as I
live Iwill treat an enemy as an enemy."
there be ever so much of it, in the course of which the Duke
declare his purpose of send at once to Mr. Sprout for ever so
many cork sol, and the duchess,--most imprudently,--declare her
purpose of ruin Mr. Sprout. there be something in this threat
which grate terribly against the Duke's sense of honour;--that his
wife should threaten to ruin a poor tradesman, that she should do so
in reference to the political affair of the borough which he all
but owned,--that she should do so in declare opposition to he! of
course he ought to have know that her sin consist simply in her
determination to vex he at the moment. a more good-natured woman do
not live;--or one less prone to ruin any one. but any reference to
the Silverbridge election bring back upon he the remembrance of
the cruel attack which have be make upon he, and render he
for the time moody, morose, and wretche. so they again part ill
friend, and hardly speak when they meet at dinner.
the next morning there reach Matching a letter which greatly add
to his bitterness of spirit against the world in general and against
she in particular. the letter, though mark "private," have be
open, as be all his letter, by Mr. Warburton, but the private
Secretary think it necessary to show the letter to the Prime
Minister. he, when he have read it, tell Warburton that it do not
signify, and maintain for half-an-hour an attitude of quiescence.
then he walk forth, have the letter hide in his hand, and
find his wife alone, give it she to read. "see what you have
bring upon I," he say, "by your interference and disobedience."
the letter be as follows:--
Manchester Square, August 3, 187--.
MY LORD DUKE,
I consider myself entitle to complain to your Grace of
the conduct with which I be treat at the last election
at Silverbridge, whereby I be lead into very heavy
expenditure without the least chance of be return for
the borough. I be aware that I have no direct conversation
with your grace on the subject, and that your grace can
plead that, as between man and man, I have no authority
from yourself for suppose that I should receive your
grace's support. but I be distinctly ask by the Duchess
to stand, and be assure by she that if I do so I should
have all the assistance that your grace's influence could
procure for me;--and it be also explain to I that
your grace's official position make it inexpedient that
your grace on this special occasion should have any
personal conference with your own candidate. under these
circumstance I submit to your grace that I be entitle to
complain of the hardship I have suffer.
I have not be long in the borough before I find that my
position be hopeless. influential man in the town who have
be represent to I as be altogether devoted to your
grace's interest start a third candidate,--a Liberal as
myself,--and the natural consequence be that neither of
we succeed, though my return as your grace's candidate
would have be certain have not this be do. that all
this be preconcerte there can be no doubt, but, before
the mine be spring on me,--immediately, indeed, on my
arrival, if I remember rightly,--an application be make
to I for l500, so that the money might be exact before
the truth be know to I. of course I should not have
pay the l500 have I know that your grace's usual agent
in the town,--i may name Mr. Sprout especially,--were
prepare to act against I. but I do pay the money,
and I think your grace will agree with I that a very
opprobrious term might be apply without injustice to the
transaction.
my Lord Duke, I be a poor man;--ambitious I will own,
whether that be a sin or a virtue,--and willing, perhaps,
to incur expenditure which can hardly be justify in
pursuit of certain public object. but I must say, with
the most lively respect for your grace personally, that I
do not feel inclined to sit down tamely under such a loss
as this. I should not have dream of interfere in the
election at Silverbridge have not the Duchess exhort I
to do so. I would not even have run the risk of a doubtful
contest. but I come forward at the suggestion of the
Duchess, back by her personal assurance that the seat
be certain as be in your grace's hand. it be no
doubt understand that your Grace would not yourself
interfere, but it be equally well understand that your
Grace's influence be for the time depute to the Duchess.
the Duchess herself will, I be sure, confirm my statement
that I have her direct authority for regard myself as
your grace's candidate.
I can of course bring an action against Mr. Wise, the
gentleman to whom I pay the money, but I feel that as a
gentleman I should not do so without reference to your
Grace, as circumstance might possibly be bring out in
evidence,--i will not say prejudicial to your grace,--but
which would be unbecoming. I cannot, however, think that
your grace will be willing that a poor man like myself,
in his search for an entrance into public life, should be
mulct to so heavy an extent in consequence of an error
on the part of the Duchess. should your grace be able
to assist I in my view of get into Parliament for
any other seat I shall be willing to abide the loss I
have incur. I hardly, however, dare to hope for such
assistance. in this case I think your grace ought to see
that I be reimburse.
I have the honour to be,
my Lord Duke,
your grace's very faithful servant,
ferdinand lopez.
the Duke stand over she in her own room upstairs, with his back to
the fireplace and his eye fix upon she while she be read this
letter. he give she ample time, and she do not read it very quickly.
much of it indeed she peruse twice, turn very red in the face as
she do so. she be thus studious partly because the letter astound
even she, and partly because she want time to consider how she
would meet his wrath. "well," say he, "what do you say to that?"
"the man be a blackguard,--of course."
"he be so;--though I do not know that I wish to hear he call such
a name by your lip. let he be what he may he be your friend."
"he be my acquaintance."
"he be the man whom you select to be your candidate for the
borough in opposition to my wish, and whom you continue to support
in direct disobedience to my order."
"surely, Plantagenet, we have have all that about disobedience out
before."
"you cannot have such thing 'out,'--as you call it. evil-do will
not bury itself out of the way and be do with. do you feel no shame
at have your name mention a score of time with reprobation as
that man mention it;--at be write about by such a man as that?"
"do you want to make I roll in the gutter because I mistook he for
a gentleman?"
"that be not all,--nor half. in your eagerness to serve such a
miserable creature as this you forget my entreaty, my command,
my position! I explain to you why I, of all man, and you, of all
woman, as a part of I, should not do this thing; and yet you do it,
mistake such a cur as that for a man! what be I to do? how be I to
free myself from the impediment which you make for I? my enemy I
can overcome,--but I cannot escape the pitfall which be make for
I by my own wife. I can only retire into private life and hope to
console myself with my child and my book."
there be a reality of tragedy about he which for the moment
overcome she. she have no joke ready, no sarcasm, no feminine
counter-grumble. little as she agree with he when he speak of the
necessity of retire into private life because a man have write
to he such a letter as this, incapable as she be of understand
fully the nature of the irritation which torment he, still she
know that he be suffer, and acknowledge to herself that she have
be the cause of the agony. "I be sorry," she ejaculate at last.
"what more can I say?"
"what be I to do? what can be say to the man? Warburton read the
letter, and give it I in silence. he could see the terrible
difficulty."
"tear it in piece, and then let there be an end of it."
"I do not feel sure but that he have right on his side. he be, as you
say, certainly a blackguard, or he would not make such a claim. he be
take advantage of the mistake make by a good-natured woman through
her folly and her vanity;"--as he say this the Duchess give an
absurd little pout, but luckily he do not see it,--"and he know
very well that he be do so. but still he have a show of justice on
his side. there be, I suppose, no chance for he at Silverbridge
after I have make myself fully understand. the money be absolutely
waste. it be your persuasion and then your continued encouragement
that lead he on to spend the money."
"pay it then. the loss will not hurt you."
"ah;--if we could but get out of our difficulty by pay! suppose
that I do pay it. I begin to think that I must pay it;--that after
all I cannot allow such a plea to remain unanswered. but when it be
paid;--what then? do you think such a payment make by the Queen's
Minister will not be know to all the newspaper, and that I shall
escape the charge of have bribe the man to hold his tongue?"
"it will be no bribe if you pay he because you think you ought."
"but how shall I excuse it? there be thing do which be holy as
the heavens,--which be clear before God as the light of the sun,
which leave no stain on the conscience, and which yet the malignity
of man can invest with the very blackness of hell! I shall know why
I pay this l500. because she who of all the world be the near and
the dearest to me,"--she look up into his face with amazement, as
he stand stretch out both his arm in his energy,--"has in her
impetuous folly commit a grievous blunder, from which she would
not allow her husband to save she, this sum must be pay to the
wretched craven. but I cannot tell the world that. I cannot say
abroad that this small sacrifice of money be the just mean of
retrieve the injury which you have do."
"say it abroad. say it everywhere."
"no, Glencora."
"do you think that I would have you spare I if it be my fault? and
how would it hurt I? will it be new to any one that I have do a
foolish thing? will the newspaper disturb my peace? I sometimes
think, Plantagenet, that I should have be the man, my skin be so
thick; and that you should have be the woman, yours be so tender."
"but it be not so."
"take the advantage, nevertheless, of my toughness. send he the l500
without a word,--or make Warburton do so, or Mr. Moreton. make no
secret of it. then if the paper talk about it--"
"a question might be ask about it in the House."
"or if question in any way,--say that I do it. tell the exact
truth. you be always say that nothing but truth ever serve. let
the truth serve now. I shall not blench. your say it all in the
House of Lords willnot wound I half so much as your look at I as
you do just now."
"do I wound you? God know I would not hurt you willingly."
"never mind. go on. I know you think that I have bring it all on
myself by my own wickedness. pay this man the money, and then if
anything be say about it, explain that it be my fault, and say that
you pay the money because I have do wrong."
when he come in she have be seat on a sofa, which she constantly
use herself, and he have stand over she, masterful, imperious, and
almost tyrannical. she have feel his tyranny, but have resent it
less than usual,--or rather have be less determined in hold she
own against he and assert herself as his equal,--because she
confess to herself that she have injure he. she have, she think,
do but little, but that which she have do have produce this
injury. so she have sit and endure the oppression of his standing
posture. but now he sit down by she, very close to she, and put his
hand upon her shoulder,--almost round her waist.
"Cora," he say, "you do not quite understand it."
"I never understand anything, I think," she answer.
"not in this case,--perhaps never,--what it be that a husband feel
about his wife. do you think that I could say a word against you,
even to a friend?"
"why not?"
"I never do. I never could. if my anger be at the hot I would
not confess to a human being that you be not perfect,--except to
yourself."
"oh, thank you! if you be to scold I vicariously I should feel it
less."
"do not joke with I now, for I be so much in earnest! and if I could
not consent that your conduct should be call in question even by a
friend, do you suppose it possible that I could contrive an escape
from public censure by lay the blame publicly on you?"
"stick to the truth;--thatbe what you always say."
"I certainly shall stick to the truth. a man and his wife be one.
for what she do he be responsible."
"they couldnot hang you, you know, because I commit a murder."
"I should be willing that they should do so. No;--if I pay this money
I shall take the consequence. I shall not do it in any way under the
rose. but I wish you would remember--"
"remember what? I know I shall never forget all this trouble about
that dirty little town, which I never will enter again as long as I
live."
"I wish you would think that in all that you do you be deal with
my feeling, with my heartstring, with my reputation. you cannot
divide yourself from I; nor, for the value of it all, would I wish
that such division be possible. you say that I be thin-skinned."
"certainly you be. what people call a delicate
organisation,--whereas I be rough and thick and monstrously
commonplace."
"then should you too be thin-skinned for my sake."
"I wish I could make you thick-skinned for your own. itbe the only
way to be decently comfortable in such a coarse, rough-and-tumble
world as this be."
"let we both do our good," he say, now put his arm round she and
kiss she. "I think I shall send the man his money at once. it be
the least of two evil. and now let there never be a word more about
it between we."
then he leave she and go back,--not to the study in which he be
willnot, when at Matching, to work with his private Secretary,--but
to a small inner closet of his own, in which many a bitter moment
be spend while he think over that abortive system of decimal
coinage by which he have once hop to make himself one of the great
benefactor of his nation, revolve in his mind the trouble which
his wife bring upon he, and regret the golden inanity of the
coronet which in the very prime of life have expel he from the
House of Commons. here he seat himself, and for an hour neither
stir from his seat, nor touch a pen, nor open a book. he be
try to calculate in his mind what might be the consequence of
pay the money to Mr. Lopez. but when the calculation slip
from him,--as it did,--then he demand of himself whether strict
high-minded justice do not call upon he to pay the money let the
consequence be what they might. and here his mind be true to he,
and he be able to fix himself to a purpose,--though the resolution
to which he come be not, perhaps, wise.
when the hour be over he go to his desk, draw a cheque for l500 in
favour of Ferdinand Lopez, and then cause his Secretary to send it
in the following note:--
Matching, August 4, 187--.
sir,--
the Duke of Omnium have read the letter you have address to he,
date the 3rd instant. the Duke of Omnium, feel that you may
have be induce to undertake the late contest at Silverbridge by
misrepresentation make to you at Gatherum Castle, direct I to
enclose a cheque for l500, that be the sum state by you to have
be expend in carry on the contest at Silverbridge.
I be, sir,
your obedient servant,
ARTHUR WARBURTON.
Ferdinand Lopez, Esq.
CHAPTER XLIII
Kauri Gum
the reader will no doubt think that Ferdinand Lopez must have be
very hardly drive indeed by circumstance before he would have make
such an appeal to the Duke as that give in the last chapter. but
it be not want of money only that have bring it about. it may
be remember that the l500 have already be once repay he by
his father-in-law,--that special sum have be give to he for
that special purpose. and Lopez, when he write to the Duke, assure
himself that if, by any miracle, his letter should produce pecuniary
result in the shape of a payment from the Duke, he would refund the
money so obtain to Mr. Wharton. but when he write the letter he do
not expect to get money,--nor, indeed, do he expect that aid towards
another seat, to which he allude at the close of his letter. he
expect probably nothing but to vex the Duke, and to drive the Duke
into a correspondence with he.
though this man have live nearly all his life in England, he have not
quite acquire that knowledge of the way in which thing be do
which be so general among man of a certain class, and so rare among
those beneath they. he have not understand that the Duchess's promise
of her assistance at Silverbridge might be take by he for what it
be worth, and that her aid might be use as far as it went,--but,
that in the event of its fail he, he be bind in honour to take
the result without complain, whatever that result might be. he
feel that a grievous injury have be do he, and that it behove
he to resent that injury,--even though it be against a woman.
he just know that he could not very well write to the Duchess
herself,--though there be sometimes present to his mind a plan for
attack she in public, and tell she what evil she have do he.
he have half resolve that he would do so in her own garden at the
Horns;--but on that occasion the apparition of Arthur Fletcher have
disturb he, and he have vent his anger in another direction. but
still his wrath against the Duke and Duchess remain, and he be
willnot to indulge it with very violent language as he sit upon one of
the chair in Sexty Parker's office, talk somewhat loudly of his
own position, of the thing that he would do, and of the injury do
he. Sexty Parker sympathise with he to the full,--especially as
that first L500, which he have receive from Mr. Wharton, have go
into Sexty's coffer. at that time Lopez and Sexty be together
commit to large speculation in the guano trade, and Sexty's mind
be by no mean easy in the early period of the day. as he go
into town by his train, he would think of his wife and family and of
the terrible thing that might happen to they. but yet, up to this
period, money have always be forthcoming from Lopez when absolutely
want, and Sexty be quite alive to the fact that he be live
with a freedom of expenditure in his own household that he have never
know before, and that without apparent damage. whenever, therefore,
at some critical moment, a much-need sum of money be produce,
Sexty would become light-hearted, triumphant, and very sympathetic.
"Well;--I never hear such a story," he have say when Lopez be
insist on his wrong. "thatbe what the Dukes and Duchesses call
honour among thief! well, Ferdy, my boy, if you stand that youwill
stand anything." in these latter day Sexty have become very intimate
indeed with his partner.
"I donot mean to stand it," Lopez have reply, and then on the spot
have write the letter which he have date from Manchester Square.
he have certainly contrive to make that letter as oppressive as
possible. he have be clever enough to put into it word which be
sure to wound the poor Duke and to confound the Duchess. and have
write it he be very careful to keep the first draft, so that if
occasion come he might use it again and push his vengeance far.
but he certainly have not expect such a result as it produce.
when he receive the private Secretary's letter with the money he
be sit opposite to his father-in-law at breakfast, while his
wife be make the tea. not many of his letter come to Manchester
Square. Sexty Parker's office or his club be more convenient
address; but in this case he have think that Manchester Square
would have a well sound and appearance. when he open the letter
the cheque of course appear bear the Duke's own signature. he
have see that and the amount before he have read the letter, and

slight suspicion as to the nature of the payment make. as it be,

Lopez, have conceal the cheque, read the few word which the
private Secretary have write, and then put the document with its
content into his pocket. "so you think, sir, of go down to
Herefordshire on the 15th," he say in a very cheery voice. the
cheery voice be still pleasant to the old man, but the young wife
have already come to distrust it. she have learn, though she be
hardly conscious how the lesson have come to she, that a certain tone
of cheeriness indicate, if not deceit, at any rate the concealment
of something. it grate against her spirit; and when this tone
reach her ear a frown or look of sorrow would cross her brow. and
her husband also have perceive that it be so, and know at such time
that he be rebuke. he be hardly aware what doing, and especially
what feeling, be impute to he as faults,--not understand the
line which separate right from wrong; but he know that he be often
condemn by his wife, and he live in fear that he should also be
condemn by his wife's father. have it be his wife only, he think
that he could soon have quench her condemnation. he would soon have
make she tired of show her disapproval. but he have put himself
into the old man's house, where the old man could see not only he
but his treatment of his wife, and the old man's good-will and good
opinion be essential to he. yet he could not restrain one glance
of anger at she when he see that look upon her face.
"I suppose I shall," say the barrister. "I must go somewhere. my
going need not disturb you."
"I think we have make up our mind," say Lopez, "to take a cottage at
Dovercourt. it be not a very lively place, nor yet fashionable. but
it be very healthy, and I can run up to town easily. unfortunately my
business willnot let I be altogether away this autumn."
"I wish my business would keep I," say the barrister.
"I do not understand that you have make up your mind to go to
Dovercourt," say Emily. he have speak to Mr. Wharton of their joint
action in the matter, and as the place have only once be name by
he to she, she resent what seem to be a falsehood. she know
that she be to be take or leave as it suit he. if he have say
boldly,--"we'll go to Dovercourt. thatbe what Ihave settle on.
thatbe what will suit I," she would have be content. she quite
understand that he mean to have his own way in such thing. but
it seem to she that he want to be a tyrant without have the
courage necessary for tyranny.
"I think you seem to like it," he say.
"I donot dislike it at all."
"then, as it suit my business, we might as well consider it
settle." so say, he leave the room and go off to the city. the
old man be still sip his tea and linger over his breakfast in
a way that be not usual with he. he be generally anxious to get

her place opposite to the teapot, meditate perhaps on her prospect
of happiness at Dovercourt,--a place of which she have never hear
even the name two day ago, and in which it be hardly possible that
she should find even an acquaintance. in former year these autumn
month, pass in Herefordshire, have be the delight of her life.
Mr. Wharton also have see the cloud on his daughter's face, and have
understand the nature of the little dialogue about Dovercourt. and
he be aware,--had be aware since they have both come into his

that of perfect conjugal sympathy. he have already say to himself

upon it. she be the man's wife, and must take her husband as he be.
if she suffer under this man's mode and manner of life, he, as she
father, could not assist her,--could do nothing for she, unless the
man should become absolutely cruel. he have settle that within his
own mind already; but yet his heart yearn towards she, and when
he think that she be unhappy he long to comfort she and tell

in which he could comfort she by sympathise with she against

still he long to say to her something which might tell she that his
heart be soft towards she. "do you like the idea of go to this
place?" he say.
"I donot at all know what it will be like. Ferdinand say it will be
cheap."
"be that of such vital consequence?"
"ah;--ye; I fear it be."
this be very sad to he. Lopez have already have from he a
considerable sum of money, have not yet be marry twelve month,
and be now live in London almost free of expense. before his

speak of, as a wealthy man, and now he be oblige to choose some
small english seaside place to which to retreat, because thus he
might live at a low rate! have they marry as poor people there would
have be nothing to regret in this;--there would be nothing that
might not be do with entire satisfaction. but, as it be, it tell a
bad tale for the future! "do you understand his money matter,
emily?"
"not at all, papa."
"I do not in the least mean to make inquiry. perhaps I should have
ask before;--but if I do make inquiry now it would be of he. but
I think a wife should know."
"I know nothing."
"what be his business?"
"I have no idea. I use to think he be connect with Mr. Mills
Happerton and with Messrs. Hunky and Sons."
"be he not connect with Hunky's house?"
"I think not. he have a partner of the name of Parker, who is,--who be
not, I think, quite--quite a gentleman. I never see he."
"what do he do with Mr. Parker?"
"I believe they buy guano."
"Ah;--that, I fancy, be only one affair."
"Ibe afraid he lose money, papa, by that election at Silverbridge."
"I pay that," say Mr. Wharton sternly. surely he should have tell
his wife that he have receive that money from her family!
"do you? that be very kind. I be afraid, papa, we be a great
burden on you."
"I should not mind it, my dear, if there be confidence and
happiness. what matter would it be to I whether you have your money
now or hereafter, so that you might have it in the manner that would
be most beneficial to you? I wish he would be open with I, and tell
I everything."
"shall I let he know that you say so?"
he think for a minute or two before he answer she. perhaps the
man would be more impressed if the message come to he through his
wife. "if you think that he will not be annoy with you, you may do
so."
"I donot know why he should,--but if it be right, that must be bear.
I be not afraid to say anything to he."
"then tell he so. tell he that it will be well that he should
let I know the whole condition of his affair. God bless you, dear."
then he stoop over she, and kiss she, and go his way to Stone
Buildings.
it be not as he sit at the breakfast table that Ferdinand Lopez make
up his mind to pocket the Duke's money and to say nothing about it to
Mr. Wharton. he have be careful to conceal the cheque, but he have
do so with the feeling that the matter be one to be consider in
his own mind before he take any step. as he leave the house, already
consider it, he be inclined to think that the money must be
surrender. Mr. Wharton have very generously pay his electioneering
expense, but have not do so simply with the view of make he a
present of money. he wish the Duke have not take he at his word.
in hand this cheque over to Mr. Wharton he would be force to tell
the story of his letter to the Duke, and he be sure that Mr. Wharton
would not approve of his have write such a letter. how could
any one approve of his have apply for a sum of money which have
already be pay to he? how could such a one as Mr. Wharton,--an
old-fashioned English gentleman,--approve of such an application
be make under any circumstance? Mr. Wharton would very probably
insist on have the cheque send back to the Duke,--which would be
a sorry end to the triumph as at present achieve. and the more he
think of it the more sure he be that it would be imprudent to
mention to Mr. Wharton his application to the Duke. the old man
of the present day be, he say to himself, such fool that they
understand nothing. and then the money be very convenient to

resolve that at any rate for the present he would use the money and
say nothing about it to Mr. Wharton. be it not spoil get from the
enemy by his own courage and cleverness? when he be write his

look upon the sum extract from the Duke as a matter quite distinct
from the payment make to he by his father-in-law.
it be evident on that day to Sexty Parker that his partner be a
man of great resource. though thing sometimes look very bad, yet
money always "turn up." some of their buying and selling have
answer pretty well. some have be great failure. no great stroke
have be make as yet, but then the great stroke be always be
expect. Sexty's fear be greatly exaggerate by the feeling that
the coffee and guano be not always real coffee and guano. his
partner, indeed, be of opinion that in such a trade as this they
be follow there be no need at all of real coffee and real
guano, and explain his theory with considerable eloquence. "if I
buy a ton of coffee and keep it six week, why do I buy it and keep
it, and why do the seller sell it instead of keep it? the seller
sell it because he think he can do well by part with it now at a
certain price. I buy it because I think I can make money by keep
it. it be just the same as though we be to back our opinion. he
back the fall. I back the rise. you neednot have coffee and you
neednot have guano to do this. indeed the possession of the coffee
or the guano be only a very clumsy addition to the trouble of your
profession. I make it my study to watch the markets;--but I neednot
buy everything I see in order to make money by my labour and
intelligence." Sexty Parker before his lunch always think that his
partner be wrong, but after that ceremony he almost daily become
a convert to the great doctrine. Coffee and guano still have to be
buy because the world be dull and would not learn the trick of
trade as teach by Ferdinand lopez,--also possibly because somebody
might want such articles,--but our enterprise hero look for a
time in which no such dull burden should be impose on he.
on this day, when the Duke's l500 be turn into the business, Sexty
yield in a large matter which his partner have be press upon
he for the last week. they buy a cargo of Kauri gum, come from
New Zealand. Lopez have reason for think that Kauri gum must have
a great rise. there be an immense demand for amber, and Kauri gum
might be use as a substitute, and in six month' time would be
double its present value. this unfortunately be a real cargo. he
could not find an individual so enterprising as to venture to deal in
a cargo of Kauri gum after his fashion. but the next good thing be
do. the real cargo be buy, and his name and Sexty's name be
on the bill give for the good. on that day he return home in
high spirit, for he do believe in his own intelligence and good
fortune.
CHAPTER XLIV
Mr. Wharton intend to make a New will
on that afternoon, immediately on the husband's return to the house,
his wife speak to he as her father have desire. on that evening Mr.
Wharton be din at his club, and therefore there be the whole
evening before they; but the thing to be do be disagreeable, and
therefore she do it at once,--rushing into the matter almost before
he have seat himself in the arm-chair which he have appropriate to
his use in the drawing-room. "Papa be talk about our affair
after you leave this morning, and he think that it would be so much
well if you would tell he all about they."
"what make he talk of that to-day?" he say, turn at she almost
angrily and think at once of the Duke's cheque.
"I suppose it be natural that he should be anxious about we,
ferdinand;--and the more natural as he have money to give if he
choose to give it."
"I have ask he for nothing lately;--though, by George, I intend to
ask he and that very roundly. three thousand pound benot much of a
sum of money for your father to have give you."
"and he pay the election bill;--didn't he?"
"he have be complain of that behind my back,--has he? I donot
ask he for it. he offer it. I benot such a fool as to refuse, but
he neednot bring that up as a grievance to you."
"it benot bring up as a grievance. I be say that your standing
have be a heavy expenditure--"
"why do you say so? what make you talk about it at all? why should
you be discuss my affair behind my back?"
"to my own father! and that too when you be tell I every day
that I be to induce he to help you!"
"not by complain that I be poor. but how do it all begin?" she
have to think for a moment before she could recollect how it do
begin. "there have be something," he say, "which you be ashamed to
tell I."
"there be nothing that I be ashamed to tell you. there never have be
and never will be anything." and she stand up as she speak, with open
eye and extended nostril. "whatever may come, however wretche it
may be, I shall not be ashamed of myself."
"but of I!"
"why do you say so? why do you try to make unhappiness between we?"
"you have be talk of--my poverty."
"my father ask why you should go to dovercourt,--and whether it be
because it would save expense."
"you want to go somewhere?"
"not at all. I be content to stay in London. but I say that I
think the expense have a good deal to do with it. of course it have."
"where do you want to be take? I suppose Dovercourt be not
fashionable."
"I want nothing."
"if you be think of travel abroad, I cannot spare the time.
it benot an affair of money, and you have no business to say so. I
think of the place because it be quiet and because I can get up and
down easily. I be sorry that I ever come to live in this house."
"why do you say that, Ferdinand?"
"because you and your father make cabal behind my back. if there be
anything I hate it be that kind of thing."
"you be very unjust," she say to he sob. "I have never
cabal. I have never do anything against you. of course papa
ought to know."
"why ought he to know? why be your father to have the right of
inquiry into all my private affair?"
"because you want his assistance. it be only natural. you always tell
I to get he to assist you. he speak most kindly, say that he
would like to know how the thing be."
"then he willnot know. as for want his assistance, of course I
want the fortune which he ought to give you. he be man of the world
enough to know that as I be in business capital must be useful to
I. I should have think that you would understand as much as that
yourself."
"I do understand it, I suppose."
"then why donot you act as my friend rather than his? why donot you
take my part? it seem to I that you be much more his daughter than
my wife."
"that be most unfair."
"if you have any pluck you would make he understand that for your
sake he ought to say what he mean to do, so that I might have the
advantage of the fortune which I suppose he mean to give you some
day. if you have the slight anxiety to help I you could influence
he. instead of that you talk to he about my poverty. I donot want
he to think that I be a pauper. thatbe not the way to get round
a man like your father, who be rich himself and who think it a
disgrace in other man not to be rich too."
"I cannot tell he in the same breath that you be rich and that you
want money."
"money be the mean by which man make money. if he be confident of
my business hewould shell out his cash quick enough! it be because he
have be teach to think that I be in a small way. hewill find his
mistake some day."
"you willnot speak to he then?"
"I donot say that at all. if I find that it will answer my own
purpose I shall speak to he. but it would be very much easy to I
if I could get you to be cordial in help I."
Emily by this time quite know what such cordiality mean. he have be
so free in his word to she that there could be no mistake. he have
instruct she to "get round" her father. and now again he speak
of her influence over her father. although her illusion be all
melting away,--oh, so quickly vanishing,--still she know that it be
her duty to be true to her husband, and to be his wife rather than
her father's daughter. but what could she say on his behalf, know
nothing of his affair? she have no idea what be his business, what
be his income, what amount of money she ought to spend as his
wife. as far as she could see,--and her common sense in see such
thing be good,--he have no regular income, and be justify in no
expenditure. on her own account she would ask for no information. she
be too proud to request that from he which should be give to she
without any request. but in her own defence she must tell he that
she could use no influence with her father as she know none of the
circumstance by which her father would be guide. "I cannot help you
in the manner you mean," she say, "because I know nothing myself."
"you know that you can trust I to do the good with your money if I
could get hold of it, I suppose?" she certainly do not know this,
and hold her tongue. "you could assure he of that?"
"I could only tell he to judge for himself."
"what you mean be that youwould see I d----d before you would open your
mouth for I to the old man!"
he have never swear at she before, and now she burst out into a flood
of tear. it be to she a terrible outrage. I do not know that a
woman be very much the bad because her husband may forget himself
on an occasion and "rap out an oath at she," as he would call it when
make the good of his own sin. such an offence be compatible with
uniform kindness and most affectionate consideration. I have know
lady who would think little or nothing about it,--who would go no
far than the mild protest,--"do remember where you be!" or,
"my dear john!"--if no stranger be present. but then a wife should
be initiate into it by degree; and there be different tone of
bad language, of which by far the most general be the good-humour
tone. we all of we know man who never damn their servant, or any
inferior, or stranger, or women,--who in fact keep it all for
their bosom friend; and if a little do sometimes flow over in
the freedom of domestic life, the wife be apt to remember that she
be the bosomest of her husband's friend, and so to pardon the
transgression. but here the word have be utter with all its
foul violence, with virulence and vulgarity. it seem to the
victim to be the sign of a terrible crisis in her early married
life,--as though the man who have so speak to she could never again
love she, never again be kind to she, never again be sweetly gentle
and like a lover. and as he speak it he look at she as though he
would like to tear her limb asunder. she be frightened as well as
horrified and astound. she have not a word to say to he. she do
not know in what language to make her complaint of such treatment.
she burst into tear, and throw herself on the sofa hide her face
in her hand. "you provoke I to be violent," he say. but still she
could not speak to he. "I come away from the city, tired with work
and trouble with a thousand thing, and you have not a kind word to
say to I." then there be a pause, during which she still sob.
"if your father have anything to say to I, let he say it. I shall
not run away. but as to go to he of my own accord with a story as
long as my arm about my own affair, I donot mean to do it." then he
pause a moment again. "come, old girl, cheer up! donot pretend to
be break-hearted because I use a hard word. there be bad thing
than that to be bear in the world."
"I--I--I be so startled, Ferdinand."
"a man cannot always remember that he benot with another man. donot
think anything more about it; but do bear this in mind,--that,
situate as we be, your influence with your father may be the making
or the marring of I." and so he leave the room.
she sit for the next ten minute think of it all. the word which
he have speak be so horrible that she could not get they out of she
mind,--could not bring herself to look upon they as a trifle. the
darkness of his countenance still dwelt with her,--and that absence
of all tenderness, that coarse un-marital and yet marital roughness,
which should not at any rate have come to he so soon. the whole man
too be so different from what she have think he to be. before
their marriage no word as to money have ever reach her ear from
his lip. he have talk to she of books,--and especially of poetry.
Shakespeare and Moliere, Dante and Goethe, have be or have seem
