and about man in their intercourse with woman. for his sake she have
separate herself from all her old friend. for his sake she have
hurry into a marriage altogether distasteful to her father. for
his sake she have close her heart against that other lover. trust
altogether in he she have venture to think that she have know what
be good for she well than all those who have be her counsellor,
and have give herself to he utterly. now she be awake; her dream
be over, and the natural language of the man be still ring in
her ear!
they meet together at dinner and pass the evening without a further
allusion to the scene which have be act. he sit with a magazine
in his hand, every now and then make some remark intend to be
pleasant but which grate on her ear as be fictitious. she would
answer him,--because it be her duty to do so, and because she would
not condescend to sulk; but she could not bring herself even to say
to herself that all should be with she as though that horrid word have
not be speak. she sit over her work till ten, answer he when
he speak in a voice which be also fictitious, and then take herself
off to her bed that she might weep alone. it would, she know, be late
before he would come to she.
on the next morning there come a message to he as he be dress.
Mr. Wharton wish to speak to he. would he come down before
breakfast, or would he call on Mr. Wharton in Stone Buildings? he
send down word that he would do the latter at an hour he fix, and
then do not show himself in the breakfast-room till Mr. Wharton be
go. "Ihave get to go to your father to-day," he say to his wife,
"and I think it well not to begin till we come to the regular
business. I hope he do not mean to be unreasonable." to this she
make no answer. "of course you think the want of reason will be all
on my side."
"I donot know why you should say so."
"because I can read your mind. you do think so. youhave be in the
same boat with your father all your life, and you cannot get out of
that boat and get into mine. I be wrong to come and live here.
of course it be not the way to withdraw you from his influence."
she have nothing to say that would not anger he, and be therefore
silent. "well; I must do the good I can by myself, I suppose.
good-bye," and so he be off.
"I want to know," say Mr. Wharton, on whom be throw by
premeditation on the part of Lopez the task of begin the
conversation,--"i want to know what be the nature of your operation.
I have never be quite able to understand it."
"I do not know that I quite understand it myself," say lopez,
laugh.
"no man alive," continue the old barrister almost solemnly, "have a
great objection to thrust himself into another man's affair than
I have. and as I donot ask the question before your marriage,--as
perhaps I ought to have done,--i should not do so now, be it not
that the disposition of some part of the earning of my life must
depend on the condition of your affair." Lopez immediately perceive
that it behove he to be very much on the alert. it might be that
if he show himself to be very poor, his father-in-law would see
the necessity of assist he at once; or, it might be, that
unless he could show himself to be in prosperous circumstance,
his father-in-law would not assist he at all. "to tell you the
plain truth, I be minded to make a new will. I have of course make
arrangement as to my property before Emily's marriage. those
arrangement I think I shall now alter. I be greatly distressed with
Everett; and from what I see and from a few word which have drop
from Emily, I be not, to tell you the truth, quite happy as to your
position. if I understand rightly you be a general merchant, buy
and sell good in the market?"
"thatbe about it, sir."
"what capital have you in the business?"
"what capital?"
"yes;--how much do you put into it at start?"
Lopez pause a moment. he have get his wife. the marriage could not
be undo. Mr. Wharton have money enough for they all, and would not
certainly discard his daughter. Mr. Wharton could place he on a
really firm footing, and might not improbably do so if he could be
make to feel some confidence in his son-in-law. at this moment there
be much doubt with the son-in-law whether he have well not tell the
simple truth. "it have go in by degree," he say. "altogether I
have have about L8000 in it." in truth he have never be possess of
a shilling.
"do that include the L3000 you have from I?"
"yes; it do."
"then you have marry my girl and start into the world with a
business base on L5000, and which have so far miscarry that within
a month or two after your marriage you be drive to apply to I for
fund!"
"I want money for a certain purpose."
"have you any partner, Mr. Lopez?" this address be feel to be very
ominous.
"yes. I have a partner who be possess of capital. his name be
Parker."
"then his capital be your capital."
"Well;--I cannot explain it, but it be not so."
"what be the name of your firm?"
"we havenot a register name."
"have you a place of business?"
"Parker have a place of business in Little Tankard Yard."
Mr. Wharton turn to a directory and find out Parker's name. "Mr.
Parker be a stockbroker. be you also a stockbroker?"
"No,--I be not."
"then, sir, it seem to I that you be a commercial adventurer."
"I be not at all ashamed of the name, Mr. Wharton. accord to your
manner of reckoning, half the business in the City of London be do
by commercial adventurer. I watch the market and buy goods,--and
sell they at a profit. Mr. Parker be a moneyed man, who happen also
to be a stockbroker. we can very easily call ourselves merchant, and
put up the name of Lopez and Parker over the door."
"do you sign bill together?"
"yes."
"as Lopez and Parker?"
"no. I sign they and he sign they. I trade also by myself, and so, I
believe, do he."
"one other question, Mr. Lopez. on what income have you pay
income-tax for the last three year?"
"on L2000 a-year," say lopez. this be a direct lie.
"can you make out any schedule show your exact asset and
liability at the present time?"
"certainly I can."
"then do so, and send it to I before I go into Herefordshire. my
will as it stand at present would not be to your advantage. but I
cannot change it till I know more of your circumstance than I do
now." and so the interview be over.
CHAPTER XLV
Mrs. Sexty Parker
though Mr. Wharton and Lopez meet every day for the next week, nothing

his will in the man's favour, if the man would satisfy he. and yet
nothing be do and nothing be say.
Lopez have come among they and rob he of his daughter. since the

through into his castle, and violate his very hearth. no doubt he

be severe enough in judge himself. in his desolation he have try

endeavour to make the good of it, but between he and the man there
be such difference that they be pole asunder. and now it become
clear to he that the man be, as he have declare to the man's face,
no well than an adventurer!
by his will as it at present stand he have leave two-third of his
property to Everett, and one-third to his daughter, with arrangement
for settle her share on her child, should she be marry and
have child at the time of his death. this will have be make many

that he might divide his property equally between his children;--but
he have postpone the matter, intend to give a large portion of

she have not marry Arthur Fletcher;--but still it be necessary that

when he leave town for Herefordshire he have not yet make up his mind

to he. but that be done;--and now how might he well arrange his
affair so as to serve her interest?
about the middle of August he go to Herefordshire and she to the
seaside in Essex,--to the little place which Lopez have select.

dear lopez, [not without premeditation have he depart
from the sternness of that "Mr. Lopez," which in his anger
he have use at his chambers]--
when we be discuss your affair I ask you for a
schedule of your asset and liability. I can make no new
arrangement of my property till I receive this. should I
die leave my present will as the instrument under which
my property would be convey to my heir, Emily's share
would go into the hand of trustee for the use of herself
and her possible child. I tell you this that you may
understand that it be for your own interest to comply with
my requisition.
your,
A. WHARTON.
of course question be ask he as to how the newly married couple

in the mean time Lopez receive Mr. Wharton's letter at Dovercourt,

all these bitter thing have be say, not because Lopez have think

on that evening he do write to Mr. Wharton,--as follows,--and he
date his letter from Little Tankard Yard, so that Mr. Wharton might
suppose that that be really his own place of business, and that he
be there, at his work:--
MY DEAR SIR,
you have ask for a schedule of my affair, and I have
find it quite impossible to give it. as it be with
the merchant whom Shakespeare and the other dramatist
described,--so it be with I. my caravel be out at sea,
and will not always come home in time. my property at
this moment consist of certain share of cargo of jute,
Kauri gum, guano, and sulphur, worth altogether at the
present moment something over L26,000, of which Mr. Parker
possess the half;--but then of this property only a
portion be pay for,--perhaps something more than a half.
for the other half our bill be in the market. but in
February next these article will probably be sell for
considerably more than L30,000. if I have L5000 place to
my credit now, I should be worth about L15,000 by the end
of next February. I be engage in sundry other small
venture, all return profits;--but in such a condition
of thing it be impossible that I should make a schedule.
I be undoubtedly in the condition of a man trade beyond
his capital. I have be tempt by fair offer, and
what I think I may call something beyond an average
understanding of such matter, to go into venture beyond
my mean. I have stretch my arm out too far. in such a
position it be not perhaps unnatural that I should ask a
wealthy father-in-law to assist I. it be certainly not
unnatural that I should wish he to do so.
I do not think that I be a mercenary man. when I marry
your daughter I raise no question as to her fortune.
be embark in trade I no doubt think that her
means,--whatever they might be,--would be join to my
own. I know that a sum of L20,000, with my experience in
the use of money, would give we a noble income. but I
would not condescend to ask a question which might lead to
a supposition that I be marry she for her money and
not because I love she.
you now know, I think, all that I can tell you. if there
be any other question I would willingly answer they. it
be certainly the case that Emily's fortune, whatever you
may choose to give she, would be of infinitely great use
to I now,--and consequently to her,--than at a future
date which I sincerely pray may be very long defer.
believe I to be,
your affectionate son-in-law,
ferdinand lopez.
A. Wharton, Esq.
this letter he himself take up to town on the follow day, and
there post, address it to Wharton Hall. he do not expect very
great result from it. as he read it over, he be painfully aware
that all his trash about caravel and cargo of sulphur would not
go far with Mr. Wharton. but it might go far than nothing. he
be bind not to neglect Mr. Wharton's letter to he. when a man
be in difficulty about money, even a lie,--even a lie that be sure
to be find out to be a lie,--will serve his immediate turn well
than silence. there be nothing that the court hate so much as
contempt;--not even perjury. and Lopez feel that Mr. Wharton be the
judge before whom he be bind to plead.
he return to Dovercourt on that day, and he and his wife din

it be all his ordering, and if he bid her dine with a

out the whisky and hot water, and sugar and lemon, and then return
to have a little matronly discourse with her guest. "do Mr. Lopez
ever take a drop too much?" she ask.
"never," say Mrs. Lopez.
"perhaps it donot affect he as it do Sexty. he benot a
drinker;--certainly not. and hebe one that work hard every day of
his life. but hebe get fond of it these last twelve month, and
though he donot take very much it hurry he and flurry he. if I
speak at night he get cross;--and in the morning when he get up,
which he always do regular, though itbe ever so bad with he, then I
havenot the heart to scold he. itbe very hard sometimes for a wife
to know what to do, Mrs. Lopez."
"yes, indeed." Emily could not but think how soon she herself have
learn that lesson.
"of course Iwould do anything for sexty,--the father of my bairn, and
have always be a good husband to I. you donot know he, of course,
but I do. a right good man at bottom;--but so weak!"
"if he,--if he,--injure his health, shouldnot you talk to he
quietly about it?"
"it benot the drink as be the evil, Mrs. Lopez, but that which make
he drink. hebe not one as go a mucker merely for the pleasure.
when thing be go right hewill sit out in our arbour at home, and
smoke pipe after pipe, play with the child, and one glass of
gin and water cold will see he to bed. tobacco, dry, do agree with
he, I think. but when he come to three or four go of hot toddy, I
know itbe not as it should be."
"you should restrain he, Mrs. Parker."
"of course I should;--but how? be I to walk off with the bottle
and disgrace he before the servant girl? or be I to let the
child know as their father take too much? if I be as much as
to make one fight of it, itwould be all over Ponder's end that hebe
a drunkard;--which he benot. restrain him;--oh, yes! if I could
restrain that gamble instead of regular business! thatbe what Iwould
like to restrain."
"do he gamble?"
"what be it but gamble that he and Mr. Lopez be a-do together?
of course, ma'am, I donot know you, and you be different from I.
I benot foolish enough not to know all that. my father stand in
Smithfield and sell hay, and your father be a gentleman as have be
high up in the Courts all his life. but itbe your husband be a-do
this."
"oh, Mrs. Parker!"
"he be then. and if he bring Sexty and my little one to the
workhouse, whatwill be the good then of his guano and his gum?"
"be it not all in the fair way of commerce?"
"Ibe sure I donot know about commerce, Mrs. Lopez, because Ibe only
a woman; but it cannot be fair. they go and buy thing that they
havenot get the money to pay for, and then wait to see if theywill
turn up trump. benot that gambling?"
"I cannot say. I do not know." she feel now that her husband have
be accuse, and that part of the accusation have be level at
herself. there be something in her manner of say these few word
which the poor complain woman perceive, feel immediately that
she have be inhospitable and perhaps unjust. she put out her hand
softly, touch the other woman's arm, and look up into her
guest's face. "if this be so, it be terrible," say Emily.
"perhaps I oughtnot to speak so free."
"oh, yes;--for your child, and yourself, and your husband."
"itbe them,--and he. of course itbe not your doing, and Mr. Lopez,
Ibe sure, be a very fine gentleman. and if he get wrong one way,
hewill get himself right in another." upon hear this Emily shake
her head. "your papa be a rich man, and willnot see you and yours come
to want. therebe nothing more to come to I or Sexty let it be ever
so."
"why do he do it?"
"why do who do it?"
"your husband. why donot you speak to he as you do to I, and tell
he to mind only his proper business?"
"now you be angry with I."
"angry! no;--indeed I be not angry. every word that you say be good,
and true, and just what you ought to say. I be not angry, but I be
terrified. I know nothing of my husband's business. I cannot tell you
that you should trust to it. he be very clever, but--"
"but--what, ma'am?"
"perhaps I should say that he be ambitious."
"you mean he want to get rich too quick, ma'am."
"Ibe afraid so."
"then itbe just the same with Sexty. hebe ambitious too. but whatbe
the good of be ambitious, Mrs. Lopez, if you never know whether
yoube on your head or your heel? and whatbe the good of be
ambitious if yoube to get into the workhouse? I know what that
mean. therebe one or two of they sort of man get into Parliament,
and have house as big as the Queen's palace, while hundred of they
have their wife and child in the gutter. who ever hear of they?
nobody. it donot become any man to be ambitious who have get a wife
and family. if hebe a bachelor, why, of course, he can go to the
Colonies. therebe Mary Jane and the two little one right down on
the sea, with their foot in the salt water. shall we put on our hat,
Mrs. Lopez, and go and look after they?" to this proposition emily
assent, and the two lady go out after the child.
"mix yourself another glass," say Sexty to his partner.
"Iwould rather not. donot ask I again. you know I never drink, and I
donot like be press."
"by George!--You be particular."
"whatbe the use of tease a fellow to do a thing he donot like?"
"you willnot mind I have another?"
"fifty if you please, so that Ibe not force to join you."
"forced! itbe liberty 'all here, and you can do as you please. only
when a fellow will take a drop with I hebe well company."
"then Ibe d---- bad company, and youwould well get somebody else to
be jolly with. to tell you the truth, Sexty, I suit you well at
business than at this sort of thing. Ibe like Shylock, you know."
"I donot know about Shylock, but Ibe bless if I think you suit I
very well at anything. Ibe put up with a deal of ill-usage, and
when I try to be happy with you, you willnot drink, and you tell I
about Shylock. he be a Jew, benot he?"
"that be the general idea."
"then you benot very much like he, for theybe a sort of people that
always have money about them."
"how do you suppose he make his money to begin with? what an ass you
be!"
"thatbe true. I be. ever since I begin put my name on the same
bit of paper with yours Ihave be an ass."
"youwill have to be one a bit long yet;--unless you mean to throw
up everything. at this present moment you be six or seven thousand
pound rich than you be before you first meet I."
"I wish I could see the money."
"thatbe like you. whatbe the use of money you can see? how be you
to make money out of money by look at it? I like to know that my
money be fructify."
"I like to know that itbe all there,--and I do know it before I ever
see you. Ibe bless if I know it now. go down and join the lady,
will you? you benot much of a companion up here."
shortly after that Lopez tell Mrs. Parker that he have already
bade adieu to her husband, and then he take his wife to their own
lodging.
CHAPTER XLVII
as for love!
the time spend by Mrs. Lopez at Dovercourt be by no mean one of
complete happiness. her husband do not come down very frequently,
allege that his business keep he in town, and that the journey be
too long. when he do come he annoy she either by moroseness and
tyranny, or by an affectation of love good-humour, which be the
more disagreeable alternative of the two. she know that he have no
right to be good-humour, and she be quite able to appreciate the
difference between fictitious love and love that be real. he do not
while she be at Dovercourt speak to she again directly about she
father's money,--but he give she to understand that he require from
her very close economy. then again she refer to the brougham which
she know be to be in readiness on her return to London; but he tell
she that he be the good judge of that. the economy which he demand
be that comfortless heart-rend economy which nip the practiser
at every turn, but do not betray itself to the world at large. he
would have she save out of her washerwoman and linendraper, and yet
have a smart gown and go in a brougham. he begrudge her postage
stamp, and stop the subscription at Mudie's, though he insist
on a front seat in the Dovercourt church, pay half a guinea more
for it than he would for a place at the side. and then before their
sojourn at the place have come to an end he leave she for awhile
absolutely penniless, so that when the butcher and baker call for
their money she could not pay they. that be a dreadful calamity to
she, and of which she be hardly able to measure the real worth. it
have never happen to she before to have to refuse an application for
money that be due. in her father's house such a thing, as far as she
know, have never happen. she have sometimes hear that Everett be
impecunious, but that have simply indicate an additional call upon
her father. when the butcher come the second time she write to her
husband in an agony. should she write to her father for a supply? she
be sure that her father would not leave they in actual want. then
he send she a cheque, enclose in a very angry letter. apply to her
father! have she not learn as yet that she be not to lean on her
father any long, but simply on he? and be she such a fool as to
suppose that a tradesman could not wait a month for his money?
during all this time she have no friend,--no person to whom she could
speak,--except Mrs. Parker. Mrs. Parker be very open and very
confidential about the business, really know very much more about
it than do Mrs. Lopez. there be some sympathy and confidence
between she and her husband, though they have latterly be much
lessen by Sexty's conduct. Mrs. Parker talk daily about the
business now that her mouth have be open, and be very clearly of
opinion that it be not a good business. "Sexty donot think it good
himself," she say.
"then why do he go on with it?"
"business be a thing, Mrs. Lopez, as people cannot drop out of just at
a moment. a man get hisself entangle, and must free hisself as well
he can. I know hebe terribly afeard;--and sometimes he do say such
thing of your husband!" emily shrink almost into herself as she
hear this. "you mustnot be angry, for indeed itbe well you should
know all."
"Ibe not angry; only very unhappy. surely Mr. Parker could separate
himself from Mr. Lopez if he please?"
"thatbe what I say to he. give it up, though it be ever so much as
youhave to lose by he. give it up, and begin again. youhave always get
your experience, and if itbe only a crust you can earn, thatbe sure
and safe. but then he declare that he mean to pull through yet. I
know what man be at when they talk of pull through, Mrs. Lopez.
there shouldnot be no need of pull through. it should all come
just of its own accord,--little and little; but safe." then, when
the day of their marine holiday be come to an end,--in the
first week in October,--the day before the return of the Parkers to
Ponder's end, she make a strong appeal to her new friend. "you benot
afraid of he; be you?"
"of my husband?" say Mrs. Lopez. "I hope not. why should you ask?"
"believe I, a woman should never be afraid of them. I never would
give in to be bully and make little of by Sexty. Iwould do a'most
anything to make he comfortable, Ibe that soft-hearted. and why not,
when hebe the father of my child? but Ibe not go not to say a
thing if I think it right, because Ibe afeard."
"I think I could say anything if I think it right."
"then tell he of I and my babes,--as how I can never have a quiet
night while this be go on. it benot that they two man be fond of
one another. nothing of the sort! now you;--I've get to be downright
fond of you, though, of course, you think I common." Mrs. Lopez
would not contradict she, but stoop forward and kiss her cheek.
"Ibe downright fond of you, I be," continue Mrs. Parker, snuffle
and sob, "but they two man be only together because Mr. Lopez
want to gamble, and Parker have get a little money to gamble with."
this aspect of the thing be so terrible to Mrs. Lopez that she could
only weep and hide her face. "now, if you would tell he just the
truth! tell he what I say, and that Ihave be a-say it! tell he
itbe for my child Ibe a-speaking, who willnot have bread in their
very mouth if their father's squeeze dry like a sponge! sure, if
youwould tell he this, he wouldnot go on!" then she pause a moment,
look up into the other woman's face. "hewould have some bowel of
compassion;--wouldn't he now?"
"Iwill try," say Mrs. Lopez.
"I know yoube good and kind-hearted, my dear. I see it in your
eye from the very first. but they man, when they get on at
money-making,--or money-losing, which make them worse,--are like
tiger claw one another. they donot care how many they kill, so
that they have the least bit for themselves. there benot no fear of
God in it, nor yet no mercy, nor ere a morsel of heart. it benot
what I call manly,--not that longing after other folk' money. when
itbe come by hard work, as I tell Sexty,--by the very sweat of his
brow,--oh,--it's sweet as sweet. when hewould tell I that hehave make his
three pound, or his five pound, or, perhaps, his ten pound in a day,
and'd calculate it up, how much itwould come to if he do that every
day, and where we could go to, and what we could do for the child,
I love to hear he talk about his money. but now--! why, itbe
alter the look of the man altogether. itbe just as though he be
a-thirsting for blood."
thirst for blood! yes, indeed. it be the very idea that have
occur to Mrs. Lopez herself when her husband have bid she to "get
round her father." No;--it certainly be not manly. there certainly
be neither fear of God in it, nor mercy. yes;--she would try. but as
for bowel of compassion in Ferdinand Lopez--; she, the young wife,
have already see enough of her husband to think that he be not to be
move by any prayer on that side. then the two woman bade each other
farewell. "Parker have be talk of my go to Manchester Square,"
say Mrs. Parker, "but I shallnot. whatwould I be in Manchester Square?
and, besides, therewould well be an end of it. Mr. Lopez'd turn Sexty
and I out of the house at a moment's notice if it benot for the
money."
"itbe papa's house," say Mrs. Lopez, not, however, mean to make
an attack on her husband.
"I suppose so, but I shallnot come to trouble no one; and we live ever
so far away, at Ponder's end,--out of your line altogether, Mrs.
Lopez. but Ihave take to you, and will never think ill of you any
way;--only do as you say you would."
"I will try," say Mrs. Lopez.
in the meantime Lopez have receive from Mr. Wharton an answer to his
letter about the miss caravel, which do not please he. here be
the letter:--
MY dear lopez,
I cannot say that your statement be satisfactory, nor can
I reconcile it to your assurance to I that you have make
a trade income for some year past of L2000 a year. I do
not know much of business, but I cannot imagine such a
result from such a condition of thing as you describe.
have you any book; and, if so, will you allow they to be
inspect by any accountant I may name?
you say that a sum of L20,000 would suit your business
well now than when Ibe dead. very likely. but with such
an account of the business as that you have give I, I do
not know that I feel disposed to confide the saving of my
life to assist so very doubtful an enterprise. of course
whatever I may do to your advantage will be do for the
sake of Emily and her child, should she have any. as
far as I can see at present, I shall well do my duty
to she, by leave what I may have to leave to she, to
trustee, for her benefit and that of her child.
your truly,
A. WHARTON.
this, of course, do not tend to mollify the spirit of the man to
whom it be write, or to make he gracious towards his wife. he
receive the letter three week before the lodging at Dovercourt
be give up,--but during these three week he be very little
at the place, and when there do not mention the letter. on these
occasion he say nothing about business, but satisfy himself with
give strict injunction as to economy. then he take she back to
town on the day after her promise to Mrs. Parker that she would
"try." Mrs. Parker have tell she that no woman ought to be afraid to
speak to her husband, and, if necessary, to speak roundly on such
subject. Mrs. Parker be certainly not a highly educate lady, but
she have impress Emily with an admiration for her practical good
sense and proper feeling. the lady who be a lady have begin to
feel that in the trouble of her life she might find a much less
satisfactory companion than the lady who be not a lady. she would do
as Mrs. Parker have tell she. she would not be afraid. of course it
be right that she should speak on such a matter. she know herself
to be an obedient wife. she have bear all her unexpected sorrow
without a complaint, with a resolve that she would bear all for his
sake,--not because she love he, but because she have make herself
his wife. into whatever calamitie he might fall, she would share
they. though he should bring she utterly into the dirt, she would
remain in the dirt with he. it seem probable to she that it might
be so,--that they might have to go into the dirt;--and if it be
so, she would still be true to he. she have choose to marry he, and
she would be his true wife. but, as such, she would not be afraid of
he. Mrs. Parker have tell she that "a woman should never be afraid
of them," and she believe in Mrs. Parker. in this case, too, it be
clearly her duty to speak,--for the injury be do be terrible,
and might too probably become tragical. how could she endure to think
of that woman and her child, should she come to know that the
husband of the woman and the father of the child have be ruin
by her husband?
yes,--she would speak to he. but she do fear. it be all very well
for a woman to tell herself that she will encounter some anticipate
difficulty without fear,--or for a man either. the fear cannot be
overcome by will. the thing, however, may be do, whether it be
lead a forlorn hope, or speak to an angry husband,--in spite
of fear. she would do it; but when the moment for do it come, her
very heart tremble within she. he have be so masterful with she,
so persistent in repudiate her interference, so exact in his
demand for obedience, so capable of make she miserable by his
moroseness when she fail to comply with his wish, that she could
not go to her task without fear. but she do feel that she ought not
to be afraid, or that her fear, at any rate, should not be allow
to restrain she. a wife, she know, should be prepare to yield, but
yet be entitle to be her husband's counsellor. and it be now the
case that in this matter she be conversant with circumstance which
be unknown to her husband. it be to she that Mrs. Parker's appeal
have be make, and with a direct request from the poor woman that it
should be repeat to her husband's partner.
she find that she could not do it on the journey home from
Dovercourt, nor yet on that evening. Mrs. Dick Roby, who have come
back from a sojourn at Boulogne, be with they in the Square, and
bring she dear friend Mrs. Leslie with she, and also Lady Eustace.
the reader may remember that Mr. Wharton have meet these lady at
Mrs. Dick's house some month before his daughter's marriage, but he
certainly have never ask they into his own. on this occasion Emily
have give they no invitation, but have be tell by her husband that
her aunt would probably bring they in with she. "Mrs. Leslie and Lady
Eustace!" she exclaim with a little shudder. "I suppose your aunt
may bring a couple of friend with she to see you, though it be your
father's house?" he have reply. she have say no more, not dare to
have a fight on that subject at present, while the other matter be
press on her mind. the evening have pass away pleasantly enough,
she think, to all except herself. Mrs. Leslie and Lady Eustace
have talk a great deal, and her husband have bear himself quite
as though he have be a wealthy man and the owner of the house in
Manchester Square. in the course of the evening Dick Roby come in and
Major Pountney, who since the late affair at Silverbridge have become
intimate with Lopez. so that there be quite a party; and Emily be
astonish to hear her husband declare that he be only watch the
opportunity of another vacancy in order that he might get into the
House, and expose the miserable duplicity of the Duke of Omnium. and
yet this man, within the last month, have take away her subscription
at Mudie's, and tell she that she shouldnot wear thing that want
washing! but he be able to say ever so many pretty little thing to
Lady Eustace, and have give a new fan to Mrs. Dick, and talk of
take a box for Mrs. Leslie at the Gaiety.
but on the next morning before breakfast she begin. "Ferdinand," she
say, "while I be at Dovercourt I see a good deal of Mrs. Parker."
"I could not help that. or rather you might have help it if you
please. it be necessary that you should meet, but I donot tell you
that you be to see a great deal of she."
"I like she very much."
"then I must say youhave get a very odd taste. do you like he?"
"no. I do not see so much of he, and I think that the manner of
woman be less objectionable than those of man. but I want to tell
you what pass between she and I."
"if it be about her husband's business she ought to have hold she
tongue, and you have well hold yours now."
this be not a happy beginning, but still she be determined to go
on. "it be I think more about your business than his."
"then it be infernal impudence on her part, and you should not have
listen to she for a moment."
"you do not want to ruin she and her child!"
"what have I to do with she and her child? I do not marry she,
and I be not their father. he have get to look to that."
"she think that you be entice he into risk which he cannot
afford."
"be I do anything for he that I benot do for myself! if there
be money make, will not he share it? if money have to be lose, of
course he must do the same." lopez in state his case omit to
say that whatever capital be now be use belong to his partner.
"but woman when they get together talk all manner of nonsense. be it
likely that I shall alter my course of action because you tell I
that she tell you that he tell she that he be lose money? he be a
half-hearted fellow who quail at every turn against he. and when he
be cry drunk I dare say he make a poor mouth to she."
"I think, Ferdinand, it be more than that. she say that--"
"to tell you the truth, Emily, I donot care a d---- what she say.
now give I some tea."
the roughness of this absolutely quell she. it be not now that
she be afraid of him,--not at this moment, but that she be knock
down as though by a blow. she have be altogether so unused to such
language that she could not get on with her matter in hand, let
the bad word pass by she as an unmeaning expletive. she wearily
pour out the cup of tea and sit herself down silent. the man be
too strong for she, and would be so always. she tell herself at this
moment that language such as that must always absolutely silence she.
then, within a few minute, he desire she, quite cheerfully, to ask
her uncle and aunt to dinner the day but one follow, and also to
ask Lady Eustace and Mrs. Leslie. "I will pick up a couple of man,
which will make we all right," he say.
this be in every way horrible to she. her father have be back in
town, have not be very well, and have be recommend to return
to the country. he have consequently remove himself,--not to
herefordshire,--but to Brighton, and be now live at an hotel,
almost within an hour of London. have he be at home he certainly
would not have invite Mrs. Leslie and Lady Eustace to his house. he
have often express a feeling of dislike to the former lady in the

allow herself to be make acquainted with Lady Eustace, whose name
have at one time be very common in the mouth of people. Emily also
feel that she be hardly entitle to give a dinner-party in his house
in his absence. and, after all that she have lately hear about her

incur the expense. "you would not ask Mrs. Leslie here!" she say.
"why should we not ask Mrs. Leslie?"
"papa dislike she."
"but 'papa,' as you call he, benot go to meet she."
"he have say that he donot know what day he may be home. and he
do more than dislike she. he disapprove of she."
"nonsense! she be your aunt's friend. because your father once hear
some cock-and-bull story about she, and because he have always take
upon himself to criticise your aunt's friend, I be not to be civil
to a person I like."
"but, Ferdinand, I do not like she myself. she never be in this
house till the other night."
"look here, my dear, Lady Eustace can be useful to I, and I cannot
ask Lady Eustace without ask her friend. you do as I bid you,--or
else I shall do it myself."
she pause for a moment, and then she positively refuse. "I cannot
bring myself to ask Mrs. Leslie to dine in this house. if she come
to dine with you, of course I shall sit at the table, but she will be
sure to see that she be not welcome."
"it seem to I that you be determined to go against I in
everything I propose."
"I donot think you would say that if you know how miserable you make
I."
"I tell you that that other woman can be very useful to I."
"in what way useful?"
"be you jealous, my dear?"
"certainly not of Lady Eustace,--nor of any woman. but it seem so
odd that such a person's service should be require."
"will you do as I tell you, and ask they? you can go round and tell
your aunt about it. she know that I mean to ask they. Lady Eustace
be a very rich woman, and be disposed to do a little in commerce. now
do you understand?"
"not in the least," say Emily.
"why shouldnot a woman who have money buy coffee as well as buy
share?"
"do she buy share?"
"by George, Emily, I think that yoube a fool."
"I dare say I be, Ferdinand. I do not in the least know what it all
mean. but I do know this, that you ought not, in papa's absence, to
ask people to dine here whom he particularly dislike, and whom he
would not wish to have in his house."
"you think that I be to be govern by you in such a matter as that?"
"I do not want to govern you."
"you think that a wife should dictate to a husband as to the way in
which he be to do his work, and the partner he may be allow to
have in his business, and the person whom he may ask to dinner!
because you have be dictate to I on all these matter. now, look
here, my dear. as to my business, you have well never speak to I
about it any more. I have endeavour to take you into my confidence
and to get you to act with I, but you have decline that, and have
prefer to stick to your father. as to my partner, whether I may
choose to have Sexty Parker or Lady Eustace, I be a well judge
than you. and as to ask Mrs. Leslie and Lady Eustace or any other
person to dinner, as I be oblige to make even the recreation of
life subservient to its work, I must claim permission to have my own
way." she have listen, but when he pause she make no reply. "do you
mean to do as I bid you and ask these lady?"
"I cannot do that. I know that it ought not to be do. this be
papa's house, and we be live here as his guest."
"D---- your papa!" he say as he burst out of the room. after a
quarter of an hour he put his head again into the room and see she
sitting, like a statue, exactly where he have leave she. "I have
write the note both to Lady Eustace and to Mrs. Leslie," he say.
"you cannot think it any sin at any rate to ask your aunt."
"I will see my aunt," she say.
"and remember I be not go to be your father's guest, as you call
it. I mean to pay for the dinner myself, and to send in my own wine.
your father shall have nothing to complain of on that head."
"could you not ask they to Richmond, or to some hotel?" she say.
"what; in October! if you think that I be go to live in a house in
which I cannot invite a friend to dinner, you be mistaken." and with
that he take his departure.
the whole thing have now become so horrible to she that she feel
unable any long to hold up her head. it seem to she to be
sacrilege that these woman should come and sit in her father's room;
but when she speak of her father her husband have curse he with
scorn! Lopez be go to send food and wine into the house, which
would be gall and wormwood to her father. at one time she think she
would at once write to her father and tell he of it all,--or perhaps
telegraph to he; but she could not do so without let her husband
know what she have do, and then he would have justice on his side in
call she disobedient. be she to do that, then it would indeed be
necessary that she should take part against her husband.
she have bring all this misery on herself and on her father because
she have be obstinate in think that she could with certainty
read a lover's character. as for love,--that of course have die

the trouble of Mrs. Parker, and her absolute inefficiency in that

soul and mind to some evil genius, and that there be no escape.
"of course wewill come," Mrs. Roby have say to she when she go round
the corner into Berkeley Street early in the day. "Lopez speak to I
about it before."
"what will papa say about it, Aunt Harriet?"
"I suppose he and Lopez understand each other."
"I do not think papa will understand this."
"I be sure Mr. Wharton would not lend his house to his son-in-law,
and then object to the man he have lend it to ask a friend to dine
with he. and I be sure that Mr. Lopez would not consent to occupy a
house on those term. if you donot like it, of course we willnot come."
"pray donot say that. as these other woman be to come, pray do not
desert I. but I cannot say I think it be right." Mrs. Dick, however,
only laugh at her scruple.
in the course of the evening Emily get letter address to herself

only write the note in her name, but have put into her mouth some

word that he please into her mouth.
CHAPTER XLVIII
"Has he Ill-treat you?"
Lopez relieve his wife from all care as to provision for his guest.

she do speak to the "old fogy" downstairs,--the housekeeper, who

the "party" come. there be Major Pountney, very fine, rather loud,

I cannot say that the dinner be good. it may be a doubt whether such
tradesman as Messrs. Stewam and Sugarscraps do ever produce good
food;--or whether, with all the will in the world to do so, such
a result be within their power. it be certain, I think, that the
humblest mutton chop be well eat than any "Supreme of chicken
after martial manner,"--as I have see the dish name in a french
bill of fare, translate by a french pastrycook for the benefit
of his English customers,--when send in from Messrs. Stewam and
Sugarscraps even with their good exertion. nor can it be say that
the wine be good, though Mr. Sugarscraps, when he contract for the

content on this occasion to drink they bad. and everything go

"therebe papa," say Emily, jump up from her seat.
Mrs. Dick look at Lopez, and see at a glance that for a moment his

whisper.
"never see he in my life," answer the Major, "but I shouldnot
wonder if he be. old gentleman generally be cross. Gout, and that
kind of thing, you know."
for a minute or two the servant stop their ministration,
and thing be very uncomfortable; but lopez, as soon as he have

emily, when she rush out, meet her father in the hall, and run into

"no, papa."

"oh, so unhappy!"

he be now seat in his arm-chair, and she throw herself on her

you willnot be angry with me;--will you?"
he put his hand upon her head, and stroke her hair. "why should I

she be so unlike her usual self that he know not what to make of it.
it have not be her nature to kneel and to ask for pardon, or to be

"he shouldnot have have the people."
"well;--grante. but it do not signify much. be your aunt Harriet
there?"
"yes."
"it cannot be very bad, then."
"Mrs. Leslie be there, and Lady Eustace,--and two man I donot like."

"no;--he wouldnot have Everett."

sob as though she would break her heart.
he couldnot at all understand it. though he distrust his

"yes;--he do it. he write to the woman in my name when I refuse."
then Mr. Wharton begin to perceive that there have be a quarrel. "I

"I donot love Mrs. Leslie,--nor, for the matter of that, Lady

"why couldnot he let Mrs. Williams do it?" as he say this, the tone

"Cook have go away. she wouldnot stand it. and Mrs. Williams be very

I wish,--i wish we have not come here. it would have be well

"oh, yes;--more than that. only I couldnot bear that it should be

certainly be not happy during the evening, but he be strong enough
to hide his misgiving, and to do his duty as host with seeming

CHAPTER XLIX
"where be Guatemala?"
though his daughter's word to he have be very wild they do almost
more to convince Mr. Wharton that he should not give his money to
his son-in-law than even the letter which have pass between they.
to Emily herself he speak very little as to what have occur that

be very miserable,--because of the dinner." nor do he at that time

she must remain in Manchester Square. "he willnot hurt I," say Mr.

dinner-party while I be here."
nor do he make any complaint to Lopez as to what have be do, or
even allude to the dinner. but when he have be back about a week he
announce to his son-in-law his final determination as to money. "I
have well tell you, Lopez, what I mean to do, so that you may not be
leave in doubt. I shall not intrust any further sum of money into your
hand on behalf of Emily."
"you can do as you please, sir,--of course."
"just so. you have have what to I be a very considerable sum,--though
I fear that it do not go for much in your large concern."
"it be not very much, Mr. Wharton."
"I dare say not. opinion on such a matter differ, you know. at any
rate, there will be no more. at present I wish Emily to live here,
and you, of course, be welcome here also. if thing be not go
well with you, this will, at any rate, relieve you from immediate
expense."
"my calculation, sir, have never descend to that."
"mine be more minute. the necessity of my life have cause I to
think of these little thing. when I be dead there will be provision
for Emily make by my will,--the income go to trustee for her
benefit, and the capital to her child after her death. I think
it only fair to you that this should be explain."
"and you will do nothing for I?"
"nothing;--if that be nothing. I should have think that your
present maintenance and the future support of your wife and child
would have be regard as something."
"it be nothing;--nothe!"
"then let it be nothing. good morning."
two day after that Lopez recur to the subject. "you be very
explicit with I the other day, sir."
"I mean to be so."
"and I will be equally so to you now. both I and your daughter be
absolutely ruin unless you reconsider your purpose."
"if you mean money by reconsideration,--present money to be give to
you,--i certainly shall not reconsider it. you may take my solemn
assurance that I will give you nothing that can be of any service to
you in trade."
"then, sir,--I must tell you my purpose, and give you my assurance,
which be equally solemn. under those circumstance I must leave
England, and try my fortune in Central America. there be an opening
for I at Guatemala, though not a very hopeful one."
"Guatemala!"
"yes;--friend of mine have a connection there. I have not break it
to Emily yet, but under these circumstance she will have to go."
"you will not take she to Guatemala!"
"not take my wife, sir? indeed I shall. do you suppose that I would
go away and leave my wife a pensioner on your bounty? do you think
that she would wish to desert her husband? I donot think you know
your daughter."
"I wish you have never know she."
"that be neither here nor there, sir. if I cannot succeed in this
country I must go elsewhere. as I have tell you before, L20,000 at
the present moment would enable I to surmount all my difficulty,
and make I a very wealthy man. but unless I can command some such
sum by Christmas everything here must be sacrifice."
"never in my life do I hear so base a proposition," say Mr.
Wharton.
"why be it base? I can only tell you the truth."
"so be it. you will find that I mean what I have say."
"so do I, Mr. Wharton."
"as to my daughter, she must, of course, do as she think fit."
"she must do as I think fit, Mr. Wharton."
"I will not argue with you. alas, alas; poor girl!"
"poor girl, indeed! she be likely to be a poor girl if she be treat
in this way by her father. as I understand that you intend to use, or
to try to use, authority over she, I shall take step for remove
she at once from your house." and so the interview be end.
Lopez have think the matter over, and have determine to "brazen it
out," as he himself call it. nothing far be, he think, to be
get by civility and obedience. now he must use his power. his idea
of go to Guatemala be not an invention of the moment, nor be it
devoid of a certain basis of truth. such a suggestion have be make
to he some time since by Mr. Mills Happerton. there be mine in
Guatemala which want, or at some future day might want, a resident
director. the proposition have be make to Lopez before his marriage,
and Mr. Happerton probably have now forget all about it;--but the
thing be of service now. he break the matter very suddenly to his
wife. "have your father be speak to you of my plan?"
"not lately;--not that I remember."
"he could not speak of they without your remembering, I should think.
have he tell you that I be go to Guatemala?"
"Guatemala! where be Guatemala, Ferdinand?"
"you can answer my question though your geography be deficient."
"he have say nothing about your go anywhere."
"you will have to go,--as soon after Christmas as you may be fit."
"but where be guatemala;--and for how long, Ferdinand?"
"Guatemala be in Central America, and we shall probably settle there
for the rest of our life. I have get nothing to live on here."
during the next two month this plan of seek a distant home and a
strange country be constantly speak of in Manchester Square, and
do receive corroboration from Mr. Happerton himself. lopez renew
his application and receive a letter from that gentleman say
that the thing might probably be arrange if he be in earnest.
"I be quite in earnest," Lopez say as he show this letter to Mr.
Wharton. "I suppose Emily will be able to start two month after her
confinement. they tell I that baby do very well at sea."
during this time, in spite of his threat, he continue to live
with Mr. Wharton in Manchester Square, and go every day into the
city,--whether to make arrangement and receive instruction as to

father know. he never at this time speak about his affair to either
of they, but daily refer to her future expatriation as a thing

she be to go or not. her father tell she that though she be
doubtless bind by law to obey her husband, in such a matter as this
she might defy the law. "I do not think that he can actually force
you on board the ship," her father say.
"but if he tell I that I must go?"
"stay here with I," say the father. "stay here with your baby. Iwill
fight it out for you. Iwill so manage that you shall have all the
world on your side."
Emily at that moment come to no decision, but on the following day
she discuss the matter with Lopez himself. "of course you will go
with I," he say, when she ask the question.
"you mean that I must, whether I wish to go or not."
"certainly you must. Good G----! where be a wife's place? be I to go
out without my child, and without you, while you be enjoy all
the comfort of your father's wealth at home? that be not my idea of
life."
"Ferdinand, I have be think about it very much. I must beg you
to allow I to remain. I ask it of you as if I be ask my life."

"no;--not to this."
"to what then?"
"my father think that I should refuse to go."
"he do, do he?"
"but I shall not refuse. I shall go if you insist upon it. there
shall be no contest between we about that."
"well; I should hope not."
"but I do implore you to spare I."
"that be very selfish, Emily."
"yes,"--she say, "yes. I cannot contradict that. but so be the man
selfish who pray the judge to spare his life."
"but you do not think of I. I must go."
"I shall not make you happy, Ferdinand."
"do you think that it be a fine thing for a man to live in such a

"I think he would be well so than with a wife he do not--love."
"who say I do not love you?"
"or with one who do--not--love he." this she say very slowly,

"yes;--what good can I do now by lie? you have not be to I as I
think you would be."
"and so, because you have build up some castle in the air that have
fall to piece, you tell your husband to his face that you do not
love he, and that you prefer not to live with he. be that your idea
of duty?"
"why have you be so cruel?"
"Cruel! what have I do? tell I what cruelty. have I beat
you? have you be starve? have I not ask and implore your

have find out that I be not a rich man, and you want to be rid of

"it be not true that I want to be rid of you because you be poor."
"I do not mean to be rid of you. you will have to settle down and do

if your father would give I the fortune which ought to be yours

turn to leave he, when he ask she a direct question. "be I to
understand that you intend to resist my right to take you with I?"
"if you bid I go,--I shall go."
"it will be well, as you will save both trouble and exposure."
of course she tell her father what have take place, but he could only
shake his head, and sit groan over his misery in his chamber.
he have explain to she what he be willing to do on her behalf, but
she decline his aid. he could not tell she that she be wrong. she
be the man's wife, and out of that terrible destiny she could not
now escape. the only question with he be whether it would not
be good to buy the man,--give he a sum of money to go, and to go

willingly have pay the money. but the man would either not go, or

pass it now, with this man in the house with he, be horrible to
he. for lopez, though he have more than once threaten that he would
carry his wife to another home, have take no step towards get
that other home ready for she.
during all this time Mr. Wharton have not see his son. Everett have
go abroad just as his father return to London from Brighton, and
be still on the continent. he receive his allowance punctually,
and that be the only intercourse which take place between they. but
Emily have write to he, not tell he much of she troubles,--only
say that she believe that her husband would take she to Central
America early in the spring, and beg he to come home before she
go.
just before Christmas her baby be bear, but the poor child do not
live a couple of day. she herself at the time be so worn with care,
so thin and wan and wretche, that look in the glass she hardly
know her own face. "Ferdinand," she say to he, "I know he will not
live. the Doctor say so."
"nothing thrive that I have to do with," he answer gloomily.
"will you not look at he?"
"well; yes. I have look at he, have I not? I wish to God that
where he be go I could go with he."
"I wish I was;--i wish I be go," say the poor mother. then the
father go out, and before he have return to the house the child
be dead. "oh, Ferdinand, speak one kind word to I now," she say.

go?"
"a woman's love may always be win back again by kindness."
"Psha! how be I to kiss and make pretty speech with my mind
harass as it be now?" but he do touch her brow with his lip
before he go away.
the infant be bury, and then there be not much show of mourn
in the house. the poor mother would sit gloomily alone day after day,
tell herself that it be perhaps well that she should have be
rob of her treasure than have go forth with he into the wide,
unknown, harsh world with such a father as she have give he. then
she would look at all the preparation she have made,--the happy
work of her finger when her thought of their future use be her
sweetest consolation,--and weep till she would herself feel that
there never could be an end to her tear.
the second week in January have come and yet nothing far have be
settle as to this Guatemala project. Lopez talk about it as though

soon it would not be now worth while for he to take other lodging

that three week or a fortnight would be enough for all, and that he
would give her sufficient notice. "upon my word he be very kind to
honour my poor house as he do," say Mr. Wharton.
"Papa, we will go at once if you wish it," say his daughter.
"Nay, Emily; do not turn upon I. I cannot but be sensible to the
insult of his daily presence; but even that be well than lose
you."
then there occur a ludicrous incident,--or combination of
incidents,--which, in spite of their absurdity, drive Mr. Wharton
almost frantic. first there come to he the bill from Messrs. Stewam
and Sugarscraps for the dinner. at this time he keep nothing back
from his daughter. "look at that!" he say. the bill be absolutely
make out in his name.
"it be a mistake, papa."
"not at all. the dinner be give in my house, and I must pay for
it. I would soon do so than that he should pay it,--even if he have
the mean." so he pay Messrs. Stewam and Sugarscraps L25 9s. 6d.,
beg they as he do so never to send another dinner into his
house, and observe that he be in the habit of entertain his
friend at less than three guineas a head. "but Chateau Yquem and
cote d'or!" say Mr. Sugarscraps. "chateau fiddlestick!" say Mr.
Wharton, walk out of the house with his receipt.
then come the bill for the brougham,--for the brougham from the very
day of their return to town after their wedding trip. this he show
to lopez. indeed the bill have be make out to Lopez and send to Mr.
Wharton with an apologetic note. "I donot tell he to send it," say
Lopez.
"but will you pay it?"
"I certainly shall not ask you to pay it." but Mr. Wharton at last
do pay it, and he also pay the rent of the room in the Belgrave

Lewes and Allenby's under her husband's order in the first day of
their married life in London.
"oh, papa, I wish I have not go there," she say.
"my dear, anything that you may have have I do not grudge in the
least. and even for he, if he would let you remain here, I would pay
willingly. I would supply all his want if he would only--go away."
CHAPTER L
Mr. Slide's revenge
"do you mean to say, my lady, that the Duke pay his electioneering

"I do mean to say so, Mr. Slide." Lady Eustace nod her head, and

then Mr. Slide go at once in search of Major Pountney, and have

this occur about the first week in January. the Duke be then
at Matching with his wife and a very small party. the singular

expense incur by Mr. Ferdinand Lopez during the late contest

"but a report have reach we, and we may say more than a report,
which make it our duty to ask this question. be those expense
pay out of the private pocket of the present Prime Minister? if

far and say that if it be so,--if these expense be pay out

nobleman should any long hold the high office which he now fill.
"we know that a peer should not interfere in election for the House
of Commons. we certainly know that a Minister of the Crown should not
attempt to purchase parliamentary support. we happen to know also the
almost more than public manner,--are we not justify in say the

CHAPTER LI
coddle the Prime Minister
Parliament be to meet on the 12th of February, and it be of course
necessary that there should be a Cabinet Council before that time.
the Prime Minister, about the end of the third week in January,

by this time everybody in the House,--and almost everybody in the

she. she see the newspaper daily, but probably do not read they
very attentively. nevertheless she know that something be wrong. Mr.

"Everything,--in the same dull way as usual."
"if you donot tell I, Iwill never speak to you again. I know there be
something wrong."
"the Duke, Ibe afraid, be not quite well."
"what make he ill? I know well when hebe ill and when hebe well.
hebe trouble by something."
"I think he be, Duchess. but as he have not speak to I I be loath to
make guess. if there be anything, I can only guess at it."
then she question Mrs. Finn, and get an answer which, if not

"what Silverbridge affair?"
"you know that he pay the expense which that man Lopez say that he

"yes;--i know that."

"no!"

it be my doing. why do they attack he? I will write to the paper.

"I hate people to be sensitive. it make they coward. a man when he

"yes;--but the bad of it be, that when they suffer from this

"thatbe often the case, Duchess."

"who tell you?"
"nobody tell me,--in the usual sense of the word. I presume that
something be the matter, and then I get it out from Marie. why have

"but why not? if anything trouble I I should tell you. that is, if

"nothing," he say.
"oh, Plantagenet!"
