a man to be put in the front of everything, and then to be judge as
though he could give all his time to the picking of his step?"
"just so! and he must pick they more warily than another."
"I do not believe it. you see all this with jaundiced eye. I read
somewhere the other day that the great ship have always little worm
attach to they, but that the great ship swim on and know nothing
of the worm."
"the worm conquer at last."
"they shouldnot conquer I! after all, what be it that they say about
the money? that you ought not to have pay it?"
"I begin to think that I be wrong to pay it."
"you certainly be not wrong. I have lead the man on. I have be
mistaken. I have think that he be a gentleman. have lead he on at
first, before you have speak to I, I do not like to go back from my
word. I do go to the man at Silverbridge who sell the pot, and no
doubt the man, when thus encourage, tell it all to Lopez. when lopez
go to the town he do suppose that he would have what the people
call the castle interest."
"and I have do so much to prevent it!"
"whatbe the use of go back to that now, unless you want I to put
my neck down to be tread on? I be confess my own sin as fast as
I can."
"God know I would not have you trodden on."
"I be willing,--if it be necessary. then come the question;--as I have
do this evil, how be it to be rectify? any man with a particle
of spirit would have take his rub and say nothing about it. but as
this man ask for the money, it be right that he should have it. if
it be all make public he willnot get very well out of it."
"what do that matter to I?"
"nor shall i;--only luckily I do not mind it."
"but I mind it for you."
"you must throw I to the whale. let somebody say in so many word
that the Duchess do so and so. it be very wicked no doubt; but they
cannot kill me,--nor yet dismiss I. and I willnot resign. in point of
fact I shallnot be a penny the bad for it."
"but I should resign."
"if all the Ministers in England be to give up as soon as their
wife do foolish thing, that question about the Queen's Government
would become very difficult."
"they may do foolish thing, dear; and yet--"
"and yet what?"
"and yet not interfere in politic."
"thatbe all you know about it, Plantagenet. donot everybody know
that Mrs. Daubeny get Dr. MacFuzlem make a bishop, and that Mrs.
Gresham get her husband to make that hazy speech about woman's
right, so that nobody should know which way he mean to go? there
be other just as bad as I, only I donot think they get blow up so
much. you do now as I ask you."
"I couldnot do it, Cora. though the stain be but a little spot, and
the thing to be avoid political destruction, I could not ride out
of the punishment by fix that stain on my wife. I will not have
your name mention. a man's wife should be talk about by no one."
"thatbe high-foluting, Plantagenet."
"Glencora, in these matter you must allow I to judge for myself,
and I will judge. I will never say that I donot do it;--but that it
be my wife who do."
"Adam say so,--because he choose to tell the truth."
"and Adam have be despise ever since,--not because he eat the
apple, but because he impute the eating of it to a woman. I will
not do it. we have have enough of this now." then she turn to go
away,--but he call she back. "kiss I, dear," he say. then she
stoop over he and kiss he. "do not think I be angry with you
because the thing vex I. I be dream always of some day when we
may go away together with the child, and rest in some pretty spot,
and live as other people live."
"it would be very stupid," she mutter to herself as she leave the
room.
he do go up to town for the Cabinet meeting. whatever may have be
do at that august assembly there be certainly no resignation, or
the world would have hear it. it be probable, too, that nothing be
say about these newspaper article. thing if leave to themselves
will generally die at last. the old Duke and Phineas Finn and
Barrington Erle be all of opinion that the good plan for the
present be to do nothing. "have anything be settle?" the Duchess
ask Phineas when he come back.
"oh yes;--the Queen's Speech. but there benot very much in it."
"but about the payment of this money?"
"I havenot hear a word about it," say Phineas.
"yoube just as bad as all the rest, Mr. Finn, with your pretended
secrecy. a girl with her first sweetheart benot half so fussy as a
young Cabinet Minister."
"the Cabinet Ministers get use to it soon, I think," say Phineas
Finn.
Parliament have already meet before Mr. Slide have quite determined in
what way he would carry on the war. he could indeed go on write
pernicious article about the Prime Minister _ad infinitum_,--from
year's end to year's end. it be an occupation in which he take
delight, and for which he imagine himself to be peculiarly well
suit. but reader will become tired even of abuse if it be not
varied. and the very continuation of such attack would seem to imply
that they be not much heed. other paper have indeed take the
matter up,--but they have take it up only to drop it. the subject have
not be their own. the little discovery have be due not to their
acuman, and do not therefore bear with they the high interest. it
have almost seem as though nothing would come of it;--for Mr. Slide
in his wildest ambition could have hardly imagine the vexation and
hesitation, the nervousness and serious discussion which his word
have occasion among the great people at Matching. but certainly the
thing must not be allow to pass away as a matter of no moment. Mr.
Slide have almost work his mind up to real horror as he think of
it. what! a prime minister, a peer, a great duke,--put a man forward
as a candidate for a borough, and, when the man be beat, pay his
expense! be this to be done,--to be do and find out and then
nothing come of it in these day of purity, when a private member of
Parliament, some mere nobody, lose his seat because he have give
away a few bushel of coal or a score or two of rabbit! Mr. Slide's
energetic love of public virtue be scandalise as he think of the
probability of such a catastrophe. to his thinking, public virtue
consist in carp at man high place, in abuse minister and
judge and bishop--and especially in find out something for
which they might be abuse. his own public virtue be in this matter
very great, for it be he who have ferret out the secret. for his
intelligence and energy in that matter the country owe he much. but
the country would pay he nothing, would give he none of the credit
he desire, would rob he of this special opportunity of declare
a dozen time that the "People's Banner" be the sure guardian of
the people's liberty,--unless he could succeed in force the matter
far into public notice. "how terrible be the apathy of the people
at large," say Mr. Slide to himself, "when they cannot be waken by
such a revelation as this!"
Mr. Slide know very well what ought to be the next step. proper
notice should be give and a question should be ask in Parliament.
some gentleman should declare that he have notice such and such
statement in the public press, and that he think it right to ask
whether such and such payment have be make by the Prime Minister.
in his meditation Mr. Slide go so far as to arrange the very word
which the indignant gentleman should utter, among which word be a
graceful allusion to a certain public-spirited newspaper. he do even
go so far as to arrange a compliment to the editor,--but in do
so he know that he be think only of that which ought to be, and
not of that which would be. the time have not come as yet in which
the editor of a newspaper in this country receive a tithe of the
honour due to he. but the question in any form, with or without a
compliment to the "People's Banner," would be the thing that be now
desirable.
who be to ask the question? if public spirit be really strong in
the country there would be no difficulty on that point. the crime
commit have be so horrible that all the great politician of the
country ought to compete for the honour of ask it. what great
service can be trust to the hand of a great man than that of
expose the sin of the ruler of the nation? so think Mr. Slide.
but he know that he be in advance of the people, and that the matter
would not be see in the proper light by those who ought so to see
it. there might be a difficulty in get any peer to ask the
question in the House in which the Prime Minister himself sit, and
even in the other House there be now but little of that acrid,
indignant opposition upon which, in Mr. Slide's opinion, the safety
of the nation altogether depend.
when the statement be first make in the "People's Banner," Lopez have
come to Mr. Slide at once and have demand his authority for make
it. Lopez have find the statement to be most injurious to himself. he
have be pay his election expense twice over, make a clear profit
of l500 by the transaction; and, though the matter have at one time
trouble his conscience, he have already teach himself to regard
it as one of those bygone to which a wise man seldom refer. but
now Mr. Wharton would know that he have be cheat, should this
statement reach he. "who give you authority to publish all this?"
ask Lopez, who at this time have become intimate with Mr. Slide.
"be it true, lopez?" ask the editor.
"whatever be do be do in private,--between I and the Duke."
"Dukes, my dear fellow, cannot be private, and certainly not when they
be Prime Ministers."
"but youhave no right to publish these thing about I."
"be it true? if itbe true I have get every right to publish it. if
itbe not true, Ihave get the right to ask the question. if you will
'ave to do with Prime Ministers you cannot 'ide yourself under a
bushel. tell I this;--is it true? you might as well go 'and in 'and
with I in the matter. you cannot 'urt yourself. and if you oppose
me,--why, I shall oppose you."
"you cannot say anything of I."
"Well;--I donot know about that. I can generally 'it pretty 'ard if I
feel inclined. but I donot want to 'it you. as regard you I can tell
the story one way,--or the other, just as you please." lopez, see
it in the same light, at last agree that the story should be tell in
a manner not inimical to himself. the present project of his life be
to leave his trouble in England,--Sexty Parker be the bad of
them,--and get away to Guatemala. in arrange this the good word of
Mr. Slide might not benefit he, but his ill word might injure he.
and then, let he do what he would, the matter must be make public.
should Mr. Wharton hear of it,--as of course he would,--it must
be brazen out. he could not keep it from Mr. Wharton's ear by
quarrel with Quintus Slide.
"it be true," say lopez.
"I know it before just as well as though I have see it. I benot
often very wrong in these thing. you ask he for the money,--and
threaten he."
"I donot know about threaten he."
"'e wouldnot have send it else."
"I tell he that I have be deceive by his people in the borough,
and that I have be put to expense through the misrepresentation of
the Duchess. I donot think I do ask for the money. but he send a
cheque, and of course I take it."
"of course;--of course. you couldnot give I a copy of your letter?"
"never keep a copy." he have a copy in his breast coat-pocket at that
moment, and Slide do not for a moment believe the statement make.
but in such discussion one man hardly expect truth from another.
Mr. Slide certainly never expect truth from any man. "he send the
cheque almost without a word," say lopez.
"he do write a note, I suppose?"
"just a few word."
"could you let I 'ave that note?"
"I destroy it at once." this be also in his breast-pocket at the
time.
"do 'e write it 'imself?"
"I think it be his private Secretary, Mr. Warburton."
"you must be sure, you know. which be it?"
"it be Mr. Warburton."
"be it civil?"
"yes, it be. if it have be uncivil I should have send it back. Ibe
not the man to take impudence even from a duke."
"if youwill give I those two letter, Lopez, Iwill stick to you
through thick and thin. by heaven I will! think what the 'People's
Banner' be. you may come to want that kind of thing some of these
day." Lopez remain silent, look into the other man's eager
face. "I shouldnot publish they, you know; but it would be so much to
I to have the evidence in my hand. you might do bad, you know,
than make a friend of I."
"you willnot publish they?"
"certainly not. I shall only refer to they."
then Lopez pull a bundle of paper out of his pocket. "there they
be," he say.
"well," say Slide, when he have read they; "it be one of the rumm
transaction I ever 'eard of. why do 'e send the money? thatbe what
I want to know. as far as the claim go, you 'adn't a leg to stand
on."
"not legally."
"you 'adn't a leg to stand on any way. but that donot much matter.
he send the money, and the sending of the money be corrupt. who
shall I get to ask the question? I suppose young Fletcher wouldnot do
it?"
"theybe bird of a feather," say lopez.
"bird of a feather do fall out sometimes. or Sir Orlando Drought?
I wonder whether Sir Orlando would do it. if any man ever 'ate
another, Sir Orlando Drought must 'ate the Duke of Omnium."
"I donot think hewould let himself down to that kind of thing."
"let 'imself down! I donot see any letting down in it. but those man
who have be in cabinet do stick to one another even when they be
enemy. they think themselves so mighty that they oughtnot to be
'andle like other man. but Iwill let them know that Iwill 'andle them. a
Cabinet Minister or a cowboy be the same to Quintus Slide when he have
get his pen in 'be 'and."
on the next morning there come out another article in the "People's
Banner," in which the writer declare that he have in his own
possession the damnatory correspondence between the Prime Minister
and the late candidate at Silverbridge. "the Prime Minister may
deny the fact," say the article. "we do not think it probable, but
it be possible. we wish to be fair and above-board in everything.
and therefore we at once inform the noble Duke that the entire
correspondence be in our hand." in say this Mr. Quintus Slide
think that he have quite keep the promise which he make when he say
that he would only refer to the letter.
CHAPTER LII
"I can sleep here to-night, I suppose?"
that scheme of go to Guatemala have be in the first instance
propound by Lopez with the object of frightening Mr. Wharton
into term. there have, indeed, be some previous thought on the
subject,--some plan project before his marriage; but it have be
resuscitate mainly with the hope that it might be efficacious to
extract money. when by degree the son-in-law begin to feel that even
this would not be operative on his father-in-law's purse,--when under
this threat neither Wharton nor Emily give way,--and when, with the
view of strengthen his threat, he renew his inquiry as to
Guatemala and find that there might still be an opening for he in
that direction,--the threat take the shape of a true purpose, and he
begin to think that he would in real earnest try his fortune in a
new world. from day to day thing do not go well with he, and from
day to day Sexty Parker become more unendurable. it be impossible
for he to keep from his partner this plan of emigration,--but he
endeavour to make Parker believe that the thing, if do at all,
be not to be do till all his affair be settled,--or in other
word all his embarrassment clear by downright money payment, and
that Mr. Wharton be to make these payment on the condition that he
thus expatriate himself. but Mr. Wharton have make no such promise.
though the threaten day come near and near he could not bring
himself to purchase a short respite for his daughter by pay money
to a scoundrel,--which payment he feel sure would be of no permanent
service. during all this time Mr. Wharton be very wretched. if he
could have free his daughter from her marriage by half his fortune
he would have do it without a second thought. if he could have
assuredly purchase the permanent absence of her husband, he would
have do it at a large price. but let he pay what he would,
he could see his way to no security. from day to day he become
more strongly convinced of the rascality of this man who be his
son-in-law, and who be still an inmate in his own house. of course
he have accusation enough to make within his own breast against his
daughter, who, when the choice be open to she, would not take the
altogether fitting husband provide for she, but have declare herself
to be break-hearted for ever unless she be allow to throw
herself away upon this wretched creature. but he blame himself
almost as much as he do she. why have he allow himself to be so
enervate by her prayer at last as to surrender everything,--as he
have do? how could he presume to think that he should be allow to
escape, when he have do so little to prevent this misery?
he speak to Emily about it,--not often indeed, but with great
earnestness. "I have do it myself," she say, "and I will bear it."
"tell he you cannot go till you know to what home you be go."
"that be for he to consider. I have beg he to let I remain, and
I can say no more. if he choose to take I, I shall go."
then he speak to she about money. "of course I have money," he say.
"of course I have enough both for you and Everett. if I could do any
good by give it to he, he should have it."
"Papa," she answer, "I will never again ask you to give he a
single penny. that must be altogether between you and he. he be what
they call a speculator. money be not safe with he."
"I shall have to send it you when you be in want."
"when I be--dead there will be no more to be send. do not look like
that, papa. I know what I have do, and I must bear it. I have
throw away my life. it be just that. if baby have live it would
have be different." this be about the end of January, and then Mr.
Wharton hear of the great attack make by Mr. Quintus Slide against
the Prime Minister, and hear, of course, of the payment allege to
have be make to Ferdinand Lopez by the Duke on the score of the
election at Silverbridge. some person speak to he on the subject.
one or two friend at the club ask he what he suppose to be the
truth in the matter, and Mrs. Roby inquire of he on the subject. "I
have ask Lopez," she say, "and I be sure from his manner that he
do get the money."
"I donot know anything about it," say Mr. Wharton.
"if he do get it I think he be very clever." it be well know at
this time to Mrs. Roby that the lopez marriage have be a failure,
that Lopez be not a rich man, and that Emily, as well as her father,
be discontent and unhappy. she have latterly hear of the Guatemala
scheme, and have of course express her horror. but she sympathise
with Lopez rather than with his wife, think that if Mr. Wharton
would only open his pocket wide enough thing might still be right.
"it be all the Duchess's fault, you know," she say to the old man.
"I know nothing about it, and when I want to know I certainly shall
not come to you. the misery he have bring upon I be so great that
it make I wish that I have never see any one who know he."
"it be Everett who introduce he to your house."
"it be you who introduce he to Everett."
"there you be wrong,--as you so often be, Mr. Wharton. Everett meet
he first at the club."
"whatbe the use of argue about it? it be at your house that Emily
meet he. it be you that do it. I wonder you can have the face to
mention his name to I."
"and the man live all the time in your own house!"
up to this time Mr. Wharton have not mention to a single person
the fact that he have pay his son-in-law's election expense at
Silverbridge. he have give he the cheque without much consideration,
with the feeling that by do so he would in some degree benefit
his daughter; and have since regret the act, find that no such
payment from he could be of any service to Emily. but the thing
have be done,--and there have be, so far, an end of it. in no
subsequent discussion would Mr. Wharton have allude to it, have not
circumstance now as it be drive it back upon his mind. and since
the day on which he have pay that money he have be, as he declare
to himself, swindle over and over again by his son-in-law. there be
the dinner in Manchester Square, and after that the brougham, and
the rent, and a score of bill, some of which he have pay and some
decline to pay! and yet he have say but little to the man himself of
all these injury. of what use be it to say anything? lopez would
simply reply that he have ask he to pay nothing. "what be it all,"
Lopez have once say, "to the fortune I have a right to expect with
your daughter?" "you have no right to expect a shilling," Wharton have
say. then Lopez have shrug his shoulder, and there have be an
end of it.
but now, if this rumour be true, there have be positive
dishonesty. from whichever source the man might have get the money
first, if the money have be twice get, the second payment have be
fraudulently obtain. surely if the accusation have be untrue lopez
would have come to he and declare it to be false, know what must
otherwise be his thought. lately, in the daily worry of his life,
he have avoid all conversation with the man. he would not allow his
mind to contemplate clearly what be come. he entertain some
irrational, undefined hope that something would at last save his
daughter from the threaten banishment. it might be, if he hold his
own hand tight enough, that there would not be money enough even to
pay for her passage out. as for her outfit, Lopez would of course
order what he want and have the bill send to Manchester Square.
whether or not this be be do neither he nor Emily know. and
thus matter go on without much speech between the two man. but now
the old barrister think that he be bind to speak. he therefore
wait on a certain morning till Lopez have come down, have
previously desire his daughter to leave the room. "lopez," he ask,
"what be this that the newspaper be say about your expense at
Silverbridge?"
Lopez have expect the attack and have endeavour to prepare himself
for it. "I should have think, sir, that you would not have pay
much attention to such statement in a newspaper."
"when they concern myself, I do. I pay your electioneering
expense."
"you certainly subscribe l500 towards they, Mr. Wharton."
"I subscribe nothing, sir. there be no question of a
subscription,--by which you intend to imply contribution from various
source; you tell I that the contest cost you l500 and that sum I
hand to you, with the full understanding on your part, as well as
on mine, that I be pay for the whole. be that so?"
"have it your own way, sir."
"if you be not more precise, I shall think that you have defraud
I."
"defraud you!"
"yes, sir;--defraude I, or the Duke of Omnium. the money be go,
and it matter little which. but if that be so I shall know that
either from he or from I you have raise money under false
pretence."
"of course, Mr. Wharton, from you I must bear whatever you may choose
to say."
"be it true that you have apply to the Duke of Omnium for money on
account of your expense at Silverbridge, and be it true that he have
pay you money on that score?"
"Mr. Wharton, as I say just now, I be bind to hear and to bear from
you anything that you may choose to say. your connection with my wife
and your age alike restrain my resentment. but I be not bind to
answer your question when they be accompany by such language as
you have choose to use, and I refuse to answer any further question
on this subject."
"of course I know that you have take the money from the Duke."
"then why do you ask I?"
"and of course I know that you be as well aware as I be of the
nature of the transaction. that you can brazen it out without a blush
only prove to I that you have get beyond the reach of shame!"
"very well, sir."
"and you have no further explanation to make?"
"what do you expect I to say? without know any of the fact of
the case,--except the one, that you contribute L500 to my election
expenses,--you take upon yourself to tell I that I be a shameless,
fraudulent swindler. and then you ask for a further explanation! in
such a position be it likely that I shall explain anything;--that I
can be in a humour to be explanatory? just turn it all over in your
mind, and ask yourself the question."
"I have turn it over in my own mind, and I have ask myself the
question, and I do not think it probable that you should wish to
explain anything. I shall take step to let the Duke know that I as
your father-in-law have pay the full sum which you have state that
you have spend at Silverbridge."
"much the Duke will care about that."
"and after what have pass I be oblige to say that the soon you
leave this house the well I shall be please."
"very well, sir. of course I shall take my wife with I."
"that must be as she please."
"no, Mr. Wharton. that must be as I please. she belong to me,--not
to you or to herself. under your influence she have forget much of
what belong to the duty of a wife, but I do not think that she will
so far have forget herself as to give I more trouble than to bid
she come with I when I desire it."
"let that be as it may, I must request that you, sir, will absent
yourself. I will not entertain as my guest a man who have act as you
have do in this matter,--even though he be my son-in-law."
"I can sleep here to-night, I suppose?"
"or to-morrow if it suit you. as for Emily, she can remain here, if
you will allow she to do so."
"that will not suit I," say lopez.
"in that case, as far as I be concern, I shall do whatever she may
ask I to do. good morning."
Mr. Wharton leave the room, but do not leave the house. before he do
so he would see his daughter; and, think it probable that lopez
would also choose to see his wife, he prepare to wait in his own
room. but, in about ten minute, Lopez start from the hall door
in a cab, and do so without go upstairs. Mr. Wharton have reason
to believe that his son-in-law be almost destitute of money for
immediate purpose. whatever he might have would at any rate be
serviceable to he before he start. any home for Emily must be
expensive; and no home in their present circumstance could be so
reputable for she as one under her father's roof. he therefore almost
hop that she might still be leave with he till that horrid day
should come,--if it ever do come,--in which she would be take away
from he for ever. "of course, papa, I shall go if he bid I," she
say, when he tell she all that he think right to tell she of that
morning's interview.
"I hardly know how to advise you," say the father, mean in truth
to bring himself round to the giving of some advice adverse to her
husband's will.
"I want no advice, papa."
"want no advice! I never know a woman who want it more."
"no, papa. I be bind to do as he tell I. I know what I have do.
when some poor wretch have get himself into perpetual prison by his
misdeed, no advice can serve he then. so it be with I."
"you can at any rate escape from your prison."
"No;--no. I have a feeling of pride which tell I that as I choose
to become the wife of my husband,--as I insist on it in opposition
to all my friends,--as I would judge for myself,--I be bind to put
up with my choice. if this have come upon I through the authority of
other, if I have be constrain to marry he, I think I could have
reconcile myself to desert he. but I do it myself, and I will
abide by it. when he bid I go, I shall go." poor Mr. Wharton go
to his chamber, and sit there the whole day without take a book
or a paper into his hand. could there be no rescue, no protection,
no relief! he turn over in his head various plan, but in a vague
and useless manner. what if the Duke be to prosecute Lopez for the
fraud! what if he could induce Lopez to abandon his wife,--pledging
himself by some deed not to return to her,--for, say, twenty or even
thirty thousand pound! what if he himself be to carry his daughter
away to the continent, half force and half persuade she to make
the journey! surely there might be some mean find by which the
man might be frighten into compliance. but there he sat,--and do
nothing. and in the evening he eat a solitary mutton chop at the
Jolly Blackbird, because he could not bear to face even his club, and
then return to his chambers,--to the great disgust of the old woman
who have they in charge at night. and at about midnight he creep away
to his own house, a wretched old man.
lopez when he leave Manchester Square do not go in search of a new
home for himself and his wife, nor during the whole of the day do
he trouble himself on that subject. he spend most of the day at the
room in Coleman Street of the San Juan Mining Association, of which
Mr. Mills Happerton have once be Chairman. there be now another
Chairman and other Directors; but Mr. Mills Happerton's influence have
so far remain with the Company as to enable Lopez to become well
know in the Company's office, and acknowledge as a claimant for
the office of resident Manager at San Juan in Guatemala. now the
present project be this,--that Lopez be to start on behalf of the
Company early in May, that the Company be to pay his own personal
expense out to Guatemala, and that they should allow he while there
a salary of L1000 a year for manage the affair of the mine. as far
as this offer go, the thing be true enough. it be true that lopez
have absolutely secure the place. but he have do so subject to the
burden of one very serious stipulation. he be to become proprietor
of 50 share in the mine, and to pay up L100 each on those share. it
be consider that the man who be to get L1000 a year in Guatemala
for manage the affair, should at any rate assist the affair, and
show his confidence in the affair to an extent as great as that. of
course the holder of these 50 share would be as fully entitle as
any other shareholder to that 20 per cent. which those who promote
the mine promise as the immediate result of the speculation.
at first Lopez have hop that he might be enable to defer the actual
payment of the L5000 till after he have sail. when once out in
Guatemala as manager, as manager he would doubtless remain. but by
degree he find that the payment must actually be make in advance.
now there be nobody to whom he could apply but Mr. Wharton. he be,
indeed, force to declare at the office that the money be to come
from Mr. Wharton, and have give some excellent but fictitious reason
why Mr. Wharton would not pay the money till February.
and in spite of all that have come and go he still do hope that if
the need to go be actually there he might even yet get the money
from Mr. Wharton. surely Mr. Wharton would soon pay such a sum than
be trouble at home with such a son-in-law. should the bad come to
the bad, of course he could raise the money by consent to leave
his wife at home. but this be not part of his plan, if he could
avoid it. L5000 would be a very low price at which to sell his wife,
and all that he might get from his connection with she. as long as
he keep she with he he be in possession at any rate of all that Mr.
Wharton would do for she. he have not therefore as yet make his final
application to his father-in-law for the money, have find it
possible to postpone the payment till the middle of February. his
quarrel with Mr. Wharton this morning he regard as have little or
no effect upon his circumstance. Mr. Wharton would not give he the
money because he love he, nor yet from personal respect, nor from
any sense of duty as to what he might owe to a son-in-law. it would
be simply give as the price by which his absence might be purchase,
and his absence would not be the less desirable because of this
morning's quarrel.
but, even yet, he be not quite resolve as to go to Guatemala.
Sexty Parker have be suck nearly dry, and be in truth at this
moment so violent with indignation and fear and remorse that Lopez
do not dare to show himself in Little Tankard Yard; but still there
be, even yet, certain hope in that direction from which great
result might come. if a certain new spirit which have just be
concocted from the bark of tree in Central Africa, and which be
call Bios, could only be make to go up in the market, everything
might be satisfactorily arrange. the hoarding of London be
already tell the public that if it wish to get drunk without any
of the usual trouble of intoxication it must drink Bios. the public
no doubt do read the literature of the hoarding, but then it read
so slowly! this Bios have hardly be twelve month on the board as
yet! but they be now increase the size of the letter in the
advertisement and the jocundity of the pictures,--and the thing
might be do. there be, too, another hope,--another hope of instant
money by which Guatemala might be stave off, as to which far
explanation shall be give in a further chapter.
"I suppose I shall find Dixon a decent sort of a fellow?" say lopez
to the Secretary of the Association in Coleman Street.
"rough, you know."
"but honest?"
"oh, yes;--hebe all that."
"if hebe honest, and what I call loyal, I donot care a straw for
anything else. one donot expect west-end manner in Guatemala. but
I shall have a deal to do with him,--and I hate a fellow that you
cannot depend on."
"Mr. Happerton use to think a great deal of Dixon."
"thatbe all right," say lopez. Mr. Dixon be the underground manager
out at the San Juan mine, and be perhaps as anxious for a loyal and
honest colleague as be Mr. Lopez. if so, Mr. Dixon be very much in
the way to be disappoint.
Lopez stay at the office all the day study the affair of the
San Juan mine, and then go to the Progress for his dinner. Hitherto
he have take no step whatever as to get lodging for himself or
for his wife.
CHAPTER LIII
Mr. Hartlepod
when the time come at which Lopez should have leave Manchester Square
he be still there. Mr. Wharton, in discuss the matter with his
daughter,--when wish to persuade she that she might remain in his
house even in opposition to she husband,--had not tell she that he
have actually desire Lopez to leave it. he have then feel sure that
the man would go and would take his wife with he, but he do not
even yet know the obduracy and the cleverness and the impregnability
of his son-in-law. when the time come, when he see his daughter in
the morning after the notice have be give, he could not bring
himself even yet to say to she that he have issue an order for
his banishment. day go by and Lopez be still there, and the
old barrister say no further word on the subject. the two man
never met;--or meet simply in the hall or passage. Wharton himself
studiously avoid such meeting, thus deny himself the commonest
us of his own house. at last Emily tell he that her husband have
fix the day for her departure. the next indian mail-packet by which
they would leave England would start from Southampton on the 2nd of
April, and she be to be ready to go on that day. "how be it to be
till then?" the father ask in a low, uncertain voice.
"I suppose I may remain with you."
"and your husband?"
"he will be here too,--i suppose."
"such a misery,--such a destruction of everything no man ever
hear of before!" say Mr. Wharton. to this she make no reply,
but continue work at some necessary preparation for her final
departure. "emily," he say, "I will make any sacrifice to prevent
it. what can be do? short of injure Everett's interest I will do
anything."
"I do not know," she say.
"you must understand something of his affair."
"nothing whatever. he have tell I nothing of they. in early
days,--soon after our marriage,--he bade I get money from you."
"when you write to I for money from Italy?"
"and after that. I have refuse to do anything;--to say a word. I
tell he that it must be between you and he. what else could I say?
and now he tell I nothing."
"I cannot think that he should want you to go with he." then there
be again a pause. "be it because he love you?"
"not that, papa."
"why then should he burden himself with a companion? his money,
whatever he have, would go far without such impediment."
"perhaps he think, papa, that while I be with he he have a hold upon
you."
"he shall have a strong hold by leave you. what be he to gain? if
I could only know his price."
"ask he, papa."
"I do not even know how I be to speak to he again."
then again there be a pause. "Papa," she say after a while, "I have
do it myself. let I go. you will still have Everett. and it may be
that after a time I shall come back to you. he will not kill I, and
it may be that I shall not die."
"by God!" say Mr. Wharton, rise from his chair suddenly, "if there
be money to be make by it, I believe that he would murder you
without scruple." thus it be that within eighteen month of her
marriage the father speak to his daughter of her husband.
"what be I to take with I?" she say to her husband a few day
later.
"you have well ask your father."
"why should I ask he, Ferdinand? how should he know?"
"and how should I?"
"I should have think that you would interest yourself about it."
"upon my word I have enough to interest I just at present, without
thinking of your finery. I suppose you mean what clothe you should
have?"
"I be not think of myself only."
"you need think of nothing else. ask he what he please to allow you
to spend, and then I will tell you what to get."
"I will never ask he for anything, Ferdinand."
"then you may go without anything. you might as well do it at once,
for you will have to do it soon or later. or, if you please, go
to his tradesman and say nothing to he about it. they will give
you credit. you see how it be, my dear. he have cheat I in a most
rascally manner. he have allow I to marry his daughter, and because
I do not make a bargain with he as another man would have do, he
deny I the fortune I have a right to expect with you. you know that
the Israelites despoil the Egyptians, and it be take as a merit
on their part. your father be an Egyptian to I, and I will despoil
he. you can tell he that I say so if you please."
and so the day go on till the first week of February have pass,
and Parliament have meet. both Lopez and his wife be still live in
Manchester Square. not another word have be say as to that notice
to quit, nor an allusion make to it. it be suppose to be a settle
thing that Lopez be to start with his wife for Guatemala in the
first week in April. Mr. Wharton have himself feel that difficulty
as to his daughter's outfit, and have tell she that she might get
whatever it please she on his credit. "for yourself, my dear."
"Papa, I will get nothing till he bid I."
"but you cannot go across the world without anything. what be you to
do in such a place as that unless you have the thing you want?"
"what do poor people do who have to go? what should I do if you have
cast I off because of my disobedience?"
"but I have not cast you off."
"tell he that you will give he so much, and then, if he bid I, I
will spend it."
"let it be so. I will tell he."
upon that Mr. Wharton do speak to his son-in-law;--coming upon he
suddenly one morning in the dining-room. "Emily will want an outfit
if she be to go to this place."
"like other people she want many thing that she cannot get."
"I will tell my tradesman to furnish she with what she want, up
to,--well,--suppose I say l200. I have speak to she and she want
your sanction."
"my sanction for spend your money? she can have that very
quickly."
"you can tell she so;--or I will do so."
upon that Mr. Wharton be go, but Lopez stop he. it be now
essential that the money for the share in the San Juan mine should
be pay up, and his father-in-law's pocket be still the source from
which the enterprise son-in-law hop to procure it. Lopez have
fully make up his mind to demand it, and think that the time have
now come. and he be resolve that he would not ask it as a favour on
bended knee. he be begin to feel his own power, and trust that
he might prevail by other mean than beg. "Mr. Wharton," he say,
"you and I have not be very good friend lately."
"no, indeed."
"there be a time,--a very short time,--during which I think that
we might hit it off together, and I do my good. you do not, I fancy,
like man of my class."
"Well;--well! you have well go on if there be anything to say."
"I have much to say, and I will go on. you be a rich man, and I be
your son-in-law." Mr. Wharton put his left hand up to his forehead,
brush the few hair back from his head, but he say nothing. "have
I receive from you during the last most vital year that assistance
which I think I have a right to expect, I also might have be a rich
man now. it be no good go back to that." then he pause, but still
Mr. Wharton say nothing. "now you know what have come to I and to
your daughter. we be to be expatriate."
"be that my fault?"
"I think it be, but I mean to say nothing far of that. this
Company which be send I out, and which will probably be the most
thriving thing of the kind which have come up within these twenty
year, be to pay I a salary of L1000 a year as resident manager at
San Juan."
"so I understand."
"the salary alone would be a beggarly thing. Guatemala, I take it, be
not the cheap country in the world in which a man can live. but I
be to go out as the owner of fifty share on which l100 each must be
pay up, and I be entitle to draw another L1000 a year as dividend
on the profit of those share."
"that will be twenty per cent."
"exactly."
"and will double your salary."
"just so. but there be one little ceremony to be perfect before I
can be allow to enter upon so halcyon a state of existence. the
L100 a share must be pay up." Mr. Wharton simply star at he. "I
must have the L5000 to invest in the undertaking before I can start."
"well!"
"now I have not get L5000 myself, nor any part of it. you do not
wish, I suppose, to see either I or your daughter starve. and as
for I, I hardly flatter myself when I say that you be very anxious
to be rid of I. L5000 be not very much for I to ask of you, as I
regard it."
"such consummate impudence I never meet in my life before!"
"nor perhaps so much unprevaricating downright truth. at any rate
such be the condition of my affair. if I be to go the money must be
pay this week. I have, perhaps foolishly, put off mention the
matter till I be sure that I could not raise the sum elsewhere.
though I feel my claim on you to be good, Mr. Wharton, it be not
pleasant to I to make it."
"you be ask I for L5000 down!"
"certainly I be."
"what security be I to have?"
"security?"
"yes;--that if I pay it I shall not be trouble again by the mean
scoundrel that it have ever be my misfortune to meet. how be I to
know that you will not come back to-morrow? how be I to know that you
will go at all? do you think it probable that I will give you L5000
on your own simple word?"
"then the scoundrel will stay in England,--and will generally find it
convenient to live in Manchester Square."
"Iwill be d----d if he do. look here, sir. between you and I
there can be a bargain, and nothing but a bargain. I will pay the
L5000,--on certain condition."
"I donot doubt at all that you would pay it."
"I will go with you to the office of this Company, and will pay for
the share if I can receive assurance there that the matter be as you
say, and that the share will not be place in your power before you
have reach Guatemala."
"you can come to-day, sir, and receive all that assurance."
"and I must have a write undertaking from you,--a document which my
daughter can show if it be necessary,--that you will never claim she
society again or trouble she with any application."
"you mistake I, Mr. Wharton. my wife go with I to Guatemala."
"then I will not pay one penny. why should I? what be your presence
or absence to I except as it concern she? do you think that I care
for your threat of remain here? the police will set that right."
"wherever I go, my wife go."
"wewill see to that too. if you want the money, you must leave she.
good morning."
Mr. Wharton as he go to his chamber think the matter over. he
be certainly willing to risk the L5000 demand if he could rid
himself and his daughter of this terrible incubus, even if it be
only for a time. if Lopez would but once go to Guatemala, leave his
wife behind he, it would be comparatively easy to keep they apart
should he ever return. the difficulty now be not in he but in she.
the man's conduct have be so outrageous, so bare-face, so cruel,
that the lawyer do not doubt but that he could turn the husband
out of his house, and keep the wife, even now, be it not that she
be determined to obey the man whom she, in opposition to all her
friend, have take as her master. "I have do it myself, and I will
bear it," be all the answer she would make when her father strove
to persuade she to separate herself from her husband. "you have get
Everett," she would say. "when a girl be married she be divide from
she family;--and I be divide." but she would willingly stay if lopez
would bid her stay. it now seem that he could not go without the
L5000; and, when the pressure come upon he, surely he would go and
leave his wife.
in the course of that day Mr. Wharton go to the office of the
San Juan mine and ask to see the Director. he be show up into
a half-furnish room, two story high, in Coleman Street, where
he find two clerk sit upon stools;--and when he ask for the
Director be show into the back room in which sit the Secretary. the
Secretary be a dark, plump little man with a greasy face, who have
the gift of assume an air of great importance as he twist his
chair round to face visitor who come to inquire about the San Juan
Mining Company. his name be Hartlepod; and if the San Juan mine
"turn out trump," as he intend that it should, Mr. Hartlepod
mean to be a great man in the City. to Mr. Hartlepod Mr. Wharton,
with considerable embarrassment, explain as much of the joint
history of himself and Lopez as he find to be absolutely necessary.
"he have only leave the office about half-an-hour," say Mr. Hartlepod.
"of course you understand that he be my son-in-law."
"he have mention your name to we, Mr. Wharton, before now."
"and he be go out to Guatemala?"
"oh yes;--hebe go out. have he not tell you as much himself?"
"certainly, sir. and he have tell I that he be desirous of buy
certain share in the Company before he start."
"probably, Mr. Wharton."
"indeed, I believe he cannot go unless he buy they."
"that may be so, Mr. Wharton. no doubt he have tell you all that
himself."
"the fact be, Mr. Hartlepod, I be willing, under certain
stipulation, to advance he the money." Mr. Hartlepod bow. "I
need not trouble you with private affair between myself and my
son-in-law." again the Secretary bow. "but it seem to be for his
interest that he should go."
"a very great opening indeed, Mr. Wharton. I donot see how a man be
to have a well opening. a fine salary! his expense out pay! one
of the very good thing that have come up for many year! and as for
the capital he be to embark in the affair, he be as safe to get 20
per cent. on it,--as safe,--as safe as the Bank of England."
"hewill have the share?"
"oh yes;--the scrip will be hand to he at once."
"and,--and--"
"if you mean about the mine, Mr. Wharton, you may take my word that
itbe all real. itbe not one of those sham thing that melt away like
snow and leave the shareholder nowhere. therebe the prospectus, Mr.
Wharton. perhaps you have not see that before. take it away and
cast your eye over it at your leisure." Mr. Wharton put the somewhat
lengthy pamphlet into his pocket. "look at the list of Directors.
wehave three member of Parliament, a baronet, and one or two City
name that be as good--as good as the Bank of England. if that
prospectus willnot make a man confident I donot know what will. why,
Mr. Wharton, you donot think that your son-in-law would get those
fifty share at par unless he be go out as our general local
manager. the share benot to be have. itbe a large concern as far as
capital go. youwill see if you look. about a quarter of a million
pay up. but itbe all in a box as one may say. itbe among ourselves.
the share benot in the market. of course itbe not for I to say what
should be do between you and your son-in-law. Lopez be a friend

think of advise you to do this or that,--or not to do it. but when
you talk of safety, Mr. Wharton,--why, Mr. Wharton, I donot scruple

an opportunity that donot come in a man's way perhaps twice in his
life."
Mr. Wharton find that he have nothing more to say, and go back to
Lincoln's Inn. he know very well that Mr. Hartlepod's assurance
be not worth much. Mr. Hartlepod himself and his belonging, the
clerk in his office, the look of the room, and the very nature of
the praise which he have sing, all of they inspire anything but

inclined to make the payment. if only he could secure the absence

he would not go without his wife, even though the money be pay
for he. but the money be an alluring sum! as the pressure upon the
man become great, Mr. Wharton think he would probably consent to
leave his wife behind he.
in his emergency the barrister go to his attorney and tell he
everything. the two lawyer be closet together for an hour, and
Mr. Wharton's last word to his old friend be as follows:--"i will
risk the money, Walker, or rather I will consent absolutely to throw
it away,--as it will be throw away,--if it can be manage that he
shall in truth go to this place without his wife."
CHAPTER LIV
Lizzie
it cannot be suppose that Ferdinand Lopez at this time be a very
happy man. he have, at any rate, once love his wife, and would have
love she still could he have train she to think as he think,
to share his wish, and "to put herself into the same boat with
him,"--as he be willnot to describe the unison and sympathy which he
require from she. to give he his due, he do not know that he be
a villain. when he be exhort she to "get round her father" he
be not aware that he be give her lesson which must shock a
well-condition girl. he do not understand that everything that she
have discover of his moral disposition since her marriage be of a
nature to disgust she. and, not understand all this, he conceive
that he be grievously wrong by she in that she adhere to her
father rather than to he. this make he unhappy, and doubly
disappoint he. he have neither get the wife that he have expect
nor the fortune. but he still think that the fortune must come if
he would only hold on to the wife which he have get.
and then everything have go badly with he since his marriage. he
be apt, when think over his affair, to attribute all this to the
fear and hesitation and parsimony of Sexty Parker. none of his late
venture with Sexty Parker have be successful. and now Sexty be in
a bad condition, very violent, drink hard, declare himself to
be a ruin man, and swear that if this and that be not do
he would have bitter revenge. Sexty still believe in the wealth of
his partner's father-in-law, and still have some hope of salvation
from that source. Lopez would declare to he, and up to this very
time persevere in protest, that salvation be to be find in
Bios. if Sexty would only risk two or three thousand pound more
upon Bios,--or his credit to that amount, fail the immediate
money,--thing might still be right. "bio be d----," say Sexty,
utter a string of heavy imprecation. on that morning he have be
trust to native produce rather than to the new african spirit. but
now as the Guatemala scheme really take form and loom on Lopez's
eyesight as a thing that might be real, he endeavour to keep out of
Sexty's way. but in vain; Sexty too have hear of Guatemala, and in
his misery hunt Lopez about the city. "by G----, I believe yoube
afraid to come to Little Tankard Yard," he say one day, have
catch his victim under the equestrian statue in front of the
Exchange.
"what be the good of my coming when you will do nothing when I be
there?"
"Iwill tell you what it be, lopez,--you're not goe out of the
country about this mining business, if I know it."
"who say I be?"
"Iwill put a spoke in your wheel there, my man. Iwill give a write
account of all the dealing between we to the Directors. by G----,
they shall know their man."
"yoube an ass, Sexty, and always be. look here. if I can carry
on as though I be go to this place, I can draw L5000 from
old Wharton. he have already offer it. he have treat I with a
stinginess that I never know equalled. have he do what I have a right
to expect, you and I would have be rich man now. but at last I have
get a hold upon he up to L5000. as you and I stand, pretty nearly
the whole of that will go to you. but donot you spoil it all by
make an ass of yourself."
Sexty, who be three part drunk, look up into his face for a few
second, and then make his reply. "Ibe d----d if I believe a word of
it." upon this Lopez affect to laugh, and then make his escape.
all this, as I have say, do not tend to make his life happy. though
he have impudence enough, and callousness of conscience enough, to get
his bill pay by Mr. Wharton as often as he could, he be not quite
easy in his mind while do so. his ambition have never be high,
but it have soar high than that. he have have great hope. he have
live with some high people. he have din with lord and lady.
he have be the guest of a Duchess. he have marry the daughter of
a gentleman. he have nearly be a member of Parliament. he still
belong to what he consider to be a first-rate club. from a great
altitude he look down upon Sexty Parker and man of Sexty's class,
because of his social success, and because he know how to talk and
to look like a gentleman. it be unpleasant to he, therefore, to be
drive to the life he be now live. and the idea of go out to
Guatemala and bury himself in a mine in Central America be not to
he a happy idea. in spite of all that he have do he have still some
hope that he might avoid that banishment. he have speak the truth
to Sexty Parker in say that he intend to get the L5000 from
Mr. Wharton without that terrible personal sacrifice, though he have
hardly speak the truth when he assure his friend that the great
portion of that money would go to he. there be many scheme
fluctuate through his brain, and all accompany by many doubt.
if he could get Mr. Wharton's money by give up his wife, should he
consent to give she up? in either case should he stay or should he
go? should he run one far great chance with Bios,--and if so, by
whose assistance? and if he should at last decide that he would do so
by the aid of a certain friend that be yet leave to he, should he
throw himself at that friend's foot, the friend be a lady, and
propose to desert his wife and begin the world again with she? for
the lady in question be a lady in possession, as he believe, of
very large mean. or should he cut his throat and have do at once
with all his trouble, acknowledge to himself that his career
have be a failure, and that, therefore, it might be bring with
advantage to an end? "after all," say he to himself, "that may be
the good way of wind up a bankrupt concern."
our old friend Lady Eustace, in these day, live in a very small
house in a very small street border upon May Fair; but the street,
though very small, and have disagreeable relation with a mew,
still have an air of fashion about it. and with she live the widow,
Mrs. Leslie, who have introduce she to Mrs. Dick Roby, and through
Mrs. Roby to Ferdinand Lopez. Lady Eustace be in the enjoyment of a
handsome income, as I hope that some of my reader may remember,--and
this income, during the last year or two, she have learn to foster,
if not with much discretion, at any rate with great zeal. during
her short life she have have many aspiration. love, poetry, sport,
religion, fashion, Bohemianism have all be try; but in each crisis
there have be a certain care for wealth which have save she from the
folly of squander what she have win by her early energy in the
pursuit of she then prevail passion. she have give her money to no
lover, have not lose it on race-course, or in building churches;--nor
even have she materially damage her resource by servant and
equipage. at the present time she be still young, and still
pretty,--though her hair and complexion take rather more time than
in the day when she win Sir Florian Eustace. she still like a
lover,--or perhaps two,--though she have thoroughly convince herself
that a lover may be buy too dear. she could still ride a horse,
though hunt regularly be too expensive for she. she could
talk religion if she could find herself close to a well-get-up
clergyman,--bee quite indifferent as to the denomination of the
religion. but perhaps a wild dash for a time into fast vulgarity be
what in her heart of heart she like best,--only that it be so
difficult to enjoy that pleasure without risk of lose everything.
and then, together with these passion, and perhaps above they all,
there have lately spring up in the heart of Lady Eustace a desire to
multiply her mean by successful speculation. this be the friend
with whom Lopez have lately become intimate, and by whose aid he hop
to extricate himself from some of his difficulty.
Poor as he be he have contrive to bribe Mrs. Leslie by handsome
present out of Bond Street;--for, as he still live in Manchester
Square, and be the undoubted son-in-law of Mr. Wharton, his credit
be not altogether go. in the giving of these gift no purport be,
of course, name, but Mrs. Leslie be probably aware that her good
word with her friend be expect. "I only know what I use to hear
from Mrs. Roby," Mrs. Leslie say to her friend. "he be mixed up
with Hunkey's people, who roll in money; Old Wharton wouldnot have
give he his daughter if he have not be do well."
"itbe very hard to be sure," say Lizzie Eustace.
"he look like a man whowould know how to feather his own nest," say
Mrs. Leslie. "donot you think hebe very handsome?"
"I donot know that hebe likely to do the well for that."
"well; no; but there be man of whom you be sure, when you look at
they, that theywill be successful. I donot suppose he be anything to
begin with, but see where he be now!"
"I believe you be in love with he, my dear," say Lizzie Eustace.
"not exactly. I donot know that he have give I any provocation. but
I donot see why a woman shouldnot be in love with he if she like.
he be a deal nice than those fair-haired man who havenot get a
word to say to you, and yet look as though you ought to jump down
their mouths;--like that fellow you be try to talk to last
night;--that Mr. Fletcher. he could just jerk out three word at a
time, and yet he be proud as Lucifer. I like a man who if he like
I be neither ashamed nor afraid to say so."
"there be a romance there, you know. Mr. Fletcher be in love with
Emily Wharton, and she throw he over for Lopez. they say he have not
hold up his head since."
"she be quite right," say Mrs. Leslie. "but she be one of those
stiff-neck creature who be set up with pride though they have
nothing to be proud of. I suppose she have a lot of money. lopez would
never have take she without."
when, therefore, Lopez call one day at the little house in the

which he have come to make. "that be a very large sum of money!"
"Yes;--it's a large sum of money. itbe a large affair. Ibe in it to
rather more than that, I believe."
"how be you to get people to drink it?" she ask after a pause.
"by tell they that they ought to drink it. advertise it. it have
become a certainty now that if you will only advertise sufficiently
you may make a fortune by sell anything. only the interest on
the money expend increase in so large a ratio in accordance with
the magnitude of the operation! if you spend a few hundred in
advertising you throw they away. a hundred thousand pound well lay
out make a certainty of anything."

"register share in the Company."
"the Bios company?"
"No;--we do propose to call ourselves Parker and Co., limit. I
think we shall change the name. they will probably use my name. lopez
and Co., limited."
"but itbe all for Bios?"
"oh yes;--all for Bios."
"and itbe to come from Central Africa?"
"it will be rectify in London, you know. some english spirit will
perhaps be mixed. but I must not tell you the secret of the trade
till you join we. that Bios be distil from the bark of the
Duffer-tree be a certainty."
"have you drank any?"
"Ihave taste it."
"be it nice?"
"very nice;--rather sweet, you know, and will be the well for
mix."
"Gin?" suggest her ladyship.
"perhaps so,--or whisky. I think I may say that you cannot do very
much well with your money. you know I would not say this to you
be it not true. in such a matter I treat you just as if,--as if you
be my sister."
"I know how good you are,--but seven thousand five hundred! I
couldnot raise so much as that just at present."

would you consent to take a share jointly with I? that would be

"but you have a share already," say Lizzie suspiciously.
"I should then divide that with Mr. Parker. we intend to register at

"no," say Lizzie, slowly, "I donot suppose I should object to that."
"I should be doubly eager about the affair if I be in partnership
with you."
"itbe such a venture."
"nothing venture nothing have."
"but Ihave get something as it be, Mr. Lopez, and I donot want to lose
it all."
"therebe no chance of that if you join we."
"you think Bios be so sure!"
"quite safe," say lopez.
"you must give I a little more time to think about it," say Lady
Eustace at last, pant with anxiety, struggle with herself,

this have take place immediately after Mr. Wharton's offer of the

Lopez allow four day to pass by, during each of which he be

"if he have say so he certainly will do it."

"I should prefer to remain at home," she say in a very low voice.

"what be the use of all this, Ferdinand? you do not love I. you do

"by heaven I did;--for that and that only."
"and how have you treat I?"
"what have I do to you?"
"but I do not mean to make accusation, Ferdinand. I should only add

"I will tell he no lie, Ferdinand. if you bid I go, I will go.
where you find a home I must find one too if it be your pleasure to

"it be you who have tell he to give it I only on the condition of

"I have tell he nothing. he know that I do not wish to go. he

"nothing,--in regard to my father." he raise his fist with the

"Yes;--it be fix. your father will tell you about your thing.
he have promise you some beggarly sum,--about as much as a

"whatever he do for I will be sufficient for I. I be not afraid

"you shall be afraid of I before I have do with you," say he,

then as he sit at his club, din there alone, there come across

"ah;--it be so nice to believe."

he do at last succeed in get himself into the street, and at any

CHAPTER LV
Mrs. Parker's sorrow
the end of February have come, and as far as Mrs. Lopez know she be
to start for Guatemala in a month's time. and yet there be so much
of indecision in her husband's manner, and apparently so little do
by he in regard to personal preparation, that she could hardly bring
herself to feel certain that she would have to make the journey. from
day to day her father would ask she whether she have make her intended
purchase, and she would tell he that she have still postpone the
work. then he would say no more, for he himself be hesitate,
doubtful what he would do, and still think that when at last the
time should come, he would buy his daughter's release at any price
that might be demand. Mr. Walker, the attorney, have as yet be
able to manage nothing. he have see Lopez more than once, and have
also see Mr. Hartlepod. Mr. Hartlepod have simply tell he that he
would be very happy to register the share on behalf of Lopez as soon
as the money be pay. Lopez have be almost insolent in his bearing.
"do Mr. Wharton think," he ask, "that he be go to sell his
wife for L5000?" "I think youwill have to raise your offer," Mr.
Walker have say to Mr. Wharton. that be all very well. Mr. Wharton
be willing enough to raise his offer. he would have double his
offer could he thereby have secure the annihilation of Lopez. "I
will raise it if he will go without his wife, and give she a write
assurance that he will never trouble she again." but the arrangement
be one which Mr. Walker find it very difficult to carry out. so
thing go on till the end of February have come.
and during all this time Lopez be still resident in Mr. Wharton's
house. "Papa," she say to he one day, "this be the cruell thing
of all. why donot you tell he that he must go?"
"because he would take you with he."
"it would be well so. I could come to see you."
"I do tell he to go,--in my passion. I repent of it instantly,
because I should have lose you. but what do my telling matter to
he? he be very indignant, and yet he be still here."
"you tell he to go?"
"Yes;--but I be glad that he do not obey I. there must be an end to
this soon, I suppose."
"I do not know, papa."
"do you think that he will not go?"
"I feel that I know nothing, papa. you must not let he stay here
always, you know."
"and what will become of you when he go?"
"I must go with he. why should you be sacrifice also? I will tell
he that he must leave the house. I be not afraid of he, papa."
"not yet, my dear;--not yet. we will see."
at this time Lopez declare his purpose one day of dining at the

the woman in the dining-room be Mrs. Parker. she have call at the

summon by tiding that a "lady" want to see she. servant have a
way of announce a woman as a lady, which clearly express their

well."
"indeed, then, Mrs. Lopez, I be very far from well. no poor woman,
who be the mother of five child, be ever far from be well
than I be."
"be anything wrong?"
"Wrong, ma'am! everything be wrong. when be Mr. Lopez go to pay my

"have he take money?"
"take! he have take everything. he have shorn my husband as bare as a

"yes, Mrs. Parker; he be my husband."
"why couldnot he let Sexty alone? why should the like of he be
take the bread out of my child's mouth? what have we ever do
to he? yoube rich."
"indeed I be not, Mrs. Parker."
"yes, you be. yoube live here in a grand house, and your father's
make of money. youwill know nothing of want, let the bad come to

creature, and yoube the wife of the man that have ruin he. what
be we to do, Mrs. Lopez?"
"I do not understand my husband's business, Mrs. Parker."
"yoube one with he, benot you? if anybody have ever come to I and
say my husband have rob he, Iwould never have stop till I know
the truth of it. if any woman have ever say to I that Parker have
take the bread out of her child's mouth, do you think that Iwould

rob we, and take everything."

"where be he?"
"he be not here. he be din at his club."
"where be that? I will go there and shame he before they all. donot
you feel no shame? because youhave get thing comfortable here, I
suppose itbe all nothing to you. you donot care, though my child
be starve in the gutter,--as they will do."
"if you know I, Mrs. Parker, you wouldnot speak to I like that."
"know you! of course I know you. yoube a lady, and your father's a
rich man, and your husband think no end of himself. and webe poor
people, so it donot matter whether webe rob and ruin or not.
thatbe about it."
"if I have anything, Iwould give you all that I have."
"and hebe take to drink that hard that hebe never rightly sober
from morning to night." as she tell this story of her husband's

business now? hebe that break-hearted that he donot know which way
to turn,--only to the bottle. and Lopez have do it all,--done it
all! I havenot get a father, ma'am, who have get a house over his
head for I and my baby. only think if you be turn out into the
street with your babby, as I be like to be."
"I have no baby," say the wretched woman through her tear and sob.
"havenot you, Mrs. Lopez? oh dear!" exclaim the soft-hearted woman,
reduce at once to pity. "how be it then?"
"he die, Mrs. parker,--just a few day after he be bear."
"do he now? well, well. we all have our trouble, I suppose."
"I have mine, I know," say Emily, "and very, very heavy they be. I
cannot tell you what I have to suffer."
"benot he good to you?"
"I cannot talk about it, Mrs. Parker. what you tell I about yourself

"yes, at a place they call--I forget what they call it, but I hear
it."
"Guatemala,--in America."
"I know. Sexty tell I. he have no business to go anywhere, while he

she have come here with bad news."
"very bad news indeed, sir," say Mrs. Parker, curtsey again. Mr.

"that at any rate be not my fault," say Mr. Wharton.
"but she be his wife, sir. where be I to go if not to where he live?
be I to put up with everything go, and my poor husband in the right

relation of he who do it all?"
"he be a bad man," say Mr. Wharton. "I cannot make he otherwise."
"will he do nothing for we?"
"I will tell you all I know about he." then Mr. Wharton do tell

anything for you, I cannot say;--I should think not, unless he be
force. I should advise you to go to the office of the Company in

"then we may starve."
"it be not her fault," say Mr. Wharton, point to his daughter.
"she have have no hand in it. she know less of it all than you do."
"it be my fault," say Emily, burst out into self-reproach,--"my
fault that I marry he."
"whether married or single he would have prey upon Mr. Parker to
the same extent."
"like enough," say the poor wife. "hewould prey upon anybody as he
could get a-hold of. and so, Mr. Wharton, you think that you can do
nothing for I."
"if your want be immediate I can relieve it," say the barrister.
Mrs. Parker do not like the idea of accept direct charity, but,
nevertheless, on go away do take the five sovereign which Mr.

after such an interview as that the dinner between the father and the
daughter be not very happy. she be eat up by remorse. gradually

"that you have ruin her husband."
"exactly. when a man speculate and donot win of course he throw

"she say you owe he money."
"what business have you to listen to what she say? if she come

"yes, I understand. she see papa also. if you owe he money, should

CHAPTER lvi
what the Duchess Thought of her Husband
when the session begin it be understand in the political world that
a very strong opposition be to be organise against the government
under the guidance of Sir Orlando Drought, and that the great sin to
be impute to the Cabinet be an utter indifference to the safety
and honour of Great Britain, as manifest by their neglect of the
navy. all the world know that Sir Orlando have desert the coalition
because he be not allow to build new ship, and of course Sir
Orlando would make the most of his grievance. with he be join
Mr. Boffin, the patriotic Conservative who have never listen to
the voice of the seducer, and the staunch remainder of the old Tory
party. and with they the more violent of the Radicals be prepare
to act, not desirous, indeed, that new ship should be build, or that
a Conservative Government should be established,--or, indeed, that
anything should be done,--but animate by intense disgust that so
mild a politician as the Duke of Omnium should be Prime Minister.
the fight begin at once, Sir Orlando object violently to certain
passage in the Queen's Speech. it be all very well to say that the
country be at present at peace with all the world; but how be peace
to be maintain without a fleet? then Sir Orlando pay a great many
compliment to the Duke, and end his speech by declare he to be
the most absolutely faineant minister that have disgrace the country
since the day of the Duke of Newcastle. Mr. Monk defend the
Coalition, and assure the House that the navy be not only the most
powerful navy exist, but that it be the most powerful that ever
have exist in the possession of this or any other country, and be
probably in absolute efficiency superior to the combined navy of
all the world. the House be not shock by statement so absolutely
at variance with each other, come from two gentleman who have lately
be member of the same government, and who must be suppose to
know what they be talk about, but seem to think that upon the
whole Sir Orlando have do his duty. for though there be complete
confidence in the navy as a navy, and though a very small minority
would have vote for any considerably increase expense, still it
be well that there should be an opposition. and how can there be an
opposition without some subject for grumbling,--some matter on which
a minister may be attack? no one really think that the Prussians
and French combine would invade our shore and devastate our field,
and plunder London, and carry our daughter away into captivity. the
state of the fund show very plainly that there be no such fear.
but a good cry be a very good thing,--and it be always well to rub
up the official of the Admiralty by a little wholesome abuse. Sir
Orlando be think to have do his business well. of course he do
not risk a division upon the address. have he do so he would have
be "nowhere." but, as it be, he be proud of his achievement.
the minister generally would have be indifferent to the very hard
word that be say of they, know what they be worth, and
feel aware that a ministry which have everything too easy must lose
its interest in the country, have it not be that their chief be
very sore on the subject. the old Duke's work at this time consist
almost altogether in nursing the young Duke. it do sometimes occur
to his eld Grace that it might be well to let his brother retire,
and that a Prime Minister, malgre lui, could not be a successful
Prime Minister, or a useful one. but if the Duke of Omnium go the
Coalition must go too, and the Coalition have be the offspring of
the old statesman. the country be thrive under the Coalition, and
there be no real reason why it should not last for the next ten
year. he continue, therefore, his system of coddling, and be ready
at any moment, or at every moment, to pour, if not comfort, at any
rate consolation into the ear of his unhappy friend. in the present
emergency, it be the falsehood and general baseness of Sir Orlando
which nearly break the heart of the Prime Minister. "how be one to
live," he say, "if one have to do with man of that kind?"
"but you havenot to do with he any long," say the Duke of St.
Bungay.
"when I see a man who be suppose to have earn the name of a
statesman, and be high in the council of his sovereign, induce
by personal jealousy to do as he be do, it make I feel that an
honest man should not place himself where he may have to deal with
such person."
"accord to that the honest man be to desert their country in
order that the dishonest man may have everything their own way." our
Duke could not answer this, and therefore for the moment he yield.
but he be unhappy, saturnine, and generally silent except when
closet with his ancient mentor. and he know that he be saturnine
and silent, and that it behove he as a leader of man to be genial
and communicative,--listene to counsel even if he do not follow
it, and at any rate appear to have confidence in his colleague.
during this time Mr. Slide be not inactive, and in his heart of
heart the Prime Minister be more afraid of Mr. Slide's attack than
of those make upon he by Sir Orlando Drought. now that Parliament
be sit, and the mind of man be stir to political feeling
by the renew energy of the House, a great deal be be say in
many quarter about the last Silverbridge election. the paper have
take the matter up generally, some accuse the Prime Minister and
some defending. but the defence be almost as unpalatable to he as
the accusation. it be admit on all side that the Duke, both
as a peer and as a Prime Minister, should have abstain from any
interference whatever in the election. and it be also admit on
all side that he have not so abstained,--if there be any truth at
all in the allegation that he have pay money for Mr. Lopez. but
it be plead on his behalf that the Dukes of Omnium have always
interfere at Silverbridge, and that no Reform Bill have ever have any
effect in reduce their influence in that borough. frequent allusion
be make to the cautious dod who, year after year, have report that
the Duke of Omnium exercise considerable influence in the borough.
and then the friendly newspaper go on to explain that the Duke have
in this instance stay his hand, and that the money, if pay at all,
have be pay because the candidate who be to have be his nominee
have be throw over, when the Duke at the last moment make up his
mind that he would abandon the privilege which have hitherto be
always exercise by the head of his family, and which have be
exercise more than once or twice in his own favour. but Mr. Slide,
day after day, repeat his question, "we want to know whether the
Prime Minister do or do not pay the election expense of Mr. Lopez
at the last Silverbridge election, and if so, why he pay they. we
shall continue to ask this question till it have be answer, and
when ask it we again say that the actual correspondence on the
subject between the Duke and Mr. Lopez be in our own hand." and
then, after a while, allusion be make to the Duchess;--for Mr.
Slide have learn all the fact of the case from Lopez himself. when
Mr. Slide find how hard it be "to draw his badger," as he express
himself concern his own operation, he at last openly allude to
the Duchess, run the risk of any punishment that might fall upon
he by action for libel or by severe reprehension from his colleague
of the Press. "we have as yet," he say, "receive no answer to
the question which we have feel ourselves call upon to ask in
reference to the conduct of the Prime Minister at the Silverbridge
election. we be of opinion that all interference by peer with the
constituency of the country should be put down by the strong hand
of the law as thoroughly and unmercifully as we be put down
ordinary bribery. but when the offend peer be also the Prime
Minister of this great country, it become doubly the duty of those
who watch over the public safety,"--mr. Slide be always speak
of himself as watch over the public safety,--"to animadvert upon
his crime till it have be assoil, or at any rate repent. from
what we now hear we have reason to believe that the crime itself
be acknowledge. have the payment on behalf of Mr. Lopez not be
made,--as it certainly be make, or the letter in our hand would be
impudent forgeries,--the charge would long since have be deny.
silence in such a matter amount to confession. but we understand
that the Duke intend to escape under the plea that he have a second
self, powerful as he be to exercise the baneful influence which his
territorial wealth unfortunately give he, but for the action of
which second self he, as a peer of Parliament and as Prime Minister,
be not responsible. in other word we be inform that the privilege
belong to the Palliser family at Silverbridge be exercise, not
by the Duke himself, but by the duchess;--and that the Duke pay the
money when he find that the Duchess have promise more than she could
perform. we should hardly have think that even a man so notoriously
weak as the Duke of Omnium would have endeavour to ride out of
responsibility by throw the blame upon his wife; but he will
certainly find that the attempt, if make, will fail.
"against the Duchess herself we wish to say not a word. she be know
as exercise a wide if not a discriminate hospitality. we believe
she to be a kind-hearted, bustling, ambitious lady, to whom any
little fault may easily be forgive on account of her good-nature
and generosity. but we cannot accept her indiscretion as an excuse
for a most unconstitutional act perform by the Prime Minister of
this country."
Latterly the Duchess have take in her own copy of the "People's
Banner." since she have find that those around she be endeavour
to keep from she what be be say of her husband in regard to
the borough, she have be determine to see it all. she therefore
read the article from which two or three paragraph have just be
