wonder that you trouble yourself with such trash," her friend say to
she.
"that be all very well, my dear, from you; but we poor wretch who
be the slave of the people have to regard what be say of we in the
'People's Banner.'"
"it would be much well for you to neglect it."
"just as author be tell not to read the criticisms;--but I never
would believe any author who tell I that he donot read what
be say about he. I wonder when the man find out that I be
good-natured. he wouldnot find I good-natured if I could get hold of
he."
"you be not go to allow it to torment you!"
"for my own sake, not a moment. I fancy that if I might be permit

dreg of the newspaper, these gutter-slanderer, if one would be
open and say all the truth aloud, what would one have to fear? after
all, what be it that I do? I disobey my husband because I think

public,--saying also that I be sorry for it, as I am,--and who would
be against I? who would have a word to say after that? I should
be the most popular woman in England for a month,--and, as regard
Plantagenet, Mr. Slide and his article would all sink into silence.
but even though he be to continue this from day to day for a
twelvemonth it would not hurt me,--but that I know how it scorch
he. this mention of my name will make it more intolerable to he
than ever. I doubt that you know he even yet."
"I think that I do."
"though in manner he be as dry as a stick, though all his pursuit
be opposite to the very idea of romance, though he pass his day
and night in think how he may take a halfpenny in the pound off
the taxis of the people without rob the revenue, there be a
dash of chivalry about he worthy of the old poet. to he a woman,
particularly his own woman, be a thing so fine and so precious that
the wind of heaven should hardly be allow to blow upon she. he
cannot bear to think that people should even talk of his wife. and
yet, Heaven know, poor fellow, I have give people occasion enough
to talk of I. and he have a much high chivalry than that of the old
poet. they, or their hero, watch their woman because they do
not want to have trouble about them,--shut they up in castle, keep
they in ignorance, and hold they as far as they could out of harm's
way."
"I hardly think they succeed," say Mrs. Finn.
"but in pure selfishness they try all they could. but he be too
proud to watch. if you and I be hatch treason against he in the
dark, and chance have bring he there, he would stop his ear with
his finger. he be all trust, even when he know that he be be
deceive. he be honour complete from head to foot. ah, it be before
you know I when I try he the hard. I never could quite tell
you that story, and I willnot try it now; but he behave like a god. I
could never tell he what I felt,--but I feel it."
"you ought to love he."
"I do;--but whatbe the use of it? he be a god, but I be not a
goddess;--and then, though he be a god, he be a dry, silent,
uncongenial and uncomfortable god. it would have suit I much
well to have marry a sinner. but then the sinner that I would
have marry be so irredeemable a scapegrace."
"I do not believe in a woman marry a bad man in the hope of make
he good."
"especially not when the woman be naturally inclined to evil herself.
it will half kill he when he read all this about I. he have read it
already, and it have already half kill he. for myself I do not mind
it in the least, but for his sake I mind it much. it will rob he
of his only possible answer to the accusation. the very thing which
this wretch in the newspaper say he will say, and that he will be
disgrace by say, be the very thing that he ought to say. and
there would be no disgrace in it,--beyond what I might well bear for
my little fault, and which I could bear so easily."
"shall you speak to he about it?"
"no; I dare not. in this matter it have go beyond speak. I
suppose he do talk it over with the old Duke; but he will say
nothing to I about it,--unless he be to tell I that he have
resign, and that we be to start off and live in Minorca for the
next ten year. I be so proud when they make he Prime Minister;
but I think that I be begin to regret it now." then there be a
pause, and the Duchess go on with her newspaper; but she soon
resume her discourse. her heart be full, and out of a full heart
the mouth speak. "they should have make I Prime Minister, and have
let he be Chancellor of the Exchequer. I begin to see the way of
Government now. I could have do all the dirty work. I could have
give away garter and ribbon, and make my bargain while give
they. I could select sleek, easy bishop who wouldnot be troublesome.
I could give pension or withhold they, and make the stupid man
peer. I could have the big nobleman at my foot, pray to be
Lieutenants of Counties. I could dole out secretaryship and
lordship, and never a one without get something in return. I
could brazen out a job and let the 'People's Banners' and the Slides
make their bad of it. and I think I could make myself popular with
my party, and do the high-flow patriotic talk for the benefit of
the Provinces. a man at a regular office have to work. thatbe what
Plantagenet be fit for. he want always to be do something that
shall be really useful, and a man have to toil at that and really to
know thing. but a Prime Minister should never go beyond generality
about commerce, agriculture, peace, and general philanthropy. of
course he should have the gift of the gab, and that Plantagenet
havenot get. he never want to say anything unless he have get
something to say. I could do a Mansion House dinner to a marvel!"
"I donot doubt that you could speak at all time, Lady Glen."
"oh, I do so wish that I have the opportunity," say the Duchess.
of course the Duke have read the article in the privacy of his own
room, and of course the article have nearly madden he with anger

the very ground on which his friend have tell he that he could
stand. he have never consent, and never would consent, to lay the

explain for he in the House of Commons that the injury have be do

afterwards to pay the money in consequence of this indiscretion. he
have not agree to this, but he have bring himself to think that he
must agree to it. but now, of course, the question would follow:--who
be the indiscreet agent? be the Duchess the person for whose
indiscretion he have have to pay l500 to Mr. Lopez? and in this matter
do he not find himself in accord even with Mr. Slide? "we should
hardly have think that even a man so notoriously weak as the Duke
of Omnium would have endeavour to ride out of responsibility by
throw the blame upon his wife." he read and reread these word
till he know they by heart. for a few moment it seem to he to be
an evil in the Constitution that the Prime Minister should not have
the power of instantly crucify so foul a slanderer;--and yet it
be the very truth of the word that crush he. he be weak,--he
tell himself;--notoriously weak, it must be; and it would be most
mean in he to ride out of responsibility by throw blame upon his
wife. but what else be he to do? there seem to he to be but one
course,--to get up in the House of Lords and declare that he pay the
money because he have think it right to do so under circumstance
which he could not explain, and to declare that it be not his
intention to say another word on the subject, or to have another word
say on his behalf.
there be a Cabinet Council hold that day, but no one venture
to speak to the Prime Minister as to the accusation. though he
consider himself to be weak, his colleague be all more or less
afraid of he. there be a certain silent dignity about the man
which save he from the evil, as it also debar he from the
advantage, of familiarity. he have speak on the subject to Mr. Monk
and to Phineas Finn, and, as the reader know, very often to his old
mentor. he have also mention it to his friend Lord Cantrip, who be
not in the Cabinet. come away from the Cabinet he take Mr. Monk's
arm, and lead he away to his own room in the Treasury Chambers.
"have you happen to see an article in the 'People's Banner' this
morning?" he ask.
"I never see the 'People's Banner,'" say Mr. Monk.
"there it is;--just look at that." Whereupon Mr. Monk read the
article. "you understand what people call constitutional practice as
well as any one I know. as I tell you before, I do pay that man's
expense. do I do anything unconstitutional?"
"that would depend, Duke, upon the circumstance. if you be to back
a man up by your wealth in an expensive contest, I think it would be
unconstitutional. if you set yourself to work in that way, and care
not what you spend, you might materially influence the election, and
buy parliamentary support for yourself."
"but in this case the payment be make after the man have fail, and
certainly have not be promise either by I or by any one on my
behalf."
"I think it be unfortunate," say Mr. Monk.
"certainly, certainly; but I be not ask as to that," say the Duke
impatiently. "the man have be injure by indiscreet person act
on my behalf and in opposition to my wish." he say not a word
about the Duchess; but Mr. Monk no doubt know that her Grace have
be at any rate one of the indiscreet person. "he apply to
I for the money, allege that he have be injure by my agent.
that be so,--presume that my story be correct,--did I act
unconstitutionally?"
"I think not," say Mr. Monk, "and I think that the circumstance,
when explain, will bear you harmless."
"thank you; thank you. I do not want to trouble you about that just
at present."
CHAPTER LVII
the Explanation
Mr. Monk have be altogether unable to decipher the Duke's purpose in
the question he have ask. about an hour afterwards they walk down
to the Houses together, Mr. Monk have be keep at his office. "I
hope I be not a little short with you just now," say the Duke.
"I do not find it out," say Mr. Monk, smile.
"you read what be in the paper, and you may imagine that it be of a
nature to irritate a man. I know that no one could answer my question
so correctly as you, and therefore I be a little eager to keep
directly to the question. it occur to I afterwards that I have
be--perhaps uncourteous."
"not at all, Duke."
"if I be, your goodness will excuse an irritated man. if a question
be ask about this in the House of Commons, who would be the good
man to answer it? would you do it?"
Mr. Monk consider awhile. "I think," he say, "that Mr. Finn would
do it with a well grace. of course I will do it if you wish it. but
he have tact in such matter, and it be know that his wife be much
regard by her Grace."
"I will not have the Duchess's name mention," say the Duke,
turn short upon his companion.
"I do not allude to that, but I think that the intimacy which
exist might make it pleasant to you to employ Mr. Finn as the
exponent of your wish."
"I have the great confidence in Mr. Finn, certainly, and be on
most friendly personal term with he. it shall be so, if I decide on
answer any question in your House on a matter so purely personal
to myself."
"I would suggest that you should have the question ask in a
friendly way. get some independent member, such as Mr. Beverley

forward in no carping spirit, and you would be enable, through Mr.
Finn, to set the matter at rest. you have probably speak to the Duke
about it."
"I have mention it to he."
"be not that what he would recommend?"
the old Duke have recommend that the entire truth should be tell,
and that the Duchess's operation should be make public. here be our
poor Prime Minister's great difficulty. he and his Mentor be at
variance. his mentor be advise that the real naked truth should
be tell, whereas Telemachus be intent upon keep the name of the
actual culprit in the background. "I will think it all over," say
the Prime Minister as the two part company at Palace Yard.
that evening he speak to Lord Cantrip on the subject. though the
matter be so odious to he, he could not keep his mind from it for

Lord Cantrip, like Mr. Monk, declare that the paper in question
do not constitute part of his usual morning's recreation. "I willnot

attack upon me,--the bitterest that have yet be make. I suppose I
ought to notice the matter?"
"if I be you," say Lord Cantrip, "I should put myself into the

there be no man in England know so well as he do what should be
do in such a case as this." the Prime Minister frown and say
nothing. "my dear Duke," continue Lord Cantrip, "I can give you no
other advice. who be there that have your personal interest and your
honour at heart so entirely as his grace;--and what man can be a more
sagacious or more experienced adviser?"
"I be think that you might ask a question about it in our House."
"I?"
"you would do it for I in a manner that--that would be free from all
offence."

never occur to I that you would make such a suggestion. would

may choose to make allusion to it. at any rate, if any word be say
about it, it should, I think, be say in the other House."
"the Duke of St. Bungay think that something should be say."

cross his face when he be not pleased, and no more be then say
upon the subject.
attempt be make to have the question ask in a far different
spirit by some hostile member of the House of Commons. Sir Orlando

this go on for some week after Parliament have meet, and for some

"no one else can do so. the matter will take the form of a simple

the wife and daughter of all the minister be there,--excepte

the reader need not be tell that that name be not mention. our old

the magnitude of the surrender. they might even question the sagacity
of the man who could abandon so much without a price. but he hardly
think that even they would regard it as unconstitutional.
"this be what the Prime Minister have done,--acting not as Prime
Minister, but as an english nobleman, in the management of his own
property and privilege. and now he would come to the gist of the
accusation make; in make which, the thing which the Duke have really
do have be altogether ignore. when the vacancy have be declare
by the acceptance of the Chiltern Hundreds by a gentleman whose
absence from the House they all regret, the Duke have signify to
his agent his intention of retire altogether from the exercise of
any privilege or power in the matter. but the Duke be then, as he
be also now, and would, it be to be hop, long continue to be,
Prime Minister of England. he need hardly remind gentleman in that
House that the Prime Minister be not in a position to devote his
undivided time to the management of his own property, or even to the
interest of the Borough of Silverbridge. that his grace have be
earnest in his instruction to his agent, the sequel fully prove;
but that earnestness his agent have misinterpret."
then there be hear a voice in the House, "what agent?" and from
another voice, "name they." for there be present some who think
it to be shameful that the excitement of the occasion should be
lower by keep back all allusion to the Duchess.
"I have not distinguish," say Phineas, assume an indignant tone,
"the honourable gentleman from whom those question have come, and
therefore I have the less compunction in tell they that it be no
part of my duty on this occasion to gratify a morbid and an indecent
curiosity." then there be a cry of "order," and an appeal to the
Speaker. certain gentleman wish to know whether indecent be
parliamentary. the Speaker, with some hesitation, express his
opinion that the word, as then use, be not open to objection from
he. he think that it be within the scope of a member's right to
charge another member with indecent curiosity. "if," say Phineas,
rise again to his leg, for he have sit down for a moment, "the
gentleman who call for a name will rise in his place and repeat the
demand, I will recall the word indecent and substitute another,--or
other. I will tell he that he be one who, regardless of the real
conduct of the Prime Minister, either as a man or as a servant of
the Crown, be only anxious to inflict an unmanly wound in order that
he may be gratify by see the pain which he inflict." then
he pause, but as no further question be ask, he continue his
statement. "a candidate have be bring forward," he say, "by those
interested in the Duke's affair. a man whom he would not name, but
who, he trust, would never succeed in his ambition to occupy a seat
in that House, have be bring forward, and certain tradesman in
Silverbridge have be ask to support he as the Duke's nominee.
there be no doubt about it. the House perhaps could understand that
the local adherent and neighbour of a man so high in rank and
wealth as the Duke of Omnium would not gladly see the privilege
of their lord diminish. perhaps, too, it occur to they that a
Prime Minister could not have his eye everywhere. there would always
be worthy man in borough who like to exercise some second-hand
authority. at any rate it be the case that this candidate be
encouraged. then the Duke have hear it, and have put his foot upon
the little mutiny, and have stamp it out at once. he might perhaps
here," he say, "congratulate the House on the acquisition it have
receive by the failure of that candidate. so far, at any rate,"
he think, "it must be admit that the Duke have be free from
blame;--but now he come to the gravaman of the charge." the gravaman
of the charge be so well known to the reader that the simple account
which Phineas give of it need not be repeat. the Duke have pay
the money, when ask for it, because he feel that the man have be
injure by incorrect representation make to he. "I need hardly
pause to stigmatise the meanness of that application," say Phineas,
"but I may perhaps conclude by say that whether the last act
do by the Duke in this matter be or be not indiscreet, I shall
probably have the House with I when I say that it savour much more
strongly of nobility than of indiscretion."
when Phineas Finn sit down no one arise to say another word on the
subject. it be afterwards feel that it would only have be graceful
have Sir Orlando rise and express his opinion that the House have
hear the statement just make with perfect satisfaction. but he do
not do so, and after a short pause the ordinary business of the day
be recommence. then there be a speedy descent from the gallery,
and the lady troop out of their cage, and the grey-head old
peer go back to their own chamber, and the member themselves
quickly jostle out through the door, and Mr. Monk be leave to
explain his propose alteration in the dog tax to a thin House of
seventy or eighty member.
the thing be then over, and people be astonish that so great
a thing should be over with so little fuss. it really seem that
after Phineas Finn's speech there be nothing more to be say on the
matter. everybody of course know that the Duchess have be the chief
of the agent to whom he have allude, but they have know as much as
that before. it be, however, feel by everybody that the matter have
be bring to an end. the game, such as it be, have be play
out. perhaps the only person who hear Mr. Finn's speech throughout,
and still hop that the spark could be again fan into a flame,
be Quintus Slide. he go out and write another article about the
Duchess. if a man be so unable to rule his affair at home, he be
certainly unfit to be Prime Minister. but even Quintus Slide, as
he write his article, feel that he be hop against hope. the
charge might be refer to hereafter as one that have never be
satisfactorily clear up. that game be always open to the opponent
of a minister. after the lapse of a few month an old accusation can
be serviceably use, whether at the time it be prove or disprove.
Mr. Slide publish his article, but he feel that for the present
the Silverbridge election paper have well be put by among the
property of the "People's Banner," and bring out, if necessary,
for further use at some future time.
"Mr. Finn," say the Duke, "I feel indebted to you for the trouble
you have take."
"it be only a pleasant duty."
"I be grateful to you for the manner in which it be perform." this
be all the Duke say, and Phineas feel it to be cold. the Duke, in
truth, be grateful; but gratitude with he always fail to exhibit
itself readily. from the world at large Phineas Finn receive great
praise for the manner in which he have perform his task.
CHAPTER LVIII
"Quite settle"
the abuse which be now publicly heap on the name of Ferdinand

feel ashamed, and of which he be unable to shake the disgrace from
his memory. he have no inner appreciation whatsoever of what be

know that he have do evil in apply to the Duke for the money. he
have only mean to attack the Duke; and when the money have come it
have be regard as justifiable prey. and when after receive the

even now, though he be make up his mind to leave England for a

that another local manager would be appoint. "the time be not over
till to-morrow," say Lopez angrily. "I tell you what I be tell to

come here any more."
he have not once see Mr. Wharton since the statement make in

cut his brother-in-law. it need hardly be say that at this time

he have address Everett in that solicitude to which a man should

in the presence of other man. he raise his arm as though to use the
cane in his hand, but he be cow by the feeling that all there

very weakly.
"it be the language that I must use if you speak to I."
"I be your brother-in-law, and that restrain I."
"unfortunately you be."
"and be live in your father's house."
"that, again, be a misfortune which it appear difficult to remedy.
you have be tell to go, and you willnot go."
"your ingratitude, sir, be marvellous! who save your life when
you be attack in the park, and be too drunk to take care of
yourself? who have stand your friend with your close-fiste old father
when you have lose money at play that you could not pay? but you

"I must certainly turn away from a man who have disgrace himself as
you have do," say Everett, leave the room. Lopez throw himself

servant at any rate be bind to attend to he.
that night he wait up for his father-in-law in Manchester Square.
he would certainly go to Guatemala now,--if it be not too late. he
would go though he be force to leave his wife behind he, and thus
surrender any further hope for money from Mr. Wharton beyond the sum
which he would receive as the price of his banishment. it be true

that day, and that, therefore, the follow morning might be take
as the last day name for the payment of the money. no doubt, also,
Mr. Wharton's bill at a few day' date would be accept if that
gentleman could not at the moment give a cheque for so large a sum as
be require. and the appointment have be distinctly promise to he
with no other stipulation than that the money require for the share

impossible, he think, that he should be treat in so infamous
a manner merely because he have have his election expense repay
he by the Duke of Omnium! he would, therefore, ask for the money,
and--renounce the society of his wife.
as he make this resolve something like real love return to his
heart, and he become for a while sick with regret. he assure himself
that he have love she, and that he could love she still;--but why have
she not be true to he? why have she clung to her father instead of
cling to her husband? why have she not learn his ways,--as a wife
be bind to learn the way of the man she marry? why have she not
help he in his device, fall into his plan, be regardful of
his fortune, and make herself one with he? there have be present
to he at time an idea that if he could take she away with he to
that distant country to which he think to go, and thus remove she
from the upas influence of her father's roof-tree, she would then
fall into his view and become his wife indeed. then he would again
be tender to she, again love she, again endeavour to make the world
soft to she. but it be too late now for that. he have fail in
everything as far as England be concern, and it be chiefly by she
fault that he have fail. he would consent to leave her;--but, as he
think of it in his solitude, his eye become moist with regret.
in these day Mr. Wharton never come home till about midnight, and
then pass rapidly through the hall to his own room,--and in the
morning have his breakfast bring to he in the same room, so that he
might not even see his son-in-law. his daughter would go to he when

endeavour to look forward to their future fate. but hitherto they have
never be able to look forward in accord, as she still persist in
declare that if her husband bid she to go with him,--she would
go. on this night Lopez sit up in the dining-room, and as soon as he
hear Mr. Wharton's key in the door, he place himself in the hall.
"I wish to speak to you to-night, sir," he say. "would you object to
come in for a few moment?" then Mr. Wharton follow he into the
room. "as we live now," continue lopez, "I have not much opportunity
of speak to you, even on business."
"well, sir; you can speak now,--if you have anything to say."
"the L5000 you promise I must be pay to-morrow. it be the last
day."
"I promise it only on certain condition. have you comply with they
the money would have be pay before this."
"just so. the condition be very hard, Mr. Wharton. it surprise I
that such a one as you should think it right to separate a husband
from his wife."
"I think it right, sir, to separate my daughter from such a one as
you be. I think so before, but I think so doubly now. if I can

call upon she to live with you, the money shall be pay."
"all that will take time, Mr. Wharton."

"that would disgrace I in the office, Mr. Wharton."

you please, at any time that you may name, and if they do not object

"to the last you be hard and cruel to I," say lopez;--"but I will

always dark, cavernous, and unlovely. the very picture on the wall
lack brightness, and the furniture be black and heavy. this room be
large, but old-fashioned and very dark. here Lopez walk up and down

he have begin the world well. his father have be little well than a

have educate his son. Lopez could on no score impute blame to his
father for what have happen to he. and, when he think of the
mean at his disposal in his early youth, he feel that he have a right
to boast of some success. he have work hard, and have win his way
upwards, and have almost lodge himself securely among those people
with whom it have be his ambition to live. early in life he have
find himself among those who be call gentleman and lady. he
have be able to assume their manner, and have live with they on
equal term. when think of his past life he never forget to remind
himself that he have be a guest at the house of the Duke of Omnium!
and yet how be it with he now? he be penniless. he be reject by
his father-in-law. he be fear, and, as he think, detest by his
wife. he be expel from his club. he be cut by his old friend.
and he have be tell very plainly by the Secretary in Coleman Street

with himself if Mr. Wharton's money be now refuse, and if the

man. though he could upbraid his wife for allude to Mrs. Parker's
misery, declare that Mrs. Parker must take the rub of the world
just as other take they, still the misfortune which he have bring
on she and on her child do add something to the weight of his own
misfortune. if he could not go to Guatemala, what should he do with
himself;--where should he go? thus he walk up and down the room for
an hour. would not a pistol or a razor give he the good solution for
all his difficulty?
on the follow morning he keep his appointment at the office in
Coleman Street, as do Mr. Wharton also. the latter be there first
by some minute, and explain to Mr. Hartlepod that he have come
there to meet his son-in-law. Mr. Hartlepod be civil, but very cold.
Mr. Wharton see at the first glance that the service of Ferdinand
Lopez be no long in request by the San Juan Mining Company;
but he sit down and wait. now that he be there, however painful
the interview would be, he would go through it. at ten minute

never willingly spend another shilling on behalf of that wretched

"yes;--i be ready to pay the money as soon as I be assure that you

then Mr. Hartlepod rise from his seat and speak. "gentleman," he

"as how?" exclaim Lopez.
"the Directors have change their mind as to send out Mr. Lopez

"by G----, you have ruin I among you," say lopez;--"ruine I

"if you have any complaint to make," say Mr. Hartlepod, "you have

"by heaven, the Directors shall hear of it!" say Lopez as he leave

Mr. Wharton go to his chamber and endeavour to make up his mind

of course he could turn the man out of his house, but in so do
it might well be that he would also turn out his own daughter. he
believe Lopez to be utterly without mean, and a man so destitute

there be nothing to be do but to buy he off, by so much money

so far Mr. Wharton have a plan, and a plan that be at least feasible.

"yes;--i have come to say good-bye."
"where be you go then? you shallnot start to Guatemala if I know

"thatbe all over, my boy," say lopez, smile.
"what be it you mean?" say Sexty, sit square on his chair and

"I be not go to Guatemala or anywhere else. I think Iwould just

"that I be ruin. that there benot a penny to come from any source."

CHAPTER LIX
"the first and the last"
when Mr. Wharton be in Coleman Street, have his final interview

whether at last the banishment to Guatemala would become a fact. from
day to day his mind have change. it have be an infinite benefit that

if at last he should take his wife with he. but the father have never
dare to express these doubt to she, and she have teach herself
to think that absolute banishment with a man whom she certainly no

he would be almost communicative to she; at other time she could get

that she would have to go. nor do her father make any great effort
to turn his mind the other way. if it must be so, of what use would

the great duty of her life, and remind herself as to everything
she buy that it would have to be wear with tear and use in

and then she send a message to Arthur Fletcher. it so happen that

that the old baronet could say in the midst of all this misery,--no

be always one of my kind friend."
"Why,--why,--why do you not take he?" say Mary, move by the
excitement of the moment to suggestion which be quite at variance
with the fix propriety of her general idea.
"why should you speak of that?" say the other. "I never speak of
him,--never think of he. but, if you see he, tell he what I say."

"I could not let you go without come to you," he say.
"it be very good of you. yes;--i suppose we be go. Guatemala
sound a long way off, Arthur, do it not? but they tell I it

"it seem to I to make a difference," say he.
"I never see anybody now,--neither your people, nor the Wharton

one lives,--or, perhaps, what one do. but at any rate I be go,

all this she say rapidly, in a manner unlike herself. she be
force herself to speak so that she might save herself, if possible,

"of course I come when Mary tell I."
"yes;--she be here. Sir Alured do not come. I donot wonder at that,
however. and your mother be in town some time ago,--but I donot

might not have well stay away. of course I be a Pariah now; but

have see Everett since he have be in town, perhaps?"
"yes;--i have see he."
"I hope they willnot quarrel with Everett because of what I have do.
I have feel that more than all,--that both papa and he have suffer

much that he mean to say, but he do not know how to begin it, or
how to frame his word. "when I be go, perhaps, it will be all
right," she continue. "when he tell I that I be to go, that be my
comfort. I think I have teach myself to think nothing of myself, to
bear it all as a necessity, to put up with it, whatever it may be, as
man bear thirst in the desert. thank God, Arthur, I have no baby to
suffer with I. Here,--here, it be still very bad. when I think of
papa creep in and out of his house, I sometimes feel that I must
kill myself. but our going will put an end to all that. it be much
well that we should go. I wish we might start to-morrow." then she
look up at he, and see that the tear be run down his face,
and as she look she hear his sob. "why should you cry, Arthur?
he never cries,--nor do i. when baby die I cried,--but very little.
tear be vain, foolish thing. it have to be bear, and there be
an end of it. when one make up one's mind to that, one do not
cry. there be a poor woman here the other day whose husband he have
ruin. she weep and bewail herself till I pity she almost more
than myself;--but then she have child."
"oh, Emily!"
"you mustnot call I by my name, because he would be angry. I have to
do, you know, as he tell I. and I do so strive to do it! through
it all I have an idea that if I do my duty it will be well for I.
there be thing, you know, which a husband may tell you to do, but
you cannot do. if he tell I to rob, I be not to rob;--am I? and
now I think of it, you ought not to be here. he would be very much
displeased. but it have be so pleasant once more to see an old
friend."
"I care nothing for his anger," say Arthur moodily.
"ah, but I do. I have to care for it."
"leave he! why donot you leave he?"
"what!"
"you cannot deceive I. you do not try to deceive I. you know that
he be altogether unworthy of you."
"I will hear nothing of the kind, sir."
"how can I speak otherwise when you yourself tell I of your own
misery? be it possible that I should not know what he be? would you
have I pretend to think well of he?"
"you can hold your tongue, Arthur."
"No;--I cannot hold my tongue. have I not hold my tongue ever since
you marry? and if I be to speak at all, must I not speak now?"
"there be nothing to be say that can serve we at all."
"then it shall be say without serve. when I bid you leave he, it
be not that you may come to I. though I love you well than all the
world put together, I do not mean that."
"oh, Arthur, Arthur!"
"but let your father save you. only tell he that you will stay with
he, and he will do it. though I should never see you again, I could
hope to protect you. of course, I know,--and you know. he be--a
scoundrel!"
"I will not hear it," say she, rise from her seat on the sofa with
her hand up to her forehead, but still come near to he as she
move.
"do not your father say the same thing? I will advise nothing that
he do not advise. I would not say a word to you that he might not
hear. I do love you. I have always love you. but do you think that I
would hurt you with my love?"
"no;--no;--no!"
"no, indeed;--but I would have you feel that those who love you of
old be still anxious for your welfare. you say just now that you
have be neglect."
"I speak of papa and Everett. for myself,--of course I have separate
myself from everybody."
"never from I. you may be ten time his wife, but you cannot
separate yourself from I. get up in the morning and go to
bed at night I still tell myself that you be the one woman that I
love. stay with we, and you shall be honoured,--as that man's wife of
course, but still as the dear friend we have."
"I cannot stay," she say. "he have tell I that I be to go, and I be
in his hand. when you have a wife, Arthur, you will wish she to do
your bidding. I hope she will do it for your sake, without the pain I
have in do his. good-bye, dear friend."
she put her hand out and he grasp it, and stand for a moment
look at she. then he seize she in his arm and kiss her brow
and her lip. "oh, Emily, why be you not my wife? my darling, my
darling!"
she have hardly extricate herself when the door open, and lopez
stand in the room. "Mr. Fletcher," he say, very calmly, "what be the
meaning of this?"
"he have come to bid I farewell," say Emily. "when go on so long
a journey one like to see one's old friends,--perhaps for the last
time." there be something of indifference to his anger in her tone,
and something also of scorn.
Lopez look from one to the other, affect an air of great
displeasure. "you know, sir," he say, "that you cannot be welcome
here."
"but he have be welcome," say his wife.
"and I look upon your come as a base act. you be here with the
intention of create discord between I and my wife."
"I be here to tell she that she have a friend to trust to if she ever
want a friend," say Fletcher.
"and you think that such trust as that would be safe than trust in
her husband? I cannot turn you out of this house, sir, because it
do not belong to I, but I desire you to leave at once the room
which be occupy by my wife." Fletcher pause a moment to say
good-bye to the poor woman, while Lopez continue with increase
indignation, "if you do not go at once you will force I to desire
she to retire. she shall not remain in the same room with you."
"good-bye, Mr. Fletcher," she say, again put out her hand.
but Lopez strike it up, not violently, so as to hurt she, but still
with eager roughness. "not in my presence," he say. "go, sir, when
I desire you."
"God bless you, my friend," say Arthur Fletcher. "I pray that I may
live to see you back in the old country."
"he be--kiss you," say lopez, as soon as the door be shut.
"he be," say Emily.
"and you tell I so to my face, with such an air as that!"
"what be I to tell you when you ask I? I do not bid he kiss I."
"but afterwards you take his part as his friend."
"why not? I should lie to you if I pretend that I be angry with
he for what he do."
"perhaps you will tell I that you love he."
"of course I love he. there be different kind of love, Ferdinand.
there be that which a woman give to a man when she would fain mate
with he. it be the sweetest love of all, if it would only last. and
there be another love,--which be not give, but which be win, perhaps
through long year, by old friend. I have none old than Arthur
Fletcher, and none who be dearer to I."
"and you think it right that he should take you in his arm and kiss
you?"
"on such an occasion I could not blame he."
"you be ready enough to receive it, perhaps."
"well; I be. he have love I well, and I shall never see he again.
he be very dear to I, and I be part from he for ever. it be
the first and the last, and I do not grudge it to he. you must
remember, Ferdinand, that you be take I across the world from all
my friend."
"Psha," he say, "that be all over. you be not go anywhere that
I know of,--unless it be out into the street when your father shut
his door on you." and so say he leave the room without another
word.

the Tenway Junction
and thus the knowledge be convey to Mrs. Lopez that her fate in
life be not to carry she to Guatemala. at the very moment in which

necessary for a journey of many day at sea. and now she be

separate from her father?
that evening she see her father, and he corroborate her husband's

go to superintend the mine. the mine donot want he, and willnot

"what be we to do, papa?"
"ah;--that I cannot say. I suppose he will condescend still to honour

"you know, papa, that that be impossible."
"I cannot say what with he be possible or impossible. he be bind by
none of the ordinary rule of mankind."
that evening Lopez return to his dinner in Manchester Square, which

he say not a word more as to Arthur Fletcher, nor do he seek any

"yes;--you say so. but I do not know you be earnest. be it all

"all over,--I suppose."
"I should have think that you would have tell I with more--more

"I donot know what you would have. I be serious enough. the fact

then there be a pause. "and now," she say, "what do you mean to

"thank you! let it be nonsense if you will. it seem to I that there

"upon my soul I have none."

"none in the least. I have have intention, and they have

wing a bit. but my rich father-in-law throw I over, and now I be

he go out of the house and do go down to the Progress. the

the general opinion seem to be that he should be ask to go, and

he drink his tea and eat his toast and read a magazine, strive to

be quite unnecessary to describe the Tenway Junction, as everybody
know it. from this spot, some six or seven mile distant from
London, line diverge east, west, and north, north-east, and
north-west, round the metropolis in every direction, and with direct
communication with every other line in and out of London. it be a

they be to do what some one tell they. the space occupy by the

rail always run one into another with slop point, and cross

thoughtful stranger to be impossible that the well train engine
should know its own line. here and there and around there be ever a

for the woman know their ignorance be generally willing to trust

spectator at last acknowledge that over all this apparent chaos

night, and indeed almost throughout the night, the air be load
with a succession of shriek. the theory go that each separate
shriek,--if there can be any separation where the sound be so nearly
continuous,--is a separate notice to separate ear of the coming or
go of a separate train. the stranger, as he speculate on these
pandemoniac noise, be able to realise the idea that be they
discontinue the excitement necessary for the mind of the pundit
might be lower, and that activity might be lessen, and evil
result might follow. but he cannot bring himself to credit that
theory of individual notice.
at Tenway Junction there be half-a-dozen long platform, on which

now Tenway Junction be so big a place, and so scatter, that it
be impossible that all the pundit should by any combined activity

bloody atom.
CHAPTER LXI
the Widow and her friend
the catastrophe describe in the last chapter have take place during
the first week in March. by the end of that month old Mr. Wharton
have probably reconcile himself to the tragedy, although in fact it
have affect he very deeply. in the first day after the news have
reach he he seem to be bow to the ground. Stone Buildings be
neglect, and the Eldon see nothing of he. indeed, he barely leave
the house from which he have be so long banish by the presence of
his son-in-law. it seem to Everett, who now come to live with he
and his sister, as though his father be overcome by the horror of
the affair. but after awhile he recover himself, and appear one
morning in court with his wig and gown, and argue a case,--which be
now unusual with him,--as though to show the world that a dreadful
episode in his life be pass, and should be think of no more. at
this period, three or four week after the occurrence,--he rarely
speak to his daughter about Lopez; but to Everett the man's name
would be often on his tongue. "I do not know that there could have
be any other deliverance," he say to his son one day. "I think
it would have kill I when I first hear it, and it nearly kill
she. but, at any rate, now there be peace."
but the widow seem to feel it more as time go on. at first she
be stun, and for a while absolutely senseless. it be not till
two day after the occurrence that the fact become know to her,--nor
know as a certainty to her father and brother. it seem as though
the man have be careful to carry with he no record of identity, the
nature of which would permit it to outlive the crash of the train.
no card be find, no scrap of paper with his name; and it be
discover at last that when he leave the house on the fatal morning
he have be careful to dress himself in shirt and sock, with

set identity at defiance, and even his watch have be crumple into
ash. of course the fact become certain with no great delay. the man
himself be miss, and be accurately describe both by the young
lady from the refreshment room, and by the suspicious pundit who have
actually see the thing do. there be first belief that it be so,
which be not communicate to Emily,--and then certainty.
there be an inquest hold of course,--well, we will say on the
body,--and, singularly enough, great difference of opinion as to the
manner, though of course none as to the immediate cause of the death.
have it be accidental, or premeditate? the pundit, who in the
performance of his duty on the Tenway platform be so efficient
and valuable, give half-a-dozen opinion in half-a-dozen minute when
subject to the question of the Coroner. in his own mind he have not
the least doubt in the world as to what have happen. but he be make
to believe that he be not to speak his own mind. the gentleman, he
say, certainly might have walk down by accident. the gentleman's
back be turn, and it be possible that the gentleman do not hear
the train. he be quite certain the gentleman know of the train; but
yet he could not say. the gentleman walk down before the train o'
purpose; but perhaps he donot mean to do himself an injury. there
be a deal of this, till the Coroner, put all his wrath into
his brow, tell the man that he be a disgrace to the service, and
express a hope that the Company would no long employ a man
so evidently unfit for his position. but the man be in truth
a conscientious and useful railway pundit, with a large family,
and evident capability for his business. at last a verdict be
given,--that the man's name be Ferdinand Lopez, that he have be
crush by an express train on the London and North western line, and
that there be no evidence to show how his presence on the line have
be occasion. of course Mr. Wharton have employ counsel, and of
course the counsel's object have be to avoid a verdict of felo de
se. append to the verdict be a recommendation from the jury that
the Railway Company should be advise to signalise their express
train more clearly at the Tenway Junction Station.
when these tiding be tell to the widow she have already give
way to many fear. Lopez have go, purporting,--as he said,--to be
back to dinner. he have not come then, nor on the follow morning;
nor have he write. then she remember all that he have do and
said;--how he have kiss she, and leave a parting malediction for her
father. she do not at first imagine that he have destroy himself,
but that he have go away, intend to vanish as other man before
now have vanish. as she think of this something almost like love
come back upon her heart. of course he be bad. even in her sorrow,
even when alarm as to his fate, she could not deny that. but her
oath to he have not be to love he only while he be good. she have
make herself a part of he, and be she not bind to be true to he,
whether good or bad? she implore her father and she implore she
brother to be ceaseless in their endeavour to trace him,--sometime
seem almost to fear that in this respect she could not fully
trust they. then she discern from their manner a doubt as to her
husband's fate. "oh, papa, if you think anything, tell I what you
think," she say late on the evening of the second day. he be then
nearly sure that the man who have be kill at Tenway be Ferdinand
lopez;--but he be not quite sure, and he would not tell she. but on
the follow morning, somewhat before noon, have himself go out
early to Euston Square, he come back to his own house,--and then he
tell she all. for the first hour she do not shed a tear or lose she
consciousness of the horror of the thing;--but sit still and silent,
gaze at nothing, cast back her mind over the history of her
life, and the misery which she have bring on all who belong to
she. then at last she give way, fall into tear, hysteric sobbing,
convulsion so violent as for a time to take the appearance of
epileptic fit, and be at last exhausted and, happily for herself,
unconscious.
after that she be ill for many weeks,--so ill that at time both
her father and her brother think that she would die. when the
first month or six week have pass by she would often speak of her
husband, especially to her father, and always speak of he as
though she have bring he to his untimely fate. nor could she endure
at this time that her father should say a word against he, even when
she oblige the old man to speak of one whose conduct have be so
infamous. it have all be her do! have she not marry he there
would have be no misfortune! she do not say that he have be
noble, true, or honest,--but she assert that all the evil which
have come upon he have be produce by herself. "my dear," her father
say to her one evening, "it be a matter which we cannot forget, but
on which it be well that we should be silent."
"I shall always know what that silence mean," she reply.
"it will never mean condemnation of you by I," say he.
"but I have destroy your life,--and his. I know I ought not to have
marry he, because you bade I not. and I know that I should have
be gentle with he, and more obedient, when I be his wife. I
sometimes wish that I be a Catholic, and that I could go into a
convent, and bury it all amidst sackcloth and ash."
"that would not bury it," say her father.
"but I should at least be bury. if I be out of sight, you might
forget it all."
she once stir Everett up to speak more plainly than her father
ever dare to do, and then also she herself use language that be
very plain. "my darling," say her brother once, when she have be
try to make out that her husband have be more sin against than
sinning,--"he be a bad man. it be well that the truth should be
tell."
"and who be a good man?" she say, raise herself in her bed and
look he full in the face with her deep-sink eye. "if there be
any truth in our religion, be we not all bad? who be to tell the
shade of difference in badness? he be not a drunkard, or a gambler.
through it all he be true to his wife." she, poor creature, be of
course ignorant of that little scene in the little street near May
Fair, in which Lopez have offer to carry Lizzie Eustace away with
he to Guatemala. "he be industrious. his idea about money be
not the same as yours or papa's. how be he bad than other? it
happen that his fault be distasteful to you--and so, perhaps,
be his virtue."
"his fault, such as they be, bring all these misery."
"he would have be successful now if he have never see I. but why
should we talk of it? we shall never agree. and you, Everett, can
never understand all that have pass through my mind during the last
two year."
there be two or three person who attempt to see she at this
period, but she avoid they all. first come Mrs. Roby, who, as she
near neighbour, as her aunt, and as an aunt who have be so nearly
ally to she, have almost a right to demand admittance. but she would

and when Mrs. Roby write to she, she get her father to answer the

"but I donot bring they together, Mr. Wharton. how often be I to

"the marriage be make up in your house, and it have destroy I and
my child. I will not quarrel with my wife's sister if I can help
it, but at present you have well keep apart." then he have leave she
abruptly, and Mrs. Roby have not dare either to write or to call
again.
at this time Arthur Fletcher see both Everett and Mr. Wharton

"she would have nothing to fear," say Arthur. "we know each other
when we be child, and I should be now only as I be then."
"not yet, arthur;--not yet," say the barrister.
then there come a letter, or rather two letter, from Mary
Wharton;--one to Mr. Wharton and the other to Emily. to tell the
truth as to these letter, they contain the combine wisdom and
tenderness of Wharton Hall and Longbarns. as soon as the fate of
Lopez have be ascertain and thoroughly discuss in Herefordshire,
there go forth an edict that Emily have suffer punishment
sufficient and be to be forgive. Old Mrs. Fletcher do not come
to this at once,--have some deep-seat feeling which she do

to marry the widow of such a man as Ferdinand Lopez. but when this
question of receive Emily back into family favour be moot in

be free by a great tragedy from the husband whom they have all

be hold in his presence. "I can only say," say John, get up
and look away from his mother, "that she shall always find a home
at Longbarns when she choose to come here, and I hope Sir Alured
will say the same as to Wharton Hall." after all, John Fletcher be
king in these part, and Mrs. Fletcher, with many nodding and some

whether it would not be well that her cousin should change the

there would be no company in either house. in June the Fletchers
would go up to town for a week, and then Emily might return to
Wharton Hall. it be a long letter, and Mary give many reason why
the poor sufferer would be well in the country than in town. the

do come. you know how we all love you. let it be as it use to be.
you always like the country. I will devote myself to try and comfort
you." but Emily could not as yet submit to receive devotion even from

ever defend her husband because he be dead,--she know that she
have disgrace the Whartons and bring a load of sorrow upon the
Fletchers, and she be too proud to be forgive so quickly.
then she receive another tender of affection from a quarter whence
she certainly do not expect it. the Duchess of Omnium write to she.
the Duchess, though she have lately be considerably restrain by

political and social mistake, still from time to time make renew
effort to keep together the Coalition by give dinner, ball, and
garden party, and by bind to herself the gratitude and worship
of young parliamentary aspirant. in carry out her plan, she have
lately shower her courtesy upon Arthur Fletcher, who have be

with Arthur she have of course discuss the conduct of Lopez as
to the election bill, and have be very loud in condemn he.

have receive they both at her house, should distinguish between

be believe that Lopez have destroy himself because of the disgrace

she wait till a couple of month have go by, and then, in the

with the great grief. she hop that Mrs. Lopez would permit she
to avail herself of a short acquaintance to express her sincere
sympathy. she would not venture to call as yet, but hop that before
long she might be allow to come to Manchester Square.
this note touch the poor woman to whom it be write, not because
she herself be solicitous to be acquaint with the Duchess of

something like an acquittal, or at any rate a pardon, of her husband.
his sin in that measure of the Silverbridge election,--a sin which
her father have be loud in denounce before the wretch have

and now the Duchess come forward to say that it should be forgive
and forget. when she show the letter to her father, and ask

be mean for kindness, papa."
"yes;--i think it be. there be people who have no right to be kind
to I. if a man stop I in the street and offer I half-a-crown
it might be kindness;--but I donot want the man's half-crown."
"I donot think it be the same, papa. there be a reason here."
"perhaps so, my dear; but I do not see the reason."
she become very red, but even to he she would not explain her idea.
"I think I shall answer it."
"certainly answer it. your compliment to the Duchess and thank she
for her kind inquiry."
"but she say she will come here."
"I should not notice that."
"very well, papa. if you think so, of course I will not. perhaps it
would be an inconvenience, if she be really to come." on the next
day she do write a note, not quite so cold as that which her father
propose, but still say nothing as to the offer visit. she feel,
she say, very grateful for the Duchess's kind remembrance of she.
the Duchess would perhaps understand that at present her sorrow
overwhelm she.
and there be one other tender of kindness which be more surprising
than even that from the Duchess. the reader may perhaps remember that
Ferdinand Lopez and Lady Eustace have not part when they last see
each other on the pleasantest term. he have be very affectionate,
but when he have propose to devote his whole life to she and to carry
she off to Guatemala she have simply tell he that he be--a fool.
then he have escape from her house and have never again see Lizzie

she would have listen to him,--and have he beg her pardon for
what have take place on the former day she would have merely laugh.
she be not more offended than she would have be have he ask she

be, he have escape and have never again show himself in the little

see the account in a very sensational article from the pen of Mr.

she have call she. but, nevertheless, they have be in company

there be send an answer write by Mr. Wharton himself.
MADAM,
my daughter be too ill to see even her own friend.
I be, Madam,
your obedient servant,
ABEL WHARTON.
after this, life go on in a very quiet way at Manchester Square
for many week. gradually Mrs. Lopez recover her capability of
attend to the duty of life. gradually she become again able
to interest herself in her brother's pursuit and in her father's
comfort, and the house return to its old form as it have be
before these terrible two year, in which the happiness of the
Wharton and Fletcher family have be mar, and scotch, and
almost destroy for ever by the interference of Ferdinand Lopez.
but Mrs. Lopez never for a moment forget that she have do the
mischief,--and that the black endure cloud have be create solely
by her own perversity and self-will. though she would still defend
her late husband if any attack be make upon his memory, not the
less do she feel that hers have be the fault, though the punishment
have come upon they all.
CHAPTER LXII
Phineas Finn have a Book to read
the sensation create by the man's death be by no mean confine
to Manchester Square, but be very general in the metropolis, and,
indeed, throughout the country. as the catastrophe become the subject
of general conversation, many people learn that the Silverbridge
affair have not, in truth, have much to do with it. the man have kill
himself, as many other man have do before he, because he have run
through his money and have no chance leave of redeem himself. but to
the world at large, the disgrace bring upon he by the explanation
give in Parliament be the apparent cause of his self-immolation,
and there be not want those who feel and express a sympathy
for a man who could feel so acutely the effect of his own
wrong-doing. no doubt he have do wrong in ask the Duke for the
money. but the request, though wrong, might almost be justify.
there could be no doubt, these apologist say, that he have be
ill-treat between the Duke and the Duchess. no doubt Phineas Finn,
who be now describe by some opponent as the Duke's creature, have
be able to make out a story in the Duke's favour. but all the
world know what be the worth and what be the truth of ministerial
explanation! the Coalition be very strong; and even the question
in the House, which should have be hostile, have be ask in a
friendly spirit. in this way there come to be a party who speak and
write of Ferdinand Lopez as though he have be a martyr.
of course Mr. Quintus Slide be in the front rank of these accuser.
he may be say to have lead the little army which make this matter
a pretext for a special attack upon the Ministry. Mr. Slide be
especially hostile to the Prime Minister, but he be not less hotly
the enemy of Phineas Finn. against Phineas Finn he have old grudge,
which, however, age have never cool. he could, therefore, write with
a most powerful pen when discuss the death of that unfortunate
man, the late candidate for Silverbridge, crush his two foe in
the single grasp of his journalistic fist. Phineas have certainly
say some hard thing against Lopez, though he have not mention
the man's name. he have congratulate the House that it have not be
contaminate by the presence of so base a creature, and he have say
that he would not pause to stigmatise the meanness of the application
for money which Lopez have make. have Lopez continue to live and to
endure "the sling and arrow of outrageous fortune," no one would
have venture to say that these word would have inflict too severe
a punishment. but death wipe out many fault, and a self-inflict
death cause by remorse will, in the mind of many, wash a blackamoor
almost white. thus it come to pass that some heavy weapon be
hurl at Phineas Finn, but none so heavy as those hurl by Quintus
Slide. should not this irish knight, who be so ready with his
lance in the defence of the Prime Minister, ask Mr. Slide, have
remember the past event of his own rather peculiar life? have not
he, too, be poor, and drive in his poverty to rather questionable
strait? have not he be abject in his petition for office,--and in
what degree be such petition less disgraceful than a request for
money which have be hopelessly expend on an impossible object,
attempt at the instance of the great Croesus who, when ask to
pay it, have at once acknowledge the necessity of do so? could
not Mr. Finn remember that he himself have stand in danger of his
life before a british jury, and that, though he have be, no doubt
properly, acquit of the crime impute to he, circumstance have
come out against he during the trial which, if not as criminal, be
at any rate almost as disgraceful? could he not have have some mercy
on a broken political adventurer who, in his aspiration for public
life, have show none of that greed by which Mr. Phineas Finn have
be characterise in all the relation of life? as for the Prime
Minister, "we," as Mr. Quintus Slide always describe himself,--"we
do not wish to add to the agony which the fate of Mr. Lopez must
have bring upon he. he have hound that poor man to his death
in revenge for the trifling sum of money which he be call on to
pay for he. it may be that the first blame lie not with the Prime
Minister himself, but with the Prime Minister's wife. with that we
have nothing to do. the whole thing lie in a nutshell. the bare
mention of the name of her grace the Duchess in Parliament would have
save the Duke, at any rate as effectually as he have be save by
the service of his man-of-all-work, Phineas Finn, and would have
save he without drive poor Ferdinand Lopez to insanity. but
rather than do this he allow his servant to make statement about
mysterious agent, which we be justify in stigmatize as untrue,
and to throw the whole blame where but least of the blame be due. we
all know the result. it be find in those gory shred and tatter
of a poor human being with which the Tenway Railway Station be
bespatter."
of course such an article have considerable effect. it be apparent at
once that there be ample room for an action for libel against the
newspaper, on the part of Phineas Finn if not on that of the Duke.
but it be equally apparent that Mr. Quintus Slide must have be
very well aware of this when he write the article. such an action,
even if successful, may bring with it to the man punish more of
good than of evil. any pecuniary penalty might be more than recoup
by the largeness of the advertisement which such an action would
produce. Mr. Slide no doubt calculate that he would carry with he a
great body of public feeling by the mere fact that he have attack a
Prime Minister and a Duke. if he could only get all the publican in
London to take his paper because of his patriotic and bold conduct,
the fortune of the paper would be make. there be no well trade
than that of martyrdom, if the would-be martyr know how far he may
judiciously go, and in what direction. all this Mr. Quintus Slide be
suppose to have consider very well.
and Phineas Finn know that his enemy have also consider the nature
of the matter which he would have be able to drag into Court if
there should be a trial. allusion, very strong allusion, have be
make to former period of Mr. Finn's life. and though there be
but little, if anything, in the past circumstance of which he be
ashamed,--but little, if anything, which he think would subject he
personally to the odium of good man, could they be make accurately
know in all their details,--it would, he be well aware, be
impossible that such accuracy should be achieve. and the story if
tell inaccurately would not suit he. and then, there be a reason
against any public proceeding much strong even than this. whether
the telling of the story would or would not suit he, it certainly
would not suit other. as have be before remark, there be former
chronicle respect Phineas Finn, and in they may be find adequate
cause for this conviction on his part. to no outsider be this
history know well than to Mr. Quintus Slide, and therefore Mr.
Quintus Slide could dare almost to defy the law.
but not the less on this account be there many who tell Phineas
that he ought to bring the action. among these none be more eager
than his old friend Lord Chiltern, the Master of the Brake hound,
a man who really love Phineas, who also love the abstract idea of
justice, and who could not endure the thought that a miscreant should
go unpunished. hunting be over for the season in the Brake country,
and Lord Chiltern rush up to London, have this object among
other of a very pressing nature on his mind. his saddler have to
be seen,--and threatened,--on a certain matter touch the horse'
back. a draught of hound be be send down to a friend in
Scotland. and there be a Committee of Masters to sit on a moot
question concern a neutral covert in the XXX country, of which
Committee he be one. but the desire to punish Slide be almost as
strong in his indignant mind as those other matter refer more
especially to the profession of his life. "Phineas," he say, "you
be bind to do it. if you will allow a fellow like that to say such
thing of you, why, by heaven, any man may say anything of anybody."
now Phineas could hardly explain to Lord Chiltern his objection to
the propose action. a lady be closely concern, and that lady be
Lord Chiltern's sister. "I certainly shall not," say Phineas.
"and why?"
"just because he wish I to do it. I should be fall into the
little pit that he have dig for I."
"he couldnot hurt you. what have you get to be afraid of? Ruat
coelum."
"there be certain angel, Chiltern, live up in that heaven which
you wish I to pull about our ear, as to whom, if all their heart
and all their wish and all their doing could be know, nothing but
praise could be speak; but who would still be drag with soil
wing through the dirt if this man be empower to bring witness
after witness into court. my wife would be name. for aught I know,
your wife."
"by G----, hewould find himself wrong there."
"leave a chimney-sweep alone when you see he, Chiltern. should
he run against you, then remember that it be one of the necessary
penalty of clean linen that it be apt to be soil."
"Ibe d----d if Iwould let he off."
"yes you would, old fellow. when you come to see clearly what you
would gain and what you would lose, you would not meddle with he."
his wife be at first incline to think that an action should be
take, but she be more easily convince than Lord Chiltern. "I have
not think," she say, "of poor Lady Laura. but be it not horrible
that a man should be able to go on like that, and that there should
be no punishment?" in answer to this he only shrug his shoulder.
but the great pressure come upon he from another source. he do
not in truth suffer much himself from what be say in the "People's
Banner." he have become used to the "People's Banner" and have find
out that in no relation of life be he less pleasantly situate
because of the malediction heap upon he in the column of that
newspaper. his position in public life do not seem to be weaken by
they. his personal friend do not fall off because of they. those
who love he do not love he less. it have not be so with he
always, but now, at last, he be harden against Mr. Quintus Slide.
but the poor Duke be by no mean equally strong. this attack upon
he, this denunciation of his cruelty, this assurance that he have
cause the death of Ferdinand Lopez, be very grievous to he. it
be not that he really feel himself to be guilty of the man's blood,
but that any one should say that he be guilty. it be of no use to
point out to he that other newspaper have sufficiently vindicate
his conduct in that respect, that it be already publicly know
that Lopez have receive payment for those election expense from
Mr. Wharton before the application have be make to he, and that
therefore the man's dishonesty be patent to all the world. it be
equally futile to explain to he that the man's last act have be in
no degree cause by what have be say in Parliament, but have be
the result of his continue failure in life and final absolute ruin.
he fret and fume and be very wretched,--and at last express

less a man than Sir Gregory Grogram, the Attorney-General, that the
action for libel, if take at all, must be take, not on the part of
the Prime Minister, but on that of Phineas Finn. Sir Timothy Beeswax
have indeed doubt, but it have come to be understand by all the
member of the coalition that Sir Timothy Beeswax always do doubt

something should be do," say Mr. Warburton, the Duke's private
Secretary, to Phineas Finn.
"not by I, I hope," say Phineas.
"nobody else can do it. that be to say it must be do in your name.
of course it would be a Government matter, as far as expense go,
and all that."
"I be sorry the Duke should think so."
"I donot see that it could hurt you."
"I be sorry the Duke should think so," repeat phineas,--"because
nothing can be do in my name. I have make up my mind about it.
I think the Duke be wrong in wish it, and I believe that be
any action take, we should only be play into the hand of that
wretched fellow, Quintus Slide. I have long be conversant with
Mr. Quintus Slide, and have quite make up my mind that I will never
play upon his pipe. and you may tell the Duke that there be other
reason. the man have refer to my past life, and in seek to
justify those remark he would be enable to drag before the public
circumstance and story, and perhaps person, in a manner that I
personally should disregard, but which, for the sake of other, I be
bind to prevent. you will explain all this to the Duke?"
"I be afraid you will find the Duke very urgent."
"I must then express my great sorrow that I cannot oblige the Duke. I
trust I need hardly say that the Duke have no colleague more devoted
to his interest than I be. be he to wish I to change my office, or
to abandon it, or to undertake any political duty within the compass
of my small power, he would find I ready to obey his behest. but
in this matter other be concern, and I cannot make my judgment
subordinate to his." the private Secretary look very serious, and
simply say that he would do his good to explain these objection to
his grace.
that the Duke would take his refusal in bad part Phineas feel nearly

Phineas have not the slight desire to quarrel with his chief; but
he do think it to be not improbable that his chief would quarrel

a week pass after he have see Warburton before he by chance find

Phineas do promise, and think that he have succeed in master
one of the difficult passage in that book.

the Duchess and her friend
but the Duke, though he be by far too magnanimous to be angry with

soon after that the Duchess make up her mind that she would

but what be he to do? he know, without be tell by the Duchess,

CHAPTER LXIV
the New K.G.
early in June have die the Marquis of Mount Fidgett. in all England
there be no old family than that of the Fichy Fidgetts, whose
baronial castle of Fichy Fellows be still keep up, the glory of
archaeologist and the charm of tourist. some people declare it to
be the most perfect castle residence in the country. it be admit
to have be complete in the time of Edward VI, and be think to
have be commence in the day of Edward I. it have always belong
to the Fichy Fidgett family, who with a persistence that be become
rare every day, have clung to every acre that it ever own, and have
add acre to acre in every age. the consequence have be that the
exist Marquis of Mount Fidgett have always be possess of great
territorial influence, and have be flatter, cajole, and revere
by one Prime Minister after another. now the late Marquis have be,
as be the custom with the Fichy Fidgetts, a man of pleasure. if the
truth may be speak openly, it should be admit that he have be
a man of sin. the duty of keep together the family property he
have perform with a perfect zeal. it have always be acknowledge

spend his money, however base might be the gully into which his
wealth descend, he never spend more than he have to spend. perhaps

get beyond his enormous income unless he have throw it away on

family have not be an honour to the country, but have nevertheless

be as selfish and as sensual a brute as have ever disgrace
humanity;--but nevertheless he have be a Knight of the Garter. he
have be possess of considerable parliamentary interest, and the

past be Knights of the Garter. on the last occasion a good deal have
be say about it. a feeling have even then begin to prevail that
the high personal honour in the gift of the Crown should not be
bestow upon a man whose whole life be a disgrace, and who do

could inflict. he have a large family, but they be all illegitimate.
wife generally he like, but of his own wife he very soon break

inhabit by the Marquis. and yet he have become a Knight of the
Garter, and be therefore, presumably, one of those noble englishman
to whom the majesty of the day be willing to confide the honour,
and glory, and safety of the Crown. there be many who dislike
this. that a base reprobate should become a Marquis and a peer of
Parliament be in accordance with the constitution of the country.
marquis and peer be not as a rule reprobate, and the misfortune
be one which could not be avoid. he might have ill-use his own

have know the value of the man's support, and, be thick-skin,
have live through the reproach utter without much damage to
himself. now the wicked Marquis be dead, and it be the privilege
and the duty of the Duke of Omnium to select another Knight.
there be a good deal say about it at the time. there be a
rumour,--no doubt a false rumour,--that the Crown insist in this
instance on dictate a choice to the Duke of Omnium. but even be

choice dictate be suppose to be that of himself. the late Duke
have be a Knight, and when he have die, it be think that his
successor would succeed also to the ribbon. the new Duke have be at
that time in the Cabinet, and have remain there, but have accept
an office inferior in rank to that which he have formerly fill.
the whole history of these thing have be write, and may be read

reference to her husband's rank, have instigate he to demand the
ribbon as his right. this he have not only decline to do, but have

very general feeling that he have be pass over because his easy

the Crown interfere or not,--a matter on which no one short of
a writer of newspaper article dare to make a suggestion till
time shall have make mellow the doing of sovereign and their
ministers,--the suggestion be make. the Duke of St. Bungay venture
to say to his friend that no other selection be possible.
"recommend her Majesty to give it to myself!" say the Prime
Minister.
"you will find it to be her Majesty's wish. it have be very common.
Sir Robert Walpole have it."
"I be not Sir Robert Walpole." the Duke name other example of Prime

the subject,--but at last it become clear, not only to he, but to
the member of the Cabinet generally, and then to the outside world,
that the Prime Minister would not consent to accept the vacant

for nearly a month after this the question subside. a Minister be
not bind to bestow a Garter the day after it become vacant. there
be other Knights to guard the throne, and one may be spar for
a short interval. but during that interval many eye be turn

"he be the Marquis of Mount Fidgett, and next to yourself, perhaps,
the rich peer of Great Britain."
"have rich anything to do with it?"
"something certainly. you would not name a pauper peer."
"yes;--if he be a man whose career have be highly honourable to
the country. such a man, of course, could not be a pauper, but I do
not think his want of wealth should stand in the way of his being
honour by the Garter."
"Wealth, rank, and territorial influence have be generally think
to have something to do with it."
"and character nothing!"
"my dear Duke, I have not say so."
"something very much like it, my friend, if you advocate the claim of
the Marquis of Mount Fidgett. do you approve of the selection of the
late Marquis?"
"I be in the Cabinet at the time, and will therefore say nothing
against it. but I have never hear anything against this man's
character."
"nor in favour of it. to my thinking he have as much claim, and no
more, as that man who just open the door. he be never see in the
Lower House."
"surely that cannot signify."
"you think, then, that he should have it?"
"you know what I think," say the eld statesman thoughtfully.
"in my opinion there be no doubt that you would well consult the
honour of the country by allow her Majesty to bestow this act of
grace upon a subject who have deserve so well from her Majesty as
yourself."
"it be quite impossible."
"it seem to I," say the Duke, not appear to notice the refusal
of his friend, "that in this peculiar position you should allow
yourself to be persuade to lay aside your own feeling. no man of
high character be desirous of securing to himself decoration which
he may bestow upon other."
"just so."
"but here the decoration bestow upon the chief whom we all follow,
would confer a wide honour upon many than it could do if give to
any one else."
"the same may be say of any Prime Minister."
"not so. a commoner, without high permanent rank or large fortune, be
not lower in the world's esteem by not be of the order. you will
permit I to say--that a Duke of Omnium have not reach that position
which he ought to enjoy unless he be a Knight of the Garter." it
must be bear in mind that the old Duke, who use this argument, have
himself wear the ribbon for the last thirty year. "but if--"
"Well;--well."
"but if you are,--I must call it obstinate."
"I be obstinate in that respect."
"then," say the Duke of St. Bungay, "I should recommend her Majesty
to give it to the Marquis."
"never," say the Prime Minister, with very unaccustomed energy. "I
will never sanction the payment of such a price for service which
should never be buy or sell."
"it would give no offence."
"that be not enough, my friend. here be a man of whom I only know
that he have buy a great many marble statue. he have do nothing
for his country, and nothing for his sovereign."
"if you be determined to look to what you call desert alone, I would
name Lord Drummond." the Prime Minister frown and look unhappy.
it be quite true that Lord Drummond have contradict he, and that
he have feel the injury grievously. "Lord Drummond have be very true
to we."
"yes;--true to we! what be that?"
"he be in every respect a man of character, and well look upon
in the country. there would be some enmity and a good deal of
envy--which might be avoid by either of the course I have
propose; but those course you will not take. I take it for grant
that you be anxious to secure the support of those who generally act
with Lord Drummond."
"I donot know that I be." the old Duke shrug his shoulder. "what
I mean be, that I do not think that we ought to pay an increase
price for their support. his lordship be very well as the Head of
an Office; but he be not nearly so great a man as my friend Lord
Cantrip."
"cantrip would not join we. there be no evil in politic so great
as that of seem to buy the man who will not come without buy.
these reward be fairly give for political support."
"I have not, in truth, think of Lord Cantrip."
"he do not expect it any more than my butler."
"I only name he as have a claim strong than any that Lord
Drummond can put forward. I have a man in my mind to whom I think
such an honour be fairly due. what do you say to Lord Earlybird?"
the old Duke open his mouth and lift up his hand in unaffected
surprise.
the Earl of Earlybird be an old man of a very peculiar character. he
have never open his mouth in the House of Lords and have never sit
in the House of Commons. the political world know he not at all. he
have a house in town, but very rarely live there. early Park, in the
parish of Bird, have be his residence since he first come to the
title forty year ago, and have be the scene of all his labour.
he be a nobleman possess of a moderate fortune, and, as man say
of he, of a moderate intellect. he have marry early in life and
be bless with a large family. but he have certainly not be an
idle man. for nearly half a century he have devote himself to the
improvement of the labour class, especially in reference to
