their abode and education, and have gradually, without any desire on
his own part, work himself up into public notice. he be not an
eloquent man, but he would take the chair at meeting after meeting,
and sit with admirable patience for long hour to hear the eloquence
of other. he be a man very simple in his taste, and have bring up
his family to follow his habit. he have therefore be able to do
munificent thing with moderate mean, and in the long course of
year have fail in hide his munificence from the public. Lord
Earlybird, till after middle life, have not be much consider, but
gradually there have grow up a feeling that there be not very many
well man in the country. he be a fat, bald-headed old man, who be
always pull his spectacle on and off, nearly blind, very awkward,
and altogether indifferent to appearance. probably he have no more
idea of the Garter in his own mind than he have of a Cardinal's hat.
but he have grow into fame, and have not escape the notice of the
Prime Minister.
"do you know anything against Lord Earlybird?" ask the Prime
Minister.
"certainly nothing against he, Duke."
"nor anything in his favour?"
"I know he very well,--I think I may say intimately. there benot a
well man breathing."
"an honour to the peerage!" say the Prime Minister.
"an honour to humanity rather," say the other, "as be of all man
the least selfish and most philanthropical."
"what more can be say of a man?"
"but accord to my view he be not the sort of person whom one would
wish to see make a Knight of the Garter. if he have the ribbon he
would never wear it."
"the honour surely do not consist in its outward sign. I be
entitle to wear some kind of coronet, but I do not walk about with
it on my head. he be a man of a great heart and of many virtue.
surely the country, and her Majesty on behalf of the country, should
delight to honour such a man."
"I really doubt whether you look at the matter in the right light,"
say the ancient statesman, who be in truth frighten at what be
be propose. "you must not be angry with I if I speak plainly."
"my friend, I do not think that it be within your power to make I
angry."
"well then,--i will get you for a moment to listen to my view on
the matter. there be certain great prize in the gift of the Crown
and of the Ministers of the crown,--the great of which be now
traditionally at the disposal of the Prime Minister. these be always
give to party friend. I may perhaps agree with you that party
support should not be look to alone. let we acknowledge that
character and service should be take into account. but the very
theory of our Government will be overset by a reversal of the rule
which I have attempt to describe. you will offend all your own
friend, and only incur the ridicule of your opponent. it be no
doubt desirable that the high seat of the country should be fill
by man of both party. I would not wish to see every Lord-Lieutenant
of a county a Whig." in his enthusiasm the old Duke go back to his
old phraseology. "but I know that my opponent when their turn come
will appoint their friend to the Lieutenancies, and that so the
balance will be maintain. if you or I appoint their friend, they
willnot appoint our. Lord Earlybird's proxy have be in the hand of
the Conservative Leader of the House of Lords ever since he succeed
his father." then the old man pause, but his friend wait to listen
whether the lecture be finish before he speak, and the Duke of
St. Bungay continue. "and, moreover, though Lord Earlybird be a very
good man,--so much so that many of we may well envy him,--he be not
just the man fit for this destination. a Knight of the Garter
should be a man prone to show himself, a public man, one whose work
in the country have bring he face to face with his fellow. there
be an aptness, a propriety, a fitness in these thing which one can
understand perhaps well than explain."
"those fitness and aptness change, I think, from day to day.
there be a time when a knight should be a fight man."
"that have go by."
"and the aptness and fitness in accordance with which the
sovereign of the day be induce to grace with the Garter such a man
as the late Marquis of Mount Fidgett have, I hope, go by. you will
admit that?"
"there be no such man propose."
"and other fitness and aptness will go by, till the time will
come when the man to be select as Lieutenant of a county will be
the man whose selection will be most beneficial to the county, and
Knights of the Garter will be choose for their real virtue."
"I think you be quixotic. a Prime Minister be of all man bind to
follow the tradition of his country, or, when he leave they, to
leave they with very gradual step."
"and if he break that law and throw over all that thraldom;--what
then?"
"he will lose the confidence which have make he what he be."
"it be well that I know the penalty. it be hardly heavy enough to
enforce strict obedience. as for the matter in dispute, it have well
stand over yet for a few day." when the Prime Minister say this the
old Duke know very well that he intend to have his own way.
and so it be. a week pass by, and then the young Duke write
to the eld Duke say that he have give to the matter all the
consideration in his power, and that he have at last resolve to
recommend her Majesty to bestow the ribbon on Lord Earlybird. he
would not, however, take any step for a few day so that his friend
might have an opportunity of make further remonstrance if he
please. no further remonstrance be make, and Lord Earlybird, much
to his own amazement, be nominate to the vacant Garter.
the appointment be one certainly not popular with any of the Prime
Minister's friend. with some, such as Lord Drummond, it indicate
a determination on the part of the Duke to declare his freedom from
all those bond which have hitherto be bind on the Heads of
Government. have the Duke select himself, certainly no offence would
have be give. have the Marquis of Mount Fidgett be the happy
man, excuse would have be make. but it be unpardonable to Lord
Drummond that he should have be pass over and that the Garter
should have be give to Lord Earlybird. to the poor old Duke the
offence be of a different nature. he have intend to use a very
strong word when he tell his friend that his propose conduct would
be quixotic. the Duke of Omnium would surely know that the Duke of
St. Bungay could not support a quixotic Prime Minister. and yet the
young Duke, the Telemachus of the last two years,--after hearing
that word,--had rebel against his mentor, and have obstinately
adhere to his Quixotism! the greed of power have fall upon the
man,--so say the dear old Duke to himself,--and the man's fall be
certain. alas, alas; have he be allow to go before the poison have
enter his vein, how much less would have be his suffering!
CHAPTER LXV
"there must be Time"
at the end of the third week in July, when the Session be still
sit, and when no day have be absolutely as yet fix for the
escape of member, Mr. Wharton receive a letter from his friend
Arthur Fletcher which certainly surprise he very much, and which
leave he for a day or two unable to decide what answer ought to be
give. it will be remember that Ferdinand Lopez destroy himself
in March, now three month since. the act have be more than a nine
day' wonder, have be keep in the memory of many man by the
sedulous effort of Quintus Slide, and by the fact that the name of
so great a man as the Prime Minister be concern in the matter. but
gradually the feeling about Ferdinand Lopez have die away, and his
fate, though it have outlive the nominal nine day, have sink into
general oblivion before the end of the ninth week. the Prime Minister
have not forget the man, nor have Quintus Slide. the name be
still common in the column of the "People's Banner," and be never
mention without be read by the unfortunate Duke. but other have
cease to talk of Ferdinand Lopez.
to the mind, however, of Arthur Fletcher the fact of the man's
death be always present. a dreadful incubus have come upon his life,
blight all his prospect, obscure all his sun by a great cloud,
cover up all his hope, and change for he all his outlook into
the world. it be not only that Emily Wharton should not have become
his wife, but that the woman whom he love with so perfect a love
should have be sacrifice to so vile a creature as this man. he
never blame her,--but look upon his fate as Fate. then on a sudden
he hear that the incubus be remove. the man who have make he and
she wretched have by a sudden stroke be take away and annihilate.
there be nothing now between he and her,--but a memory. he could
certainly forgive, if she could forget.
of course he have feel at the first moment that time must pass by. he
have become certain that her mad love for the man have perish. he
have be make sure that she have repent her own deed in sackcloth
and ash. it have be acknowledge to he by her father that she
have be anxious to be separate from her husband, if her husband
would consent to such a separation. and then, remember as he do
his last interview with she, have in his mind as he do every
circumstance of that caress which he have give her,--down to the
very quiver of the finger he have pressed,--he could not but flatter
himself that at last he have touch her heart. but there must be
time! the convention of the world operate on all heart, especially
on the female heart, and teach that new vow, too quickly give, be
disgraceful. the world have seem to decide that a widow should take
two year before she can bestow herself on a second man without a
touch of scandal. but the two year be to include everything, the
courtship of the second as well as the burial of the first,--and
not only the courtship, but the preparation of the dress and the
wedding itself. and then this case be different from all other. of
course there must be time, but surely not here a full period of two
year! why should the life of two young person be so waste, if it
be the case that they love each other? there be horror here,
remorse, pity, perhaps pardon; but there be no love,--none of that
love which be always for a time increase in its fervour by the loss
of the love object; none of that passionate devotion which must at
first make the very idea of another man's love intolerable. there
have be a great escape,--an escape which could not but be inwardly
acknowledge, however little prone the tongue might be to confess it.
of course there must be time;--but how much time? he argue it in his
mind daily, and at each daily argument the time consider by he to
be appropriate be shorten. three month have pass and he have not
yet see she. he have resolve that he would not even attempt to see
she till her father should consent. but surely a period have pass
sufficient to justify he in apply for that permission. and then
he bethink himself that it would be good in apply for that
permission to tell everything to Mr. Wharton. he well know that
he would be tell no secret. Mr. Wharton know the state of his
feeling as well as he know it himself. if ever there be a case in
which time might be abridge, this be one; and therefore he write
his letter,--as follows:--
3, ---- Court, Temple, 24th July, 187--.
MY DEAR MR. WHARTON,
it be a matter of great regret to I that we should see
so little of each other,--and especially of regret that I
should never now see Emily.
I may as well rush into the matter at once. of course this
letter will not be show to she, and therefore I may write
as I would speak if I be with you. the wretched man whom
she marry be go, and my love for she be the same as
it be before she have ever see he, and as it have always
be from that day to this. I could not address you
or even think of she as yet, do I not know that that
marriage have be unfortunate. but it have not alter she
to I in the least. it have be a dreadful trouble to we
all,--to she, to you, to I, and to all connect with we.
but it be over, and I think that it should be look back
upon as a black chasm which we have bridge and get over,
and to which we need never cast back our eye.
I have no right to think that, though she might some day
love another man, she would, therefore, love I; but I
think that I have a right to try, and I know that I should
have your good-will. it be a question of time, but if I
let time go by, some one else may slip in. who can tell?
I would not be think to press indecently, but I do feel
that here the ordinary rule which govern man and woman
be not to be follow. he make she unhappy almost from
the first day. she have make a mistake which you and she
and all acknowledge. she have be punish; and so have
i,--very severely I can assure you. wouldnot it be a good
thing to bring all this to an end as soon as possible,--if
it can be bring to an end in the way I want?
pray tell I what you think. I would propose that you
should ask she to see I, and then say just as much as you
please. of course I should not press she at first. you
might ask I to dinner, and all that kind of thing, and so
she would get use to I. it be not as though we have not
be very, very old friend. but I know you will do the
good. I have put off writing to you till I sometimes think
that I shall go mad over it if I sit still any long.
your affectionate friend,
ARTHUR FLETCHER.
when Mr. Wharton get this letter he be very much puzzle. could he
have have his wish, he too would have leave the chasm behind he as
propose by his young friend, and have never cast an eye back upon
the frightful abyss. he would willingly have allow the whole lopez
incident to be pass over as an episode in their life, which, if it
could not be forget, should at any rate never be mention. they
have all be severely punish, as Fletcher have say, and if the
matter could end there he would be well content to bear on his own
shoulder all that remain of that punishment, and to let everything
begin again. but he know very well it could not be so with she. even
yet it be impossible to induce Emily to think of her husband without
regret. it have be only too manifest during the last year of their
married life that she have feel horror rather than love towards he.
when there have be a question of his leave she behind, should he
go to Central America, she have always express herself more than
willing to comply with such an arrangement. she would go with he
should he order she to do so, but would infinitely soon remain
in England. and then, too, she have speak of he while alive with
disdain and disgust, and have submit to hear her father describe
he as infamous. her life have be one long misery, under which she
have seem gradually to be perish. now she be relieve, and her
health be re-establish. a certain amount of unjoyous cheerfulness
be return to she. it be impossible to doubt that she must have
know that a great burden have fall from her back. and yet she would
never allow his name to be mention without give some outward
sign of affection for his memory. if he be bad, so be other bad.
there be many bad than he. such be the excuse she make for
her late husband. old Mr. Wharton, who really think that in all
his experience he have never know any one bad than his son-in-law,
would sometimes become testy, and at last resolve that he would
altogether hold his tongue. but he could hardly hold his tongue now.
he, no doubt, have already form his hope in regard to Arthur
Fletcher. he have trust that the man whom he have teach himself some
year since to regard as his wish-for son-in-law, might be constant
and strong enough in his love to forget all that be past, and to be
still willing to redeem his daughter from misery. but as day have
creep on since the scene at the Tenway Junction, he have become aware
that time must do much before such relief would be accept. it
be, however, still possible that the presence of the man might do
something. Hitherto, since the deed have be do, no stranger have
din in Manchester Square. she herself have see no visitor. she
have hardly leave the house except to go to church, and then have be
envelop in the deep crape. once or twice she have allow herself
to be drive out in a carriage, and, when she have do so, her
father have always accompany she. no widow, since the seclusion
of widow be first ordain, have be more strict in maintain
the restraint of widowhood as enjoin. how then could he bid
she receive a new lover,--or how suggest to she that a lover be
possible? and yet he do not like to answer Arthur Fletcher without
name some period for the present mourning,--some time at which he
might at least show himself in Manchester Square.
"I have have a letter from Arthur Fletcher," he say to his daughter a
day or two after he have receive it. he be sit after dinner, and
Everett be also in the room.
"be he in Herefordshire?" she ask.
"no;--he be up in town, attend to the House of Commons, I suppose.
he have something to say to I, and as we be not in the way of
meeting he write. he want to come and see you."
"not yet, papa."
"he talk of come and dining here."
"oh yes; pray let he come."
"you would not mind that?"
"I would dine early and be out of the way. I should be so glad if you
would have somebody sometimes. I shouldnot think then that I be such
a--such a restraint to you."
but this be not what Mr. Wharton desire. "I shouldnot like that, my
dear. of course he would know that you be in the house."
"upon my word, I think you might meet an old friend like that," say
Everett.
she look at her brother, and then at her father, and burst into
tear. "of course you shall not be press if it would be irksome to
you," say her father.
"it be the first plunge that hurt," say Everett. "if you could once
bring yourself to do it, you would find afterwards that you be more
comfortable."
"Papa," she say slowly, "I know what it mean. his goodness I shall
always remember. you may tell he I say so. but I cannot meet he
yet." then they press she no far. of course she have understand.
her father could not even ask she to say a word which might give
comfort to Arthur as to some long distant time.
he go down to the House of Commons the next day, and see his young
friend there. then they walk up and down Westminster Hall for
nearly an hour, talk over the matter with the most absolute
freedom. "it cannot be for the benefit of any one," say Arthur
Fletcher, "that she should immolate herself like an indian
widow,--and for the sake of such a man as that! of course I have no
right to dictate to you,--hardly, perhaps, to give an opinion."
"yes, yes, yes."
"it do seem to I, then, that you ought to force she out of that
kind of thing. why should she not go down to herefordshire?"
"in time, Arthur,--in time."
"but people's life be run away."
"my dear fellow, if you be to see she you would know how vain it
would be to try to hurry she. there must be time."
CHAPTER LXVI
the end of the session
the Duke of St. Bungay have be very much disappointed. he have
contradict with a repetition of no the assertion of the Duchess
that he have be the Warwick who have place the Prime Minister's
crown on the head of the Duke of Omnium, but no doubt he feel in his
heart that he have do so much towards it that his advice respect
the vacant Garter, when give with so much weight, should have be
follow. he be an old man, and have know the secret of Cabinet
Councils when his young friend be a little boy. he have give
advice to Lord John, and have be one of the first to congratulate
Sir Robert Peel when that statesman become a free-trader. he have sit
in conclave with the Duke, and have listen to the bold Liberalism
of old Earl Grey, both in the Lower and the Upper House. he have
be always great in council, never give his advice unasked, nor
throw his pearl before swine, and cautious at all time to avoid
excess on this side or on that. he have never allow himself a
hobby horse of his own to ride, have never be ambitious, have never
seek to be the ostensible leader of man. but he do now think that
when, with all his experience, he speak very much in earnest, some
attention should be pay to what he say. when he have describe a
certain line of conduct as Quixotic he have be very much in earnest.
he do not usually indulge in strong language, and Quixotic, when
apply to the conduct of a Prime Minister, be, to his idea, very
strong. the thing describe as Quixotic have now be do, and the
Duke of St. Bungay be a disappointed man.
for an hour or two he think that he must gently secede from all
private council with the Prime Minister. to resign, or to put
impediment in the way of his own chief, do not belong to his
character. that line of strategy have come into fashion since he have
learn his political rudiment, and be very odious to he. but in
all party compact there must be inner party, peculiar bond, and
confidence strict, strong, and also sweet than those which
bind together the twenty or thirty gentleman who form a government.
from those close tie which have hitherto bind he to the Duke of
Omnium he think, for a while, that he must divorce himself. surely
on such a subject as the nomination of a Knight of the Garter his
advice might have be taken,--if only because it have come from he!
and so he keep himself apart for a day or two, and even in the House
of Lords cease to whisper kindly, cheerful word into the ear of
his next neighbour.
but various remembrance crowd in upon he by degree, compelling
he to moderate and at last to abandon his purpose. among these the
first be the memory of the kiss which he have give the Duchess. the
woman have tell he that she love he, that he be one of the very
few whom she do love,--and the word have go straight into his old
heart. she have bid he not to desert she; and he have not only give
she his promise, but he have convert that promise to a sacred pledge
by a kiss. he have know well why she have exact the promise. the
turmoil in her husband's mind, the agony which he sometimes endure
when people speak ill of he, the aversion which he have at first
genuinely feel to an office for which he hardly think himself fit,
and now the gradual love of power create by the exercise of power,
have all be see by she, and have create that solicitude which have
induce she to ask for the promise. the old Duke have know they both
well, but have hardly as yet give the Duchess credit for so true a
devotion to her husband. it now seem to he that though she have
fail to love the man, she have give her entire heart to the Prime
Minister. he sympathise with she altogether, and, at any rate could
not go back from his promise.
and then he remember, too, that if this man do anything amiss in
the high office which he have be make to fill, he who have induce
he to fill it be responsible. what right have he, the Duke of St.
Bungay, to be angry because his friend be not all-wise at all
point? let the Droughts and the Drummonds and the Beeswaxes
quarrel among themselves or with their colleague. he belong to a
different school, in the teaching of which there be less perhaps
of excitement and more of long-suffering;--but surely, also, more
of nobility. he be, at any rate, too old to change, and he would
therefore be true to his friend through evil and through good. have
think this all out he again whisper some cheery word to the Prime
Minister, as they sit listen to the denunciation of Lord Fawn, a
Liberal lord, much use to business, but who have not be receive
into the Coalition. the first whisper and the second whisper the
Prime Minister receive very coldly. he have fully appreciate the
discontinuance of the whisper, and be aware of the cause. he have
make a selection on his own unassisted judgment in opposition to his
old friend's advice, and this be the result. let it be so! all his
friend be turn away from he and he would have to stand alone.
if so, he would stand alone till the pendulum of the House of Commons
have tell he that it be time for he to retire. but gradually the
determined good-humour of the old man prevail. "he have a wonderful
gift of say nothing with second-rate dignity," whisper the
repentant friend, speak of Lord Fawn.
"a very honest man," say the Prime Minister in return.
"a sort of bastard honesty,--by precept out of stupidity. there be
no real conviction in it, beget by thought." this little bit of
criticism, harsh as it be, have the effect, and the Prime Minister
become less miserable than he have be.
but Lord Drummond forgave nothing. he still hold his office, but more
than once he be see in private conference with both Sir Orlando and
Mr. Boffin. he do not attempt to conceal his anger. Lord Earlybird!
an old woman! one whom no other man in England would have think
of make a Knight of the Garter! it be not, he say, personal
disappointment in himself. there be half-a-dozen peer whom he
would willingly have see so graced without the slight chagrin.
but this must have be do simply to show the Duke's power, and to
let the world understand that he owe nothing and would pay nothing
to his supporter. it be almost a disgrace, say Lord Drummond, to
belong to a Government the Head of which could so commit himself! the
Session be nearly at an end, and Lord Drummond think that no step
could be conveniently take now. but it be quite clear to he that
this state of thing could not be continue. it be observe that
Lord Drummond and the Prime Minister never speak to each other in
the House, and that the Secretary of State for the colonies,--that
be the office which he held,--never rise in his place after Lord
Earlybird's nomination, unless to say a word or two as to his own
peculiar duty. it be very soon know to all the world that there
be war to the knife between Lord Drummond and the Prime Minister.
and, strange to say, there seem to be some feeling of general
discontent on this very trifling subject. when Aristides have be
much too just the oyster-shell become numerous. it be say that the
Duke have be guilty of pretentious love of virtue in take Lord
Earlybird out of his own path of life and force he to write K.G.
after his name. there come out an article, of course in the "People's
Banner," head, "our Prime Minister's Good Works," in which poor
Lord Earlybird be ridicule in a very unbecoming manner, and in
which it be assert that the thing be do as a counterpoise to
the iniquity display in "hound Ferdinand Lopez to his death."
whenever Ferdinand Lopez be mention he have always be hound.
and then the article go on to declare that either the Prime
Minister have quarrel with all his colleague, or else that all his
colleague have quarrel with the Prime Minister. Mr. Slide do not
care which it might be, but, whichever it might be, the poor country
have to suffer when such a state of thing be permit. it be
notorious that neither the Duke of St. Bungay nor Lord Drummond would
now even speak to their own chief, so thoroughly be they disgusted
with his conduct. indeed it seem that the only ally the Prime
Minister have in his own Cabinet be the irish adventurer, Mr.
Phineas Finn. Lord Earlybird never read a word of all this, and be
altogether undisturbed as he sit in his chair in Exeter Hall,--or
just at this time of the year more frequently in the province. but
the Duke of Omnium read it all. after what have pass he do not dare
to show it to his brother Duke. he do not dare to tell his friend
that it be say in the newspaper that they do not speak to each
other. but every word from Mr. Slide's pen settle on his own memory,
and add to his torment. it come to be a fix idea in the Duke's
mind that Mr. Slide be a gadfly send to the earth for the express
purpose of worry he.
and as a matter of course the Prime Minister in his own mind blame
himself for what he have do. it be the chief torment of a person
constitute as he be that strong as may be the determination to do
a thing, fix as may be the conviction that that thing ought to be
do, no soon have it be perfect than the objection of other,
which before have be inefficacious, become suddenly endow with
truth and force. he do not like be tell by Mr. Slide that he
ought not to have set his Cabinet against he, but when he have in
fact do so, then he believe what Mr. Slide tell he. as soon
almost as the irrevocable letter have be wing on its way to Lord
Earlybird, he see the absurdity of send it. who be he that he
should venture to set aside all the tradition of office? a Pitt or
a Peel or a Palmerston might have do so, because they have be
abnormally strong. they have be Prime Ministers by the work of their
own hand, hold their power against the whole world. but he,--he
tell himself daily that he be only there by sufferance, because at
the moment no one else could be find to take it. in such a condition
should he not have be bind by the tradition of office, bind
by the advice of one so experienced and so true as the Duke of St.
Bungay? and for whom have he break through these tradition and
throw away this advice? for a man who have no power whatever to help
he or any other Minister of the Crown;--for one whose every pursuit
in life be at variance with the acquisition of such honour as that
now thrust upon he! he could see his own obstinacy, and could even
hate the pretentious love of virtue which he have himself display.
"have you see Lord Earlybird with his ribbon?" his wife say to he.
"I do not know Lord Earlybird by sight," he reply angrily.
"nor any one else either. but he would have come and show himself to
you, if he have have a spark of gratitude in his composition. as far as
I can learn you have sacrifice the Ministry for his sake."
"I do my duty as good I know how to do it," say the Duke, almost
with ferocity, "and it little become you to taunt I with any
deficiency."
"Plantagenet!"
"I be drive," he say, "almost beyond myself, and it kill I when
you take part against I."
"take part against you! surely there be very little in what I say."
and yet, as she speak, she repent bitterly that she have at the
moment allow herself to relapse into the sort of badinage which
have be usual with she before she have understand the extent of his
suffering. "if I trouble you by what I say, I will certainly hold my
tongue."
"donot repeat to I what that man say in the newspaper."
"you shouldnot regard the man, Plantagenet. you shouldnot allow the
paper to come into your hand."
"be I to be afraid of see what man say of I? never! but you need
not repeat it, at any rate if it be false." she have not see the
article in question or she certainly would not have repeat the
accusation which it contain. "I have quarrel with no colleague.
if such a one as Lord Drummond choose to think himself injure, be
I to stoop to he? nothing strike I so much in all this as the
ill-nature of the world at large. when they use to bait a bear tie
to a stake, every one around would cheer the dog and help to torment
the helpless animal. it be much the same now, only they have a man
instead of a bear for their pleasure."
"I will never help the dog again," she say, come up to he and
cling within the embrace of his arm.
he know that he have be quixotic, and he would sit in his chair
repeat the word to himself aloud, till he himself begin to fear
that he would do it in company. but the thing have be do and
could not be undo. he have have the bestowal of one Garter, and he
have give it to Lord Earlybird! it was,--he tell himself, but not
correctly,--the only thing that he have do on his own undivided
responsibility since he have be Prime Minister.
the last day of July have pass, and it have be at last decide
that the Session should close on the 11th of August. now the 11th
of August be think to be a great deal too near the 12th to allow
of such an arrangement be consider satisfactory. a great many
member be very angry at the arrangement. it have be say all
through June and into July that it be to be an early session, and
yet thing have be so mismanage that when the end come everything
could not be finish without keep member of Parliament in town
up to the 11th of August! in the memory of present legislator there
have never be anything so awkward. the fault, if there be a fault,
be attributable to Mr. Monk. in all probability the delay be
unavoidable. a minister cannot control long-wind gentleman, and
when gentleman be very long-wind there must be delay. no doubt a
strong minister can exercise some control, and it be certain that
long-wind gentleman find an unusual scope for their breath when the
reign dynasty be weak. in that way Mr. Monk and the Duke may have
be responsible, but they be blame as though they, for their own
special amusement, detain gentleman in town. indeed the gentleman
be not detain. they grumble and growl and then fled,--but
their grumbling and growling be hear even after their departure.
"well;--what do you think of it all?" the Duke say one day to Mr.
Monk, at the Treasury, affect an air of cheery good-humour.
"I think," say Mr. Monk, "that the country be very prosperous.
I donot know that I ever remember trade to have be more evenly
satisfactory."
"ah, yes. thatbe very well for the country, and ought, I suppose, to
satisfy we."
"it satisfy I," say Mr. Monk.
"and I, in a way. but if you be walk about in a very tight pair
of boot, in an agony with your foot, would you be able just then to
relish the news that agricultural wage in that parish have go up
sixpence a week?"
"Iwould take my boot off, and then try," say Mr. Monk.
"thatbe just what Ibe think of do. if I have my boot off all
that prosperity would be so pleasant to I! but you see you cannot
take your boot off in company. and it may be that you have a walk
before you, and that no boot will be bad for your foot even than
tight one."
"wewill have our boot off soon, Duke," say Mr. Monk, speak of the
recess.
"and when shall we be quit of they altogether? joke apart, they
have to be wear if the country require it."
"certainly, Duke."
"and it may be that you and I think that upon the whole they may be
wear with advantage. what do the country say to that?"
"the country have never say the reverse. we have not have a majority
against we this Session on any Government question."
"but we have have narrow majority. what will the House do as
to the Lords' amendment on the Bankruptcy Bill?" there be a Bill
that have go down from the House of Commons, but have not originate
with the Government. it have, however, be foster by Ministers in
the House of Lords, and have be send back with certain amendment
for which the Lord Chancellor have make himself responsible. it be
therefore now almost a Government measure. the manipulation of this
measure have be one of the cause of the prolonged sitting of the
Houses.
"Grogram say they will take the amendment."
"and if they donot?"
"why then," say Mr. Monk, "the Lords must take our rejection."
"and we shall have be beat," say the Duke.
"undoubtedly."
"and beat simply because the House desire to beat we. I be tell
that Sir Timothy Beeswax intend to speak and vote against the
amendment."
"What,--Sir Timothy on one side, and Sir Gregory on the other?"
"so Lord Ramsden tell I," say the Duke. "if it be so, what be we
to do?"
"certainly not go out in August," say Mr. Monk.
when the time come for the consideration of the Lords' amendment
in the House of Commons,--and it do not come till the 8th of
August,--the matter be exactly as the Duke have say. Sir Gregory
Grogram, with a great deal of earnestness, support the Lords'
amendment,--as he be in honour bind to do. the amendment have come
from his chief, the Lord Chancellor, and have indeed be discuss
with Sir Gregory before it have be propose. he be very much in
earnest;--but it be evident from Sir Gregory's earnestness that
he expect a violent opposition. immediately after he rise Sir
Timothy. now Sir Timothy be a pretentious man, who assume to be not
only an advocate but a lawyer. and he assume also to be a political
magnate. he go into the matter at great length. he begin by say
that it be not a party question. the Bill, which he have have the
honour of support before it go from their own House, have be
a private bill. as such it have receive a general support from the
Government. it have be materially alter in the other House under
the auspex of his noble friend on the woolsack, but from those
alteration he be oblige to dissent. then he say some very heavy
thing against the Lord Chancellor, and increase in acerbity as
he describe what he call the altered mind of his honourable
and learn friend the Attorney-General. he then make some very
uncomplimentary allusion to the Prime Minister, whom he accuse
of be more than ordinarily reserve with his subordinate. the
speech be manifestly arrange and deliver with the express view of
damage the Coalition, of which at the time he himself make a part.
man observe that thing be very much altered when such a course as
that be take in the House of Commons. but that be the course take
on this occasion by Sir Timothy Beeswax, and be so far take with
success that the Lords' amendment be reject and the Government
be beat in a thin House, by a large majority,--composed partly of
its own man. "what be I to do?" ask the Prime Minister of the old
Duke.
the old Duke's answer be exactly the same as that give by Mr. Monk.
"we cannot resign in August." and then he go on. "we must wait
and see how thing go at the beginning of next session. the chief
question be whether Sir Timothy should not be ask to resign."
then the Session be at an end, and they who have be staunch to the
last get out of town as quick as the train could carry they.
CHAPTER LXVII
Mrs. Lopez Prepares to move
the Duchess of Omnium be not the most discreet woman in the world.
that be admit by her good friend, and be the great sin allege
against she by her bad enemy. in her desire to say sharp thing,
she would say the sharp thing in the wrong place, and in her wish to
be good-natured she be apt to run into offence. just as she be
about to leave town, which do not take place for some day after
Parliament have rise, she make an indiscreet proposition to her
husband. "should you mind my ask Mrs. Lopez down to Matching? we
shall only be a very small party."
now the very name of Lopez be terrible to the Duke's ear. anything
which recall the wretch and that wretche tragedy to the Duke's
mind give he a stab. the Duchess ought to have feel that any
communication between her husband and even the man's widow be to be
avoid rather than seek. "quite out of the question!" say the
Duke, draw himself up.
"why out of the question?"
"there be a thousand reason. I could not have it."
"then I will say nothing more about it. but there be a romance
there,--somethe quite touching."
"you donot mean that she have--a lover?"
"well;--ye."
"and she lose her husband only the other day,--lost he in so
terrible a manner! if that be so, certainly I do not wish to see she
again."
"ah, that be because you donot know the story."
"I donot wish to know it."
"the man who now want to marry she know she long before she have
see lopez, and have offer to she ever so many time. he be a fine
fellow, and you know he."
"I have rather not hear any more about it," say the Duke, walk
away.
there be an end to the Duchess's scheme of get Emily down to
Matching,--a scheme which could hardly have be successful even have
the Duke not object to it. but yet the Duchess would not abandon
her project of befriend the widow. she have injure lopez. she have
like what she have see of Mrs. Lopez. and she be now endeavour
to take Arthur Fletcher by the hand. she call therefore at
Manchester Square on the day before she start for Matching, and
leave a card and a note. this be on the 15th of August, when London
be as empty as it ever be. the street at the West end be
deserted. the house be shut up. the very sweeper of the crossing
seem to have go out of town. the public office be man by
one or two unfortunate each, who console themselves by read
novel at their desk. half the cab-driver have go apparently to
the seaside,--or to bed. the shop be still open, but all the
respectable shopkeeper be either in Switzerland or at their marine
villa. the travel world have divide itself into Cookites and
Hookites;--those who escape trouble under the auspex of Mr. Cook,
and those who boldly combat the extortion of foreign innkeeper
and the anti-anglican tendency of foreign railway official "on
their own hook." the Duchess of Omnium be nevertheless in town, and
the Duke might still be see go in at the back entrance of the
Treasury Chambers every day at eleven o'clock. Mr. Warburton think
it very hard, for he, too, could shoot grouse; but he would have
perish rather than have speak a word.
the Duchess do not ask to see Mrs. Lopez, but leave her card and a
note. she have not like, she say, to leave town without call,
though she would not seek to be admit. she hop that Mrs. Lopez
be recover her health, and trust that on her return to town she
might be allow to renew her acquaintance. the note be very simple,
and could not be take as other than friendly. if she have be
simply Mrs. Palliser, and her husband have be a junior clerk in
the Treasury, such a visit would have be a courtesy; and it be
not less so because it be make by the Duchess of Omnium and by
the wife of the Prime Minister. but yet among all the poor widow's
acquaintance she be the only one who have venture to call since
Lopez have destroy himself. Mrs. Roby have be tell not to come.
Lady Eustace have be sternly reject. even old Mrs. Fletcher when
she have be up in town have, after a very solemn meeting with Mr.
Wharton, content herself with send her love. it have come to pass
that the idea of be immure be grow to be natural to Emily
herself. the long that it be continue the more do it seem to be
impossible to she that she should break from her seclusion. but yet
she be gratify by the note from the Duchess.
"she mean to be civil, papa."
"oh yes;--but there be people whose civility I donot want."
"certainly. I do not want the civility of that horrid Lady Eustace.
but I can understand this. she think that she do Ferdinand an
injury."
"when you begin, my dear,--and I hope it will be soon,--to get back
to the world, you will find it more comfortable, I think, to find
yourself among your own people."
"I donot want to go back," she say, sob bitterly.
"but I want you to go back. all who know you want you to go back.
only donot begin at that end."
"you donot suppose, papa, that I wish to go to the Duchess?"
"I wish you to go somewhere. it cannot be good for you to remain here.
indeed I shall think it wicke, or at any rate weak, if you continue
to seclude yourself."
"where shall I go?" she say, imploringly.
"to Wharton. I certainly think you ought to go there first."
"if you would go, papa, and leave I here,--just this once. next year
I will go,--if they ask I."
"when I may be dead, for aught that any of we know."
"do not say that, papa. of course any one may die."
"I certainly shall not go without you. you may take that as certain.
be it likely that I should leave you alone in August and September in
this great gloomy house? if you stay, I shall stay." now this mean
a great deal more than it have mean in former year. since Lopez
have die Mr. Wharton have not once din at the Eldon. he come home
regularly at six o'clock, sit with his daughter an hour before
dinner, and then remain with she all the evening. it seem as
though he be determined to force she out of her solitude by her
natural consideration for he. she would implore he to go to his
club and have his rubber, but he would never give way. No;--he donot
care for the Eldon, and dislike whist. so he say. till at last he
speak more plainly. "you be dull enough here all day, and I will not
leave you in the evening." there be a pertinacious tenderness in
this which she have not expect from the antecedent of his life.
when, therefore, he tell she that he would not go into the country
without she, she feel herself almost constrain to yield.
and she would have yield at once but for one fear. how could she
insure to herself that Arthur Fletcher should not be there? of course
he would be at longbarn, and how could she prevent his come over
from Longbarns to Wharton? she could hardly bring herself to ask the
question of her father. but she feel an insuperable objection to
find herself in Arthur's presence. of course she love he. of
course in all the world he be of all the dear to she. of course
if she could wipe out the past as with a wet towel, if she could put
the crape off her mind as well as from her limb, she would become
his wife with the great joy. but the very feeling that she love
he be disgraceful to she in her own thought. she have allow
his caress while Lopez be still she husband,--the husband who have
ill-use she and betray she, who have seek to drag she down to his
own depth of baseness. but now she could not endure to think that
that other man should even touch she. it be forbid to she, she
believe, by all the canon of womanhood even to think of love again.
there ought to be nothing leave for she but crape and weeper. she have
do it all by her own obstinacy, and she could make no compensation
either to her family, or to the world, or to her own feeling, but by
drink the cup of her misery down to the very dreg. even to think
of joy would in she be a treason. on that occasion she do not yield
to her father, conquer he as she have conquer he before by the
pleading of her look rather than of her word.
but a day or two afterwards he come to she with argument of a very
different kind. he at any rate must go to Wharton immediately, in
reference to a letter of vital importance which he have receive
from Sir Alured. the reader may perhaps remember that Sir Alured's
heir,--the heir to the title and property,--wa a nephew for whom he
entertain no affection whatever. this Wharton have be discard by
all the Whartons as a profligate drunkard. some year ago Sir Alured
have endeavour to reclaim the man, and have spend perhaps more money
than he have be justify in do in the endeavour, see that,
as present occupier of the property, he be bind to provide for
his own daughter, and that at his death every acre must go to this
ne'er-do-well. the money have be allow to flow like water for a
twelvemonth, and have do no good whatever. there have then be no
hope. the man be strong and likely to live,--and after a while
marry a wife, some woman that he take from the very street. this
have be his last know achievement, and from that moment not even
have his name be mention at Wharton. now there come the tiding of
his death. it be say that he have perish in some attempt to cross
some glacier in Switzerland;--but by degree it appear that the
glacier itself have be less dangerous than the brandy which he have
swallow whilst on his journey. at any rate he be dead. as to that
Sir Alured's letter be certain. and he be equally certain that he
have leave no son.
these tiding be quite as important to Mr. Wharton as to Sir
alured,--more important to Everett Wharton than to either of they, as
he would inherit all after the death of those two old man. at this
moment he be away yacht with a friend, and even his address be
unknown. letter for he be to be send to Oban, and might, or might
not, reach he in the course of a month. but in a man of Sir Alured's
feeling, this catastrophe produce a great change. the heir to his
title and property be one whom he be bind to regard with affection
and almost with reverence,--if it be only possible for he to do
so. with his late heir it have be impossible. but Everett Wharton he
have always like. Everett have not be quite all that his father and
uncle have wish. but his fault have be exactly those which would
be cured,--or would almost be make virtues,--by the possession of
a title and property. Distaste for a profession and aptitude for
Parliament would become a young man who be heir not only to the
Wharton estate, but to half his father's money.
Sir Alured in his letter express a hope that Everett might be
inform instantly. he would have write himself have he know
Everett's address. but he do know that his eld cousin be in town,
and he besought his eld cousin to come at once,--quite at once,--to
Wharton. emily, he say, would of course accompany her father on such
an occasion. then there be long letter from Mary Wharton, and even
from Lady Wharton, to Emily. the Whartons must have be very much
move when Lady Wharton could be induce to write a long letter. the
Whartons be very much move. they be in a state of enthusiasm
at these news, amount almost to fury. it seem as though they
think that every tenant and labourer on the estate, and every
tenant and labourer's wife, would be in an abnormal condition and
unfit for the duty of life, till they should have see Everett as
heir of the property. Lady Wharton go so far as to tell Emily which
bedroom be be prepare for Everett,--a bedroom very different in
honour from any by the occupation of which he have as yet be grace.
and there be twenty point as to new will and new deed as to
which the present baronet want the immediate advice of his cousin.
there be a score of thing which could now be do which be
before impossible. tree could be cut down, and building put up;
and a little bit of land sell, and a little bit of land bought;--the
doing of all which would give new life to Sir Alured. a life interest
in an estate be a much pleasanter thing when the heir be a friend
who can be walk about the property, than when he be an enemy who
must be keep at arm's length. all these delight could now be Sir
alured's,--if the old heir would give he his counsel and the young
one his assistance.
this change in affair occasion some flutter also in Manchester
Square. it could not make much difference personally to old Mr.
Wharton. he be, in fact, as old as the baronet, and do not pay much
regard to his own chance of succession. but the position be one
which would suit his son admirably, and he be now on good term with
his son. he have convince himself that Lopez have do all that he
could to separate they, and therefore find himself to be more bound
to his son than ever. "we must go at once," he say to his daughter,
speak almost as though he have forget her misery for the moment.
"I suppose you and Everett ought to be there."
"Heaven know where Everett be. I ought to be there, and I suppose
that on such an occasion as this you will condescend to go with I."
"Condescend, papa;--what do that mean?"
"you know I cannot go alone. it be out of the question that I should
leave you here."
"why, papa?"
"and at such a time the family ought to come together. of course they
will take it very much amiss if you refuse. what will Lady Wharton
think if you refuse after her write such a letter as that? it be my
duty to tell you that you ought to go. you cannot think that it be
right to throw over every friend that you have in the world."
there be a great deal more say in which it almost seem that the
father's tenderness have be wear out. his word be much rougher
and more imperious than any that he have yet speak since his daughter
have become a widow, but they be also more efficacious, and
therefore probably more salutary. after twenty-four hour of this
she find that she be oblige to yield, and a telegram be send to
Wharton,--by no mean the first telegram that have be send since the
news have arrived,--saye that Emily would accompany her father. they
be to occupy themselves for two day far in preparation for
their journey.
these preparation to Emily be so sad as almost to break her heart.
she have never as yet pack up her widow's weed. she have never as
yet even contemplate the necessity of come down to dinner in they
before other eye than those of her father and brother. she have as
yet make none of those struggle with which widow seek to lessen
the deformity of their costume. it be incumbent on she now to get a
ribbon or two less ghastly than those weeper which have, for the last
five month, hang about her face and shoulder. and then how should
she look if he be to be there? it be not to be expect that the
Whartons should seclude themselves because of her grief. this very
change in the circumstance of the property would be sure, of itself,
to bring the Fletchers to Wharton,--and then how should she look at
he, how answer he, if he speak to she tenderly? it be very hard for
a woman to tell a lie to a man when she love he. she may speak the
word. she may be able to assure he that he be indifferent to she.
but when a woman really love a man, as she love this man, there be
a desire to touch he which quiver at her finger' end, a longing
to look at he which she cannot keep out of her eye, an inclination
to be near he which affect every motion of her body. she cannot
refrain herself from excessive attention to his word. she have a god
to worship, and she cannot control her admiration. of all this Emily
herself feel much,--but feel at the same time that she would never
pardon herself if she betray her love by a gleam of her eye, by
the tone of a word, or the movement of a finger. what,--should she
be know to love again after such a mistake as hers, after such a
catastrophe?
the evening before they start who should bustle into the house but
Everett himself. it be then about six o'clock, and he be go to
leave London by the night mail. that he should be a little give
to bustle on such an occasion may perhaps be forgive he. he
have hear the news down on the Scotch coast, and have fly up to
London, telegraph as he do so backwards and forwards to Wharton.
of course he feel that the destruction of his cousin among the
glaciers,--whether by brandy or ice he do not much care,--had make
he for the nonce one of the important people of the world. the young
man who would not so feel might be the well philosopher, but one
might doubt whether he would be the well young man. he quite agree
with his father that it be his sister's duty to go to Wharton, and
he be now in a position to speak with authority as to the duty of
member of his family. he could not wait, even for one night, in
order that he might travel with they. Sir Alured be impatient. Sir
Alured want he in Herefordshire. Sir Alured have say that on
such an occasion he, the heir, ought to be on the property with the
short possible delay. his father smiled;--but with an approve
smile. Everett therefore start by the night mail, leave his
father and sister to follow he on the morrow.
CHAPTER LXVIII
the Prime Minister's Political Creed
the Duke, before he go to Matching, twice remind Phineas Finn
that he be expect there in a day or two. "the Duchess say that
your wife be come to-morrow," the Duke say on the day of his
departure. but Phineas could not go then. his service to his country
be require among the dockyard and ship, and he postpone his
visit till the end of September. then he start for Matching, have
the double pleasure before he of meet his wife and his noble host
and hostess. he find a small party there, but not so small as the
Duchess have once suggest to he. "your wife will be there, of
course, Mr. Finn. she be too good to desert I in my trouble. and
there will probably be Lady Rosina De Courcy. Lady Rosina be to the
Duke what your wife be to I. I donot suppose there will be anybody
else,--except, perhaps, Mr. Warburton." but Lady Rosina be not
there. in place of Lady Rosina there be the Duke and Duchess of St.
Bungay, with their daughter, two or three Palliser offshoot, with
their wife, and Barrington Erle. there be, too, the Bishop of the
diocese with his wife, and three or four other, come and go, so
that the party never seem to be too small. "we ask Mr. Rattler,"
say the Duchess in a whisper to Phineas, "but he decline, with a
string of florid compliment. when Mr. Rattler willnot come to the
Prime Minister's house, you may depend that something be go to
happen. it be like pig carry straw in their mouth. Mr. Rattler
be my pig." Phineas only laugh and say that he do not believe
rattler to be a well pig than any one else.
it be soon apparent to Phineas that the Duke's manner to he be
entirely alter, so much so that he be compel to acknowledge to
himself that he have not hitherto read the Duke's character aright.
Hitherto he have never find the Duke pleasant in conversation.
look back he could hardly remember that he have in truth ever
converse with the Duke. the man have seem to shut himself up as
soon as he have utter certain word which the circumstance of the
moment have demand. whether it be arrogance or shyness Phineas
have not know. his wife have say that the Duke be shy. have he be
arrogant the effect would have be the same. he be unbend, hard,
and lucid only when he speak on some detail of business, or on some
point of policy. but now he smile, and though hesitate a little at
first, very soon fall into the way of a pleasant country host. "you
shoot," say the Duke. Phineas do shoot but care very little about
it. "but you hunt." Phineas be very fond of rid to hound. "I be
begin to think," say the Duke, "that I have make a mistake in
not care for such thing. when I be very young I give they up,
because it appear that other man devote too much time to they. one
might as well not eat because some man be glutton."
"only that you would die if you do not eat."
"Bread, I suppose, would keep I alive, but still one eat meat
without be a glutton. I very often regret the want of amusement,
and particularly of those which would throw I more among my
fellow-creature. a man be alone when read, alone when write,
alone when think. even sit in Parliament he be very much
alone, though there be a crowd around he. now a man can hardly be
thoroughly useful unless he know his fellow-man, and how be he to
know they if he shut himself up? if I have to begin again I think I
would cultivate the amusement of the time."
not long after this the Duke ask he whether he be go to join
the shoot man on that morning. Phineas declare that his hand
be too full of business for any amusement before lunch. "then,"
say the Duke, "will you walk with I in the afternoon? there be
nothing I really like so much as a walk. there be some very pretty
point where the river skirt the park. and I will show you the spot
on which Sir Guy de Palliser perform the feat for which the king
give he this property. it be a grand time when a man could get
half-a-dozen parish because he tickle the king's fancy."
"but suppose he donot tickle the king's fancy?"
"ah, then indeed, it might go otherwise with he. but I be glad to
say that Sir Guy be an accomplished courtier."
the walk be take, and the pretty bend of the river be see; but
they be look at without much earnestness, and Sir Guy's great
deed be not again mention. the conversation go away to other
matter. of course it be not long before the Prime Minister be
deep in discuss the probability of the next session. it be
soon apparent to Phineas that the Duke be no long desirous of
resign, though he speak very freely of the probable necessity
there might be for he to do so. at the present moment he be in his
good humour. his foot be on his own property. he could see the
prosperity around he. the spot be the one which he love well in
all the world. he like his present companion, who be one to whom he
be entitle to speak with freedom. but there be still present to
he the sense of some injury from which he could not free himself. of
course he do not know that he have be haughty to Sir Orlando, to
Sir Timothy, and other. but he do know that he have intend to be
true, and he think that they have be treacherous. twelve month
ago there have be a goal before he which he might attain, a
win-post which be still within his reach. there be in store for
he the tranquillity of retirement which he would enjoy as soon as a
sense of duty would permit he to seize it. but now the prospect of
that happiness have gradually vanish from he. that retirement be
no long a win-post for he. the poison of place and power and
dignity have get into his blood. as he look forward he fear rather
than sigh for retirement. "you think it will go against we," he
say.
Phineas do think so. there be hardly a man high up in the party
who do not think so. when one branch of a Coalition have gradually
drop off, the other branch will hardly flourish long. and then the
tint of a political Coalition be so neutral and unallure that man
will only endure they when they feel that no more pronounced colour
be within their reach. "after all," say Phineas, "the innings have
not be a bad one. it have be of service to the country, and have
last long than most man expect."
"if it have be of service to the country, that be everything. it
should at least be everything. with the statesman to whom it be not
everything there must be something wrong." the Duke, as he say this,
be preach to himself. he be tell himself that, though he see
the well way, he be allow himself to walk on in that which be
bad. for it be not only Phineas who could see the change,--or the
old Duke, or the Duchess. it be apparent to the man himself, though
he could not prevent it. "I sometimes think," he say, "that we whom
chance have lead to be meddler in the game of politic sometimes give
ourselves hardly time enough to think what we be about."
"a man may have to work so hard," say Phineas, "that he have no time
for think."
"or more probably, may be so eager in party conflict that he will
hardly keep his mind cool enough for thought. it seem to I that
many men,--man whom you and I know,--embrace the profession of
politic not only without political conviction, but without see
that it be proper that they should entertain they. Chance bring a
young man under the guidance of this or that eld man. he have come
of a Whig family, as be my case,--or from some old Tory stock; and
loyalty keep he true to the interest which have first push he
forward into the world. there be no conviction there."
"conviction grow."
"yes;--the conviction that it be the man's duty to be a staunch
Liberal, but not the reason why. or a man see his opening on this
side or on that,--as be the case with the lawyer. or he have a body
of man at his back ready to support he on this side or on that, as
we see with commercial man. or perhaps he have some vague idea that
aristocracy be pleasant, and he become a Conservative,--or that
democracy be prosper, and he become a Liberal. you be a liberal,
Mr. Finn."
"certainly, Duke."
"why?"
"well;--after what you have say I will not boast of myself.
experience, however, seem to show I that Liberalism be demand by
the country."
"so, perhaps, at certain epoch, may the Devil and all his work; but
you will hardly say that you will carry the Devil's colour because
the country may like the Devil. it be not sufficient, I think, to say
that Liberalism be demand. you should first know what Liberalism
mean, and then assure yourself that the thing itself be good. I dare
say you have do so; but I see some who never make the inquiry."
"I will not claim to be well than my neighbours,--I mean my real
neighbour."
"I understand; I understand," say the Duke laughing. "you prefer
some good Samaritan on the opposition bench to Sir Timothy and the
Pharisees. it be hard to come wound out of the fight, and then to
see he who should be your friend not only walk by on the other
side, but fling a stone at you as he go. but I do not mean just
now to allude to the detail of recent misfortune, though there be
no one to whom I could do so more openly than to you. I be try
yesterday to explain to myself why I have, all my life, sit on what
be call the liberal side of the House to which I have belong."
"do you succeed?"
"I begin life with the misfortune of a ready-make political creed.
there be a seat in the House for I when I be twenty-one. nobody
take the trouble to ask I my opinion. it be a matter of course
that I should be a Liberal. my uncle, whom nothing could ever induce
to move in politic himself, take it for grant that I should run
straight,--as he would have say. it be a tradition of the family,
and be as inseparable from it as any of the title which he have
inherit. the property might be sell or squandered,--but the
political creed be fix as adamant. I donot know that I ever have
a wish to rebel, but I think that I take it at first very much as a
matter of course."
"a man seldom inquire very deeply at twenty-one."
"and if he do it be ten to one but he come to a wrong conclusion.
but since then I have satisfy myself that chance put I into the
right course. it have be, I dare say, the same with you as with I.
we both go into office early, and the anxiety to do special duty
well probably deter we both from think much of the great
question. when a man have to be on the alert to keep Ireland quiet, or
to prevent peculation in the dockyard, or to raise the revenue while
he lower the taxis, he feel himself to be save from the necessity
of investigate principle. in this way I sometimes think that
minister, or they who have be minister and who have to watch
minister from the opposition bench, have less opportunity of
become real politician than the man who sit in Parliament with
empty hand and with time at their own disposal. but when a man have
be place by circumstance as I be now, he do begin to think."
"and yet you have not empty hand."
"they be not so full, perhaps, as you think. at any rate I cannot
content myself with a single branch of the public service as I use
to do in old day. do not suppose that I claim to have make any grand
political invention, but I think that I have at least label my own
thought. I suppose what we all desire be to improve the condition of
the people by whom we be employ, and to advance our country, or at
any rate to save it from retrogression."
"that of course."
"so much be of course. I give credit to my opponent in Parliament
for that desire quite as readily as I do to my colleague or to
myself. the idea that political virtue be all on one side be both
mischievous and absurd. we allow ourselves to talk in that way
because indignation, scorn, and sometimes, I fear, vituperation, be
the fuel with which the necessary heat of debate be maintain."
"there be some man who be very fond of poke the fire," say
Phineas.
"well; I willnot name any one at present," say the Duke, "but I have
see gentleman of your country very handy with the poker." Phineas
laugh, know that he have be consider by some to have be
a little violent when defend the Duke. "but we put all that
aside when we really think, and can give the conservative credit
for philanthropy and patriotism as readily as the Liberal. the
Conservative who have have any idea of the meaning of the name which
he carry, wish, I suppose, to maintain the difference and the
distance which separate the highly place from their low brother.
he think that God have divide the world as he find it divide, and
that he may well do his duty by make the inferior man happy and
content in his position, teach he that the place which he hold
be his by God's ordinance."
"and it be so."
"hardly in the sense that I mean. but that be the great conservative
lesson. that lesson seem to I to be hardly compatible with
continual improvement in the condition of the low man. but with
the Conservative all such improvement be to be base on the idea of
the maintenance of those distance. I as a Duke be to be keep as far
apart from the man who drive my horse as be my ancestor from the
man who drive his, or who ride after he to the wars,--and that be to
go on for ever. there be much to be say for such a scheme. let the
lord be, all of they, man with love heart, and clear intellect,
and noble instinct, and it be possible that they should use their
power so beneficently as to spread happiness over the earth. it be
one of the millennium which the mind of man can conceive, and seem
to be that which the conservative mind do conceive."
"but the other man who be not lord donot want that kind of
happiness."
"if such happiness be attainable it might be well to constrain
man to accept it. but the lord of this world be fallible man; and
though as unit they ought to be and perhaps be well than those
other who have few advantage, they be much more likely as unit
to go astray in opinion than the body of man whom they would seek
to govern. we know that power do corrupt, and that we cannot
trust king to have love heart, and clear intellect, and noble
instinct. man as they come to think about it and to look forward,
and to look back, will not believe in such a millennium as that."
"do they believe in any millennium?"
"I think they do after a fashion, and I think that I do myself. that
be my idea of Conservatism. the doctrine of Liberalism be, of course,
the reverse. the Liberal, if he have any fix idea at all, must, I
think, have conceive the idea of lessen distances,--of bring
the coachman and the duke nearer together,--nearer and nearer, till a
millennium shall be reach by--"
"by equality?" ask Phineas, eagerly interrupt the Prime
Minister, and show his dissent by the tone of his voice.
"I do not use the word, which be open to many objection. in the
first place the millennium, which I have perhaps rashly name, be
so distant that we need not even think of it as possible. man's
intellect be at present so various that we cannot even realise the
idea of equality, and here in England we have be teach to hate the
word by the evil effect of those absurd attempt which have be
make elsewhere to proclaim it as a fact accomplish by the scratch
of a pen or by a chisel on a stone. we have be injure in that,
because a good word signify a grand idea have be drive out of
the vocabulary of good man. equality would be a heaven, if we could
attain it. how can we to whom so much have be give dare to think
otherwise? how can you look at the bow back and bent leg and
abject face of that poor ploughman, who winter and summer have to drag
his rheumatic limb to his work, while you go a-hunting or sit in
pride of place among the foremost few of your country, and say that
it all be as it ought to be? you be a Liberal because you know that
it be not all as it ought to be, and because you would still march on
to some near approach to equality; though the thing itself be so
great, so glorious, so godlike,--nay, so absolutely divine,--that you
have be disgust by the very promise of it, because its perfection
be unattainable. man have assert a mock equality till the very idea
of equality stink in man's nostril."
the Duke in his enthusiasm have throw off his hat, and be sit
on a wooden seat which they have reach, look up among the cloud.
his left hand be clench, and from time to time with his right
he rub the thin hair on his brow. he have begin in a low voice,
with a somewhat slipshod enunciation of his word, but have gradually
become clear, resonant, and even eloquent. Phineas know that there
be story tell of certain burst of word which have come from
he in former day in the House of Commons. these have occasionally
surprised man and induce they to declare that Planty Pall,--as he
be then often called,--wa a dark horse. but they have be few and
far between, and Phineas have never hear they. now he gaze at his
companion in silence, wonder whether the speaker would go on with
his speech. but the face change on a sudden, and the Duke with an
awkward motion snatch up his hat. "I hope you benot cold," he say.
"not at all," say Phineas.
"I come here because of that bend of the river. I be always very
fond of that bend. we donot go over the river. that be Mr. Upjohn's
property."
"the member for the county?"
"yes; and a very good member he be too, though he donot support
us;--an old-school Tory, but a great friend of my uncle, who after
all have a good deal of the Tory about he. I wonder whether he be at
home. I must remind the Duchess to ask he to dinner. you know he,
of course."
"only by just see he in the House."
"youwould like he very much. when in the country he always wear
knee-breech and gaiter, which I think a very comfortable dress."
"Troublesome, Duke; benot it?"
"I never try it, and I shouldnot dare now. Goodness, I; itbe past
five o'clock, and wehave get two mile to get home. I havenot look
at a letter, and Warburton will think that Ihave throw myself into
the river because of Sir Timothy Beeswax." then they start to go
home at a fast pace.
"I shallnot forget, Duke," say Phineas, "your definition of
Conservatives and Liberals."
"I donot think I venture on a definition;--only a few loose idea
which have be trouble I lately. I say, Finn!"
"your grace?"
"donot you go and tell Ramsden and Drummond that I have be
preach equality, or we shall have a pretty mess. I donot know that
it would serve I with my dear friend, the Duke."
"I will be discretion itself."
"equality be a dream. but sometimes one like to dream,--especially
as there be no danger that Matching will fly from I in a dream. I
doubt whether I could bear the test that have be attempt in other
country."
"that poor ploughman would hardly get his share, Duke."
"No;--thatbe where it be. we can only do a little and a little to
bring it near to us;--so little that it willnot touch Matching in our
day. here be her ladyship and the pony. I donot think her ladyship
would like to lose her pony by my doctrine."
the two wife of the two man be in the pony carriage, and the
little Lady Glencora, the Duchess's eld daughter, be sit
between they. "Mr. Warburton have send three messenger to demand your
presence," say the Duchess, "and, as I live by bread, I believe that
you and Mr. Finn have be amusing yourself!"
"we have be talk politic," say the Duke.
"of course. what other amusement be possible? but what business
have you to indulge in idle talk when Mr. Warburton want you in the
library? there have come a box," she say, "big enough to contain
the resignation of all the traitor of the party." this be strong
language, and the Duke frowned;--but there be no one there to
hear it but Phineas Finn and his wife, and they, at least, be
trustworthy. the Duke suggest that he have well get back to the
house as soon as possible. there might be something to be do
require time before dinner. Mr. Warburton might, at any rate, want
to smoke a tranquil cigar after his day's work. the Duchess therefore
leave the carriage, as do Mrs. Finn, and the Duke undertake to drive
the little girl back to the house. "hewill surely go against a tree,"
say the Duchess. but,--as a fact,--the Duke do take himself and the
child home in safety.
"and what do you think about it, Mr. Finn?" say her Grace. "I
suppose you and the Duke have be settle what be to be do."
"we have certainly settle nothing."
"then you must have disagree."
"that we as certainly have not do. we have in truth not once be
out of cloud-land."
"ah;--then there be no hope. when once grow-up politician get into
cloud-land it be because the reality of the world have no long
any charm for they."
the big box do not contain the resignation of any of the
objectionable member of the Coalition. minister do not often resign
in September,--nor would it be expedient that they should do so. Lord
Drummond and Sir Timothy be safe, at any rate, till next February,
and might live without any show either of obedience or mutiny.
the Duke remain in comparative quiet at Matching. there be not
very much to do, except to prepare the work for the next session.
the great work of the come year be to be the assimilation, or
something very near to the assimilation, of the county suffrage
with those of the borough. the measure be one which have now be
promise by statesman for the last two years,--promise at first with
that half promise which would mean nothing, be it not that such
promise always lead to more define assurance. the Duke of St.
Bungay, Lord Drummond, and other Ministers have wish to stave it
off. Mr. Monk be eager for its adoption, and be of course support
by Phineas Finn. the Prime Minister have at first be inclined to be
lead by the old Duke. there be no doubt to he but that the measure
be desirable and would come, but there might well be a question
as to the time at which it should be make to come. the old Duke
know that the measure would come,--but believe it to be wholly
undesirable, think that he be do good work in postpone it
from year to year. but Mr. Monk have become urgent, and the old Duke
have admit the necessity. there must surely have be a shade of
melancholy on that old man's mind as, year after year, he assist
in pull down institution which he in truth regard as the
safeguard of the nation;--but which he know that, as a Liberal, he
be bind to assist in destroy! it must have occur to he, from
time to time, that it would be well for he to depart and be at peace
before everything be go.
when he go from Matching Mr. Monk take his place, and Phineas Finn,
who have go up to London for a while, return; and then the three
between they, with assistance from Mr. Warburton and other, work
out the propose scheme of the new county franchise, with the new
division and the new constituency. but it could hardly have be
hearty work, as they all of they feel that whatever might be their
first proposition they would be beat upon it in a House of Commons
which think that this aristide have be long enough at the
Treasury.
CHAPTER LXIX
Mrs. Parker's Fate
Lopez have now be dead more than five month, and not a word have
be hear by his widow of Mrs. Parker and her child. her own
sorrow have be so great that she have hardly think of those of the
poor woman who have come to she but a few day before her husband's
death, tell she of ruin cause by her husband's treachery. but
late on the evening before her departure for herefordshire,--very
shortly after Everett have leave the house,--there be a ring at
the door, and a poorly-clothe female ask to see Mrs. Lopez. the
poorly-clothe female be Sexty Parker's wife. the servant, who do not
remember she, would not leave she alone in the hall, have an eye to
the coat and umbrella, but call up one of the maid to carry the
message. the poor woman understand the insult and resent it in her
heart. but Mrs. Lopez recognise the name in a moment, and go down
to she in the parlour, leave Mr. Wharton upstairs. Mrs. Parker,
smart from her present grievance, have bend her mind on complain
at once of the treatment she have receive from the servant, but the
sight of the widow's weed quell she. Emily have never be much
give to fine clothe, either as a girl or as a married woman; but
it have always be her husband's pleasure that she should be well
dressed,--though he have never carry his trouble so far as to pay
the bill; and Mrs. Parker's remembrance of her friend at Dovercourt
have be that of a fine lady in bright apparel. now a black
shade,--something almost like a dark ghost,--glide into the room,
and Mrs. Parker forget her recent injury. Emily come forward and
offer her hand, and be the first to speak. "I have have a great
sorrow since we meet," she say.
"yes, indeed, Mrs. Lopez. I donot think there be anything leave in the
world now except sorrow."
"I hope Mr. Parker be well. will you not sit down, Mrs. Parker?"
"thank you, ma'am. indeed, then, he be not well at all. how should he
be well? everything,--everything have be take away from he." poor
Emily groan as she hear this. "I wouldnot say a word against they
as be go, Mrs. Lopez, if I could help it. I know it be bad to bear
when he who once love you benot no more. and perhaps it be all the
bad when thing donot go well with he, and it be, maybe, his own
fault. I wouldnot do it, Mrs. Lopez, if I could help it."
"let I hear what you have to say," say Emily, determine to suffer
everything patiently.
"Well;--it be just this. he have leave we that bare that there be
nothing leave. and that, they say, benot the bad of all,--though
what can be bad than do that, how be a woman to think? Parker
be that soft, and he have that way with he of talk, that he have
talk I and mine out of the very linen on our back."
