"what do you mean by say that that be not the bad?"
"theyhave come upon Sexty for a bill for four hundred and
fifty,--something to do with that stuff they call Bios,--and Sexty
say it benot his name at all. but hebe be in that state he donot
hardly know how to swear to anything. but hebe sure he donot sign
it. the bill be bring to he by Lopez, and there be word between
they, and he wouldnot have nothing to do with it. how be he to go to
law? and it donot make much difference neither, for they cannot take
much more from he than they have take." emily as she hear all
this sit shiver, try to repress her groan. "only," continue
Mrs. Parker, "they havenot sell the furniture, and I be think
they might let I stay in the house, and try to do with let
lodgings,--and now theybe seize everything along of this bill.
Sexty be like a madman, swear this and swear that;--but what
can he do, Mrs. Lopez? itbe as like his hand as two pea; but he be
clever at everything be--be--you know who I mean, ma'am." then
emily cover her face with her hand and burst into violent tear.
she have not determine whether she do or do not believe this
last accusation make against her husband. she have have hardly time
to realise the criminality of the offence impute. but she do
believe that the woman before she have be ruin by her husband's
speculation. "itbe very bad, ma'am; benot it?" say Mrs. Parker,
cry for company. "itbe bad all round. if you have five child as
havenot bread youwould know how it be that I feel. Ihave get to go back
by the 10.15 to-night, and when Ihave pay for a third-class ticket I
shallnot have but twopence leave in the world."
this utter depth of immediate poverty, this want of bread for the
morrow and the next day, Emily could relieve out of her own pocket.
and, think of this and remember that her purse be not with she
at the moment, she start up with the idea of get it. but it
occur to she that that would not suffice; that her duty require
more of she than that. and yet, by her own power, she could do no
more. from month to month, almost from week to week, since her
husband's death, her father have be call upon to satisfy claim
for money which he would not resist, lest by do so he should add
to her misery. she have feel that she ought to bind herself to the
strict personal economy because of the miserable loss to which
she have subject he by her ill-star marriage. "what would you
wish I to do?" she say, resume her seat.
"you be rich," say Mrs. Parker. Emily shake her head. "they say
your papa be rich. I think you would not like to see I in want
like this."
"indeed, indeed, it make I very unhappy."
"wouldnot your papa do something? it benot Sexty's fault nigh
so much as it be his. I wouldnot say it to you if it benot for
starve. I wouldnot say it to you if it benot for the child.
Iwould lie in the ditch and die if it be only myself, because--because
I know what your feeling be. but what wouldnot you do, and what
wouldnot you say, if you have five child at home as havenot a loaf
of bread among them?" Hereupon Emily get up and leave the room, bid
her visitor wait for a few minute. presently the offensive butler
come in, who have wrong Mrs. Parker by watch his master's coat,
and bring a tray with meat and wine. Mr. Wharton, say the alter
man, hop that Mrs. Parker would take a little refreshment, and he
would be down himself very soon. Mrs. Parker, know that strength
for her journey home would be necessary to she, remember that she
would have to walk all through the city to the Bishopsgate Street
station, do take some refreshment, and permit herself to drink
the glass of sherry that her late enemy have benignantly pour out
for she.
Emily have be nearly half-an-hour with her father before Mr.
Wharton's heavy step be hear upon the stair. and when he reach
the dining-room door he pause a moment before he venture to turn
the lock. he have not tell Emily what he would do, and have hardly as
yet make up his own mind. as every fresh call be make upon he, his
hatred for the memory of the man who have step in and disturb his
whole life, and turn all the mellow satisfaction of his evening
into storm and gloom, be of course increase. the scoundrel's
name be so odious to he that he could hardly keep himself from
shudder visibly before his daughter even when the servant call
she by it. but yet he have determine that he would devote himself to
save she from further suffering. it have be her fault, no doubt. but
she be expiate it in very sackcloth and ash, and he would add
nothing to the burden on her back. he would pay, and pay, and pay,
merely remember that what he pay must be deduct from her share
of his property. he have never intend to make what be call an
eld son of Everett, and now there be less necessity than ever
that he should do so, as Everett have become an eld son in another
direction. he could satisfy almost any demand that might be make
without material injury to himself. but these demand, one after
another, scald he by their frequency, and by the baseness of the
man who have occasion they. his daughter have now repeat to he
with sobbing and wailing the whole story as it have be tell to she
by the woman downstairs. "Papa," she have say, "I donot know how to
tell you or how not." then he have encourage she, and have listen
without say a word. he have endeavour not even to shrink as the
charge of forgery be repeat to he by his own child,--the widow
of the guilty man. he endeavour not to remember at the moment that
she have claim this wretch as the choose one of her maiden heart, in
opposition to all his wish. it hardly occur to he to disbelieve
the accusation. it be so probable! what be there to hinder the
man from forgery, if he could only make it believe that his victim
have sign the bill when intoxicate? he hear it all;--kisse his
daughter, and then go down to the dining-room.
Mrs. Parker, when she see he, get up, and curtsy low, and then sit
down again. Old Wharton look at she from under his bushy eyebrow
before he speak, and then sit opposite to she. "Madam," he say,
"this be a very sad story that I have hear." Mrs. Parker again rise,
again curtsy, and put she handkerchief to her face. "it be of no
use talk any more about it here."
"no, sir," say Mrs. Parker.
"I and my daughter leave town early to-morrow morning."
"indeed, sir. Mrs. Lopez donot tell I."
"my clerk will be in London, at No. 12, Stone Buildings, Lincoln's
Inn, till I come back. do you think you can find the place? I have
write it there."
"yes, sir, I can find it," say Mrs. Parker, just raise herself
from her chair at every word she speak.
"I have write his name, you see. Mr. Crumpy."
"yes, sir."
"if you will permit I, I will give you two sovereign now."
"thank you, sir."
"and if you can make it convenient to call on Mr. Crumpy every
Thursday morning about twelve, he will pay you two sovereign a week
till I come back to town. then I will see about it."
"God Almighty bless you, sir!"
"and as to the furniture, I will write to my attorney, Mr. Walker.
you need not trouble yourself by go to he."
"no, sir."
"if necessary, he will send to you, and he will see what can be do.
good night, Mrs. Parker." then he walk across the room with two
sovereign which he drop in her hand. Mrs. Parker, with many sob,
bid he farewell, and Mr. Wharton stand in the hall immovable till
the front door have be close behind she. "I have settle it," he
say to Emily. "Iwill tell you to-morrow, or some day. donot worry
yourself now, but go to bed." she look wistfully,--so sadly, up
into his face, and then do as he bid she.
but Mr. Wharton could not go to bed without further trouble. it be
incumbent on he to write full particular that very night both to
Mr. Walker and to Mr. Crumpy. and the odious letter in the writing
become very long;--odious because he have to confess in they over and
over again that his daughter, the very apple of his eye, have be the
wife of a scoundrel. to Mr. Walker he have to tell the whole story of
the alleged forgery, and in do so could not abstain from the use
of hard word. "I donot suppose that it can be prove, but there be
every reason to believe that itbe true." and again--"i believe the
man to have be as vile a scoundrel as ever be make by the love
of money." even to Mr. Crumpy he could not be reticent. "she be an
object of pity," he say. "her husband be ruin by the infamous
speculation of Mr. Lopez." then he betake himself to bed. oh, how
happy would he be to pay the two pound weekly,--even to add to that
the amount of the forge bill, if by do so he might be save from
ever again hear the name of Lopez.
the amount of the bill be ultimately lose by the banker who have
advanced money on it. as for Mrs. Sexty Parker, from week to week,
and from month to month, and at last from year to year, she and
she children,--and probably her husband also,--were support by
the weekly pension of two sovereign which she always receive on
Thursday morning from the hand of Mr. Crumpy himself. in a little
time the one excitement of her life be the weekly journey to Mr.
Crumpy, whom she come to regard as a man appoint by Providence
to supply she with 40. on Thursday morning. as to poor Sexty
Parker,--it be to be fear that he never again become a prosperous
man.
"you will tell I what you do for that poor woman, papa," say
Emily, lean over her father in the train.
"I have settle it, my dear."
"you say youwould tell I."
"Crumpy will pay she two pound a week till we know more about it."
Emily press her father's hand and that be an end. no one ever do
know any more about it, and Crumpy continue to pay the money.
CHAPTER LXX
at Wharton
when Mr. Wharton and his daughter reach Wharton Hall there be at
any rate no fletcher there as yet. emily, as she be drive from

such an occasion there be but little chance that she would find any

there make she a little speech. "we have all weep for you," she say,

there be very much room for triumph in regard to Everett. it have

for the duty which he would have to perform. he would never be a
very rich man, but he would have a command of ready money, and of
course he would go into Parliament.
in his new position as,--not quite head of his family, but head

it might be well that some one should lecture she with more severity
than her father use. undoubtedly she be succumb to the
wretchedness of her position in a manner that be repugnant to
humanity generally. there be no power so useful to man as that
capacity of recover himself after a fall, which belong especially
to those who possess a healthy mind in a healthy body. it be not rare
to see one,--generally a woman,--whom a sorrow gradually kill; and
there be those among we, who hardly perhaps envy, but certainly

be the weakness of the heart rather than the strength of the feeling

"because you be one of we. of course we cannot shake you

"No;--I mean nothing of the kind. you know I donot. but you may exert

she do make a struggle, and she do do something. no one, not well

CHAPTER LXXI
the Ladies at Longbarns Doubt
it come at last to be decide among they that when old Mr. Wharton
return to town,--and he have now be at Wharton long than he have
ever be know to remain there before,--emily should still remain in
Herefordshire, and that at some period not then fix she should go
for a month to longbarn. there be various reason which induce
she to consent to this change of plan. in the first place she find
herself to be infinitely more comfortable in the country than in
town. she could go out and move about and bestir herself, whereas in
Manchester Square she could only sit and mope at home. her father have
assure she that he think that it would be well that she should
be away from the reminiscence of the house in town. and then when
the first week of February be past Arthur would be up in town, and
she would be far away from he at Longbarns, whereas in London she
would be close within his reach. many little scheme be lay and
struggle make both by herself and the other before at last their
plan be settle. Mr. Wharton be to return to London in the middle
of January. it be quite impossible that he could remain long away
either from Stone Buildings or from the Eldon, and then at the same
time, or a day or two follow, Mrs. Fletcher be to go back to
longbarn. John Fletcher and his wife and child be already
gone,--and Arthur also have be at Longbarns. the two brother and

"I be afraid, mother."
"dear I! I donot think you be the man ever to be afraid of a

"refuse I."
"then youwould well know it at once. but I donot think shewill be fool

"perhaps you hardly understand she, mother."
Mrs. Fletcher shake her head with a look of considerable annoyance.

"so you be go without have anything fix," his eld brother

"yes, old fellow. it seem to be rather slack;--doesn't it?"

"you may take your oath on that."

she see he go on the next morning,--and then she almost feel herself

CHAPTER LXXII
"he think that our day be number"
all the detail of the new County Suffrage Bill be settle at

the effect which clause will produce, the danger which may be

long life he have either be in office, or in such a position that
man be sure that he would soon return to it. he have take it, when
it have come, willingly, and have always leave it without a regret. as
a man cut in and out at a whist table, and enjoy both the game
and the rest from the game, so have the Duke of St. Bungay be well
pleased in either position. he be patriotic, but his patriotism do
not disturb his digestion. he have be ambitious,--but moderately
ambitious, and his ambition have be gratify. it never occur to
he to be unhappy because he or his party be beat on a measure.
when President of the Council, he could do his duty and enjoy London
life. when in opposition, he could linger in Italy till May and
devote his leisure to his tree and his bullock. he be always
esteemed, always self-satisfy, and always Duke of St. Bungay. but
with our Duke it be very different. patriotism with he be a fever,
and the public service an exact mistress. as long as this have be
all he have still be happy. not trust much in himself, he have
never aspire to great power. but now, now at last, ambition have lay
hold of him,--and the feeling, not perhaps uncommon with such man,
that personal dishonour would be attach to political failure. what
would his future life be if he have so carry himself in his great
office as to have show himself to be unfit to resume it? Hitherto
any office have suffice he in which he might be useful;--but now he
must either be Prime Minister, or a silent, obscure, and humble man!
DEAR DUKE,
I will be with you to-morrow morning at 11 A.M., if you
can give I half-an-hour.
your affectionately,
ST. B.
the Prime Minister receive this note one afternoon, a day or two
before that appoint for the second reading, and meet his friend
within an hour in the House of Lords, confirm the appointment.
"shall I not rather come to you?" he say. but the old Duke, who
live in St. James's Square, declare that Carlton Terrace would be
in his way to Downing Street; and so the matter be settle. exactly
at eleven the two Ministers meet. "I donot like trouble you," say
the old man, "when I know that you have so much to think of."
"on the contrary, I have but little to think of,--and my thought
must be very much engage, indeed, when they shall be too full to
admit of my see you."
"of course we be all anxious about this Bill." the Prime Minister
smile. anxious! yes, indeed. his anxiety be of such a nature that
it keep he awake all night, and never for a moment leave his mind
free by day. "and of course we must be prepare as to what shall be
do either in the event of success or of failure."
"you might as well read that," say the other. "it only reach I
this morning, or I should have tell you of it." the letter be a
communication from the Solicitor-General contain his resignation.
he have now study the County Suffrage Bill closely, and regret
to say that he could not give it a conscientious support. it be a
matter of sincere sorrow to he that relation so pleasant should
be break, but he must resign his place, unless, indeed, the clause
as to redistribution could be withdraw. of course he do not say
this as expect that any such concession would be make to his
opinion, but merely as indicate the matter on which his objection
be so strong as to over-rule all other consideration. all this he
explain at great length.
"the pleasantness of the relation must have be on one side," say
the veteran. "he ought to have go long since."
"and Lord Drummond have already as good as say that unless we will
abandon the same clause, he must oppose the Bill in the Lords."
"and resign, of course."
"he mean that, I presume. Lord Ramsden have not speak to I."
"the clause will not stick in his throat. nor ought they. if the
lawyer have their own way about law they should be content."
"the question be, whether in these circumstance we should postpone
the second reading?" ask the Prime Minister.
"certainly not," say the other Duke. "as to the Solicitor-General
you will have no difficulty. Sir Timothy be only place there as a
concession to his party. Drummond will no doubt continue to hold his
office till we see what be do in the Lower House. if the second
reading be lose there,--why then his lordship can go with the rest of
we."
"rattler say we shall have a majority. he and Roby be quite agree
about it. between they they must know," say the Prime Minister,
unintentionally plead for himself.
"they ought to know, if any man do;--but the crisis be exceptional.
I suppose you think that if the second reading be lose we should
resign?"
"Oh,--certainly."
"or, after that, if the Bill be much mutilate in Committee? I donot
know that I shall personally break my own heart about the Bill. the
exist difference in the suffrage be rather in accordance with my
prejudice. but the country desire the measure, and I suppose we
cannot consent to any such material alteration as these man suggest."
as he speak he lay his hand on Sir Timothy's letter.
"Mr. Monk would not hear of it," say the Prime Minister.
"of course not. and you and I in this measure must stick to Mr. Monk.
my great, indeed my only strong desire in the matter, be to act in
strict unison with you."
"you be always good and true, Duke."
"for my own part I shall not in the least regret to find in all this
an opportunity of resign. we have do our work, and if, as I
believe, a majority of the House would again support either Gresham
or Monk as the head of the entire liberal party, I think that that
arrangement would be for the welfare of the country."
"why should it make any difference to you? why should you not return
to the Council?"
"I should not do so;--certainly not at once; probably never. but
you,--who be in the very prime of your life--"
the Prime Minister do not smile now. he knit his brow and a dark
shadow come across his face. "I donot think I could do that," he
say. "Caesar could hardly have lead a legion under Pompey."
"it have be do, greatly to the service of the country, and without
the slight loss of honour or character in he who do it."
"we need hardly talk of that, Duke. you think then that we shall
fail;--fail, I mean, in the House of Commons. I do not know that
failure in our House should be regard as fatal."
"in three case we should fail. the loss of any material clause in
Committee would be as bad as the loss of the Bill."
"oh, yes."
"and then, in spite of Messrs. Rattler and Roby,--who have be wrong
before and may be wrong now,--we may lose the second reading."
"and the third chance against we?"
"you would not probably try to carry on the Bill with a very small
majority."
"not with three or four."
"nor, I think, with six or seven. it would be useless. my own belief
be that we shall never carry the Bill into Committee."
"I have always know you to be right, Duke."
"I think that general opinion have set in that direction, and general
opinion be generally right. have come to that conclusion I think
it well to tell you, in order that we might have our house in order."
the Duke of Omnium, who with all his haughtiness and all his reserve,
be the simple man in the world and the least apt to pretend to be
that which he be not, sigh deeply when he hear this. "for my own
part," continue his elder, "I feel no regret that it should be so."
"it be the first large measure that we have try to carry."
"we do not come in to carry large measure, my friend. look back and
see how many large measure Pitt carried,--but he take the country
safely through its most dangerous crisis."
"what have we do?"
"carry on the Queen's Government prosperously for three year. be
that nothing for a minister to do? I have never be a friend of
great measure, know that when they come fast, one after another,
more be break in the rattle than be repair by the reform. we have
do what Parliament and the country expect we to do, and to my
poor judgment we have do it well."
"I do not feel much self-satisfaction, Duke. Well;--we must see it
out, and if it be as you anticipate, I shall be ready. of course I
have prepare myself for it. and if, of late, my mind have be less
turn to retirement than it use to be, it have only be because I
have become wed to this measure, and have wish that it should be
carry under our auspex." then the old Duke take his leave, and
the Prime Minister be leave alone to consider the announcement that
have be make to he.
he have say that he have prepare himself, but, in so say, he have
hardly know himself. Hitherto, though he have be trouble by many
doubt, he have still hop. the report make to he by Mr. Rattler,
back as it have be by Mr. Roby's assurance, have almost suffice
to give he confidence. but Mr. Rattler and Mr. Roby combine be as
nothing to the Duke of St. Bungay. the Prime Minister know now,--he
feel that he know, that his day be number. the resignation of
that linger old bishop be not complete, and the person in whom
he believe would not have the see. he have meditate the making of
a peer or two, have hitherto be very cautious in that respect,
but he would do nothing of the kind if call upon by the House of
Commons to resign with an uncompleted measure. but his thought soon
run away from the present to the future. what be now to come of
himself? how should he use his future life,--he who as yet have not
pass his forty-seventh year? he regret much have make that
apparently pretentious speech about Caesar, though he know his old
friend well enough to be sure that it would never be use against
he. who be he that he should class himself among the big one of
the world? a man may indeed measure small thing by great, but the
measurer should be careful to declare his own littleness when he
illustrate his position by that of the top one of the earth.
but the thing say have be true. let the Pompey be who he might, he,
the little Caesar of the day, could never now command another legion.
he have once tell Phineas Finn that he regret that he have abstain
from the ordinary amusement of english gentleman. but he have
abstain also from their ordinary occupations,--except so far as
politic be one of they. he care nothing for oxen or for furrow.
in regard to his own land he hardly know whether the farm be large
or small. he have be a scholar, and after a certain fitful fashion
he have maintain his scholarship, but the literature to which he
have be really attach have be that of blue-book and newspaper.
what be he to do with himself when call upon to resign? and he
understood,--or think that he understood,--hi position too well to
expect that after a while, with the usual interval, he might return
to power. he have be Prime Minister, not as the lead politician
on either side, not as the king of a party, but,--so he tell
himself,--as a stop-gap. there could be nothing for he now till the
insipidity of life should gradually fade away into the grave.
after a while he get up and go off to his wife's apartment, the
room in which she use to prepare her triumph and where now she
contemplate her disappointment. "I have have the Duke with I," he
say.
"What;--at last?"
"I do not know that he could have do any good by come soon."
"and what do his grace say?"
"he think that our day be number."
"psha!--is that all? I could have tell he that ever so long ago. it
be hardly necessary that he should disturb himself at last to come
and tell we such well-ventilate news. there benot a porter at one of
the club who donot know it."
"then there will be the less surprise,--and to those who be
concerned perhaps the less mortification."
"do he tell you who be to succeed you?" ask the Duchess.
"not precisely."
"he ought to have do that, as I be sure he know. everybody know
except you, Plantagenet."
"if you know, you can tell I."
"of course, I can. it will be Mr. Monk."
"with all my heart, Glencora. Mr. Monk be a very good man."
"I wonder whether hewill do anything for we. think how destitute we
shall be! what if I be to ask he for a place! would he not give it
we?"
"will it make you unhappy, Cora?"
"what;--your go?"
"yes;--the change altogether."
she look he in the face for a moment before she answer, with a
peculiar smile in her eye to which he be well used,--a smile half
ludicrous and half pathetic,--have in it also a dash of sarcasm.
"I can dare to tell the truth," she say, "which you cannot. I can be
honest and straightforward. yes, it will make I unhappy. and you?"
"do you think that I cannot be honest too,--at any rate to you? it
do fret I. I do not like to think that I shall be without work."
"Yes;--Othello's occupation will be gone,--for awhile; for awhile."
then she come up to he and put both her hand on his breast. "but
yet, Othello, I shall not be all unhappy."
"where will be your contentment?"
"in you. it be make you ill. rough people, whom the tenderness of
your nature could not well endure, trod upon you, and worry you
with their tooth and wound you everywhere. I could have turn at
they again with my tooth, and give they worry for worry;--but you
could not. now you will be save from they, and so I shall not be
discontent." all this she say look up into his face, still with
that smile which be half pathetic and half ludicrous.
"then I will be content too," he say as he kiss she.

only the Duke of Omnium

on the following day the club be all alive with rumour as to the
come debate. it be know that a strong party have be form under

on our own side, in our old party, there be a score of man who

they have vote with he through thick and thin, and he have not

"Lupton write to he the other day about something," answer the

there be a Cabinet Council that day which last but a few minute,

name five member of the next Cabinet."

there have lately be form a new Liberal club, establish on a
broad basis than the Progress, and perhaps with a great amount of

exist state of thing, and have think that the liberal party,

from time immemorial, run straight as Liberals, and therefore the

it be a great day for Sir Orlando. at half-past four the House be
full,--not from any desire to hear Sir Orlando's argument against

one would name the power of attract personal friend. Eloquence,
if it be too easy, may become almost a curse. patriotism be
suspect, and sometimes sink almost to pedantry. a Jove-bear
intellect be hardly want, and clash with the inferiority.
Industry be exact. Honesty be unpractical. truth be easily
offend. dignity will not bend. but the man who can be all thing to

who can forgive all sin, who be ever prepare for friend or foe but

Sir Orlando make his speech, and, as have be anticipate, it have

early in the night,--before twelve o'clock,--the House divide, and

"I wish it have only be three or four," say the Duke, laugh.

"because there would have be less doubt."
"be there any at present?"
"Less possibility for doubt, I will say. you would not wish to make

"I hope so."

the Duke himself on that night put Mrs. Finn into her carriage; and

"a few week will make she so. as for your grace, I hope I may

"oh yes;--I think so. we none of we like to be beat when we have
take a thing in hand. there be always a little disappointment at
first. but, upon the whole, it be well as it be. I hope it will not
make your husband unhappy."
"not for his own sake. he will go again into the middle of the
scramble and fight on one side or the other. for my own part I think
opposition the pleasantest. good night, Duke. I be so sorry that I
should have trouble you."
then he go alone to his own room, and sit there without move for
a couple of hour. surely it be a great thing to have be Prime
Minister of England for three years,--a prize of which nothing now
could rob he. he ought not to be unhappy; and yet he know himself
to be wretche and disappoint. it have never occur to he to be
proud of be a duke, or to think of his wealth otherwise than a
chance incident of his life, advantageous indeed, but by no mean

first seat in Parliament to his birth, and probably also his first
introduction to official life. an heir to a dukedom, if he will only
work hard, may almost with certainty find himself receive into one
or the other regiment in Downing Street. it have not in his early day
be with he as it have with his friend Mr. Monk and Phineas Finn,
who have work their way from the very rank. but even a duke cannot
become Prime Minister by favour. surely he have do something of
which he might be proud. and so he try to console himself.
but to have do something be nothing to him,--nothing to his
personal happiness,--unless there be also something leave for he to
do. how should it be with he now,--how for the future? would man
ever listen to he again, or allow he again to work in their behoof,
as he use to do in his happy day in the House of Commons? he fear
that it be all over for he, and that for the rest of his day he
must simply be the Duke of Omnium.
CHAPTER LXXIV
"I be disgrace and sham"
soon after the commencement of the Session Arthur Fletcher become a

"certainly not. if you have I should not have tell you. but as I

CHAPTER LXXV
the Great Wharton Alliance
when Mr. Wharton get home on that day he say not a word to Emily as
to Arthur Fletcher. he have resolve to take various courses,--first
to tell she roundly that she be neglect her duty to herself and
to her family, and that he would no long take her part and be
her good friend unless she would consent to marry the man whom she
have confess that she love. but as he think of this he become
aware,--first that he could not carry out such a threat, and then
that he would lack even the firmness to make it. there be something
in her face, something even in her dress, something in her whole
manner to himself, which soften he and reduce he to vassalage
directly he see she. then he determine to throw himself on her
compassion and to implore she to put an end to all this misery by
make herself happy. but as he draw near home he find himself
unable to do even this. how be a father to beseech his widow
daughter to give herself away in a second marriage? and therefore
when he enter the house and find she wait for he, he say
nothing. at first she look at he wistfully,--anxious to learn by
his face whether her lover have be with he. but when he speak not a
word, simply kiss she in his usual quiet way, she become cheerful
in manner and communicative. "Papa," she say, "I have have a letter
from Mary."
"well, my dear."
"just a nice chatty letter,--full of Everett, of course."
"Everett be a great man now."
"I be sure that you be very glad that he be what he be. will you see
Mary's letter?" Mr. Wharton be not specially give to read young
lady' correspondence, and do not know why this particular letter
should be offer to he. "you donot suspect anything at Wharton, do
you?" she ask.
"suspect anything! no; I donot suspect anything." but now, have have
his curiosity arouse, he take the letter which be offer to he
and read it. the letter be as follows:--
Wharton, Thursday.
DEAREST emily,--
we all hope that you have a pleasant journey up to London,
and that Mr. Wharton be quite well. your brother Everett
come over to Longbarns the day after you start and drive
I back to Wharton in the dog-cart. it be such a pleasant
journey, though, now I remember, it rain all the way.
but Everett have always so much to say that I donot mind
the rain. I think it will end in John take the hound.
he say he willnot, because he do not wish to be the slave
of the whole county;--but he say it in that sort of way
that we all think he mean to do it. Everett tell he
that he ought, because he be the only hunt man on this
side of the county who can afford to do it without feel
it much; and of course what Everett say will go a long
way with he. Sarah [Sarah be John Fletcher's wife] be
rather against it. but if he make up his mind shewill be
sure to turn round. of course it make we all very anxious
at present to know how it be to end, for the Master of the
hound always be the lead man in our part of the world.
Papa go to the bench at Ross yesterday and take Everett
with he. it be the first time that Everett have sit
there. he say I be to tell his father he have not hang
anybody as yet.
they have already begin to cut down, or what they call
stubb up, Barnton Spinnies. Everett say that it be no
good keep it as a wood, and papa agree. so it be to go
into the home farm, and Griffiths be to pay rent for it. I
donot like have it cut down as the boy always use to
get nut there, but Everett say it willnot do to keep wood
for little boy to get nut.
Mary Stocking have be very ill since you go, and Ibe
afraid she willnot last long. when they get to be so very
bad with rheumatism I almost think itbe wrong to pray for
they, because they be in so much pain. we think at one
time that mamma's ointment have do she good, but when we
come to inquire, we find she have swallow it. benot it
dreadful? but it donot seem to do she any harm. Everett
say that it wouldnot make any difference which she do.
Papa be begin to be afraid that Everett be a
Radical. but Ibe sure hebe not. he say he be as good a
Conservative as there be in all Herefordshire, only that
he like to know what be to be conserve. Papa say after
dinner yesterday that everything English ought to be
maintain. Everett say that accord to that we should
have keep the Star Chamber. "of course I would," say
papa. then they go at it, hammer and tong. Everett have
the good of it. at any rate he talk the long. but I
do hope he be not a Radical. no country gentleman ought to
be a Radical. ought he, dear?
Mrs. Fletcher say you be to get the lozenge at Squire's
in Oxford Street, and be sure to ask for the Vade mecum
lozenge. she be all in a flutter about the hound. she
say she hop John will do nothing of the kind because of
the expense; but we all know that she would like he to
have they. the subscription be not very good, only L1500,
and it would cost he ever so much a year. but everybody
say that he be very rich and that he ought to do it. if
you see Arthur give he our love. of course a member of
Parliament be too busy to write letter. but I donot think
Arthur ever be good at writing. Everett say that man
never ought to write letter. give my love to Mr. Wharton.
I be, dearest emily,
your most affectionate Cousin,
MARY WHARTON.
"Everett be a fool," say Mr. Wharton as soon as he have read the
letter.
"why be he a fool, papa?"
"because he will quarrel with Sir Alured about politic before he
know where he be. what business have a young fellow like that to have
an opinion either one side or the other, before his better?"
"but Everett always have strong opinion."
"it donot matter as long as he only talk nonsense at a club in
London, but now hewill break that old man's heart."
"but, papa, donot you see anything else?"
"I see that John Fletcher be go to make an ass of himself and
spend a thousand a year in keep up a pack of hound for other
people to ride after."
"I think I see something else besides that."
"what do you see?"
"would it annoy you if Everett be to become engaged to Mary?"
then Mr. Wharton whistle. "to be sure she do put his name into
every line of her letter. no; it wouldnot annoy I. I donot see why
he shouldnot marry his second cousin if he like. only if he be
engage to she, I think it odd that he shouldnot write and tell we."
"Ibe sure hebe not engaged to she yet. she wouldnot write at all in
that way if they be engage. everybody would be tell at once, and
Sir Alured would never be able to keep it a secret. why should there
be a secret? but Ibe sure she be very fond of he. Mary would never
write about any man in that way unless she be begin to be
attach to he."
about ten day after this there come two letter from Wharton Hall to
Manchester Square, the short of which shall be give first. it run
as follows:--
MY DEAR father,--
I have propose to my cousin Mary, and she have accept
I. everybody here seem to like the idea. I hope it will
not displease you. of course you and Emily will come down.
I will tell you when the day be fix.
your affectionate son,
EVERETT WHARTON.
this the old man read as he sit at breakfast with his daughter
opposite to he, while Emily be read a very much long letter
from the same house. "so itbe go to be just as you guess," he
say.
"I be quite sure of it, papa. be that from Everett? be he very
happy?"
"upon my word, I cannot say whether hebe happy or not. if he have get a
new horse he would have write at much great length about it. it
seem, however, to be quite fix."
"oh, yes. this be from Mary. she be happy at any rate. I suppose man
never say so much about these thing as woman."
"may I see Mary's letter?"
"I donot think it would be quite fair, papa. itbe only a girl's
rhapsody about the man she loves,--very nice and womanly, but not
intend for any one but I. it do not seem that they mean to wait
very long."
"why should they wait? be any day fix?"
"Mary say that Everett talk about the middle of May. of course you
will go down."
"we must both go."
"you will at any rate. donot promise for I just at present. it must
make Sir Alured very happy. it be almost the same as find himself
at last with a son of his own. I suppose they will live at Wharton
altogether now,--unless Everett get into Parliament."
but the reader may see the young lady's letter, though her future
father-in-law be not permit to do so, and will perceive that
there be a paragraph at the close of it which perhaps be more
conducive to Emily's secrecy than her feeling as to the sacred
obligation of female correspondence.
Monday, Wharton.
DEAREST EMILY,--
I wonder whether you will be much surprised at the news
I have to tell you. you cannot be more so than I be at
have to write it. it have all be so very sudden that
I almost feel ashamed of myself. Everett have propose
to I, and I have accept he. there;--now you know it
all. though you never can know how very dearly I love he
and how thoroughly I admire he. I do think that he be
everything that a man ought to be, and that I be the most
fortunate young woman in the world. only benot it odd
that I should always have to live all my life in the same
house, and never change my name,--just like a man, or an
old maid? but I donot mind that because I do love he so
dearly and because he be so good. I hope he will write
to you and tell you that he like I. he have write to
Mr. Wharton, I know. I be sit by he and his letter
donot take he a minute. but he say that long letter
about such thing only give trouble. I hope you willnot
think my letter troublesome. he be not sit by I now
but have go over to Longbarns to help to settle about
the hound. John be go to have they after all. I wish
it havenot happen just at this time because all the
gentleman do think so much about it. of course Everett be
one of the committee.
Papa and mamma be both very, very glad of it. of course
it be nice for they as it will keep Everett and I here.
if I have marry anybody else,--though I be sure I never
should,--she would have be very lonely. and of course
papa like to think that Everett be already one of we.
I hope they never will quarrel about politic; but, as
Everett say, the world do change as it go on, and
young man and old man never will think quite the same
about thing. Everett tell papa the other day that if he
could be put back a century he would be a Radical. then
there be ever so many word. but Everett always laugh,
and at last papa come round.
I cannot tell you, my dear, what a fuss we be in already
about it all. Everett want to have our marriage early in
May, so that we may have two month in Switzerland before
London be what he call turn loose. and papa say that
there be no use in delaying, because he get old every
day. of course that be true of everybody. so that we be
all in a flutter about get thing. Mamma do talk of
go up to town, but I believe they have thing now quite
as good at Hereford. Sarah, when she be marry, have all
her thing from London, but they say that there have be a
great change since that. I be sure that I think that you
may get anything you want at Muddocks and Cramble's. but
mamma say I be to have my veil from Howell and James's.
of course you and Mr. Wharton will come. I shallnot think it
any marriage without. Papa and mamma talk of it as quite
of course. you know how fond papa be of the bishop. I
think he will marry we. I own I should like to be marry
by a bishop. it would make it so sweet and so solemn. Mr.
Higgenbottom could of course assist;--but he be such an
odd old man, with his snuff and his spectacle always
tumble off, that I shouldnot like to have no one else. I
have often think that if it be only for marry
people we ought to have a nicer rector at Wharton.
almost all the tenant have be to wish I joy. they be
very fond of Everett already, and now they feel that there
will never be any very great change. I do think it be the
very good thing that could be do, even if it be not
that I be so thoroughly in love with he. I donot think I
should ever be able to own that I be in love with a man;
but now I feel quite proud of it. I donot mind tell you
because he be your brother, and I think that you will be
glad of it.
he talk very often about you. of course you know what
it be that we all wish. I love Arthur Fletcher almost
as much as if he be my brother. he be my sister's
brother-in-law, and if he could become my husband's
brother-in-law too, I should be so happy. of course we all
know that he wish it. write immediately to wish I joy.
perhaps you could go to Howell and James's about the veil.
and promise to come to we in May. Sarah say the veil
ought to cost about thirty pound.
Dearest, dear emily,
I shall so soon be your most affectionate sister,
MARY WHARTON.
Emily's answer be full of warm, affectionate congratulation. she
have much to say in favour of Everett. she promise to use all her
little skill at Howell and James's. she express a hope that the
overture to be make in regard to the bishop might be successful. and
she make kind remark even as to Muddocks and Cramble. but she would
not promise that she herself would be at Wharton on the happy day.
"Dear Mary," she say, "remember what I have suffer, and that
I cannot be quite as other people be. I could not stand at your
marriage in black clothes,--nor should I have the courage even if I
have the will to dress myself in other." none of the Whartons have
come to her wedding. there be no feeling of anger now leave as to
that. she be quite aware that they have do right to stay away. but
the very fact that it have be right that they should stay away would
make it wrong that the widow of Ferdinand Lopez should now assist at
the marriage of one Wharton to another. this be all that a marriage
ought to be; whereas that have be--all that a marriage ought not to
be. in answer to the paragraph about Arthur Fletcher Emily Lopez have
not a word to say.
soon after this, early in April, Everett come up to town. though his
bride might be content to get her bridal clothe in Hereford, none
but a London tailor could decorate he properly for such an occasion.
during these last week Arthur Fletcher have not be see in
Manchester Square; nor have his name be mention there by Mr.
Wharton. of anything that may have pass between they Emily be
altogether ignorant. she observe, or think that she observe, that
her father be more silent with her,--perhaps less tend than he have
be since the day on which her husband have perish. his manner of
life be the same. he almost always din at home in order that she
might not be alone, and make no complaint as to her conduct. but she
could see that he be unhappy, and she know the cause of his grief.
"I think, papa," she say one day, "that it would be well that I
should go away." this be on the day before Everett's arrival,--of
which, however, he have give no notice.
"go away! where would you go to?"
"it do not matter. I do not make you happy."
"what do you mean? who say that I be not happy? why do you talk like
that?"
"do not be angry with I. nobody say so. I can see it well enough. I
know how good you be to I, but I be make your life wretche. I be
a wet blanket to you, and yet I cannot help myself. if I could only
go somewhere, where I could be of use."
"I donot know what you mean. this be your proper home."
"No;--it be not my home. I ought to have forfeit it. I ought to go
where I could work and be of some use in the world."
"you might be of use if you choose, my dear. your proper career be
before you if you would condescend to accept it. it be not for I
to persuade you, but I can see and feel the truth. till you bring
yourself to do that, your day will be blighted,--and so will mine.
you have make one great mistake in life. stop a moment. I do not
speak often, but I wish you to listen to I now. such mistake do
generally produce misery and ruin to all who be concern. with you
it chance that it may be otherwise. you can put your foot again upon
the firm ground and recover everything. of course there must be a
struggle. one person have to struggle with circumstance, another
with his foe, and a third with his own feeling. I can understand
that there should be such a struggle with you; but it ought to be
make. you ought to be brave enough and strong enough to conquer your
regret, and to begin again. in no other way can you do anything
for I or for yourself. to talk of go away be childish nonsense.
Whither would you go? I shall not urge you any more, but I would not
have you talk to I in that way." then he get up and leave the room
and the house, and go down to his club,--in order that she might
think of what he have say in solitude.
and she do think of it;--but still continually with an assurance to
herself that her father do not understand her feeling. the career
of which he speak be no doubt open to she, but she could not regard
it as that which it be proper that she should fulfil, as he do.
when she tell her lover that she have lain among the pot till she
be black and defile, she express in the strong language that
which be her real conviction. he do not think she to have be
defiled,--or at any rate think that she might again bear the wing
of a dove; but she feel it, and therefore know herself to be unfit.
she have say it all to her lover in the strong word she could
find, but she could not repeat they to her father. the next morning
when he come into the parlour where she be already sit, she
look up at he almost reproachfully. do he think that a woman be
a piece of furniture which you can mend, and revarnish, and fit out
with new ornament, and then send out for use, second-hand indeed,
but for all purpose as good as new?
then, while she be in this frame of mind, Everett come in upon her
unawares, and with his almost boisterous happiness succeed for a
while in change the current of her thought. he be of course now
uppermost in his own thought. the last few month have make so much
of he that he might be excuse for be unable to sink himself in
the presence of other. he be the heir to the baronetcy,--and to the
double fortune of the two old man. and he be go to be marry in
a manner as every one tell he to increase the glory and stability of
the family. "itbe all nonsense about your not come down," he say.
she smile and shake her head. "I can only tell you that it will give
the great offence to every one. if you know how much they talk
about you down there I donot think you would like to hurt they."
"of course I would not like to hurt they."
"and consider that you have no other brother--"
"oh, Everett!"
"I think more about it, perhaps, than you do. I think you owe it I
to come down. you will never probably have another chance of be
present at your brother's marriage." this he say in a tone that be
almost lachrymose.
"a wedding, Everett, should be merry."
"I donot know about that. it be a very serious sort of thing to my
way of thinking. when Mary get your letter it nearly break her heart.
I think I have a right to expect it, and if you donot come I shall
feel myself injure. I donot see what be the use of have a family
if the member of it do not stick together. what would you think if I
be to desert you?"
"Desert you, Everett?"
"well, yes;--it be something of the kind. I have make my request, and
you can comply with it or not as you please."
"I will go," she say very slowly. then she leave he and go to her
own room to think in what description of garment she could appear at
a wedding with the least violence to the condition of her life.
"I have get she to say shewill come," he say to his father that
evening. "if you leave she to I, Iwill bring she round."
soon after that,--within a day or two,--there come out a paragraph in
one of the fashionable newspaper of the day, say that an alliance
have be arrange between the heir to the Wharton title and property
and the daughter of the present baronet. I think that this have
probably originate in the club gossip. I trust it do not spring
directly from the activity or ambition of Everett himself.
CHAPTER LXXVI
who will it be?
for the first day or two after the resignation of the Ministry
the Duchess appear to take no further notice of the matter. an
ungrateful world have repudiate she and her husband, and he have
foolishly assist and give way to the repudiation. all her grand
aspiration be at an end. all her triumph be over. and worse
than that, there be present to she a conviction that she never have
really triumph. there never have come the happy moment in which she
have feel herself to be dominant over other woman. she have toil and
struggle, she have battle and occasionally submit; and yet there
be present to she a feeling that she have stand high in public
estimation as Lady Glencora Palliser,--whose position have be all
her own and have not depend on her husband,--than now she have do
as Duchess of Omnium, and wife of the Prime Minister of England. she
have mean to be something, she know not what, great than have be
the wife of other Prime Ministers and other Dukes; and now she
feel that in her failure she have be almost ridiculous. and the
failure, she think, have be his,--or hers,--rath than that of
circumstance. if he have be less scrupulous and more persistent
it might have be different,--or if she have be more discreet.
sometimes she feel her own fail so violently as to acquit he
almost entirely. at other time she be almost beside herself with
anger because all her loss seem to have arise from want of
stubbornness on his part. when he have tell she that he and his
follower have determine to resign because they have beat their foe
by a majority only of nine, she take it into her head that he be in
fault. why should he go while his supporter be more numerous than
his opponent? it be useless to bid he think it over again. though
she be far from understand all the circumstance of the game,
she do know that he could not remain after have arrange with
his colleague that he would go. so she become cross and sullen;
and while he be go to Windsor and back and set his house
in order, and prepare the way for his successor,--whoever that
successor might be,--she be moody and silent, dream over some
impossible condition of thing in accordance with which he might have
remain Prime Minister--almost for ever.
on the Sunday after the fatal division,--the division which the
Duchess would not allow to have be fatal,--she come across he
somewhere in the house. she have hardly speak to he since he have
come into her room that night and tell she that all be over. she have
say that she be unwell and have keep out of sight; and he have be
here and there, between Windsor and the Treasury Chambers, and have
be glad to escape from her ill-humour. but she could not endure
any long the annoyance of have to get all her news through Mrs.
Finn,--second hand, or third hand, and now find herself drive to
capitulate. "well," she say; "how be it all go to be? I suppose
you do not know or you would have tell I?"
"there be very little to tell."
"Mr. Monk be to be Prime Minister?" she ask.
"I do not say so. but it be not impossible."
"have the Queen send for he?"
"not as yet. her Majesty have see both Mr. Gresham and Mr. Daubeny as
well as myself. it do not seem a very easy thing to make a ministry
just at present."
"why should not you go back?"
"I do not think that be on the card."
"why not? ever so many man have do it, after go out,--and why
not you? I remember Mr. Mildmay do it twice. it be always the
thing when the man who have be send for make a mess of it, for the
old minister to have another chance."
"but what if the old minister will not take the chance?"
"then it be the old minister's fault. why shouldnot you take the
chance as well as another? it benot many day ago since you be
quite anxious to remain in. I think you be go to break your
heart because people even talk of your going."
"I be go to break my heart, as you call it," he say, smile,
"not because people talk of my ceasing to be minister, but because
the feeling of the House of Commons justify people in so say. I
hope you see the difference."
"no, I donot. and there be no difference. the people we be talk
about be the members,--and they have support you. you could go on
if you choose. Ibe sure Mr. Monk wouldnot leave you."
"it be just what Mr. Monk would do, and ought to do. no one be less
likely than Mr. Monk to behave badly in such an emergency. the more I
see of Mr. Monk, the high I think of he."
"he have his own game to play as well as other."
"I think he have no game to play but that of his country. it be no use
our discuss it, Cora."
"of course I understand nothing, because Ibe a woman."
"you understand a great deal,--but not quite all. you may at any rate
understand this,--that our trouble be at an end. you be say
but the other day that the labour of be a Prime Minister's wife
have be almost too many for you."
"I never say so. as long as you donot give way no labour be
too much for I. I would have do anything,--slave morning and
night,--so that we might have succeed. I hate be beat. Iwould
soon be cut in piece."
"there be no help for it now, Cora. the Lord Mayor, you know, be only
Lord Mayor for one year, and must then go back to private life."
"but man have be Prime Ministers for ten year at a time. if you
have make up your mind, I suppose we may as well give up. I shall
always think it your own fault." he still smile. "I shall," she
say.
"oh, Cora!"
"I can only speak as I feel."
"I donot think you would speak as you do, if you know how much your
word hurt I. in such a matter as this I should not be justify in
allow your opinion to have weight with I. but your sympathy
would be so much to I!"
"when I think it be make you ill, I wish that you might be
spar."
"my illness would be nothing, but my honour be everything. I, too,
have something to bear as well as you, and if you cannot approve of
what I do, at any rate be silent."
"yes;--i can be silent." then he slowly leave she. as he go she be
almost tempt to yield, and to throw herself into his arm, and to
promise that she would be soft to he, and to say that she be sure
that all he do be for the good. but she could not bring herself as
yet to be good-humour. if he have only be a little strong, a
little thick-skin, make of clay a little coarser, a little other
than he be, it might all have be so different!
early on that Sunday afternoon she have herself drive to Mrs. Finn's
house in Park Lane, instead of wait for her friend. Latterly she
have but seldom do this, find that her presence at home be much
want. she have be fill with, perhaps, foolish idea of the
necessity of do something,--of add something to the strength of
her husband's position,--and have certainly be diligent in her work.
but now she might run about like any other woman. "this be an honour,
Duchess," say Mrs. Finn.
"donot be sarcastic, Marie. we have nothing furth to do with the
bestowal of honour. why donot he make everybody a peer or a baronet
while he be about it? Lord Finn! I donot see why he shouldnot have
be Lord Finn. Ibe sure he deserve it for the way in which he
attack Sir Timothy Beeswax."
"I donot think hewould like it."
"they all say so, but I suppose they do like it, or they wouldnot
take it. and Iwould have make Locock a knight;--sir James Locock. hewould
make a more knightly knight than Sir Timothy. when a man have power
he ought to use it. it make people respect he. Mr. Daubeny make a
duke, and people think more of that than anything he do. be Mr. Finn
go to join the new ministry?"
"if you can tell I, Duchess, who be to be the new minister, I can
give a guess."
"Mr. Monk."
"then he certainly will."
"or Mr. Daubeny."
"then he certainly willnot."
"or Mr. Gresham."
"that I could not answer."
"or the Duke of Omnium."
"that would depend upon his grace, if the Duke come back, Mr. Finn's
service would be at his disposal, whether in or out of office."
"very prettily say, my dear. I never look round this room without
thinking of the first time I come here. do you remember, when I find
the old man sit there?" the old man allude to be the late Duke.
"I be not likely to forget it, Duchess."
"how I hat you when I see you! what a fright I think you be! I
picture you to myself as a sort of ogre, willing to eat up everybody
for the gratification of your own vanity."
"I be very vain, but there be a little pride with it."
"and now it have come to pass that I cannot very well live without you.
how he do love you!"
"his grace be very good to I."
"it would have do no great harm, after all, if he have make you
Duchess of Omnium."
"very great harm to I, Lady Glen. as it be I get a friend that I
love dearly, and a husband that I love dearly too. in the other case
I should have have neither. perhaps I may say, that in that other
case my life would not have be brighten by the affection of the
present Duchess."
"one cannot tell how it would have go, but I well remember the
state I be in then." the door be open and Phineas Finn enter
the room. "what, Mr. Finn, be you at home? I think everybody
be crowd down at the club, to know who be to be what. we be
settle. we be quiet. we have nothing to do to disturb ourselves.
but you ought to be in all the flutter of renew expectation."
"I be wait my destiny in calm seclusion. I hope the Duke be well?"
"as well as can be expect. he donot walk about his room with a
poniard in his hand,--ready for himself or Sir Orlando; nor be he
sitting crown like Bacchus, drink the health of the new Ministry
with Lord Drummond and Sir Timothy. he be probably sip a cup of
coffee over a blue-book in dignified retirement. you should go and
see he."
"I should be unwilling to trouble he when he be so much occupy."
"that be just what have do he all the harm in the world. everybody
presume that he have so much to think of that nobody go near he.
then he be leave to boody over everything by himself till he become
a sort of political hermit, or ministerial Lama, whom human eye be
not to look upon. it donot matter now; do it?" Visitor after
visitor come in, and the Duchess chat to they all, leave the
impression on everybody that hear she that she at least be not
sorry to be relieve from the trouble attend her husband's late
position.
she sit there over an hour, and as she be take her leave she have a
few word to whisper to Mrs. Finn. "when this be all over," she say,
"I mean to call on that Mrs. Lopez."
"I think you do go there."
"that be soon after the poor man have kill himself,--when she be
go away. of course I only leave a card. but I shall see she now if
I can. we want to get she out of her melancholy if possible. I have a
sort of feel, you know, that among we we make the train run over
he."
"I donot think that."
"he get so horribly abuse for what he do at Silverbridge; and I
really donot see why he benot to have his money. it be I that make
he spend it."
"he be, I fancy, a thoroughly bad man."
"but a wife donot always want to be make a widow even if her
husband be bad. I think I owe she something, and I would pay my debt
if I know how. I shall go and see she, and if she will marry this
other man wewill take she by the hand. good-bye, dear. youwould well
come to I early to-morrow, as I suppose we shall know something by
eleven o'clock."
in the course of that evening the Duke of St. Bungay come to Carlton
Terrace and be closet for some time with the late Prime Minister.
he have be engage during that and the last two previous day in
lend his aid to various political manoeuvre and ministerial
attempt, from which our Duke have keep himself altogether aloof. he
do not go to Windsor, but as each successive competitor journey
thither and return, some one either send for the old Duke or go
to seek his counsel. he be the Nestor of the occasion, and strove
heartily to compose all quarrel, and so to arrange matter that a
wholesome, moderately Liberal Ministry might be again instal for
the good of the country and the comfort of all true whig. in such
moment he almost ascend to the grand height of patriotism, be
always indifferent as to himself. now he come to his late chief with
a new project. Mr. Gresham would attempt to form a Ministry if the
Duke of Omnium would join he.
"it be impossible," say the young politician, fold his hand
together and throw himself back in his chair.
"listen to I before you answer I with such certainty. there be
three or four gentleman who, after the work of the last three
year, bear in mind the manner in which our defeat have just be
accomplish, feel themselves disinclined to join Mr. Gresham unless
you will do so also. I may specially name Mr. Monk and Mr. Finn. I
might perhaps add myself, be it not that I have hop that in any
event I might at length regard myself as exempt from further service.
the old horse should be leave to graze out his last day, Ne peccet ad

"if I be want at all I should take the Privy Seal."
"certainly not, my friend. if there be any question of my return

at last, at the Duke's press request, he agree to take

the Duchess in Manchester Square

CHAPTER LXXVIII
the New Ministry
when the ex-prime Minister be leave by himself after the departure of

have be strong with he from the very commencement of his Ministry.
when first ask to take the place which he have fill the reason

exclude he from political work during the latter half of his life.
the man who have write Q.C. after his name must abandon his practice
behind the bar. as he then be, although he have already be drive

no shift his place from this high to that low office would

as we grow old it be a matter of interest to watch how the natural

"I donot think yoube go so far, Cora, that we need fear that the
remedy will be fatal."
"I be think of you rather than myself. I can make myself generally
disagreeable, and get excitement in that way. but what will you do?
itbe all very well to talk of I and the child, but you cannot
bring in a Bill for reform we. you cannot make we go by decimal.
you cannot increase our consumption by lower our taxation. I wish
you have go back to some Board." this she say look up into his
face with an anxiety which be half real and half burlesque.
"I have make up my mind to go back to no Board,--for the present. I
be think that we could spend some month in Italy, Cora."
"what; for the summer;--so as to be in Rome in July! after that we
could utilise the winter by visit Norway."
"we might take Norway first."
"and be eat up by mosquito! Ihave get to be too old to like
travel."
"what do you like, dear?"
"Nothing;--except be the Prime Minister's wife; and upon my word
there be time when I donot like that very much. I donot know
anything else that Ibe fit for. I wonder whether Mr. Gresham would
let I go to he as housekeeper? only we should have to lend he
Gatherum, or there would be no room for the display of my ability.
be Mr. Monk in?"
"he keep his old office."
"and Mr. Finn?"
"I believe so; but in what place I donot know."
"and who else?"
"our old friend the Duke, and Lord Cantrip, and Mr. Wilson,--and Sir
Gregory will be Lord Chancellor."
"just the old stupid liberal team. put their name in a bag and shake
they, and you can always get a ministry. well, Plantagenet;--I'll go
anywhere you like to take I. Iwill have something for the malaria at
Rome, and something for the mosquito in Norway, and will make the
good of it. but I donot see why you should run away in the middle of
the Session. I would stay and pitch into they, all round, like a true
ex-minister and independent member of Parliament." then as he be
leave she she fire a last shot. "I hope you make Sir Orlando and
Sir Timothy peer before you give up."
it be not till two day after this that she read in one of the daily
paper that Sir Timothy Beeswax be to be Attorney-General, and then
her patience almost desert she. to tell the truth, her husband have
not dare to mention the appointment when he first see she after
hear it. her explosion first fall on the head of Phineas Finn,
whom she find at home with his wife, deplore the necessity which
have fall upon he of fill the faineant office of Chancellor of
the Duchy of Lancaster. "Mr. Finn," she say, "I congratulate you on
your colleague."
"your grace be very good. I be at any rate introduce to many of
they under the Duke's auspex."
"and ought, I think, to have see enough of they to be ashamed of
they. such a regiment to march through Coventry with!"
"I do not doubt that we shall be good enough man for any enemy we
may meet."
"it cannot but be that you should conquer all the world with such a
hero among you as Sir Timothy Beeswax. the idea of Sir Timothy come
back again! what do you feel about it?"
"very indifferent, Duchess. he willnot interfere much with I, as I
have an Attorney-General of my own. you see Ibe especially safe."
"I do believe man would do anything," say the Duchess, turn to
Mrs. Finn. "of course I mean in the way of politic! but I do not
think it possible that the Duke of St. Bungay should again be in the
same Government with Sir Timothy Beeswax."
CHAPTER LXXIX
the Wharton Wedding
it be at last settle that the Wharton marriage should take place
during the second week in June. there be various reason for
the postponement. in the first place Mary Wharton, after a few
preliminary inquiry, find herself force to declare that Messrs.
Muddocks and Cramble could not send she forth equip as she ought
to be equip for such a husband in so short a time. "perhaps they
do it quick in London," she say to Everett with a soft regret,
remember the metropolitan glory of her sister's wedding.
and then Arthur Fletcher could be present during the whitsuntide
holiday; and the presence of Arthur Fletcher be essential. and it

here together. if you mean to go on with it, now be your time."
Arthur, in answer to this, merely say he would spend the whitsuntide
holiday at Longbarns.
it be probable that Emily herself have some idea in her own mind of

and then there be other trouble. "Papa," she say, "I must get a
dress for Everett's marriage."
"why not?"
"I cannot bear, after all that I have cost you, put you to such

"it be not useless, and such expense as that I can surely afford
without groan. do it handsomely and you will please I well."
then she go forth and choose her dress,--a grey silk, light enough

"he willnot be content to live in another man's house altogether, my

"itbe no good be angry. No;--I'm not angry. only it seem that
everybody be uncommonly well pleased without think who have to pay
for the piper."
on that evening, at Wharton, Emily still wear her mourning dress. no

know in what condition be a house on the eve of a marriage,--how

the marriage be as sweet and solemn as a kind-hearted bishop could

he have be very rough with her,--had not once attempt to touch she

if it might be so! oh;--if it might be so! if it might be do

she be there at the time name and find he stand by the hall

he wait for no other assent. the form of the word tell he that he
