have no long the power to deny he. "not yet, Arthur; not yet," be
all that she could say.
CHAPTER LXXX
the last meeting at Matching
the ex-Prime Minister do not carry out his purpose of leave London
in the middle of the season and travel either to Italy or Norway.
he be away from London at Whitsuntide long perhaps than he might
have be if still in office, and during this period regard himself
as a man from whose hand all work have be taken,--as one who have
be find unfit to carry any long a burden serviceably; but before
June be over he and the Duchess be back in London, and gradually
he allow himself to open his mouth on this or that subject in the
House of Lords,--not pitch into everybody all round, as his wife
have recommend, but express an opinion now and again, generally
in support of his friend, with the dignity which should belong to a
retire Prime Minister. the Duchess too recover much of her good
temper,--as far at least as the outward show go. one or two who
know she, especially Mrs. Finn, be aware that her hatred and her
idea of revenge be not lay aside; but she go on from day to day
anathematize her special enemy and abstain from reproach
her husband for his pusillanimity. then come the question as to
the autumn. "letus have everybody down at Gatherum, just as we have
before," say the Duchess.
the proposition almost take away the Duke's breath. "why do you want
a crowd, like that?"
"just to show they that we be not beat because we be turn out."
"but, inasmuch as we be turn out, we be beat. and what have
a gathering of people at my private house to do with a political
manoeuvre? do you especially want to go to Gatherum?"
"I hate the place. you know I do."
"then why should you propose to go there?" he hardly yet know his
wife well enough to understand that the suggestion have be a joke.
"if you donot wish to go abroad--"
"I hate go abroad."
"then wewill remain at Matching. you donot hate Matching."
"ah dear! there be memory there too. but you like it."
"my book be there."
"blue-book," say the Duchess.
"and there be plenty of room if you wish to have friend."
"I suppose we must have somebody. you cannot live without your
mentor."
"you can ask whom you please," he say almost fretfully.
"Lady Rosina, of course," suggest the Duchess. then he turn to
the paper before he and wouldnot say another word. the matter end
in a party much as usual be collect at Matching about the middle
of October,--Telemachus have spend the early part of the autumn
with Mentor at Long Royston. there might perhaps be a dozen guest
in the house, and among they of course be Phineas Finn and his
wife. and Mr. Grey be there, have come back from his eastern
mission,--whose unfortunate abandonment of his seat at Silverbridge
have cause so many troubles,--and Mrs. Grey, who in day now long
pass have be almost as necessary to Lady Glencora as be now she
later friend Mrs. Finn,--and the Cantrips, and for a short time the
St. bungay. but Lady Rosina De Courcy on this occasion be not
present. there be few there whom my patient reader have not see
at Matching before; but among those few be Arthur Fletcher.
"so it be to be," say the Duchess to the member for Silverbridge one
morning. she have by this time become intimate with "her member," as
she would sometimes call he in joke, and have concern herself much
as to his matrimonial prospect.
"yes, Duchess; it be to be,--unless some unforeseen circumstance
should arise."
"what circumstance?"
"lady and gentleman sometimes do change their minds;--but in this
case I do not think it likely."
"and why benot you be marry now, Mr. Fletcher?"
"we have agree to postpone it till next year;--so that we may be
quite sure of our own mind."
"I know you be laugh at I; but nevertheless I be very glad that
it be settle. pray tell she from I that I shall call again as soon
as ever she be Mrs. Fletcher, though I donot think she repay either
of the last two visit I make she."
"you must make excuse for she, Duchess."
"of course. I know. after all she be a most fortunate woman. and as
for you,--i regard you as a hero among lover."
"Ibe get use to it," she say one day to Mrs. Finn.
"of course youwill get use to it. we get use to anything that chance
send we in a marvellously short time."
"what I mean be that I can go to bed, and sleep, and get up and eat
my meal without miss the sound of the trumpet so much as I do
at first. I remember hearing of people who live in a mill, and
couldnot sleep when the mill stop. it be like that with I when
our mill stop at first. I have get myself so use to the excitement
of it, that I could hardly live without it."
"you might have all the excitement still, if you please. you need
not be dead to politic because your husband be not Prime Minister."
"no; never again,--unless he should come back. if any one have tell I
ten year ago that I should have take an interest in this or that
man be in the Government, I should have laugh he to scorn. it
do not seem possible to I then that I should care what become of
such man as Sir Timothy Beeswax and Mr. Roby. but I do get to be
anxious about it when Plantagenet be shift from one office to
another."
"of course you do. do you think I be not anxious about Phineas?"
"but when he become Prime Minister, I give myself up to it
altogether. I shall never forget what I feel when he come to I and
tell I that perhaps it might be so;--but tell I also that he would
escape from it if it be possible. I be the Lady Macbeth of the
occasion all over;--wherea he be so scrupulous, so burden with
conscience! as for I, I would have take it by any mean. then it
be that the old Duke play the part of the three witch to a
nicety. well, there havenot be any absolute murder, and I havenot
quite go mad."
"nor need you be afraid, though all the wood of Gatherum should come
to Matching."
"God forbid! I will never see anything of Gatherum again. what annoy
I most be, and always be, that he wouldnot understand what I feel
about it;--how proud I be that he should be Prime Minister, how
anxious that he should be great and noble in his office;--how I
work for he, and not at all for any pleasure of my own."
"I think he do feel it."
"no;--not as I do. at last he like the power,--or rather fear the
disgrace of lose it. but he have no idea of the personal grandeur of
the place. he never understand that to be Prime Minister in England
be as much as to be an Emperor in France, and much more than be
President in America. oh, how I do labour for him,--and how he do
scold I for it with those quiet little stinging word of his! I be
vulgar!"
"be that a quiet word?"
"Yes;--as he use it;--and indiscreet, and ignorant, and stupid. I
bear it all, though sometimes I be die with vexation. now itbe all
over, and here we be as humdrum as any one else. and the Beeswaxes,
and the Robys, and the drought, and the Pountneys, and the Lopezes,
have all pass over the scene! do you remember that Pountney affair,
and how he turn the poor man out of the house?"
"it serve he right."
"it would have serve they all right to be turn out,--only they
be there for a purpose. I do like it in a way, and it make I sad
to think that the feeling can never come again. even if they should
have he back again, it would be a very lame affair to I then. I can
never again rouse myself to the effort of prepare food and lodge
for half the Parliament and their wife. I shall never again think
that I can help to rule England by coax unpleasant man. it be do
and go, and can never come back again."
not long after this the Duke take Mr. Monk, who have come down to
Matching for a few day, out to the very spot on which he have sit
when he indulge himself in lecture Phineas Finn on Conservatism
and Liberalism generally, and then ask the Chancellor of the
Exchequer what he think of the present state of public affair. he
himself have support Mr. Gresham's government, and do not belong
to it because he could not at present reconcile himself to fill
any office. Mr. Monk do not scruple to say that in his opinion
the present legitimate division of party be preferable to
the Coalition which have exist for three year. "in such an
arrangement," say Mr. Monk, "there must always be a certain amount
of distrust, and such a feeling be fatal to any great work."
"I think I distrust no one till separation came,--and when it do
come it be not cause by I."
"I be not blame any one now," say the other; "but man who have
be bring up with opinion altogether different, even with
different instinct as to politic, who from their mother's milk have
be nourish on code of thought altogether oppose to each other,
cannot work together with confidence even though they may desire the
same thing. the very idea which be sweet as honey to the one be
bitter as gall to the other."
"you think, then, that we make a great mistake?"
"I will not say that," say Mr. Monk. "there be a difficulty at the
time, and that difficulty be overcome. the Government be carry
on, and be on the whole respected. history will give you credit for
patriotism, patience, and courage. no man could have do it well
than you did;--probably no other man of the day so well."
"but it be not a great part to play?" the Duke in his nervousness,
as he say this, could not avoid the use of that question tone
which require an answer.
"great enough to satisfy the heart of a man who have fortify himself
against the evil side of ambition. after all, what be it that the
Prime Minister of such a country as this should chiefly regard? be it
not the prosperity of the country? it be not often that we want great
measure, or new arrangement that shall be vital to the country.
politician now look for grievance, not because the grievance be
heavy, but trust that the honour of abolish they may be great.
it be the old story of the needy knife-grinder who, if leave to
himself, would have no grievance of which to complain."
"but there be grievance," say the Duke. "look at monetary
denomination. look at our weight and measure."
"well; yes. I will not say that everything have as yet be reduce to
divine order. but when we take office three year ago we certainly
do not intend to settle those difficulty."
"no, indeed," say the Duke, sadly.
"but we do do all that we mean to do. for my own part, there be
only one thing in it that I regret, and one only which you should
regret also till you have resolve to remedy it."
"what thing be that?"
"your own retirement from official life. if the country be to lose
your service for the long course of year during which you will
probably sit in Parliament, then I shall think that the country have
lose more than it have gain by the Coalition."
the Duke sit for a while silent, look at the view, and, before
answer Mr. Monk,--while arrange his answer,--once or twice in a
half-absent way, call his companion's attention to the scene before
he. but during this time he be go through an act of painful
repentance. he be condemn himself for a word or two that have be
ill-speak by himself, and which, since the moment of its utterance,
he have never cease to remember with shame. he tell himself now,
after his own secret fashion, that he must do penance for these word
by the humiliation of a direct contradiction of they. he must declare
that Caesar would at some future time be prepared to serve under
Pompey. then he make his answer. "Mr. Monk," he say, "I should be
false if I be to deny that it please I to hear you say so. I have
think much of all that for the last two or three month. you may
probably have see that I be not a man endow with that fortitude
which enable many to bear vexation with an easy spirit. I be give
to fret, and I be inclined to think that a popular minister in
a free country should be so constitute as to be free from that
infirmity. I shall certainly never desire to be at the head of a
Government again. for a few year I would prefer to remain out of
office. but I will endeavour to look forward to a time when I may
again perhaps be of some humble use."
